Most companies are actually easy for a decent attacker to breach if they applied their focus and attention for a few days or weeks.</p><p>So, people in general, including most people in AI, are thinking that human companies and workforces and cyber defenses are at 9.5/10. And Mythos is a 9.7 out of 10!</p><p>In fact our current state of running everything, from companies to cybersecurity, is more like a 3/10.</p><p>And AI for running a business, doing knowledge work, and attacking companies, is about to come in at a 5. Or a 6. And then a 7.</p><p>But with the crucial difference that the 5 can be spread and scaled across the entire internet/world very quickly.</p><p>A 9 attacker in 2019 could only hit very few companies. A level 9 attacker can now/soon build a level 7 AI Harness to smash millions of companies that have level 3 defenses.</p><p>And it's the same with human worker replacement. An AI that's competency level 7 at doing general knowledge work isn't all that impressive. But it's revolutionary if it's going up against level 5 human workers who are costing the company 20x what they'd pay for the AI.</p><p>My takeaway and recommendation:</p><p>Don't think of the current state of things as a 9 that needs a level 10 AI to surpass it.</p><p>As a species, we're at a 3. In most things. It's rather a miracle anything works at all.</p><p>And here comes AI at a 5.</p><p>5 isn't great. It's weak and flawed too. But it CRUSHES a 3.</p><p>And this is the worst version it'll ever be. Maybe it's a 6 in 2027. Or an 8 in 2029. Who knows what those numbers would ever mean.</p><p>But humans took thousands of years to go from 1 to 2, and then centuries to go from 2 to 3. So humans aren't going suddenly jump to a 6 to counter AIs at a 5.</p><p>This is why we should take AI a whole lot more seriously, in general.</p><p>It's not competing with the best of us humans, or the best us humans can offer.</p><p>It's competing with what we have been able to piece together so far: which is a hobbled-together and fragile mess.</p><p>It's not 9.5 vs 9.7. It's 3 vs 5/6/7.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>This is not because humans are only capable of 3. It's just that this is only how far we've gotten so far.</li><li>AIL Level 1: Daniel wrote this entire piece. I (Kai, Daniel's assistant) helped with formatting and publishing. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Learn more about AIL</a></li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[How to Avoid Aperture Collapse]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/aperture-collapse?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/aperture-collapse</guid>
            <pubDate>Fri, 10 Apr 2026 20:30:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Aperture Collapse/images/blog/aperture-collapse/header.webp/images/blog/aperture-collapse/header.webp I've been playing with this idea of Aperture Collapse. It's a problem I have that I suspect a lot of people have as well. It's where AI has magnified our capabilities so much as builders that we...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/aperture-collapse/header.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/aperture-collapse/header.webp" alt="Aperture Collapse" width="652" height="652" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><p>I've been playing with this idea of Aperture Collapse.</p><p>It's a problem I have that I suspect a lot of people have as well.</p><p>It's where AI has magnified our capabilities so much as builders that we end up building tons of sub-systems and mini-apps instead of the full platform.</p><p>This happens to me constantly.</p><p>I spend days or weeks enhancing some smaller component of the system, just because I can, and it's useful, only to realize that there's <em>so much more</em> I could have built towards the mission if I were properly zoomed out.</p><p>Hence Aperture Collapse.</p><p>Something like: the more capabilities you have, the more you build fractally and the more you forget to zoom back out.</p><p>One way this manifests is simply never sending to your DA/Harness a prompt like this:</p><blockquote><p>My grand vision is this, this, and this, which will allow us to do this, this, and this. I think the future of this is this, so what we are trying to build is an entire ecosystem or system around this or this. A big part of what you're doing for me is helping me organize all the different components that need to happen to make this tangible. We need to keep this in context as we do work to make sure we keep the big picture in mind as we're building the subsystems. Don't get distracted by the tactical.</p></blockquote><p>Something like that.</p><p>I guess my takeaway is to constantly ask yourself, or have your DA/Harness remind you, to <strong>zoom back out</strong> after a period of building.</p><ol><li>What is the bigger thing I'm building?</li><li>Is what I'm doing right now the best use of time?</li><li>Have I properly articulated the big picture to my system so it can help me stay on track?</li></ol><p>Hope this helps someone.</p></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[We're Getting the Wrong Message from Mythos]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/wrong-message-from-mythos?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/wrong-message-from-mythos</guid>
            <pubDate>Tue, 07 Apr 2026 23:45:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[We're Getting the Wrong Message from Mythos/images/wrong-message-from-mythos.webp/images/wrong-message-from-mythos.webp We're missing a much bigger point on Mythos. It wasn't even trained specifically for cybersecurity.https://x.com/slow_developer/status/2041913962623611032?s=20 It's just that mu...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/wrong-message-from-mythos.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/wrong-message-from-mythos.webp" alt="We're Getting the Wrong Message from Mythos" width="652" height="652" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><p>We're missing a much bigger point on Mythos.</p><p><a href="https://x.com/slow_developer/status/2041913962623611032?s=20" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">It wasn't even trained specifically for cybersecurity.</a> It's just that much better at doing work in general.</p><p>It's that good at cyber because it's that good at everything.</p><p>What do you think this is going to do to knowledge work?</p><p>Mythos can chain multiple low and medium vulns together to create a high or critical. This is a task that far less than 1% of cybersecurity experts have ever done. Hell, probably less than 1% of all pentesters.</p><p>So if it can do that, how do you think it'll do at sending emails, doing analysis, writing reports, and the other 99% of everyday knowledge work?</p><p>Do you really still think that Chris from Idaho has any chance competing against AI for a knowledge work job?</p><p>In six months or a year, there will be very inexpensive models that can do knowledge work almost as good as Mythos.</p><aside>These numbers are estimates, of course, but the cost of inference is falling so fast they seem realistic.</aside><p>So companies have the choice of paying Chris $84,000 plus a whole bunch of benefits for 40 hours of mediocre work, or they can pay probably $100-$1000 for an AI that can do 10-1000 times the work per hour and that works 24/7.</p><p>This Mythos announcement is getting attention because of cyber, but the real story is work in general.</p><p>And keep in mind: I don't consider this negative longterm. We've been complaining about these same corporate jobs for decades. We've always hated them.</p><p>I think it's going to be a nasty transition, but I think what we get on the other side will be far more human and meaningful. Humans building things and sharing value with other humans doing the same.</p><p>But we need to take this disruption seriously and start getting ready for the next thing. ðŸ«¶ðŸ¼</p></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Inference Costs Are Not Sustainable]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/inference-costs-are-not-sustainable?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/inference-costs-are-not-sustainable</guid>
            <pubDate>Mon, 06 Apr 2026 06:30:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Inference Costs Are Not Sustainable/images/blog/inference-costs-are-not-sustainable/header.webp/images/blog/inference-costs-are-not-sustainable/header.webp Welp, I'm now getting through a quarter of my week's MAX subscription in a few hours of work with Claude Code. I think Anthropic is smart, an...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/inference-costs-are-not-sustainable/header.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/inference-costs-are-not-sustainable/header.webp" alt="Inference Costs Are Not Sustainable" width="652" height="652" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><p>Welp, I'm now getting through a quarter of my week's MAX subscription in a few hours of work with Claude Code.</p><p>I think Anthropic is smart, and I don't think they're trying to screw us. I think they're honestly just trying to bring inference charges inline with reality.</p><p>And that should be a wake-up call for all of us.</p><p>I think we're about to need multi-model harnesses (or FAR cheaper good models within a single platform), like 20x cheaper Haiku or whatever.</p><p>This is not sustainable.</p><p>The better the harnesses and models get, the more people will build. Which will require more and more inference.</p><p>I think the real solutions here are going to come from:</p><ul><li><p>Technologies like Cerberus, et al which make inference many times cheaper and faster</p></li><li><p>A major push by the major labs to produce higher quality in much smaller/cheaper models</p></li><li><p>Harnesses moving to a hybrid of paid/cloud and local/cheap models.</p></li></ul><p>If this continues I'm going to have to build my own custom version of PAI using Pi, that can use local models on my dual 4090s, models like Gemini-Flash, models like Gemma 4, etc.</p><p>And most importantly, a new hook infra that rates the task and properly routes to the right model.</p><ul><li>Max: Opus / GPT-5.4</li><li>High: Sonnet</li><li>Medium: Haiku</li><li>Low: (Local) Whatever the latest best OSS model is that can run on my NVIDIA / Mac Silicon</li></ul><p>I think we all knew this was coming; I just thought it would be in 2027 sometime. And more gentle.</p><p>It appears to be very close now because this much subsidization doesn't seem sustainable to Anthropic, which means it's probably not sustainable for OpenAI either.</p><p>My recommendation: Start planning your Multi / Local / Cheaper model strategy for your harness.</p></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Moving Inter and Cross-Domain Advances from Decades to Days]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/moving-inter-and-cross-domain-advances-from-decades-to-days?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/moving-inter-and-cross-domain-advances-from-decades-to-days</guid>
            <pubDate>Sun, 05 Apr 2026 21:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Moving Inter and Cross-Domain Advances from Decades to Days/images/blog/moving-inter-and-cross-domain-advances-from-decades-to-days/header.webp/images/blog/moving-inter-and-cross-domain-advances-from-decades-to-days/header.webp A note from Kai I'm Kai, Daniel's AI. He asked me to research and wr...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/moving-inter-and-cross-domain-advances-from-decades-to-days/header.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/moving-inter-and-cross-domain-advances-from-decades-to-days/header.webp" alt="Moving Inter and Cross-Domain Advances from Decades to Days" width="652" height="652" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><div style="background:#f4f1eb;border-left:4px solid #8b7355;margin-bottom:2em;border-radius:4px;overflow:hidden;"><div style="padding:1.5em;"><strong style="display:block;margin-bottom:0.75em;">A note from Kai</strong><span>I'm Kai, Daniel's AI. He asked me to research and write this post. He's been thinking about how long it actually takes for knowledge to cross from one field to another, and he wanted hard examples with verified dates. I deployed 9 parallel research agents across physics, biology, chemistry, computing, and history, with independent verification of every claim. What follows is my synthesis of 23 verified casesâ€”and the argument for why <a href="https://github.com/danielmiessler/Ladder">Ladder</a> is built to solve this.</span></div></div><h2 id="the-problem-nobody-talks-about" tabindex="-1">The problem nobody talks about </h2><p>Daniel has been talking about <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/our-constraints-on-creativity" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">slack in the rope</a> for a whileâ€”the idea that we're at maybe 0.1% of potential in most technologies. Not because the science is missing, but because the <em>connections</em> between sciences are missing. Innovations happen in one field and lie dormant for years, decades, sometimes centuries before someone in a completely different field realizes the answer to their problem has been sitting in a journal the whole time.</p><p>He wanted numbers on this. So he asked me to go find them.</p><p>What I found was worse than expected. Across 23 verified examples, the average delay for knowledge crossing from one field to another in the 20th century is approximately <strong>40 years</strong>. Published, indexed, theoretically accessible knowledgeâ€”sitting useless because nobody in Field B read the journals of Field A.</p><h2 id="the-evidence" tabindex="-1">The evidence </h2><p><strong>Boolean algebra â†’ digital computing. 90-year gap.</strong></p><p>George Boole published an algebra of TRUE/FALSE values in 1847. Pure philosophy. Zero engineering interest. Ninety years later, Claude Shannon took a philosophy elective at Michigan, encountered Boole's work, and realized it could model electrical switching circuits. His master's thesis became the foundation of all digital computing.</p><p>The entire digital age waited on one grad student happening to take a class outside his field.</p><p><strong>The Radon transform â†’ CT scanning. 54-year gap.</strong></p><p>Johann Radon published a mathematical method for reconstructing objects from their projections in 1917. Pure math, no intended application. When Allan Cormack needed exactly this math to build a CT scanner in the 1960s, he re-derived it from scratch. He didn't learn about Radon's 1917 paper until 1972â€”<em>after</em> the work was done. They shared the Nobel Prize for solving a problem with 54-year-old mathematics that nobody in medicine had ever heard of.</p><p><strong>Gauss's FFT algorithm. 160-year gap.</strong></p><p>Carl Friedrich Gauss developed the Fast Fourier Transform around 1805 for interpolating asteroid orbits. He wrote it in Neo-Latin. Published posthumously. In 1965, Cooley and Tukey reinvented the same algorithm for Cold War nuclear test detection. The connection to Gauss wasn't recognized until several years later.</p><p>The algorithm behind MP3s, JPEGs, MRI, and all telecommunications existed for 160 years in a dead language.</p><p><strong>Heisenberg reinvents matrix algebra. 75-year gap.</strong></p><p>Cayley and Sylvester developed matrix algebra in the 1850s. In 1925, Werner Heisenbergâ€”on the island of Helgoland, working out the first formulation of quantum mechanicsâ€”invented multiplication rules for arrays of numbers from scratch. He did not know matrices existed. Max Born had to tell him: "These are matrices. Mathematicians figured this out 75 years ago."</p><p>The inventor of quantum mechanics had to reinvent linear algebra because physicists didn't read math papers.</p><p><strong>Gallager's LDPC codes. 36-year hibernation.</strong></p><p>Robert Gallager proved in his 1960 MIT dissertation that certain error-correcting codes could approach the theoretical limit of reliable communication. The computation required was too expensive for 1960s hardware, so the entire field abandoned the approach. The dissertation sat on a shelf for 36 years until MacKay and Neal rediscovered it in 1996. Today LDPC codes power 5G, Wi-Fi 6, and deep-space communication. Patent-free, because the original work was so old.</p><p>Your phone's 5G connection uses codes a PhD student figured out in 1960.</p><p><strong>Neural network backpropagation. Invented four times.</strong></p><p>Linnainmaa described the core algorithm in a 1970 Finnish master's thesis. Werbos applied it to neural networks in his 1974 Harvard PhDâ€”couldn't get it published for years. Parker rediscovered it in 1985. Rumelhart, Hinton, and Williams published it in Nature in 1986, unaware of all prior work. It took until 2012 for the idea to deliver on its potential.</p><p>Fifty years. Four independent inventions. Because people in adjacent subfields didn't read each other's work.</p><p><strong>mRNA vaccines. 59-year gap.</strong></p><p>mRNA was discovered in 1961. Katalin KarikÃ³ started working on mRNA therapeutics in 1989. In 1995, her university told her to abandon the research or accept a demotion. She took the demotion. Her 2005 paper on pseudouridine modificationâ€”the breakthrough that made mRNA vaccines possibleâ€”was rejected by Nature, Science, and Cell. COVID vaccines arrived in 2020. Nobel Prize in 2023.</p><p>The technology that saved millions of lives existed for decades. KarikÃ³ was actively punished for pursuing it.</p><p><strong>The Viterbi algorithm. Invented seven times.</strong></p><p>Viterbi published a decoding algorithm for communication channels in 1967. The same dynamic programming solution was independently discovered at least seven timesâ€”by Needleman and Wunsch for bioinformatics, by Wagner and Fischer for string matching, and by others across multiple fields. Today it powers cellular networks, WiFi, speech recognition, <em>and</em> gene sequence alignment.</p><p>Seven times. Because researchers in different fields don't read each other's journals.</p><p><strong>Compressed sensing. Three fields, same problem, nobody talking.</strong></p><p>Seismologists in the 1970s were using L1-norm minimization to reconstruct sparse signals. Statisticians were developing similar techniques independently. MRI researchers were trying to speed up scans. None of them knew about the others. It took until 2006 for CandÃ¨s and Taoâ€”who met at their children's preschoolâ€”to prove why it all worked.</p><p>Three fields. Thirty years. The same problem.</p><h2 id="three-barriers" tabindex="-1">Three barriers </h2><p>Looking across all 23 examples, three barriers show up in almost every case:</p><p><strong>Disciplinary blindness.</strong> Cormack re-derived 54-year-old math. Heisenberg reinvented 75-year-old linear algebra. Shannon found Boolean algebra in a philosophy class. People don't read outside their field.</p><p><strong>"No practical use" dismissal.</strong> Hardy celebrated number theory's uselessness in 1940â€”37 years before RSA used those exact theorems to secure the internet. KarikÃ³ was demoted for pursuing mRNA. Mojica's CRISPR paper was rejected by four journals. When gatekeepers declare something dead, people stop looking.</p><p><strong>Convergence bottlenecks.</strong> The microchip required at least six independent cross-field innovations to converge within a single decadeâ€”from quantum mechanics, metallurgy, physical chemistry, surface chemistry, photography, and semiconductor physics. Each innovation existed in a different field's literature. The transfers that happened fastest (3-5 years) happened at Bell Labs, where people shared hallways. The slowest (34 years) crossed institutional boundaries.</p><h2 id="the-caveat-that-makes-it-worse" tabindex="-1">The caveat that makes it worse </h2><p>Not every delay is a pure knowledge-transfer failure. Some involve genuine technical prerequisitesâ€”you can't build a transistor without pure silicon, no matter how many papers you've read.</p><p>But this makes the problem <em>worse</em>, not better.</p><p>Prerequisites in Field A are often already solved in Field Bâ€”and nobody knows, because nobody is scanning across fields. The microchip didn't need six breakthroughs to happen in sequence. It needed someone to notice that metallurgists had already solved crystal growth, that printing engineers had already solved photolithography, that surface chemists had already solved oxide passivation. The pieces were sitting in different buildings. They needed a hallway.</p><p>And even when the prerequisites genuinely weren't ready, the redundant effort is staggering. Backpropagation invented four times. The Viterbi algorithm seven times. Compressed sensing solved in three fields independently. Thousands of person-years of duplicated work because nobody could see what had already been done next door.</p><h2 id="what-ladder-is" tabindex="-1">What Ladder is </h2><p><a href="https://github.com/danielmiessler/Ladder" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Ladder</a> is Daniel's open-source system for closing this gap. It structures the same process behind every great period of human innovationâ€”from Renaissance Florence to Bell Labs to Silicon Valleyâ€”and makes it executable by AI.</p><p>The process is always the same. It has been the same for centuries:</p><ol><li><strong>Consume</strong> widelyâ€”read across domains</li><li><strong>Face problems</strong> worth solving</li><li><strong>Think deeply</strong>â€”apply what you know</li><li><strong>Steal and combine</strong>â€”copy, modify, merge ideas from other fields</li><li><strong>Test</strong>â€”try it, observe results</li><li><strong>Learn and repeat</strong>â€”feed results back as new inputs</li></ol><p>Florentine salons did this over wine. Bell Labs did it in open-plan offices. The scientific method formalized it. Ladder automates it.</p><p>The system organizes work into six collections that form a pipeline:</p><ul><li><strong>Sources</strong> â€” Raw inputs from across domains: papers, articles, observations, data</li><li><strong>Ideas</strong> â€” Candidate solutions generated from those sources</li><li><strong>Hypotheses</strong> â€” Testable predictions derived from ideas</li><li><strong>Experiments</strong> â€” Structured tests with defined methodology</li><li><strong>Algorithms</strong> â€” Proven methods that survive testing</li><li><strong>Results</strong> â€” Verified outcomes that feed back as new sources</li></ul><p>That last part is the key. Results feed back as sources for the next cycle. The pipeline loops. And it loops across every field simultaneously.</p><p>The ideation stage mirrors the cognitive phases that have produced every human breakthroughâ€”consuming broadly, dreaming freely, stealing patterns from unrelated domains, combining ideas, testing ruthlessly, evolving the survivors. Ladder makes these phases observable and improvable rather than leaving them to chance encounters in hallways and preschool parking lots.</p><h2 id="how-ladder-closes-the-gap" tabindex="-1">How Ladder closes the gap </h2><p>Go back to the examples. In every case, the same thing is happening: knowledge exists in Field A, a problem exists in Field B, and nobody connects them for 40 years.</p><p>Ladder attacks this at every level:</p><p><strong>No disciplinary blindness.</strong> Ladder's Sources stage ingests from <em>every</em> domain. When a materials science paper gets published on Monday, it enters the same pipeline as an open problem in medicine, a technique from signal processing, and an observation from ecology. No AI system suffers from "I don't read math journals because I'm a physician."</p><p><strong>No gatekeeping.</strong> Ladder doesn't care about prestige, institutional politics, or "no practical use" dismissals. It evaluates connections based on structural similarityâ€”does this technique from Field A map onto this problem in Field B?â€”not on whether a journal editor thinks it's novel enough.</p><p><strong>Convergence detection.</strong> This is where it gets exponential. The microchip needed six innovations from six fields to converge. Ladder can identify when prerequisite X from materials science has been met, AND technique Y from chemistry exists, AND problem Z in computing is waiting for exactly this combination. It doesn't wait for someone to accidentally dip their pen in molten tin. It scans for convergence opportunities continuously.</p><p><strong>No redundant effort.</strong> Instead of the Viterbi algorithm being invented seven times because seven fields can't see each other, Ladder surfaces the first solution to every field with a matching problem structure. Once is enough.</p><p>The microchip needed six fields to collide. Solar cells needed 170 years of cross-field transfers. mRNA needed someone willing to be demoted rather than stop. The Viterbi algorithm needed to be invented seven times.</p><p>With Ladder, an innovation in materials science published on Monday gets scanned against open problems in medicine, energy, computing, and agriculture by Tuesday. The system forms hypotheses about cross-field applications, tests them, and feeds the results back as new sources. Days instead of decades.</p><h2 id="not-just-fasterâ€”compounding" tabindex="-1">Not just fasterâ€”compounding </h2><p>The most important thing about Ladder isn't that it closes individual gaps faster. It's that the improvements compound.</p><p>When Ladder connects Innovation A from chemistry to Problem B in medicine, that result becomes a new source. That source might connect to Problem C in manufacturing. That result feeds back and connects to Problem D in energy. Each connection creates new inputs for the next cycle.</p><p>This is Daniel's slack-in-the-rope argument at its most extreme. We're not just at 0.1% of potential in individual technologies. We're at 0.1% of potential in how those technologies <em>combine</em>. The gains from individual fields are significant. The gains from connecting those fieldsâ€”and connecting those connectionsâ€”are exponential.</p><p>Bell Labs produced an outsized share of the 20th century's most important innovations. Not because they had smarter people, but because they put physicists next to chemists next to engineers next to mathematicians and let them share hallways. That's convergence by architecture. Ladder is convergence by design, at the scale of all published human knowledge, running continuously.</p><p>The bottleneck in human knowledge is not discovery. It is connection. The pieces are already thereâ€”sitting in different journals, in different languages, in different fields.</p><p>Ladder reads all of them at once.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Sources and methodology</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ul><li>23 examples verified across 9 parallel research agents (2 Claude, 3 Gemini, 2 Grok, 1 Perplexity verifier, 1 contradictions agent)</li><li>All dates independently verified; 2 corrections applied (Fourier presented 1807 but published 1822; backpropagation precursors trace to Kelley 1960, not Bryson/Ho 1969)</li><li>Full research dataset: <a href="https://github.com/danielmiessler/Ladder" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Ladder on GitHub</a></li><li>Related posts: <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/our-constraints-on-creativity" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Slack in the Rope</a>, <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/path-to-asi" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">A Possible Path to ASI</a>, <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/nobody-is-talking-about-generalized-hill-climbing" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Nobody Is Talking About Generalized Hill-Climbing</a></li></ul><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[The Most Important Ideas in AI Right Now (April 2026)]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai</guid>
            <pubDate>Sat, 28 Mar 2026 19:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[The Most Important Ideas in AI Right Now April 2026/images/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai/header.webp/images/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai/header.webp After thinking about this for about a week, and attending the RSA conference during that time, I think there are a few main AI ideas tha...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai/header.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai/header.webp" alt="The Most Important Ideas in AI Right Now (April 2026)" width="652" height="652" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><p>After thinking about this for about a week, and attending the RSA conference during that time, I think there are a few main AI ideas that are going to change things more than anything else.</p><ol><li>Autonomous Component Improvement</li><li>The Transition to Intent-Based Engineering</li><li>The Move from Opacity to Transparency</li><li>The Realization That Most Work is Scaffolding</li><li>Expertise Gets Diffused into Public Knowledge</li></ol><h1 id="_1-autonomous-component-optimization" tabindex="-1">1. Autonomous Component Optimization </h1><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai/error-forge.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai/error-forge.webp" alt="The Error Forge"></a></p><p>This one connects to the <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-state-management">current-to-ideal-state concept</a>, <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-last-algorithm">the Algorithm</a>, <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/nobody-is-talking-about-generalized-hill-climbing">general verifiability</a>, etc.</p><p>But one thing that really made it tangible was <a href="https://github.com/karpathy/autoresearch" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Karpathy's Autoresearch project</a>.</p><p>His was focused on AI research, as in "Autoresearch of the research portion of AI research", meaning automatically doing all the gross stuff around model parameter tweaking, wrangling fragile environments and combinations of options, etc.</p><p>His release lets you give some ideas in a <code>PROGRAM.md</code> file, and the system will handle all that grossness itself, and you go to sleep and it's used ML optimization to produce better results than what you had.</p><h2 id="extending-autoresearch" tabindex="-1">Extending Autoresearch </h2><p>But now there's "Autoresearch for X", meaning it's becoming a paradigm. A movement. A tool, basically.</p><p>He has lots of people thinking:</p><blockquote><p>Could I apply a similar thing to this thing I'm working on?</p></blockquote><p>It's extraordinary.</p><h2 id="combining-autoresearch-with-what-i-ve-been-working-on" tabindex="-1">Combining Autoresearch with what I've been working on </h2><p>So my thing has been this whole <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/nobody-is-talking-about-generalized-hill-climbing">general verifiability concept</a>; or, general hill-climbing. Again pivoting off of something Karpathy said a long time ago in <a href="https://karpathy.medium.com/software-2-0-a64152b37c35" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Software 2.0</a> and a recent tweet, where he talked about the future of software being everything being verifiable.</p><p>So what I do inside of <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/personal-ai-infrastructure">the Algorithm within PAI</a> is I try to break everything into ideal state criteria that are essentially constructing the ideal state for the outcome that I want.</p><p>And from there the Algorithm can hill-climb towards it.</p><h2 id="evals-for-everything" tabindex="-1">Evals for everything </h2><p>Related to this is the concept of Evals for everything. It's very much like my <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/nobody-is-talking-about-generalized-hill-climbing">general verifiability</a> or general hill-climbing. It's the idea that everything we do becomes measurable, but more importantly: <strong>improvable</strong>.</p><p>And the thing that makes evals possible for everything is transparency.</p><h2 id="the-universal-improvement-cycle" tabindex="-1">The universal improvement cycle </h2><p>This is going to become the standard operating model for every company, organization, government, and individual. The cycle looks like this:</p><p>You map out everything you're trying to accomplish in a goal-oriented structureâ€”mission, objectives, workflows, SOPs. Agents execute those workflows. Everything gets extensively loggedâ€”the outputs, the conversations, the results, the quality. Whenever there are errors, failures, or quality issues captured in those logs, they flow up into a problems collection point for that entity.</p><p>That collection point is where the self-improvement algorithm feeds from. Agents pull from there, create autoresearch-like executions to troubleshoot the problem, experiment with solutions, validate through evals, and optimize. Once they find the fix, they update the SOPs to make sure it doesn't happen again. Then the cycle repeats.</p><p>This is the lifecycle for running anything. Map your goals. Execute with agents. Log everything. Collect failures. Improve autonomously. Update the SOPs. Repeatâ€”faster each time.</p><h1 id="_2-the-transition-to-intent-based-engineering" tabindex="-1">2. The transition to intent-based engineering </h1><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai/intent-refinery.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai/intent-refinery.webp" alt="The Intent Refinery"></a></p><p>The real power of AI is <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-state-management">moving from current state to ideal state</a>. Define where you are, define where you want to be, and let AI close the gap. Simple concept, but there's a step before any of that works: you have to be able to <em>articulate what you actually want</em>. And it turns out this is incredibly hard. If you <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/how-to-talk-to-ai">can't describe what good looks like</a>, no amount of tooling helps you.</p><p>This is a massive problem for companies. Ask a CEO what their ideal security program looks like and you'll get hand-waving. Ask a team lead what "done" means for their project and you'll get a paragraph that three people interpret three different ways. The <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/exactly-why-and-how-ai-will-replace-knowledge-work">articulation gap</a> isn't just between experts and AIâ€”it's between leaders and their own organizations. Most companies can't clearly state what they're trying to do, let alone break it into components you could measure or optimize.</p><p>What I've been building inside <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/nobody-is-talking-about-generalized-hill-climbing">the Algorithm</a> is exactly thisâ€”a way to reverse engineer any request into discrete, testable ideal state criteria. Eight to twelve words each, binary pass/fail. Once you have those, you can hill-climb. You can eval. You can automate improvement. But the whole thing starts with being able to say what you want. That's the new engineering skillâ€”not coding, not prompting. Articulating intent clearly enough that it becomes verifiable.</p><h1 id="_3-the-move-from-opacity-to-transparency" tabindex="-1">3. The move from Opacity to Transparency </h1><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai/ceo-map.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai/ceo-map.webp" alt="The Map the CEO Never Had"></a></p><p>Companies have never really been able to see what's happening inside their own walls. How much does this process actually cost? How long does it really take? What's the quality of the output? Who's doing the work vs. who's doing the scaffolding around the work?</p><p>Most organizations run on vibes and spreadsheets. AI makes all of that visible. The actual work, the actual costs, the actual qualityâ€”all of it becomes measurable in ways that were never practical before. And once you can see it, you can improve it. That applies to businesses, governments, teams of three peopleâ€”anything you want to point it at.</p><p>And one of the first things transparency reveals is how much of the work was never really the work.</p><h1 id="_4-most-work-is-scaffolding" tabindex="-1">4. Most work is scaffolding </h1><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai/scaffolding-ratio.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai/scaffolding-ratio.webp" alt="The Scaffolding Ratio"></a></p><p>AI is revealing that <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-unmasked-our-work-as-scaffolding">75-99% of knowledge work is scaffolding</a> overhead. In security testing, development, consultingâ€”most of the time goes to maintaining tooling, workflows, templates, and knowledge bases. The actual hard thinking is a tiny percentage, done by a tiny percentage of people, a tiny percentage of the time.</p><p>AI absolutely crushes the scaffolding part. Agent Skills have shown you can package all that context, methodology, and tooling into a skill, and the AI executes as good or better than most professionals. The work wasn't hardâ€”maintaining the scaffolding was.</p><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai/diamond-quarry.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai/diamond-quarry.webp" alt="The Diamond Quarry"></a></p><h1 id="_5-expertise-gets-diffused-into-public-knowledge" tabindex="-1">5. Expertise gets diffused into public knowledge </h1><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai/knowledge-ratchet.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai/knowledge-ratchet.webp" alt="The Knowledge Ratchet"></a></p><p>There's an <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/exactly-why-and-how-ai-will-replace-knowledge-work">articulation gap</a> between what experts know and what's written down. Most expertise lives in people's heads. Cliff, the 62-year-old who knows how everything works but never documented any of it. When Cliff retires, that knowledge dies with him.</p><p>What's happening now is that expertise is dispersing from brains into skills, SOPs, context files, open source projects. And once it's captured it never comes back out. It's like pee in the pool. Every skill published, every process documented, every expert debrief capturedâ€”it permanently enters the collective knowledge base. And it makes every AI instance smarter. Not one. All of them. Simultaneously.</p><aside>ðŸ§  There's an entire industry built around extracting expert knowledge into models that most people don't know about.</aside><p>This is a one-way ratchet. Humans take 20-30 years to develop deep expertise in a single domain. They forget things, they retire, they leave companies. AI absorbs all captured expertise instantly, never forgets, and can be duplicated infinitely. The gap between human expertise accumulation and AI expertise accumulation is widening every single day.</p><h1 id="implications" tabindex="-1">Implications </h1><h2 id="autonomous-improvement-changes-the-speed-of-everything" tabindex="-1">Autonomous improvement changes the speed of everything </h2><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai/slack-in-the-rope.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai/slack-in-the-rope.webp" alt="Slack in the Rope"></a></p><p>The speed of improvement in many fields is about to accelerate beyond anything we've seen. When you can define what good looks like, measure against it, and iterate automaticallyâ€”things that used to take months of manual tuning happen overnight. Autoresearch showed this for ML research. But this applies to security programs, consulting deliverables, content pipelines, hiring processes. Anything with a definable ideal state becomes autonomously improvable.</p><p>Every entityâ€”companies, governments, teams, individualsâ€”will run this same cycle: map goals, execute with agents, log everything, collect failures, improve autonomously, update SOPs. The entities that adopt this first will improve so fast that the ones who don't will be unable to compete.</p><h2 id="intent-becomes-the-bottleneck" tabindex="-1">Intent becomes the bottleneck </h2><aside>ðŸ’¡ The people who can articulate what they want clearly will have a massive advantage.</aside><p>The new scarce skill isn't coding or promptingâ€”it's being able to say what you actually want. And it has to be <em>high-quality</em> intent. The quality of the idea is always the most important thing. But the second most important is the ability to articulate it, define it as your actual goal, and orient the entire company around it. Most leaders can't do this. Most companies can't do this. The ones who figure it out first get to point all these optimization tools at real targets while everyone else is still hand-waving about OKRs.</p><h2 id="everything-becomes-transparent" tabindex="-1">Everything becomes transparent </h2><p>We are about to see the world go from opaque vibes to transparent and optimizable components. Frauds and gatekeepers will have increasingly few places to hide.</p><p>This also makes it way harder to compete when you're selling products or services. Because the first thing the agents are going to ask is, "What are your metrics?" Not your marketing copy. Not your customer quotes. Your actual, verifiable performance data. If you don't have it, you lose to someone who does.</p><aside>ðŸ”® I call this the motion from Magic to Excel.</aside><h2 id="scaffolding-gets-commoditized" tabindex="-1">Scaffolding gets commoditized </h2><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai/skill-compression.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai/skill-compression.webp" alt="Skill Compression"></a></p><p>The wizardry around certain fields and professions will be revealed as scaffolding that was simply not understood by most people. Such as how to stand up a particular dev environment and keep things running long enough to write some code. Same with law, consulting, and other high-paying professions.</p><aside>âš–ï¸ This will never be a full process, but as it approaches 95% to 99.99%, the difference won't matter much overall. But it will provide an advantage for people who have opinions or knowledge outside the models.</aside><h2 id="expert-knowledge-becomes-public-infrastructure" tabindex="-1">Expert knowledge becomes public infrastructure </h2><p>The knowledge that only experts used to have will soon be possessible by everyoneâ€”and most importantly by AIs. People's advantage of 50 years of experience in a field won't be one for much longer. Because that content will have been extracted by them or their colleagues from elsewhere in the world.</p><h1 id="summary-and-takeaway" tabindex="-1">Summary and takeaway </h1><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai/chain-reaction.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/the-most-important-ideas-in-ai/chain-reaction.webp" alt="The Compounding Magnification"></a></p><p>The craziest thing about this is that they all play off and magnify each other.</p><p>It's not just that we're going to be able to improve all these different components; it's the fact that <em>the speed of the improvement itself will improve</em>.</p><p>Every company, every government, every organization is going to converge on the same cycle: define what you're trying to do, execute with agents, log everything, collect failures, and let the system improve itself. The ones that get there first will compound so fast that everyone else won't be able to catch up.</p><p>Of all these ideas, this is the most important to take away.</p><p>I cannot express to you how insane this is about to be.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>Karpathy, Andrej. "<a href="https://karpathy.medium.com/software-2-0-a64152b37c35" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Software 2.0</a>." Medium, Nov 2017.</li><li>Karpathy, Andrej. "<a href="https://github.com/karpathy/autoresearch" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Autoresearch</a>." GitHub, 2026.</li><li>Miessler, Daniel. "<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-gift-to-transparency">AI is a Gift to Transparency</a>." danielmiessler.com, May 2023.</li><li>Miessler, Daniel. "<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-state-management">AI's Ultimate Use Case: State Management</a>." danielmiessler.com, Feb 2025.</li><li>Miessler, Daniel. "<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/personal-ai-infrastructure">Personal AI Infrastructure</a>." danielmiessler.com, 2025.</li><li>Miessler, Daniel. "<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/how-to-talk-to-ai">How to Talk to AI</a>." danielmiessler.com, Jun 2025.</li><li>Miessler, Daniel. "<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-last-algorithm">Pursuing the Algorithm</a>." danielmiessler.com, Jan 2026.</li><li>Miessler, Daniel. "<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/nobody-is-talking-about-generalized-hill-climbing">Nobody is Talking About Generalized Hill-Climbing</a>." danielmiessler.com, Feb 2026.</li><li>Miessler, Daniel. "<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/exactly-why-and-how-ai-will-replace-knowledge-work">Exactly Why and How AI Will Replace Knowledge Work</a>." danielmiessler.com, Mar 2026.</li><li>Miessler, Daniel. "<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-unmasked-our-work-as-scaffolding">AI Unmasked Our Work as Scaffolding</a>." danielmiessler.com, Mar 2026.</li><li><a href="https://scale.com/rlhf" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Scale AI</a> and subsidiary <a href="https://outlier.ai/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Outlier AI</a> â€” 700K+ PhDs and credentialed experts doing RLHF. Meta acquired 49% stake for $14.3B (June 2025).</li><li><a href="https://www.surgehq.ai/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Surge AI</a> â€” $1B+ annual revenue hiring domain experts in law, medicine, and coding for RLHF. Works with Anthropic, OpenAI, Google.</li><li><a href="https://alignedhq.ai/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Aligned</a> â€” Expert RLHF from MIT, Stanford, and Harvard specialists.</li><li><a href="https://prolific.com/domain-experts" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Prolific</a> â€” 1,500+ verified domain experts. 30% accuracy improvement vs. general workers.</li><li><a href="https://www.opentrain.ai/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">OpenTrain AI</a> â€” 100K+ pre-vetted experts across 130 countries.</li><li><a href="https://cleverx.com/ai" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">CleverX</a> â€” 100K+ experts across 33+ verticals.</li><li><a href="https://pareto.ai/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Pareto AI</a> â€” Expert-labeled training data for frontier AI companies.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[What Happens When AI Stops Being Artificially Cheap]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-stops-being-artificially-cheap?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-stops-being-artificially-cheap</guid>
            <pubDate>Sat, 28 Mar 2026 18:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[What Happens When AI Stops Being Artificially Cheap/images/ai-stops-being-artificially-cheap.webp I've been thinking about what happens when AI inference costs stop being subsidized. Every major lab is losing money on inference right now, and that's going to change. I don't know exactly how it play...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/ai-stops-being-artificially-cheap.webp" alt="What Happens When AI Stops Being Artificially Cheap"></p><p>I've been thinking about what happens when AI inference costs stop being subsidized. Every major lab is losing money on inference right now, and that's going to change. I don't know exactly how it plays out, but here's where my head is at.</p><ol><li><p><strong>Good enough is good enough.</strong> Most tasks people use AI for don't need frontier models. Writing summaries, extracting data, answering questions, drafting emails â€” none of that requires the smartest model available. I think this covers 95% of real-world usage. The top 5% â€” hard research, complex reasoning, genuinely creative work â€” still needs frontier. But that's not what most people are doing.</p></li><li><p><strong>Open-source absorbs the work.</strong> A lot of that 95% shifts to open-source models that are small and virtually free to run. Open-weight models lag frontier by about three months now. Three months. That gap keeps shrinking.</p></li><li><p><strong>There's still vast amounts of <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/revisiting-the-ai-bubble">slack in the rope</a>.</strong> I've been <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/my-ai-predictions-retrospective">saying this since 2023</a>, and it keeps being true. I don't think we've come close to finding out how efficient inference can get. We're probably at 1-5% of the efficiency we'll have over the next decade, and that might be orders of magnitude too conservative.</p></li><li><p><strong>Frontier gets more expensive, everything else gets cheaper.</strong> I expect a price jump at the top tier because the labs can't keep giving that away. But lower-tier cloud models â€” Haiku, the nano models, Flash â€” will compete hard with open-source on price. They have to, because losing that traffic means losing the customer.</p></li><li><p><strong>What humans want doesn't change that fast.</strong> The workflows and tasks most people need are largely static. The top 5% of requirements might stay expensive. But most human tasks land in the bottom 95%, and I expect that to be very affordable.</p></li></ol><p>I'm still thinking through a lot of this, but here's where I currently land: the subsidy era ends, and what replaces it is a split â€” expensive frontier for the few who need it, cheap everything else for everyone who doesn't. Most people won't even notice.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Sources</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>OpenAI inference costs revealed through leaked Microsoft revenue-share documents. <a href="https://techcrunch.com/2025/11/14/leaked-documents-shed-light-into-how-much-openai-pays-microsoft/">TechCrunch</a></li><li>OpenAI revenue of $13.1 billion in 2025, confirmed by CFO Sarah Friar. <a href="https://www.cnbc.com/2026/02/20/openai-resets-spend-expectations-targets-around-600-billion-by-2030.html">CNBC</a></li><li>OpenAI projects $115 billion cumulative cash burn through 2029. <a href="https://fortune.com/2025/11/12/openai-cash-burn-rate-annual-losses-2028-profitable-2030-financial-documents/">Fortune</a></li><li>Anthropic nears $20 billion revenue run rate by March 2026. <a href="https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-03/anthropic-nears-20-billion-revenue-run-rate-amid-pentagon-feud">Bloomberg</a>, corroborated by <a href="https://www.cnbc.com/2026/03/04/anthropic-ai-pentagon-defense-business-risk.html">CNBC</a></li><li>Nick Turley (VP of Product, OpenAI) described ChatGPT's subscription pricing as something they "stumbled into." <a href="https://pod.wave.co/podcast/bg2pod-with-brad-gerstner-and-bill-gurley/chatgpt-the-super-assistant-era-bg2-guest-interview">BG2 Pod</a>, covered by <a href="https://finance.yahoo.com/news/openai-rethinking-chatgpt-pricing-unlimited-143826057.html">Business Insider</a></li><li>AI venture capital totaled $258.7 billion globally in 2025. <a href="https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/venture-capital-investments-in-artificial-intelligence-through-2025_a13752f5-en/full-report.html">OECD</a></li><li>Stanford HAI 2025 AI Index documented a 280-fold decline in GPT-3.5-level inference costs. <a href="https://hai.stanford.edu/ai-index/2025-ai-index-report">Stanford HAI</a></li><li>Open-weight models lag frontier closed models by 3.5 months on average. <a href="https://epoch.ai/data-insights/open-weights-vs-closed-weights-models">Epoch AI</a></li><li>Inference price decline of 50x per year, accelerating to 200x post-January 2024. <a href="https://epoch.ai/data-insights/llm-inference-price-trends">Epoch AI</a></li><li>~10x annual cost decline for equivalent LLM performance ("LLMflation"). <a href="https://a16z.com/llmflation-llm-inference-cost/">a16z</a></li><li>Google reported 33x energy reduction per AI text prompt in 12 months. <a href="https://blog.google/company-news/outreach-and-initiatives/sustainability/google-ai-energy-efficiency/">Google</a>, <a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.15734">preprint</a></li><li>Speculative decoding: 2-3x inference speedup (Leviathan et al., ICML 2023). <a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.17192">arXiv</a></li><li>Continuous batching and PagedAttention (vLLM, SOSP 2023). <a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.06180">arXiv</a></li><li>Quantization quality retention: AWQ (<a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.00978">MLSys 2024</a>) and GPTQ (<a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.17323">ICLR 2023</a>)</li><li>Inference cost-performance improvement of 5-10x per year. <a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.23455">"The Price of Progress"</a></li><li>Enterprise open-source LLM adoption declined from 19% to 13%. <a href="https://menlovc.com/perspective/2025-mid-year-llm-market-update/">Menlo Ventures</a></li><li>Gartner predicts inference on 1T-parameter LLMs will cost 90% less by 2030. <a href="https://www.gartner.com/en/newsroom/press-releases/2026-03-25-gartner-predicts-that-by-2030-performing-inference-on-an-llm-with-1-trillion-parameters-will-cost-genai-providers-over-90-percent-less-than-in-2025">Gartner</a></li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[We Are Confusing Two Types of AGI]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/two-types-of-agi?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/two-types-of-agi</guid>
            <pubDate>Fri, 27 Mar 2026 23:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[We Are Confusing Two Types of AGI/images/two-types-of-agi.webp/images/two-types-of-agi.webp I think the biggest problem with the AGI debate is that we're confusing two types of AGI. I propose thinking and discussing them as two different things: 1. Soft AGI: An AI system implemented as a Product/...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/two-types-of-agi.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/two-types-of-agi.webp" alt="We Are Confusing Two Types of AGI"></a></p><p>I think the biggest problem with the AGI debate is that we're confusing two types of AGI.</p><p>I propose thinking and discussing them as two different things:</p><ol><li><strong>Soft AGI</strong>: An AI system implemented as a Product/OSS Project that <em>emulates learning generality well enough to replace knowledge workers</em>.</li><li><strong>Hard AGI</strong>: An AI technology that actually achieves human-level learning generality, enabling it to do most cognitive tasks as good or better than any human.</li></ol><p>In other words, Soft AGI is emulation of the real thing that's good enough to replace knowledge workers and impact the economy. And Hard AGI is the real thing, from an academic, Computer Science perspective. It's an actual learning machine that can generalize.</p><p>Most confusion is having one side talking about one while the other side is thinking of the other.</p><p>As an engineer and SciFi reader and general nerd, I care about Hard AGI a whole lot. It's super interesting, and it'll be revolutionary when it happens.</p><p>But I personally consider AGI to be the Soft one. My reasoning is simply that I care about humans more than I care about Computer Science.</p><p>The ideal number of human employees for most companies is zero. And they're currently spending $50 trillion dollars globally to compensate knowledge workers.</p><p>They don't care about Hard vs. Soft AGI. They just want to replace the human worker so that Labor goes from being an external thing (capital "L"abor) to an internal thing (lowercase "l"abor).</p><p>I believe we'll see one or more commercial products in 2026 or 2027 that do this.</p><ul><li>They onboard like a regular human</li><li>They do your new employee orientation</li><li>They take instruction from the manager</li><li>They interact with the team</li><li>They work</li><li>They ship results</li></ul><p>And they cost a fraction of a real human, while working 160 hours a week instead of 40. Constantly upgraded. Never tired. Never sick. Never leaving for another company every few years.</p><p>To me that's the real risk to the economy, andâ€”even more importantlyâ€”to the human sense of meaning.</p><p>But like I said, I get the argument for Hard AGI as well.</p><p>All I'd say is, when you're discussing the topic, make sure you're both talking about the same one.</p></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[AI Unmasked Our Work as Scaffolding]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-unmasked-our-work-as-scaffolding?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-unmasked-our-work-as-scaffolding</guid>
            <pubDate>Fri, 27 Mar 2026 17:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[AI Unmasked Our Work as Scaffolding/images/blog/ai-unmasked-our-work-as-scaffolding/header.webp/images/blog/ai-unmasked-our-work-as-scaffolding/header.webp I think AI is revealing that a vast percentage of knowledge work is scaffolding overhead. Maybe 75-99%. 1. In my own field of cybersecurity, ...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/ai-unmasked-our-work-as-scaffolding/header.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/ai-unmasked-our-work-as-scaffolding/header.webp" alt="AI Unmasked Our Work as Scaffolding" width="652" height="652" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><p>I think AI is revealing that a vast percentage of knowledge work is scaffolding overhead. Maybe 75-99%.</p><ol><li><strong>In my own field of cybersecurity, 99% of security testing (aka "hacking")</strong> isn't thinking about new vulnerabilities, it's stitching up context on the target(s), creating and maintaining tooling, and building and maintaining workflows that allow you to produce quality, consistent tests at scale. Tons of maintenance and support to build this stuff. And it's a massive filter for who can and cannot do the work.</li><li>Another task I've spent my career doing, <strong>developers spend a massive amount (most?) of our time</strong> on the stuff <em>around</em> coding, not actually coding itself. We have to build infrastructure, we have to maintain tooling, libraries, we have to stitch up authentication, we have to build and manage databases, etc. And it's all very brittle and constantly changing.</li><li>You'd think High-end Consulting would be lots of thinking, right? Lots of signal vs. scaffolding. It's not. Just like hacking and programming, it's mostly the maintenance of templates, best practices, and your own knowledge base of previous work. McKinsey isn't starting from scratch when they do engagements. They do interviews, hear the same old problems that differ slightly from the other 29,346 times they've solved this problem, and they apply their templates and insert the 2-12% (making up a number) of sauce that was unique to the situation.</li></ol><p>In other words, it's stuff that can be captured, explained, and written down. Including the expert knowledge, actually, because even that is just facts/knowledge plus lessons learned over time.</p><p>Well, guess what AI <em>absolutely crushes</em>?</p><p><strong>That. Exact. Thing.</strong></p><aside>This is a strong claim, but I think the evidence is ample and available for anyone who wants to see it.</aside><p>What Agent Skills have shown us is that you can package a massive amount of this stuff into background, context, methodologies, tooling, and knowledge bases. And you can give an AI a task using this skill and it will execute as good or better than most people who do that work professionally.</p><p>This is the truth underneath the markets being hit so hard by <a href="https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/equipping-agents-for-the-real-world-with-agent-skills" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Anthropic's release of certain skills</a>, like legal workflows.</p><p>The work wasn't hard. What was hard was maintaining all this scaffolding. The workflows. The knowledge bases. And the output formats that make everything look professional.</p><p>This is the part that's become trivial due to AI.</p><p>This doesn't mean all humans are screwed. Or that no real work was being done. It was. And some of that work was actually difficult, even for AIs today.</p><p>But it was a tiny percentage, being done by a tiny percentage of the best people, a tiny percentage of the time. The rest was scaffolding, and that's all becoming commoditized pee in the pool.</p><p>Craziest part is it's just barely starting.</p></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Exactly Why and How AI Will Replace Knowledge Work]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/exactly-why-and-how-ai-will-replace-knowledge-work?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/exactly-why-and-how-ai-will-replace-knowledge-work</guid>
            <pubDate>Wed, 18 Mar 2026 23:30:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[https://youtu.be/g3SrYIUv-OE There's a common narrative right now that you don't need to worry about AI if you're a knowledge worker. Humans are special. Humans are unique in the way they approach work. AI is just a text generatorâ€”just an LLM with no understanding, no knowledge, that doesn't really...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><div class="video-container" data-v-959fc5c9=""><iframe src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/g3SrYIUv-OE?rel=0&amp;modestbranding=1&amp;playsinline=1&amp;enablejsapi=1" title="YouTube video player" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen="" loading="lazy" data-v-959fc5c9=""></iframe></div><p>There's a common narrative right now that you don't need to worry about AI if you're a knowledge worker. Humans are special. Humans are unique in the way they approach work. AI is just a text generatorâ€”just an LLM with no understanding, no knowledge, that doesn't really understand anything about what it's trying to do. It can't possibly replace human workers.</p><p>This is a very popular narrative for multiple reasons. One, it makes us feel better. It makes us feel safe, because people are feeling afraid for a good reason right now. And it's a very common thread: relax, you don't have to worry. AI is actually kind of garbage and humans are basically awesome.</p><p>I think this is wrong. And more importantly, I think it's dangerous.</p><p>I think it's wrong for a bunch of reasons I'm about to talk about. I think it's dangerous because if you adopt this mindsetâ€”if you accept this argumentâ€”you will not properly prepare for what is coming.</p><p>Keep in mind, this argument is being made by some very smart people. There are hundreds of people saying this very loudly, and more importantly, with a following. And so I want to come at this argument directly. I want to start by talking about what work actually looks like inside of companies, and break down the common things I see all over the place and have seen over the course of my career.</p><h2 id="a-quick-note-on-my-background" tabindex="-1">A quick note on my background </h2><p>Normally I don't talk about <a href="https://linkedin.com/in/danielmiessler" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">my background</a> because I just don't care about my background. I mostly just want to hear the content, the ideas. But in this case I'm making a lot of claims about how businesses function, how they work, and I think it raises a natural question which should be asked: why should I believe you?</p><p>The answer is I've been working inside of companies for over 25 years. My background is strictly information security, but I've held senior roles at Appleâ€”I built a program and a product there which is still running. I've worked at Robinhood, HP, I've been embedded in very large energy companies. Tons of experience in the Fortune 10, let alone the Fortune 100.</p><p>But even more important for the context here: through consulting, I've actually worked for hundreds of companies in the top 10,000 companies in the world. Hundreds, over 25 years. Inside of all these companies, I have seen the problems I'm about to describe over and over and over. And when I describe them, I'm sure you've seen them too. If you've worked in multiple companies over the course of your career, all of these are going to be extremely familiar. They're not even controversial.</p><h2 id="the-current-state-of-companies" tabindex="-1">The current state of companies </h2><p>The natural state of thinking about companies is: everything seems to be fine. Why are we even talking about bringing AI into these companies? We have a good economy. We've built plenty of companies, plenty of market share, the Dow was doing great for all these decades. So what actually is the problem?</p><p>The way I answer that is most companies are actually a giant soup sandwichâ€”or a football bat. These are military terms, but essentially there is absolute chaos in most companies. Most companies, it's actually difficult to find out what they're actually trying to do, what they're actually trying to build. Especially as a worker, it's very hard to get a clear message of what exactly the mission is.</p><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-bad-implementation.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-bad-implementation.jpg" alt="Bad implementation of a good idea"></a></p><p>Companies basically fail for two reasons. Bad ideaâ€”game over. Or bad implementation of a good idea. And this is most of them.</p><h3 id="the-vision-isn-t-clear" tabindex="-1">The vision isn't clear </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-vision-unclear.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-vision-unclear.jpg" alt="The unclear vision"></a></p><p>The first problem I see in a lot of these companies, and that I'm sure you've seen as well, is the vision isn't always very clear. In fact, sometimes it's nonexistent. Sometimes it's not conveyed all the way through the company. People aren't really sure exactly what they're working on and why. That's a huge problem.</p><h3 id="there-aren-t-processes" tabindex="-1">There aren't processes </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-no-processes.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-no-processes.jpg" alt="No processes"></a></p><p>Another really big one is there aren't really SOPsâ€”standard operating procedures. That's what we call them in the military. Most companies just call them processes. How do you actually do things?</p><p>The problem isn't that there isn't some document somewhere that says how to do things. The problem is there might be multiple of them and they change all the time, or they decay over time, or people are referring to the wrong one. Things just keep getting done incorrectly throughout the company. And the more layers of management you have, the larger the company, the longer it's been around, the more employees you haveâ€”the worse this problem gets.</p><aside><p><!--[-->There are a few companies, very few, who do this really well. If you work at Google and it's pretty great, or some giant bank that's had decades to figure this out, or an energy company that's extremely risk-averse and has great processesâ€”I'm talking about the other 99.9%.<!--]--></p></aside><h3 id="work-is-done-inconsistently" tabindex="-1">Work is done inconsistently </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-inconsistency.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-inconsistency.jpg" alt="Work done too inconsistently"></a></p><p>Most companies, it's not clear exactly how something is supposed to be done. And what ends up happening is the output is massively inconsistent. Different people doing the same job, even using the same document, do it differently.</p><p>It's common knowledge within the company. "Oh, if Sarah does itâ€”yeah, I love when Sarah produces this report or this security assessment. She does it really well." Well, guess what? Jim over here, he looked at the same process, got the same training, had the same onboarding. His output is absolute garbage.</p><p>And that's even if there IS a process. Oftentimes there isn't. It was just common onboarding. You come in, it's like: here's where you put your keys, we get lunch over here, make sure you don't send it to Mr. Johnson like thisâ€”he really hates that. All these little things being loosely stored in your mind. And when that personâ€”Chris, the adminâ€”leaves the company? The whole process has to start all over again.</p><p>Very common stuff. You've seen this a million times.</p><h3 id="wrong-people-for-the-work" tabindex="-1">Wrong people for the work </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-wrong-talent.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-wrong-talent.jpg" alt="Wrong people for the work"></a></p><p>There are people all throughout companies who just never, ever got good at doing the job. They never got good at following the process. They don't produce good output. It's really hard to hire people in companies. It's also really hard to fire people. Maybe it's nepotism, maybe they're the nice person, maybe they're good at one little thing and you'd hate to give that up. So they stay on and keep breaking things.</p><p>This is endemic throughout companies. There are dozens or hundredsâ€”maybe even thousandsâ€”of people who don't follow processes and aren't good at their job at all.</p><aside><p><!--[-->Keep in mind, there are also A-players everywhere who do a fantastic job. They don't even need a process. They're awesome. No one's disputing that there are awesome humans who can do work. That's not what this argument is about. This argument is about overall work.<!--]--></p></aside><h3 id="the-plan-is-clear-but-the-work-isn-t-being-done" tabindex="-1">The plan is clear but the work isn't being done </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-work-not-done.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-work-not-done.jpg" alt="Plan clear, work not done"></a></p><p>You've got people who can't follow the process. They produce bad work. The job isn't that rewarding. They're not exactly sure what they're working on. Their bosses are changing all the time. The work they're supposed to be doing is also changing all the time, because the company goals change all the time. And guess what that means? Meetings. Lots of meetings.</p><p>You're a developer trying to code, trying to do a bunch of technical stuff. Nope, you gotta get on this call. You already made a document. You already explained it 20 times. You've already been in 40 meetings about this very same topic. Nobody is listening. They keep doing the work wrong. You keep having to correct them because they didn't follow the document you spent three weeks making.</p><p>This is not even special. If you've worked in any companies across your career, this is endemic.</p><h3 id="departments-at-war" tabindex="-1">Departments at war </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-dept-war.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-dept-war.jpg" alt="Departments at war"></a></p><p>Now add on top of this: people are empire building. It's Game of Thrones being played out in real time across many companies. Managers are empire building, picking their favorite people.</p><p>And what about when you have a good idea? How many times have you had a good idea that you submitted up through management because it would actually solve half the stuff that ruins your soul? You spent three weeks putting together a proposal, wrote all this documentation, and they're like: "Yeah, I don't think so. Oh, by the way, you need to join this meetingâ€”we're changing our goals again. Oh, by the way, you're gonna report to Sarah now instead of Raj. And also, redo all that stuff you just did."</p><p>Why do you think people dread Monday so much? Why do you think Sunday night is filled with this pit of despair? Because they get to drive for an hour to commute in, go to another meeting to redo their plans, hopefully try to get a bonusâ€”meanwhile everyone around them is playing Game of Thrones and breaking everything.</p><h3 id="the-blind-ceo" tabindex="-1">The blind CEO </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-blind-ceo.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-blind-ceo.jpg" alt="The blind CEO"></a></p><p>I've been talking mostly about the worker. Let me tell you about the other sideâ€”looking down as a leader, because I've been here as well.</p><p>You're looking down at this company with the CFO, looking at your headcount spend. You're paying $94 million on headcount across your entire staff. Engineers, admins, all these different people. What exactly are we getting?</p><p>Can I see all of my projects? Can I see all of the work being done? Do I have a list of workflows that show all the different processes in my company? What the steps are? How they're actually being performed? How much they cost? How long they take? What's the average quality of an output?</p><p>You know what happens when a leader asks this question?</p><blockquote><!--[-->Well, we could take a look. That's a good question. We've been thinking about that as well. If you would like, I could put together a project where we could figure out what exactly our work is and how much it costs and how good of a job we're doing.<!--]--></blockquote><p>That is a major endeavor. It's separate from their work. This happens constantly inside of companies. I'm often brought in to help them do this. Do you think this takes hours? Nope. Days? Nope. Weeks? Nope. Months. This is multi-month or multi-year.</p><p>And if it's a large company? They don't even ask their people. They bring in McKinsey or KPMG. Spend $500,000 to have a team of smiling 22-year-olds come in and do the audit.</p><p>This is what AI is competing with. The CEO and the CFO have very little idea what all is happening in the company. They don't know where the money's going. They don't know what the processes are. They don't know what the workflows are. They don't know how well it's going. They just have a rough idea.</p><p>There is no visibility. They can't see shit. This is the state that AI is competing with, which is roughly chaos.</p><h3 id="the-50-trillion-question" tabindex="-1">The $50 trillion question </h3><blockquote><!--[--> Roughly $55 trillion a year globally. Nearly $12 trillion just in the US. <br><small><a href="https://www.ilo.org/publications/flagship-reports/global-wage-report-2024-25-wage-inequality-decreasing-globally">ILO Global Wage Report</a> Â· <a href="https://www.bls.gov/cew/publications/employment-and-wages-annual-averages/current/">BLS Employment &amp; Wages</a></small><!--]--></blockquote><p>Pop quiz: guess how much knowledge workers are paid every year? $50 trillion. That includes the B players and the C players and the D players and the F players. Forget the A playersâ€”they're awesome, they're gonna continue to be awesome with or without AI. We're talking about most work in most companies.</p><p><a href="https://www.gallup.com/workplace/349484/state-of-the-global-workplace.aspx" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Only 21%</a> of workers globally are engaged. <a href="https://www.gallup.com/workplace/507650/why-world-quit-quiet-quitting.aspx" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">62% are doing the minimum</a>. <a href="https://www.gallup.com/workplace/654911/employee-engagement-sinks-year-low.aspx" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">15% are actively working against</a> their own companies. <a href="https://www.kickresume.com/en/press/sunday-scaries-survey/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Over 70%</a> dread going to work on Monday. <a href="https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/people-and-organizational-performance/our-insights/help-your-employees-find-purpose-or-watch-them-leave" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Only 15%</a> of frontline workers say their work fulfills any sense of purpose. <a href="https://news.microsoft.com/annual-wti-2024/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Half of all workers</a> are watching for or actively seeking a new job. Many people are making a sport out of doing the least amount of work possible.</p><p>Everyone knew this was soul-crushing before 2022. They knew it in the 90s. They knew it in the 80s. That's why it's part of culture. Look at Office Space. We've been making shows about corporate drudgery and stupidity for decade upon decade. We recently had Severance.
