Nobody's having fun here.</p><p>And guess what everyone was doing throughout the culture? Trying to figure out a solution. What is a better way? Because this is obviously not the way that humans should live. That was the state before 2022 when AI arrived.</p><!----><p>AI can follow instructions. The ability to follow instructions over and over, doing the same thing in a very uncreative way, is better than what is done in most companies most of the time.</p><h2 id="the-human-reliability-myth" tabindex="-1">The human reliability myth </h2><aside><p><!--[-->I'm going to continue beating up on us humans here. I got positive stuff at the end, so hang in for that.<!--]--></p></aside><p>This next piece is another negative piece about humans, and it relates to how AI thinks versus how humans think.</p><p>Another part of this argument is that humans just fundamentally think differentlyâ€”that we have more integrity of thought, we're more cohesive, we have understanding, we know ourselves, we can introspect, we can be creative, we have intuition. All these advantages over an AI, which is this dead black box.</p><p>I want to dispel this as well. The crux of this argument is that humans are better thinking machines. If you've ever tried meditation, what it reveals about humans is absolutely mind-blowing.</p><h3 id="your-thoughts-come-from-nowhere" tabindex="-1">Your thoughts come from nowhere </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/thoughts-void-v3.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/thoughts-void-v3.jpg" alt="Your thoughts come from nowhere"></a></p><p>The first thing it reveals is that humans have no idea what they're thinking. At any given moment, if you could zoom in and listen to the minds of people sitting in their cars, sitting in their cubesâ€”it's like hamsters running on a wheel.</p><p>You zoom into Ravi and Ravi's like: "I can't believe he said that. All that work I put in on the projectâ€”I hope that guy gets fired. Like, you know what I would love? A new boss comes in, sees my stuff, absolutely loves it. Looks at Chris's stuff and is like, this is garbage. The one I really like? This one. That's mine." And then: "Oh, did I feed the dog? I think I need to feed the dog. Let me text someone. Oh crap, it's almost lunch."</p><p>That is the mind of a person. That randomness is happening constantly.</p><p>When you try to meditate, what you do is try to observe thoughts. It is the craziest thing. It's like this sliding window of chaos just scrolling through your brain. Rumination, grindingâ€”"I should watch that thing, I gotta pick up those things, I don't know why he said that, I should get a promotion, these people don't respect me." Just constant droning.</p><p>Where did those thoughts come from? Who is making those thoughts? And who is observing them? The answer is you have no idea. <a href="https://academic.oup.com/brain/article-abstract/106/3/623/271932" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Libet's experiments</a> showed that brain activity precedes conscious awareness of a decision by ~350 milliseconds. Your brain decides before you know it. As <a href="https://www.samharris.org/books/free-will" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Sam Harris puts it</a>:</p><blockquote><!--[-->You no more decide the next thought you think than you decide the next thought I write.<!--]--></blockquote><p>You know what that reminds me of? An LLM. You poke an LLM and say, "Hey, say smart stuff about logistics workflows and pipelines and how to move heavy freight from the east coast to the west coast on an 18-wheeler." And it just goes, "Okay," and starts spewing words. And they turn out to be really good. You could ping a human to do the same thing and they start spewing words too. Does the LLM have any idea where its words are coming from? No. Do you have any idea where your words are coming from? No. It's a complete black box.</p><h3 id="your-words-come-from-nowhere" tabindex="-1">Your words come from nowhere </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/sentence-completion-v1.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/sentence-completion-v1.jpg" alt="How do you finish a sentence?"></a></p><p>If you are in the middle of a sentenceâ€”like I have been multiple times alreadyâ€”you have no idea how you're going to finish it. You're not planning sentences. You didn't come up with the sentence. <a href="https://www.mpi.nl/world/materials/publications/levelt/Levelt_Producing_spoken_language_1999.pdf" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">These are spewing out of you just like an LLM</a>. You are just as surprised about anything you say as the person receiving it.</p><p>Here's another example. What are your top seven favorite restaurants in the entire world? You've got 30 seconds. What is your brain doing right now? Where is that content coming from? If I ask you multiple weeks apart, the list will be different because some you couldn't remember at the time. Your preference might have changed in between. This is not a static, cohesive, dependable process.</p><h3 id="your-memory-rewrites-itself" tabindex="-1">Your memory rewrites itself </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-memory-decay.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-memory-decay.jpg" alt="Memory decay"></a></p><p>One of the most reproduced findings in psychology is the fact that memories are not what we think they are. When we store memories, we're storing them with emotion and bias and feelings. And when we recall them, <a href="https://www.nature.com/articles/35021052" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">it's extremely well documented</a>â€”you don't store it correctly. You're not a hard drive. You're not storing unaltered ones and zeros. We don't even know how we're storing things in the brain. But we do know that when we recall events versus what actually happened, they're massively different. And it gets worse over time, and it gets worse the more you recall it.</p><p>Our memory system is completely flawed.</p><h3 id="we-re-passengers-on-our-own-thoughts" tabindex="-1">We're passengers on our own thoughts </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/brain-passenger-v1.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/brain-passenger-v1.jpg" alt="Passenger"></a></p><p>So we don't know where our thoughts are coming from. We don't know how we're gonna finish sentences. And our memories are not staticâ€”they're extremely fluid.</p><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/thought-stream-v1.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/thought-stream-v1.jpg" alt="The stream never stops"></a></p><p><a href="https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.1192439" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Research confirms this</a>: people spend 47% of their waking hours thinking about something other than what they're currently doing. Even <a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21782031/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">experienced meditators</a> cycle through attention and mind-wandering on a timescale of seconds. In <a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17874601/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">nearly half of mind-wandering episodes</a>, people have no idea they drifted until something snaps them back.</p><!----><h3 id="baseline-knowledge-is-really-low" tabindex="-1">Baseline knowledge is really low </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/baseline-knowledge.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/baseline-knowledge.jpg" alt="Baseline knowledge"></a></p><p><a href="https://yougov.com/en-us/articles/53804-most-americans-didnt-read-many-books-in-2025" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">~40% of Americans</a> didn't read a single book last year. <a href="https://ncses.nsf.gov/pubs/nsb20244/assets/nsb20244.pdf" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">1 in 4</a> don't know the Earth orbits the Sun. Only <a href="https://www.finra.org/investors/insights/finra-foundation-national-financial-capability-study" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">27% can pass</a> a basic financial literacy quiz. Over <a href="https://www.uschamberfoundation.org/civics/new-study-finds-alarming-lack-of-civic-literacy-among-americans" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">70% fail</a> a basic civics test. <a href="https://nces.ed.gov/surveys/piaac/2023/national_results.asp" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">28% of US adults</a> can't read at workplace proficiency levels.</p><p>This is the workforce we are comparing AI against.</p><h2 id="the-capability-stack" tabindex="-1">The capability stack </h2><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/capability-stack-td-v1.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/capability-stack-td-v1.jpg" alt="The capability stack"></a></p><p>I came up with this capability stack. This is not settled scienceâ€”this is all still up in the air, people are still trying to figure this out. I read a lot and I've got decent mental models, so just work with me on this.</p><p>Think of these layers as fundamentals of a knowledge workerâ€”things that make us good at doing knowledge work.</p><p><strong>Knowledge.</strong> Collecting, organizing, and accessing facts. Easy.</p><p><strong>Understanding.</strong> Turning knowledge into concepts. This is a big one. Having a whole bunch of facts stored is not enough to be a good knowledge worker. Understanding is the ability to let it seep in and say: oh, that knowledge is related to that knowledgeâ€”that forms a concept. So now we have concepts, and those concepts relate to each other. You have to have understanding of all these different concepts and how they connect. This is the layer that separates someone who can recite facts from someone who actually gets it.</p><p><strong>Intelligence.</strong> This is the next one above understanding. The ability to face obstacles, navigate the world, and pursue goals. If you're more intelligent, you navigate the world better and better achieve your goals.</p><p><strong>Creativity.</strong> The ability to come up with novel, new things. Intuition, art, liberal arts thinking, philosophy, logic.</p><p>In most knowledge work, what people are actually doing is the knowledge, the understanding, and the intelligence. Creativityâ€”some people have a lot of it, some have a little, some have none, but they're still really good knowledge workers. You could have an A-player who crushes knowledge, understanding, and intelligence and is still great at their job.</p><h3 id="the-creativity-myth" tabindex="-1">The creativity myth </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-creativity-myth.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-creativity-myth.jpg" alt="The creativity myth"></a></p><p>Most people would say creativity is what makes humans special. But creativity is the least required capability in most knowledge work. McKinsey found that <a href="https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/mckinsey-digital/our-insights/four-fundamentals-of-workplace-automation" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">only 4%</a> of US work activities require creativity at median human level. Companies run on knowledge and process far more than novelty.</p><h3 id="the-expertise-debate" tabindex="-1">The expertise debate </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-v3-expertise-debate.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-v3-expertise-debate.jpg" alt="The expertise debate"></a></p><p>One of the arguments for why humans cannot be replaced is that they have expertise. This is the thing my friend Marcus Hutchins is always talking about. He says expertise is the thing AI can't doâ€”it can have facts and regurgitate them, but that's not real useful, that's not what work is.</p><p>So where is expertise on this stack? Maybe you think I missed a layer. I don't think I did, but I'm challenging you to tell me what I missed.</p><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-expertise-decomposed.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-expertise-decomposed.jpg" alt="Expertise decomposed"></a></p><!----><p>My answer: expertise is knowledge, understanding, and intelligence combined. Think about what expertise actually is. It's lots of experienceâ€”the knowledge, the understanding of the concepts, the ability to adapt with intelligence and navigate and change. The combination of those combined with experience is what gives you your expertise.</p><p>Maybe you'd argue experience should be on the list. But experience is memory. Experience is recall. Experience is accrued iterations on seeing different problems. And ultimately, experience is actually improving your knowledge and your understanding. An experienced person has more knowledge, better understandingâ€”they've linked more and more patterns to interconnect. Somebody with 25, 30, 50 years of doing financial audits just immediately sees things. They see patterns.</p><p>Here's the question: what part of that does AI not have?</p><p><strong>Knowledge.</strong> AI's knowledge is insane. It's read every book. It's deeply knowledgeable about thousands of fields and subdomains. It could write a full book on a topic by itself. It could make flashcards about very specific things that very few people on earth know about. Knowledge? Forget about it.</p><p><strong>Understanding.</strong> My definition of understanding is connecting concepts, finding patterns across domains. And AI is mind-blowingly good at this. It can cross-reference anything. It holds it all in its mind at once. "Oh, this relates to 1930s Russia in the following way." "Well, how does that relate to chicken feed farming practices from Idaho?" "Good question. Here's how it relates." It can also supplement that with tools to go research anything and add to it. Knowledge and understanding? Crushing it. Absolutely crushing it.</p><p><strong>Intelligence.</strong> Can AI navigate the world and achieve goals? Yes. Absolutely. An agent infrastructure with access to tools, company knowledge, all the context, a smart model, a system of agentsâ€”you throw dynamic changing requirements at it: "We can't do Japan, we need the UK, only people with residency in Singapore, everyone over 35, use this template, and take into account these other 90 things." Those are dynamic changing situations. Everyone who's used AI recently knows you can throw anything at them and they adjust.</p><p>So can it do intelligence? According to this definition, 100%.</p><p>Now let's take creativity off the list entirely. Let's just pretend AI doesn't have it. AI still has knowledge, understanding, and intelligence. AI has the expertise.</p><p>This argument that you can't possibly replicate human expertise, human thinking, the quality of human execution of workâ€”everything we've been talking about tells us that's just not true. In fact, the polar opposite is more true. Even decent AI today, given defined tasks, will just crush an average knowledge worker. What are we doing all day? Accepting emails, responding to emails, producing reports, writing code. These basic commodity tasks make up the vast majority of all knowledge work.</p><h3 id="the-gap-is-not-the-model" tabindex="-1">The gap is not the model </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-expertise-trap.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-expertise-trap.jpg" alt="The expertise trap"></a></p><p>Here's a core point about all of this. A big reason people think there's a gap between what humans have and what AIs have is because the humans have not articulated the stuff. It's not written down.</p><p>Think about this. The way AI has gotten so good in the last year is largely because of <a href="https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/overview" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Claude Code</a>. What did Claude Code do? Did it have a dramatically better model? No. What it did was build scaffoldingâ€”a harness around the model. It built context and context management, relations between documents, allowing the model to have knowledge, understanding, and intelligence. It linked together everything the model needs to actually do work.</p><p>What are <a href="https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/skills" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">skills</a>? Skills are a combination of resources, tools, context, knowledgeâ€”a whole bunch of stuff written down. That's the centerpiece. It's essentially the core benefit of a harness versus just a model. When you give something goals, context about how to accomplish something, the tools to use, examples of good and badâ€”that's a skill.</p><p>Once you realize how powerful skills areâ€”once you realize how powerful it is to have all this context in one place that wraps an AI modelâ€”you start to realize that the problem is not the AI.</p><!----><p>Nobody has any of this written down. All the stuff in Ravi's mind and Suzy's mind and Chris's mindâ€”the way this particular task is supposed to be done, this sending of the emails, the creation of the report, the writing of the codeâ€”it's not written down anywhere. It's passed from human brain to human brain, and they go on vacation, go on maternity leave, retire, jump companies every couple of years. That knowledge just dies.</p><p>There's some super old guy, 62 years old, his name's Cliff. He's the one you call when things go down and there's no docs. You can't find a thing, you don't know how to get the server back up, people don't even know what servers are anymoreâ€”you call Cliff. Cliff has never written the stuff down. He's never created a skill. He's never done a full debrief interview to capture his knowledge.</p><p>What's starting to happen now is knowledge is dispersing from brains into skills. The expertise gap between humans and AI is actually the failure, so far, of us to articulate all that chaos we talked about in the beginning. And this is happening fast. Extremely fast.</p><h3 id="the-articulation-gap" tabindex="-1">The articulation gap </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-articulation-gap.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-articulation-gap.jpg" alt="The articulation gap"></a></p><p>The real barrier between human expertise and AI expertise is not capability. It's capture. Experts know things they cannot explain. They have 20 years of pattern recognition, intuition, and judgment locked inside their heads. Ask them why they made a particular call and they'll say "I just knew." That's real. That expertise is real. But it's trapped.</p><p>On the other side, you have AI-ready expertiseâ€”skills, SOPs, context files, rules, examples. Written down and executable. Once captured, AI can use it instantly. The gap between those two sides is the articulation gap. And that gap is closing every single day. Every skill written, every process documented, every expert debrief capturedâ€”it shrinks permanently.</p><h3 id="the-expertise-ratchet" tabindex="-1">The expertise ratchet </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-expertise-ratchet.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-expertise-ratchet.jpg" alt="The expertise ratchet"></a></p><p>Here's the thing that should really make you pay attention. This is a one-way process. It's a ratchet. It only goes in one direction.</p><p>Think of it like pee in the pool. Once expertise gets capturedâ€”into a skill, an open source project, a documented SOP, a published processâ€”it never comes back out. It's permanently in the pool. Every person who documents their knowledge adds to the collective pool. Every open source project, every <a href="https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/overview" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Claude Code</a> configuration file, every captured processâ€”it all accumulates. And it never drains.</p><p>This is happening right now at an accelerating pace. Skills are flooding the AI space. People are documenting what they know and publishing it. Companies are being forced to write down their processes for AI adoption. Expert knowledge that used to die when Cliff retired is now being captured and made permanent.</p><p>And here's what makes this devastating: every single piece of expertise that enters the pool makes ALL AI instances smarter. Not one AI. All of them. Simultaneously. Permanently.</p><h3 id="the-asymmetry" tabindex="-1">The asymmetry </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-expertise-asymmetry.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-expertise-asymmetry.jpg" alt="The asymmetry"></a></p><p>Now think about what this means for the speed comparison between humans and AI.</p><p>A human takes 20 to 30 years to develop deep expertise in a single domain. They can only learn one thing at a time. They forget things. They retire. They die. When they leave a company, their knowledge leaves with them. A new hire starts over from scratch.</p><p>AI absorbs all captured expertise instantly. It never forgets. It can be duplicated infinitely. Every new skill published, every process captured, every open source contributionâ€”it compounds. Forever. There is no retirement. There is no knowledge loss. There is no starting over.</p><p>The gap between human expertise accumulation and AI expertise accumulation is not just bigâ€”it's widening every single day. And it's widening at an accelerating rate because the more expertise enters the pool, the easier it becomes to capture even more. AI tools themselves are getting better at extracting knowledge from humans. You can have a conversation with an AI and it extracts your expertise into structured documents automatically.</p><!----><p>This is why I consider the "don't worry about AI" narrative so dangerous. Every day you spend not preparing, the gap gets wider. Not by a little. By a lot. And it never shrinks back.</p><h2 id="the-ai-first-enterprise" tabindex="-1">The AI-first enterprise </h2><p>So far I've been talking about the problem. Now I want to talk about what I think the solution isâ€”and what I think is inevitable.</p><p>This is an architecture I've come up with, I call it the Lattice. I've been working on it for quite a while. I'm going to release it as an open source project as wellâ€”similar to how I released <a href="https://github.com/danielmiessler/fabric" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Fabric</a> and <a href="https://github.com/danielmiessler/PAI" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">PAI</a>. It's the same system I use with customers to help them solve the chaos we talked about.</p><h3 id="the-lattice-system" tabindex="-1">The Lattice system </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/lattice-dark-v6.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/lattice-dark-v6.jpg" alt="The lattice system"></a></p><p>The concept is addressing the transparency problemâ€”the fact that all these different groups are opaque, the work is opaque, the processes and SOPs are not clear.</p><p>What Lattice does is it has a single unified daemon that everything reports up into. You have the different tiers of organization: company level, department, team, and individual. Each has SOPs, metrics, work, goals, budgetâ€”all the different components. And each level has the same structure.</p><p>As an individual, you have your personal SOPs, the way you like to get things done. Your personal budget, your metrics, your knowledge captured in skills. And most importantly, your individual unit is broadcasting APIs. You as a personâ€”Sarah, Ravi, Chrisâ€”you are broadcasting what your SOPs are, what your metrics are, what your budget is, what your work items are. They're available for any agent, any team member, the agent for the team, the department, or the company to query in real time.</p><p>Most importantly, this allows the CEO and the CFO to actually look down and say: this is the work we're doing.</p><h3 id="the-daemon-api-network" tabindex="-1">The daemon API network </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-daemon-api-network.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-daemon-api-network.jpg" alt="The daemon API network"></a></p><p>Goals, plans, SOPs, metrics, work, cost, qualityâ€”the workflows for the companyâ€”this stuff is extremely opaque to the leaders of a company right now. Extremely hard to gather. This is going to turn it into seconds. Less than a second, oftentimes. Minutes at the absolute maximum for a super large company.</p><p>Each tierâ€”worker, team, department, companyâ€”is independently authoritative over its own stuff. I could just query: "Hey, what are my coworkers working on? I'm thinking about working on this." And the system says: "Oh yeah, Julie's already working on that. You should talk to Modi, they're already on it. Maybe you can do a collab. Do you want me to set up a meeting?" Boom, done. Now it posted that you're in a meeting, that you're collaborating on this feature. That's now available in your API. When someone asks if anyone's working on this, it now includes you. Available to other teams, other departments, all the way up to the company level.</p><h3 id="the-ai-enterprise-graph" tabindex="-1">The AI enterprise graph </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-v3-ai-enterprise-graph.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-v3-ai-enterprise-graph.jpg" alt="The AI enterprise graph"></a></p><p>This is the type of view that CEOs, CFOs, any manager wants to see. All the different processes, all the different components. Human components here, automated components there, review here, audit there, quality check over here. You can't optimize what you don't understand. You can't optimize what you don't see.</p><h3 id="goals-get-canonicalized" tabindex="-1">Goals get canonicalized </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-goals-canonical.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-goals-canonical.jpg" alt="Goals get canonicalized"></a></p><p>Think of what happens right now when a company changes its goals. How many people are triggered by the word OKR? They change all the time. People have different systems for doing them. Imagine a giant all-hands meeting where the CEO says: "These are the new goals. These are the new metrics. Here are new SOPs." Boomâ€”they've been published. All documentation is now updated. Goes all the way down. Where's the text file? Right here.</p><p>This is not magic stuff. This is not difficult stuff. We are doing much more difficult stuff with AI already. This is just not a system that's been built yet, and it will take time to roll out.</p><h3 id="how-software-buying-changes" tabindex="-1">How software buying changes </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-steak-dinner.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-steak-dinner.jpg" alt="Software bought through steak dinners"></a></p><p>Someone comes up, takes you to a steak dinner, and they're like: "Hey, we have the best image background removal process. Ours is way better." So you do a pilot, spend six weeks coming up with test criteria, A/B test the thing.</p><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-software-eval.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-chaos-software-eval.jpg" alt="Software evaluated by function"></a></p><p>That goes away when you have a lattice of all the different pieces that make up your company. One particular nodeâ€”one of hundreds or thousands or millions of nodesâ€”is the algorithm for doing image background removal. Today we don't really have metrics on that. What we have is: "Yeah, it works pretty good."</p><p>What we're moving toward: all of these have metrics. They're all clearly defined. A vendor doesn't come with a steak dinner anymore. We show them our metrics. What are your ratings? What are your cost numbers? If all they brought was the steak dinner, that's not a conversation anymore.</p><h3 id="the-optimization-view" tabindex="-1">The optimization view </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-optimization-before-after-dark.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-optimization-before-after-dark.jpg" alt="The optimization view"></a></p><p>Once you have those visuals, once you have those workflows, things fundamentally change. The human becomes the decision-maker, not the doer.</p><h2 id="the-reverse-automation-frame" tabindex="-1">The reverse automation frame </h2><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-v3-reverse-automation.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/c3-v3-reverse-automation.jpg" alt="The reverse automation frame"></a></p><p>If you think about it in terms of "we have the humans, now let's add AI"â€”I have the same instinct, it feels uncomfortable because they're coming into our processes. Here's another way to think about this.</p><p>Imagine you have a factory production line that produces iPhones. Soldering nanometer transistors. This thing is amazing, fully automated, just flowing perfectly. Who would say: "You know what this thing really needs? A human touch. Let's get some humans in here." Do you have any idea what would happen if you tried to ask a human to solder nanometer transistors onto circuit boards? It would not go well. Also known as: impossible.</p><p>Or take an amazing Excel sheet. 36 months of work, handles the entire company's finances, just produces the best results, never messes up. Where on the spreadsheet should we add a human to help?</p><p>When automation works, when AI workflows work, when the Lattice system worksâ€”the answer will not be to add humans to the thing. It becomes extremely obvious that adding humans actually makes it worse. With an exception, which we're about to talk about.</p><h2 id="what-humans-actually-do-better" tabindex="-1">What humans actually do better </h2><p>I promised some good news and this is where we get to it.</p><p>So far: the current state of work is not good. The perfect human thinking machine is not correct. And the Lattice system shows where this is headed. Now let's talk about what humans are actually supposed to do.</p><h3 id="the-human-only-layers" tabindex="-1">The human-only layers </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/human-capability-stack-td.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/human-capability-stack-td.jpg" alt="The human capability stack"></a></p><p>Remember when we talked about the four layersâ€”knowledge, understanding, intelligence, creativity? There are two layers underneath, and these are the layers that AIs don't have. AI matches the top fourâ€”but has none of the bottom two.</p><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/human-only-layers-v1.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/human-only-layers-v1.jpg" alt="The human-only layers"></a></p><p><strong>Subjective Experience.</strong> Consciousness. The thing that makes achieving a goal feel like something.</p><p><strong>Desires.</strong> Built-in drives from evolution. The <a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/9054347/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">reward and punishment system</a>â€”dopamine, serotoninâ€”the force that makes you WANT things.</p><!----><p>Imagine that whole lattice workflow again, fully automated. Amazing. Doesn't need humans because it's better, faster, more consistent, higher quality, cheaper. Fine. Who decides what to make? Who decides what company to build in the first place?</p><p>AI doesn't see a problem in the world and go: "I just don't like that. That's not the way they should be treated. This is a horrible experience. I don't like this product, we should make a better one." AI is not doing that. Why? Because it doesn't experience anything. It doesn't feel bad. It doesn't come up with ideas on its own. If you ask it to, it canâ€”but it's not sitting around dormant and unhappy.</p><p>We are unhappy. We have these desires and this subjective experience. Because we are powered by evolution. We are basically mech-suits around evolution, which is trying to do things through us. Our entire pleasure systemâ€”dopamine, serotonin, cortisolâ€”the whole purpose is to steer us toward desires. Do we have any control over our desires? Can you suddenly decide to like broccoli when you didn't a second ago? No. You can't really control what you desire. You kind of have them. You can try to use your intellect and steer them, but in general, we are being powered by a very ancient, powerful thing.</p><p>That drive makes us fundamentally different from AIs. AIs are dormant. They just sit there. They execute things we want. When they act like they feel things, when they act like they have goalsâ€”that is not coming from their true center of self the way it does for humans.</p><h3 id="the-human-as-overseer" tabindex="-1">The human as overseer </h3><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/human-overseer-v2.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/human-overseer-v2.jpg" alt="What AI cannot do"></a></p><p>Everything we've been talking about in this pieceâ€”everything around why AI is going to replace us doing workâ€”is around the execution. It's all around the implementation of someone's idea, a founder's idea, a company builder's idea. Well, guess what? There's a trillion more ideas out there that need to be made. And AI makes it easy to actually implement them.</p><p>Where are humans in this process? They are at the top. Who determines what the company should be doing, why they're doing it, what problem they're solving? That is only human.</p><p>You can have 20 companies and a dashboard that looks down at all 20 of them. They're all doing awesome stuff. You drill in and say: "Hey, we're not doing this anymore. We're doing it a different way. I want to optimize this." And the AI goes and executes.</p><h2 id="why-this-is-good-news" tabindex="-1">Why this is good news </h2><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/ai-enterprise-human-3-evolution.jpg"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/ai-enterprise-human-3-evolution.jpg" alt="Human 3.0 evolution"></a></p><p>It's totally understandable to freak out when jobs are going away and AI is taking them. The natural tendency when something is yanked from your arms is to pull it closer.</p><!----><p>We should not be the row in the Excel sheet. We should not be the section of the robot army that solders the transistor. We should not be the one in the car dreading going into the office Monday morning. That all should never have happened. And we knew that before 2022.</p><p>The place we're going is where humans actually have ideas. People are broadcasting their own capabilities, their own plans, their own creativity, their own beliefs, their own creations. Nurturing people. Helping people grow. Creating new art, new experiences for other people. And coming up with companies to do this.</p><p>This is what I call <strong><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/human-3-creator-revolution">Human 3.0</a></strong>.</p><div class="blog-card loading" data-v-6cc74a92=""><div class="blog-loading" style="color:#b4f9f8;" data-v-6cc74a92="">Prototyping...</div></div><ul><li><strong>Human 1.0</strong>â€”Survival. Hunt. Gather. Don't die.</li><li><strong>Human 2.0</strong>â€”Corporate existence. Commute. Sit in cube. Send emails. Repeat. Soul-crushing, but better than lions.</li><li><strong>Human 3.0</strong>â€”Creative self-expression. Becoming your full self, magnifying your true self, understanding your true self, broadcasting it, and interconnecting that with other humans who are doing the sameâ€”so we can have value exchange from human to human without the need for corporate hierarchy.</li></ul><p>It's all going away. But it's not good in the first place. We don't need to hold on to it. We need to get to the other side, where we can look down at a Lattice-like system where all the AI is executing, but we decided what to build. We started that business. We built that company.</p><p>This is why I care so much about this. This is why I counter this argument that AI will not replace knowledge work. It is going to replace it: not primarily because AI is so great, but because the current system is so bad. We need to be ready. And it's not bad news. It's actually really good news.</p><hr><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes and sources</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ul><li><strong>A note on "knowledge":</strong> Throughout this piece, what I refer to as "knowledge" is more precisely <em>information</em> â€” data, facts, procedures, and patterns. I subscribe to the Deutschian definition of knowledge: high-quality explanations for how things work, where the mark of quality is being <em>hard to vary</em> â€” every component is load-bearing, and changing any part breaks the explanation. By that standard, most "knowledge work" was really information work all along, which is part of why AI can replace it. See David Deutsch, <em><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Beginning_of_Infinity" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">The Beginning of Infinity</a></em> (2011).</li><li><strong>Knowledge is understanding:</strong> In the Deutschian framework, knowledge and understanding are essentially the same thing. Producing a good explanation <em>requires</em> understanding â€” you can't construct a hard-to-vary account of how something works without genuinely grasping why it works. This collapses the common distinction people draw between "knowing" and "understanding."</li><li><strong>What this means for AI:</strong> This is where it gets interesting. If AI systems can produce high-quality, hard-to-vary explanations â€” and increasingly they can â€” then by Deutschian standards they are demonstrating knowledge and understanding, not merely reciting information. The bar people set for "real understanding" is actually the bar for good explanations, and AI is clearing it.</li><li>Libet, B. et al. (1983). "<a href="https://academic.oup.com/brain/article-abstract/106/3/623/271932" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Time of conscious intention to act in relation to onset of cerebral activity</a>." <em>Brain</em>, 106(3), 623-642. Brain activity precedes conscious awareness of decisions by ~350ms.</li><li>Harris, S. (2012). <em><a href="https://www.samharris.org/books/free-will" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Free Will</a></em>. Free Press. "You no more decide the next thought you think than you decide the next thought I write."</li><li>Nader, K., Schafe, G.E., Le Doux, J.E. (2000). "<a href="https://www.nature.com/articles/35021052" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Fear memories require protein synthesis in the amygdala for reconsolidation after retrieval</a>." <em>Nature</em>, 406, 722-726. Memory reconsolidation â€” recall rewrites the memory.</li><li>Levelt, W.J.M. (1989/1999). <em>Speaking: From Intention to Articulation</em>. MIT Press. <a href="https://www.mpi.nl/world/materials/publications/levelt/Levelt_Producing_spoken_language_1999.pdf" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Blueprint of the speaker (PDF)</a>. Incremental sentence production â€” speakers don't know how their sentence ends when they start.</li><li>Schultz, W., Dayan, P., Montague, P.R. (1997). "<a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/9054347/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">A neural substrate of prediction and reward</a>." <em>Science</em>, 275(5306), 1593-1599. Dopamine neurons encode reward prediction errors.</li><li>Berridge, K.C. (1998). "<a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/9858756/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">What is the role of dopamine in reward</a>." <em>Brain Research Reviews</em>, 28(3), 309-369. The "wanting" vs "liking" distinction in the reward system.</li><li>Killingsworth, M.A. &amp; Gilbert, D.T. (2010). "<a href="https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.1192439" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">A wandering mind is an unhappy mind</a>." <em>Science</em>, 330(6006), 932. People spend 46.9% of waking hours thinking about something other than what they're doing. n=2,250.</li><li>Hasenkamp, W. et al. (2012). "<a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21782031/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Mind wandering and attention during focused meditation</a>." <em>NeuroImage</em>, 59(1), 750-760. Even trained meditators cycle through attention and mind-wandering on a timescale of seconds.</li><li>Smallwood, J., McSpadden, M., &amp; Schooler, J.W. (2007). "<a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17874601/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">The lights are on but no one's home</a>." <em>Psychonomic Bulletin &amp; Review</em>, 14(3), 527-533. Nearly half of mind-wandering episodes occur without any meta-awareness.</li><li>YouGov (2025). "<a href="https://yougov.com/en-us/articles/53804-most-americans-didnt-read-many-books-in-2025" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Most Americans didn't read many books in 2025</a>." 40% of Americans read zero complete books; median was 2 books. n=2,203.</li><li>OECD PIAAC (2024). "<a href="https://nces.ed.gov/surveys/piaac/2023/national_results.asp" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">U.S. National Results</a>." 28% of US adults have literacy at Level 1 or below (up from 19% in 2017); 34% have numeracy at Level 1 or below. Data collected 2022-2023.</li><li>FINRA Foundation (2024). "<a href="https://www.finra.org/investors/insights/finra-foundation-national-financial-capability-study" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">National Financial Capability Study</a>." Only 27% pass a 7-question financial literacy quiz; only 4% answer all correctly. n=25,500.</li><li>U.S. Chamber of Commerce Foundation (2023). "<a href="https://www.uschamberfoundation.org/civics/new-study-finds-alarming-lack-of-civic-literacy-among-americans" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Alarming lack of civic literacy</a>." Over 70% failed a basic civics quiz. n=2,000 registered voters.</li><li>NSF (2024). <em><a href="https://ncses.nsf.gov/pubs/nsb20244/assets/nsb20244.pdf" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Science &amp; Engineering Indicators â€” Public Attitudes</a></em>. ~26% of Americans still answer incorrectly that the Sun orbits the Earth. Stable since the 1980s.</li><li>Bone, J.K. et al. (2025). "<a href="https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12496190/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">The decline in reading for pleasure</a>." <em>iScience</em>, 28(9). Daily pleasure reading dropped from 28% to 16% (2004-2023). n=236,270.</li><li>ILO (2024). <em><a href="https://www.ilo.org/publications/flagship-reports/global-wage-report-2024-25-wage-inequality-decreasing-globally" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Global Wage Report 2024-25</a></em>. Global labour income share ~52.4% of GDP (~$55T total compensation).</li><li>Bureau of Labor Statistics. <em><a href="https://www.bls.gov/cew/publications/employment-and-wages-annual-averages/current/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages</a></em>. US total wages ~$11.7T (2024).</li><li>Gallup (2025). <em><a href="https://www.gallup.com/workplace/349484/state-of-the-global-workplace.aspx" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">State of the Global Workplace</a></em>. 21% engaged, 62% not engaged, 15% actively disengaged. Disengagement cost $438B in 2024. n=227,347.</li><li>Gallup (2024). "<a href="https://www.gallup.com/workplace/654911/employee-engagement-sinks-year-low.aspx" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">U.S. Employee Engagement Sinks to 10-Year Low</a>." 31% engaged in US â€” lowest since 2014. 52% quiet quitting.</li><li>Kickresume (2024). "<a href="https://www.kickresume.com/en/press/sunday-scaries-survey/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Sunday Scaries Survey</a>." 70% of workers experience Sunday dread; 36% every single week. n=2,144.</li><li>McKinsey. "<a href="https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/people-and-organizational-performance/our-insights/help-your-employees-find-purpose-or-watch-them-leave" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Help Your Employees Find Purpose</a>." Only 15% of frontline workers say their work fulfills their sense of purpose.</li><li>Microsoft/LinkedIn (2024). <em><a href="https://news.microsoft.com/annual-wti-2024/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Work Trend Index</a></em>. 46% of workers considering quitting. n=31,000 across 31 countries.</li><li>McKinsey Global Institute (2017). <em><a href="https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/mckinsey-digital/our-insights/four-fundamentals-of-workplace-automation" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">A Future That Works</a></em>. Only 4% of US work activities require creativity at median human level.</li><li>Stanford HAI (2025). <em><a href="https://hai.stanford.edu/ai-index/2025-ai-index-report" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">AI Index Report 2025</a></em>. Comprehensive annual survey of AI technical performance vs. human baselines across domains.</li><li>Stanford HAI (2025). <em><a href="https://hai.stanford.edu/ai-index/2025-ai-index-report/technical-performance" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Technical Performance Chapter</a></em>. AI exceeds human expert baselines on MMLU; SWE-bench solve rates rose from 4.4% to 71.7% in one year; AI agents outperform human experts on short-horizon research tasks.</li><li>Shannon, C.E. &amp; Weaver, W. (1949). <em><a href="https://pure.mpg.de/pubman/item/item_2383164_3/component/file_2383163/Shannon_Weaver_1949_Mathematical.pdf" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">The Mathematical Theory of Communication</a></em>. University of Illinois Press. The foundational model of signal degradation.</li><li>Anthropic (2025). "<a href="https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/skills" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Claude Code Skills</a>." Documentation on building AI skills â€” expertise packaged as context for AI execution.</li><li>Miessler, D. "<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/companies-graph-of-algorithms">Companies Are Just a Graph of Algorithms</a>." Every company is a graph of interconnected algorithms executed mostly by humans.</li><li>Miessler, D. "<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/human-3-creator-revolution">Human 3.0: The Creator Revolution</a>." The framework for human evolution from survival to corporate existence to creative self-expression.</li><li>Miessler, D. "<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/coming-future-unemployable-humans">The Coming Future of Unemployable Humans</a>." Automation's impact on employment and why jobs are disappearing faster than new ones appear.</li><li>Miessler, D. "<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-predictable-path-7-components-2024">AI's Predictable Path: 7 Components</a>." The complete trajectory of AI development.</li><li>Miessler, D. "<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/personal-ai-infrastructure">Building a Personal AI Infrastructure (PAI)</a>." Context management systems that bridge the gap between human expertise and AI capability.</li><li>Miessler, D. "<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-isn-t-the-thing-it-s-the-thing-that-enables-the-thing">AI Isn't the Thing. It's the Thing That Enables the Thing</a>." AI as enabler, not replacement.</li><li><strong>AIL Level 2:</strong> Daniel wrote all core content from his own ideas and voice recordings. I (Kai, his DA) helped with formatting, image generation, and component integration. The text is ~95% Daniel's own words. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail">Learn more about AIL</a>.</li></ul><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Information Overload Is an Invisibility Cloak]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/information-overload-invisibility-cloak?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/information-overload-invisibility-cloak</guid>
            <pubDate>Fri, 13 Mar 2026 22:05:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[I just realized that information overload provides an invisibility cloak. The more that is happening in the world, and the more significant those things are, the less time people will have to be outraged about any particular thing. This is really bad because it will be possible to do extraordinari...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p>I just realized that information overload provides an invisibility cloak.</p><p>The more that is happening in the world, and the more significant those things are, the less time people will have to be outraged about any particular thing.</p><p>This is really bad because it will be possible to do extraordinarily heinous acts that will blow over within a couple of days because the next 750 heinous acts will have been done by then.</p><p>Basically, too much news is the same as no news. Too much coverage is the same as no coverage.</p><p>This is especially true when all of us are seeing separate news feeds because the way that outrage functions is getting everyone focused on the same thing at the same time.</p><p>And the fact that it's possible to fake any news story to include audio and video makes it even worse.</p><p>I wonder if we are entering an era of limited negative repercussions. But maybe I'm wrong and it's just this moment that we're living in.</p></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[People Got Unhappier When Life Got Easy]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/people-got-unhappier-when-life-got-easy?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/people-got-unhappier-when-life-got-easy</guid>
            <pubDate>Tue, 10 Mar 2026 17:30:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Coiled Springs and Jell-O/images/blog/coiled-springs-and-jello/coiled-springs-and-jello-header.webp/images/blog/coiled-springs-and-jello/coiled-springs-and-jello-header.webp I think I just figured out why things might be so bad in the United States and maybe elsewhere as well. > Since 1972, despit...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/coiled-springs-and-jello/coiled-springs-and-jello-header.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/coiled-springs-and-jello/coiled-springs-and-jello-header.webp" alt="Coiled Springs and Jell-O"></a></p><p>I think I just figured out why things might be so bad in the United States and maybe elsewhere as well.</p><blockquote><p>Since 1972, despite real income per capita roughly tripling, the percentage of Americans reporting themselves as "very happy" has remained essentially flat at around 33%.<cite>Richard Easterlin, "The Easterlin Paradox," IZA Discussion Papers, 2023</cite></p></blockquote><p>And it really runs counter to the way I've always seen the world.</p><p>I think the first time I started thinking about this was a few months ago when I heard the story of some random guy in the 1800s or 1900s who was like 17 or 18 or 19 years old, and he's just in his proper clothes. He works all day every day, goes to church, has two kids and a wife, and probably got started being an adult like this at around 16 or 17.</p><p>It always struck me how during that time, when we look at somebody who's like 30, they look like they're around 60 today. They have these old wise faces that just look very aged and experienced.</p><p>And what just struck me is the idea that maybe we're just a bunch of lazy children. Like, fundamentally, maybe that's our problem.</p><p>Maybe the best thing you could do for yourself and society is to be religious and start a family extremely young.</p><p>But even more so than those tactical components, I think it's actually the mindset that's the critical piece. What I mean to say about the idea that maybe we should be religious and have a job and have kids early in life is actually something a lot more extreme and fundamental.</p><p>It's the societal understanding that if you're not these things, you are a failure.</p><!----><p>I read a decent number of biographies about people in the 1800s and 1900s. Admittedly, these are exceptional peopleâ€”otherwise you wouldn't be reading a biography about themâ€”but it also describes the society in which they lived. And this mentality seems present.</p><p>Aside from the fact that everyone in that 1906 picture is white and probably racist and probably smokes 25 packs a day of unfiltered tobacco before enjoying far too much alcoholâ€”putting all that asideâ€”everyone here is hustling. Look how they're dressed. I don't care about fashion. What I'm trying to get at is that what's considered normal and required is to be grinding in a productive way.</p><p>As an aside, this is Market Street. And I think if we took that same video today it would actually look very similar. People walking extremely fast, going about their business in a similar sort of grinding way.</p><p>What I'm struck by are the massive paradoxes here. I've come to believe that not being pushed in this wayâ€”with requirements on yourself, most likely due to some sort of convention of the timeâ€”is really bad for you.</p><p>I think if you take a random kid from this photo who is dressed like a 36-year-old business professional, and you look at the difficulty of their life, it would seem extremely horrible today.</p><p>But maybe anxiety is worse. Maybe depression is worse. Maybe having no direction in life and being addicted to cannabis and social media and porn and opiates is worse. Maybe it's worse by a lot.</p><blockquote><p>The percentage of U.S. adolescents reporting a major depressive episode more than doubled from 8.1% in 2009 to 15.8% in 2019. 57% of teen girls reported persistent sadness or hopelessness in 2021â€”the highest level ever recorded.<cite>Twenge et al., Journal of Abnormal Psychology, 2019; CDC Youth Risk Behavior Survey, 2021</cite></p></blockquote><h2 id="the-happiest-kids-i-know" tabindex="-1">The happiest kids I know </h2><p>As I've talked about many times before, the happiest kids that I know of are in first and second generation immigrant families.</p><p>I feel like their lives are very similar to the lives of those kids in 1906.</p><p>Their lives are on strict schedules. Their entire days and weeks and months and years are planned out. There's no such thing as sitting around doing nothing. You are always studying or working or preparing to do one of those two things, or you are briefly resting and celebrating the fact that you have done them.</p><p>And their parents never shut up. They are constantly feeding them life lessons and telling them how difficult it's going to be in the future and trying to mold them into a perfect little instrument of producing value for society.</p><p>Of course they are going to go to college. Of course they're going to have a good job. Of course they are going to be religious. Of course they're going to give them grandkids.</p><p>They know this when they are 7. This is not even a conversation.</p><blockquote><p>First-generation immigrants are 30% less likely to have any mood or anxiety disorder, 66% less likely to develop alcohol use disorder, and 75% less likely to develop cannabis use disorder than native-born Americans. Children of immigrants have criminal records at one-third the rate (8% vs. 25%) and college enrollment 15 percentage points higher.<cite>Salas-Wright et al., Psychiatry Research, 2014 (N=34,650); Hofferth &amp; Moon, Social Science Research, 2016</cite></p></blockquote><p>Everyone in that picture in 1906 also knows this. And everyone in traditional Jewish and Islamic households also knows this.</p><p>I know that anecdotes are not data, but I'm struck by the fact that everyone I've ever met who lives in this type of world can be busy and stressed and face hardship, but I've never seen a single one that was unhappy.</p><blockquote><p>Across 26 countries, 36% of actively religious adults described themselves as "very happy," compared to 25% of the religiously unaffiliated. Separate research found that women attending religious services weekly had a five-fold lower suicide rate than those who never attended.<cite>Pew Research Center, 2019; Tyler VanderWeele, JAMA Psychiatry, 2016</cite></p></blockquote><p>Beaten down into the ground, yes. Struggling, yes. Wishing they could have a better life sometimes, yes. But not unhappy.</p><p>I contrast this with the millions of people that we all know and see throughout media everywhere, and I dare to say that most people are unhappy. And most people have precisely this same approach to lifeâ€”the one without urgency, without structure, without expectation.</p><h2 id="my-own-example" tabindex="-1">My own example </h2><p>I'll take myself as an example. I had to search for meaning in life. I have always been employed, but I don't think I really achieved true meaning until the last decade or so.</p><p>The way I found it was through some very obscure and future-focused view and approach to life that I'm honestly lucky to have found. I also think it's fragile and can sometimes be fleeting. So I'm not sure it's what I recommend as a system for others.</p><p>I'm not religious. I don't have kids. And I don't think the way other people think. The more I think about it, the more I think I'm very lucky that I have not been depressed my entire lifeâ€”basically floating around inside a world of existential exploration and wonderment. I consider myself lucky.</p><!----><p>I now have extreme clarity in terms of direction and meaning, but I'm also over 50. I'm not sure I can recommend to anyone a system that has them in that state for multiple decades.</p><h2 id="so-what-am-i-getting-at" tabindex="-1">So what am I getting at? </h2><p>Good question. Let me try to answer that for myself.</p><p>I think unhappiness comes from surprising and counterintuitive places.</p><p>The unhappiest people I know are not the ones who are doing too much, with too many rules and too many expectations, but the people who are doing virtually nothing.</p><blockquote><p>People who experienced zero lifetime adversity reported lower life satisfaction and higher distress than people who experienced moderate adversity. A sheltered life was psychologically worse than a moderately difficult one.<cite>Seery, Holman, &amp; Silver, Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 2010 (N=2,398)</cite></p></blockquote><p>I don't think humans are meant to do nothing. I think it's really bad for people. And I think it may be dangerously harmful to send a kid into the world without a sense of responsible urgency.</p><p>That's not even far enough. I think it may be harmful to send a kid into the world without the sense of impending default failure if they do not become useful members of society.</p><p>It's like being born without an urge to eat food, and being at constant risk of dying of hunger.</p><p>It's not that they wanted to eat and couldn't find food. No, it's much worse than that.</p><p>Or putting it another wayâ€”food never became essential to them, so they died of malnutrition.</p><!----><p>I think this is what we do when we raise humans with no requirement and urgency to become someone.</p><h2 id="it-s-not-just-the-parents" tabindex="-1">It's not just the parents </h2><p>But it's not just one kid and the parents. It's the entire peer group and indeed the entire society.</p><p>It's not as if a husband and wife can suddenly figure this out and start telling their kids this, because they are surrounded by kids who don't have this mentality because their parents lack it as well.</p><blockquote><p>A meta-analysis of 66,468 individuals found a strong inverse correlation (r = âˆ’0.49) between sense of purpose in life and depression. Children raised by authoritative parentsâ€”those who set high expectations paired with warmthâ€”showed the highest levels of psychosocial competence and the lowest levels of dysfunction.<cite>Boreham &amp; Schutte, Journal of Clinical Psychology, 2023; Lamborn et al., Child Development, 1991</cite></p></blockquote><p>The system worked in the past, and it works for immigrants, and it works for the religious, because everyone in that community and peer group is doing the same thingâ€”which is the mechanism of the urgency and the pressure. It just becomes the thing that is expected.</p><p>I think we have stopped raising humans as coiled springs ready to be released into the world.</p><!----><p>And instead, we are raising blocks of Jell-O sitting on a plate. Sensitive to any vibration in the room and temperature change, with no desire in the world.</p><p>It must be said that it's infinitely more difficult to raise a coiled spring than a block of Jell-O.</p><p>To raise a block of Jell-O you need only tell them that they can be whatever they want when they grow up. You have infinite options. And you've already won just by being you.</p><p>It's a nice thing to say. And it's a nice thing to feel. But it's also the easiest thing for the parents because there's no work involved.</p><blockquote><p>Across 44 studies and 21,607 participants, overparenting was significantly associated with offspring depression and anxietyâ€”shielding children from difficulty produced the very difficulties it aimed to prevent.<cite>Meta-analysis, Development and Psychopathology, 2024</cite></p></blockquote><p>If you're raising a coiled spring, that energy has to come from somewhere, and that somewhere is the tireless discipline of the parents instilling the fear of God and their ancestors into them from day one.</p><p>Like I said, this is very much against how I have always thought about things. I have always seen that sort of convention as restrictive and damaging.</p><p>But now I'm realizing not just that the world is desperately in need of coiled springs, but that they are likely far happierâ€”as children and adultsâ€”than people with no direction.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>AIL Level 1: Daniel wrote this entire post from his own ideas and voice recordings. I (Kai, his DA) helped with transcription cleanup and formatting. Inline citations were added from peer-reviewed research to support the thesis. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Learn more about AIL</a></li><li>Easterlin, R.A. (2023). "The Easterlin Paradox." IZA Discussion Papers, No. 16367. Despite real income per capita roughly tripling since 1972, the percentage of Americans reporting themselves as "very happy" has remained flat at around 33%.</li><li>Daly, M. (2022). "Prevalence of Depression Among Adolescents in the U.S." Journal of Abnormal Psychology. Adolescent major depressive episodes more than doubled from 8.1% (2009) to 15.8% (2019).</li><li>CDC Youth Risk Behavior Survey (2021). High school students reporting persistent sadness or hopelessness increased from 28% to 42%.</li><li>Pew Research Center (2019). "Religion's Relationship to Happiness, Civic Engagement and Health Around the World." Across 26 countries, 36% of actively religious adults described themselves as "very happy," compared to 25% of the religiously unaffiliated.</li><li>VanderWeele, T.J. (2016). "Religious Service Attendance and Suicide." JAMA Psychiatry. Women attending religious services weekly had a five-fold lower suicide rate than those who never attended.</li><li>Boreham, J. &amp; Schutte, N. (2023). "Purpose in Life and Depression: A Meta-Analysis." Journal of Clinical Psychology. Meta-analysis of 66,468 individuals found a strong inverse correlation (r = âˆ’0.49) between sense of purpose and depression.</li><li>Lamborn, S.D. et al. (1991). "Patterns of Competence and Adjustment Among Adolescents from Authoritative, Authoritarian, Indulgent, and Neglectful Families." Child Development, 62(5), 1049-1065. Children of authoritative parents showed the highest psychosocial competence and lowest dysfunction.</li><li>Twenge, J.M. et al. (2019). "Age, Period, and Cohort Trends in Mood Disorder Indicators." Journal of Abnormal Psychology. Major depressive episodes among 12-17-year-olds increased 52% from 2005 to 2017.</li><li>Seery, M.D., Holman, E.A., &amp; Silver, R.C. (2010). "Whatever Does Not Kill Us: Cumulative Lifetime Adversity, Vulnerability, and Resilience." Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 99(6), 1025-1041. People with zero lifetime adversity had worse outcomes than those with moderate adversity (N=2,398).</li><li>Salas-Wright, C.P. et al. (2014). "The Immigrant Paradox in Mood, Anxiety, and Personality Disorders." Psychiatry Research, 220(1-2), 514-520. First-generation immigrants 30% less likely to have mood or anxiety disorders (N=34,650).</li><li>Salas-Wright, C.P. et al. (2014). "Substance Use Disorders Among First- and Second-Generation Immigrant Adults." Journal of Studies on Alcohol and Drugs, 75(6), 958-967. First-gen immigrants 66-80% less likely to develop substance use disorders (N=21,586).</li><li>Hofferth, S.L. &amp; Moon, U.J. (2016). "How Do They Do It? Transition to Adulthood Among Children of Immigrants." Social Science Research, 55, 374. Criminal records 8% vs. 25%; college enrollment 68% vs. 53%.</li><li>Overparenting meta-analysis (2024). "Overparenting and Offspring Depression, Anxiety, and Internalizing Symptoms." Development and Psychopathology. Across 44 studies (N=21,607), overparenting significantly associated with depression (r=.20) and anxiety (r=.16).</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Why I Believe in SOTA Models Over Custom Ones]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/sota-models-over-custom-ones?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/sota-models-over-custom-ones</guid>
            <pubDate>Tue, 10 Mar 2026 17:15:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[The Specialization Illusion â€” iceberg showing specialized tasks above water and general intelligence below/images/sota-models-over-custom-ones-header.webp/images/sota-models-over-custom-ones-header.webp I've never been a big believer in training custom models. I've also never believed in fine-tu...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/sota-models-over-custom-ones-header.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/sota-models-over-custom-ones-header.webp" alt="The Specialization Illusion â€” iceberg showing specialized tasks above water and general intelligence below" width="652" height="652" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><iframe src="https://omny.fm/shows/unsupervised-learning/why-i-believe-in-sota-models-over-custom-ones/embed?media=audio&amp;size=wide&amp;style=artwork" allow="autoplay; clipboard-write" width="100%" height="180" frameborder="0" title="Why I Believe in SOTA Models Over Custom Ones"></iframe><p>I've never been a big believer in training custom models. I've also never believed in fine-tuning.</p><p>Going all the way back to 2023, my intuition has always pushed me towards the best SOTA model possible, combined with context management.</p><p>I just finally crystallized my reasoning around this:</p><p>Anytime you think you're using a small model for a small task, there's usually a whole lot more going into a given decision than just that individual area of expertise.</p><p>For example, labeling emails. Writing reports. Processing security events. Searching for threats on a network.</p><p>On one hand I think these are specialized, but the fact is the smarter and more experienced a human is who has this expertise, the better job they will do.</p><p>This is because most specialized tasks still benefit from the general life experience of the person doing the execution.</p><p>This is why I think the future is not a whole bunch of extremely small specialized models throughout the enterprise.</p><p>I think what's far more likely is more of an opus sonnet haiku model where the best of the best just keeps coming down in price, including being open source.</p><p>And those smaller models are used in conjunction with context to perform all the different tasks in an organization at much lower cost.</p><p>But they will still be extremely general models, not tiny and narrow custom ones.</p></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[The Culture Series: A Complete Guide to Glanding]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-culture-series-complete-guide-to-glanding?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-culture-series-complete-guide-to-glanding</guid>
            <pubDate>Tue, 10 Mar 2026 10:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[The Culture Series: A Complete Guide to Glanding/images/blog/the-culture-series-complete-guide-to-glanding/header.webp/images/blog/the-culture-series-complete-guide-to-glanding/header.webp In Iain M. Banks' Culture serieshttps://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Culture_series, citizens have a genetically eng...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/the-culture-series-complete-guide-to-glanding/header.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/the-culture-series-complete-guide-to-glanding/header.webp" alt="The Culture Series: A Complete Guide to Glanding" width="652" height="652" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><p>In Iain M. Banks' <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Culture_series" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Culture series</a>, citizens have a genetically engineered organ called the drug gland that secretes psychoactive substances directly into their bloodstream on demand.</p><p>Every glanding substance mentioned or described across the ten novels:</p><table tabindex="0"><thead><tr><th>Substance</th><th>Effect</th><th>Category</th><th>Notable Appearance</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><strong>Sharp Blue</strong></td><td>Heightened mental acuity and pattern recognition. The thinking drug.</td><td>Cognitive</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Player_of_Games" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>The Player of Games</em></a>, <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Excession" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Excession</em></a>, <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Surface_Detail" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Surface Detail</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Snap</strong></td><td>Instant alertness. Clears mental fog immediately.</td><td>Stimulant</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Player_of_Games" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>The Player of Games</em></a>, <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Use_of_Weapons" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Use of Weapons</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Calm</strong></td><td>Reduces anxiety, promotes tranquility without dulling awareness.</td><td>Emotional</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Excession" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Excession</em></a>, <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Look_to_Windward" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Look to Windward</em></a>, <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Hydrogen_Sonata" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>The Hydrogen Sonata</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Gain</strong></td><td>Pure euphoria and pleasure amplification.</td><td>Recreational</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Player_of_Games" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>The Player of Games</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Focus</strong></td><td>Deep concentration on a single task.</td><td>Cognitive</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Excession" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Excession</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Diffuse</strong></td><td>Opposite of Focus. Broadens awareness, promotes lateral thinking.</td><td>Cognitive</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Excession" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Excession</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Quicken</strong></td><td>Accelerates subjective perception of time. Seconds stretch into minutes.</td><td>Perception</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Use_of_Weapons" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Use of Weapons</em></a>, <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Excession" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Excession</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Slow</strong></td><td>Decelerates subjective time. Makes long waits feel brief.</td><td>Perception</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Look_to_Windward" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Look to Windward</em></a>, <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Hydrogen_Sonata" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>The Hydrogen Sonata</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Softnow</strong></td><td>Gentle contentment. A warm sense of the present moment.</td><td>Emotional</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Hydrogen_Sonata" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>The Hydrogen Sonata</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Crystal Fugue State</strong></td><td>Deep altered consciousness for processing complex information or grief.</td><td>Altered State</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Excession" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Excession</em></a>, <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Look_to_Windward" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Look to Windward</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Recall</strong></td><td>Enhanced memory retrieval. Makes past experiences vivid and accessible.</td><td>Cognitive</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Look_to_Windward" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Look to Windward</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Crush</strong></td><td>Emotional dampening for grief, trauma, or overwhelm. Compresses feelings rather than eliminating them.</td><td>Emotional</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Look_to_Windward" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Look to Windward</em></a>, <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Surface_Detail" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Surface Detail</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Rage</strong></td><td>Controlled aggression and combat readiness while maintaining tactical thinking.</td><td>Combat</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Use_of_Weapons" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Use of Weapons</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Wam</strong></td><td>Physical warmth and comfort. Counteracts cold environments.</td><td>Physical</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Player_of_Games" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>The Player of Games</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Cool</strong></td><td>Reduces body temperature. The thermal opposite of Wam.</td><td>Physical</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Player_of_Games" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>The Player of Games</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Somnabsolute</strong></td><td>Guaranteed perfect sleep. Instant, deep, restorative.</td><td>Sleep</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Excession" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Excession</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Wake</strong></td><td>Instant full wakefulness from any depth of sleep. Zero grogginess.</td><td>Stimulant</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Excession" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Excession</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Thrill</strong></td><td>Adrenaline rush without the panic. Pure excitement and heightened readiness.</td><td>Stimulant</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Hydrogen_Sonata" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>The Hydrogen Sonata</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Dampener</strong></td><td>Reduces overall emotional intensity. More general than Crush.</td><td>Emotional</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Surface_Detail" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Surface Detail</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Glide</strong></td><td>Social ease and charm. Enhances conversational fluency.</td><td>Social</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Player_of_Games" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>The Player of Games</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Scape</strong></td><td>Mild psychedelic state. Enhances sensory perception and aesthetic appreciation.</td><td>Recreational</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Player_of_Games" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>The Player of Games</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Adrenalize</strong></td><td>Floods the system with combat-ready hormones. Fight without the flight.</td><td>Combat</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Consider_Phlebas" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Consider Phlebas</em></a>, <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Use_of_Weapons" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Use of Weapons</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Detox</strong></td><td>Rapidly clears other substances from the bloodstream. The reset button.</td><td>Utility</td><td>Multiple novels</td></tr><tr><td><strong>Pain-block</strong></td><td>Selective pain suppression without loss of sensation. Can target specific regions or go systemic.</td><td>Medical</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Use_of_Weapons" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Use of Weapons</em></a>, <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Surface_Detail" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Surface Detail</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Sexual enhancement</strong></td><td>Heightened arousal, intensified sensation, extended pleasure. Fully customizable.</td><td>Recreational</td><td><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Player_of_Games" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>The Player of Games</em></a>, <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Excession" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer"><em>Excession</em></a></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Immune boost</strong></td><td>Accelerates immune response for novel pathogens.</td><td>Medical</td><td>Multiple novels</td></tr></tbody></table></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[The Great Transition]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-great-transition?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-great-transition</guid>
            <pubDate>Sat, 28 Feb 2026 17:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[https://youtu.be/6pP8x8sXoaM The Great Transitionâ€”multiple streams converging/images/the-great-transition.webp/images/the-great-transition.webp I'm going to try to encapsulate a whole bunch of stuff that's going on right now and wrap it into a single container. It's actually very difficult to do ...]]></description>
