            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><div class="video-container" data-v-959fc5c9=""><iframe src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/6pP8x8sXoaM?rel=0&amp;modestbranding=1&amp;playsinline=1&amp;enablejsapi=1" title="YouTube video player" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen="" loading="lazy" data-v-959fc5c9=""></iframe></div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/the-great-transition.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/the-great-transition.webp" alt="The Great Transitionâ€”multiple streams converging" width="1024" height="1024" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><p>I'm going to try to encapsulate a whole bunch of stuff that's going on right now and wrap it into a single container. It's actually very difficult to do because there's so much change, and things are getting crazier every single week, every single day almost.</p><p>I've noticed a whole bunch of transitions happening at the same time, and I'm calling it the great transition. It's really many smaller transitions, but they have a theme and a direction. And I think I know roughly where they're going.</p><p>What I want to give you is something where if you think about all of these ideas and just let them stew, the news that comes out over the next weeks, months, even years will just make more sense. You can put it into this container, this mental model of thinking about things.</p><h2 id="knowledge-goes-from-private-to-public" tabindex="-1">Knowledge goes from private to public </h2><p>There are a few different things making this happen. One is just LLMs in general, AI in general. The concept is that it consumes all the stuff from the internetâ€”all the books, all the blogs, forum conversationsâ€”all this training that's been done on these models. All of that condenses into a model that's kind of representative of all this knowledge. Everybody kind of knows that already.</p><p>What's not so much understood is what this is actually doing to knowledge work.</p><p>In the past, going back 10, 20, 30, 50 years, if you were an expert in something, you had knowledge that no one else had. If you were a specialist consultant at McKinsey or you were a heart doctor or whatever, you had special knowledge. And you hadn't captured even a 10th of it. Let's say you've written two booksâ€”you still haven't captured a 10th of your knowledge. You just know things that other people don't. If you're a security professional who's been doing this for 20 years, you just understand things. If you're a CISO that's done this multiple times, you just understand things and get things that nobody else has. And importantly, it's not in a book somewhere. Even if you've written books, your knowledge is still not fully in the books.</p><p>That has always protected smart peopleâ€”the ones with both the smarts and the experience. That combination has made them very special.</p><p>What is happening now is completely changing that.</p><aside>ðŸ§  <a href="https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/agents-and-tools/claude-code/skills">Skills</a>â€”folders of markdown filesâ€”are how specialized knowledge gets captured and made portable.</aside><p>Especially with skillsâ€”this whole concept that <a href="https://www.anthropic.com" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Anthropic</a> came up with. We're talking about a folder full of markdown files that can encapsulate a decent amount of your knowledge. You still have the capture problem where they don't know exactly what to say, how to capture it, but here's the situation: many, many smart people are producing skills and many, many other smart people are going to collect specialized knowledge from all over the internet, anywhere it's been written down, and bring that into a skill. Plus all these specialist peopleâ€”they're writing books, doing presentations, writing blogs, doing interviews, doing podcasts.</p><p>In the past we'd never had a system that could basically say, go get all of that. Go get everything Dr. Huberman has ever said about health or morning routines, bring it all together and turn that into a skill. This is one prompt. Find everything Huberman has said about morning routines from every podcast, every blog, every article, every interview and put that into a skill. That new thing combined with the models just getting betterâ€”it feeds on itself. The model then can consume all those skills.</p><p>The gap between specialized privatized knowledgeâ€”inside of someone's mind, some specialist doctor, some specialist psychiatrist who's been doing this work for 40 or 50 yearsâ€”the delta between what they know and no one else knows is getting smaller. That is massively impactful for humanity in general.</p><p>Then there's another layer on this. All of that is being consumed by these labs who are spending billions of dollars bringing that knowledge into the models. But what we just saw from Anthropicâ€”and this is happening all over the placeâ€”a bunch of Chinese labs are doing it in mass, very organized. China is known for doing this. They are famous for stealing ideas and stealing content.</p><p>They're also massively going all in on open source models. I believe they have a very clear strategy: you don't have to compete to be a pinnacle lab. They don't have an Anthropic. They don't have a Google DeepMind. They don't have an OpenAI. But they do have <a href="https://www.deepseek.com" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">DeepSeek</a>, and DeepSeek has been called out for doing this for a very long time. They are capturing the knowledge of all the billions of dollars of work and bringing it into open source.</p><p>What they are doing as a Chinese strategy for AI is releasing it, diffusing it, absorbing it into the pool. You've heard the metaphor peeing in the pool. Our specialized knowledgeâ€”what specialized humans could do that no one else could doâ€”that is the pee that's going into the pool. You can't pull it out. It's just going to be in there.</p><p>And the techniques that make those premier labs betterâ€”those are also being diffused. Somehow when the major labs have a major advantage and jump ahead, the Chinese models seem to get it a few months later. The specialized knowledge is being diffused into public domain. That's just a transition that's happening.</p><h2 id="products-go-from-standalone-software-to-apis" tabindex="-1">Products go from standalone software to APIs </h2><p>I talked about this in <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-real-internet-of-things">my book from 2016</a>â€”basically said that <strong><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/mobile-ai-digital-assistants-business-apis">businesses become APIs</a></strong>. And we're finally now starting to see this.</p><aside>ðŸ“š From <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-real-internet-of-things"><em>The Real Internet of Things</em></a>, published in 2016.</aside><p>All these people releasing tools, models, functionalityâ€”a company that does remove background, Excalidraw just came out with a new piece of functionality where you could just describe what you want to make and it will build all the different objects for you in your favorite fonts and your favorite aesthetic. It'll just build you diagrams.</p><p>My first question when I saw this was hold on. I went and looked at the documentation and it basically said you just go into the interface and type into Excalidraw what you want. And I'm like, what are you talking about? Do you honestly think in early 2026, I'm going to open up Excalidraw and type in a prompt? Are you kidding me?</p><p>So I posted: this looks amazing. Looks fantastic. There's no way I'm going to use it. Can you make this available as an <a href="https://modelcontextprotocol.io" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">MCP</a>? Can you make this available as an API? I'm not going to do any of this ever. <strong>If I have to open an app, I've already lost.</strong> My AI should be doing all of this for me.</p><p>When I posted that on Twitter, a whole bunch of people showed up and they're like, yeah, a hundred percent. I need an MCP for this. Otherwise it's not useful.</p><p>That is the way everything is going. If you notice, most of the releases coming out for products now, they're like, here's the MCP for it. Here's how your agents can do this automatically. This is just becoming the new way to release software. And this is heading in the exact direction that I put in that book in 2016: businesses become APIs.</p><h2 id="the-consumer-disappearsâ€”your-agent-decides" tabindex="-1">The consumer disappearsâ€”your agent decides </h2><p>Why is this important? Because the consumer is not so much making the choice anymore. The consumer is not going to be like, hmm, yeah, there's 47,938 different options for removing backgrounds from images. Let me pull up GitHub and Google and spend two and a half hours sampling and trying different ones. Noâ€”there's too many apps. And because of AI, there's too many apps being made right on top of that. There'll be hundreds more of these things coming out all the time.</p><p>The only way this resolves is there are directories. If you have a background remover toolâ€”by the way, my favorite is Remove BG, and they have an API. That's what my system Kai actually calls.</p><p>If you have one of these agents, there will basically be orchestration layers, directories of services labeled and categorizedâ€”taxonomy, folksonomy, whateverâ€”saying if you want to remove backgrounds from images, here's your list of 27,000, but they will be rated. Different services with different ratings. And my system Kai will know which services it prefers. He's going to select the highest rated one with the most ratings, the least negative ratings, whatever algorithm Kai wants to use. Pulls that in, drops it into our workflow inside of our skill. That's it.</p><p>Where's the website? Where's the website for remove background? Who needs a website? This is a directory service, like the old days, like Yahoo directories. It's already been rated. My agents are going to check those ratings and find the API and integrate it.</p><p>This old way of making the software, packaging it, oh, it's got to have a nice UI, a nice websiteâ€”when the person comes to the website, they got to really like it, then they click the buy buttonâ€”it's all going away.</p><h2 id="interface-and-seo-are-dying" tabindex="-1">Interface and SEO are dying </h2><p>This is tightly coupled with another transition: interface is going away. SEO is going away.</p><p>Interface used to be for humans. We make software, we have services, and we have to have an interface for that. Not just the interface you use day to day to interact with it, but also the marketing and documentation interface. Front end in general is going away. It's not that the content won't be there. It's that it will be designed to be consumed by agents. AI will be the main consumers.</p><p><strong>Everyone gets a digital assistant</strong>â€”I wrote about this in <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-real-internet-of-things"><em>The Real Internet of Things</em></a> in 2016. Everyone gets a digital assistant, <strong>everything gets an API</strong>.</p><blockquote> Every object has a daemonâ€”an API to the world that all other objects understand. <cite><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-real-internet-of-things">The Real Internet of Things</a>, 2016</cite></blockquote> Our AI, because it knows us so well, when we make a request, it goes and gets the thing from the API and brings it back to us. <p>When we want an interfaceâ€”I'm buying shoes, I want to see what they look likeâ€”our AI will be presenting the interface to us. People are already building bespoke software. Custom software is the direction it's going. Software goes from everyone using the same packages to everyone getting bespoke software.</p><p>The core part of your business, the core part of your product is the API, which will be used by the agents of the consumer. And the interface will be between their agent and them. That's the interface.</p><p>SEO goes from trying to attract the user to trying to attract the user's AI. When I say, hey, I need a new mattress, I'm not saying that to the internet. I'm not saying that to Google. I'm saying that to my DA. And my DA knows my sleeping habits, knows my routine, knows if I like a firm mattress or a soft one, knows my girl likes a softer bed, I like a harder one on my side.</p><p>Your agent knows you, therefore it can do smarter queries for you. But the point is it's the one doing the queries. If it's going to be tricked into picking one mattress versus another, the tricking needs to happen at the AI layer because I'm just going to do whatever my agent tells me. The agent's going to be like, yeah, I found the best one. It's this much. Do you want me to get it for you? I'll have it here tomorrow. And that's the end of that.</p><h2 id="enterprise-the-graph-of-operations" tabindex="-1">Enterprise: the graph of operations </h2><p>Much of the same stuff happening on the consumer side is also going to happen on the enterprise side. And those changes are massive. Absolutely massive.</p><p>One of the big transitions is going from humans creating processes and following them to AI running the business based on SOPsâ€”basically building out a graph structure of all the work that needs to be done.</p><p>I did <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/companies-graph-of-algorithms">a post in 2024</a> talking about how companies are just a graph of APIs, a graph of operations. Take the insurance example: you have to look at photos, look at their account, they're making a claim, you need to filter for fraud. Is this legit? Does the picture look real? Do they have a real account? Are they making lots of claims? Does the account look compromised? All these different things. And if it looks legit, here's how much we're going to pay you out.</p><p>That's the type of thing where, currently in the enterprise, there is not a graph that the CEO could look down and say, <strong>this is my entire business</strong>. This is every task happening in my company and the process of how it's done. The SOPâ€”standard operating procedureâ€”for how this thing is done.</p><p>AI is going to have this for every company. That's the major transition, and it's just now starting. It's very slow. Much slower than all the consumer adoption we've seen over the last couple of years.</p><blockquote> You've probably never seen your company in this way, but AI soon will. <cite><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/companies-graph-of-algorithms">Companies are a graph of algorithms</a></cite></blockquote><p>In the past, people were basically the company. The people were doing the work. Yes, they had documents. Yes, they had processes. But it's the people doing the work. They're supposed to follow this policy, but it's just a doc. Do they follow it? Not really. The main person who maintained them went on maternity leave and never came back. Those docs get old. Nobody's following the policy.</p><p>That is completely different than an AI saying, I now own all these SOPs. Here is the map of all work that's being done. Humans are still thereâ€”humans are the ones responsible for improving the AI, for telling the AI, hey, we need to change this SOP. You have a conversation with the AI, it makes the change, all the documentation is updated, all the SOPs are updated, the cross references are updated. That is the new model for business.</p><p>Now a software vendor comes in. Before, they would bring their salesperson, take someone out to a steak dinner. Yeah, it does background removal way better than anything else. If they convince this person and the manager and procurement, they buy it. It's humans buying software. Software is a package sitting on a shelf.</p><p>In this new model, this graph system includes all the tools. The conversation becomes completely different with the software vendor. Here is my map. Here's all the processes, all the work, every single workflow. What are you replacing? What are you doing better? Click on the node for background image removal. Here's the metricsâ€”how fast it is, how cheap it is, how many times it failed, how many times it succeeded. Now what are yours?</p><p>They're going to have to produce metrics that say they can do that function better. It's no longer about a software package that some human is buying and maybe they use it, maybe they don't. It's an AI saying, here are my metrics for this function. Can you prove that your metrics are better? That's a completely different way of thinking about software.</p><h2 id="automation-goes-from-helper-to-replacement" tabindex="-1">Automation goes from helper to replacement </h2><p>The transition here is automation going from a thing that helps humans do their jobs betterâ€”improves productivity and efficiencyâ€”to being a way for companies to get to their ideal state of being able to do all the work themselves.</p><p>This is colossal. This is economy changing. This really is the end of labor.</p><p>There's labor and there's capital and these have always been in balance. It gets disrupted because companies have always wished they could do all the work without employees. If you're a single founder and you don't have much work to do, you do all the work yourself, you get all the profits yourself.</p><p>If your business is washing clothes and you buy a washing machineâ€”before, you were doing it by hand in the river. You weren't making that much money. You saved up for a year and bought a washing machine. Now you're able to do way more clothes and make way more money. If a whole bunch of people come to you and say, hey, I can also wash clothes in the river and you need to hire me because that's what's fairâ€”you're going to be like, are you kidding me? I can do all this work myself. I literally am doing all the work myself.</p><p>If I have a clothes washing business and I have 10 washing machines behind me, that is me doing all the work myself. That is the transition that is happening. That is what AI is.</p><p>The total amount of compensation that knowledge workers receive is somewhere around <strong>$50 trillion per year globally</strong>. I think it's somewhere around $10 trillion for the US. That is how much money companies are spending to pay humans. And the major transition here is they don't want to be paying those humans. They actually never did.</p><!----><p>There are exceptions. If you're a small spunky founder and you want to work with your friendsâ€”you build a small startup, you're all kind of owners at that point. You'll still have elite employees, cadre, co-founders. That's not going to go away. But we're talking about going from tens of thousands of employees to a few hundred, maybe eventually a few dozen. Massive reduction because of this different way of thinking about automation. It's not a thing that helps a human do a task. It is a way to get to the state of the company doing the work itself, which is a natural, clean, happy state for any company.</p><h2 id="human-3-0-the-post-corporate-world" tabindex="-1">Human 3.0: the post-corporate world </h2><p>So what are we supposed to do? If everyone gets fired, who's going to buy all the stuff?</p><p>There's going to be money. People are going to receive money to pay their bills. Otherwise you just don't have a society. That will get solved one way or another, hopefully gently and fast.</p><p>But the question is, how's this actually going to happen? What are people going to do? How are people going to work?</p><p>The transition is going from work meaning you work for a big corporation to work meaning you do things yourself. You have offerings yourself. You produce value yourself and you broadcast that value out.</p><p>What's going to happen is there will be a technology layer that links people. It links projects with services. It links people who have capabilities with those who need them.</p><p>Instead of people working for companies en masseâ€”because those companies are trying to get rid of everyoneâ€”we're going to make money by producing value ourselves, by articulating the skills that we have, the capabilities that we have, the products that we provide, the services that we provide, broadcasting that out. That is going to go up into one of these directories, like I talked about before with products that AI can look at. But this will also be the substrate for all work to be done.</p><p>Humans will broadcast their capabilities. I'm a systems engineer, eight years experience, here's all the different stuff I can do, here's my portfolio. By the way, I like to mountain bike. This is your <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/launching-daemon-personal-api">daemon</a>. This is your broadcast system, describing the people you've worked with, your reputation score, people give you upvotesâ€”kind of similar to what LinkedIn was doing, but this is the actual play.</p><p>There's a substrate that connects all these different people. When I need a cat sitter because I'm going on vacation, I broadcast out, hey, I need someone to watch my cat. My AI is broadcasting that for me. Everyone's AI is watching the substrate. Someone's like, hey, I'm a cat person, I love cats, I live two blocks away. Her AI tells her, there's a cat sitting job over here, it's going to pay $84. Do you accept? Boom. Yes.</p><p>Someone injured themselves on the corner. Does anyone have medical professional training? EMT skills? It's going to beacon for people nearby who have a reputation score above a certain threshold. Someone takes the job. They go help the person.</p><p>Same for gardening, engineering services, tutoring, meal prep, personal training. Everyone who has services, capabilities, value to offerâ€”they are beaconing out onto this system.</p><blockquote> Humans and objects will broadcast daemons around them, advertising their attributes and interaction capabilities. <cite><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/universal-daemonization-future-internet-iot">Universal daemonization</a></cite></blockquote><p>That is what I'm calling Human 3.0. It's the state we're going to get to.</p><p>I love this because it's more human focused. It's humans connecting with humans. It's not hierarchicalâ€”I work for Sarah, Sarah works for Joe, Joe works for Raj, and oh I'm having a meeting with Raj, oh my God he's three skip levels above me. This whole military structure, this whole dreading Mondayâ€”it's toxic and poisonous. And it has been for decades, people have been so unhappy with this. And now that it's actually under threat, people are like, well, don't get rid of my job. Don't fire me. Because they're worried about losing their livelihood, paying rent and a mortgage and school and groceries. That's understandable. But remember we shouldn't be clinging to a thing we hate and have hated.</p><p>This new human-based substrate where things are a lot more equal: you get hired based on your skills. That's a relationship you can get out of any time. You can be in multiple of theseâ€”ongoing retainer type things with 20 different customers. You're on a big project for six months getting paid from that. Plus you're doing the cat sitting. Plus you're a part-time EMT at night. You're broadcasting everything that you are. Not just "here's my resume, I'm a tech engineer level three, I worked at Intuit." That is not you.</p><p>What this is going to allow us to do is broadcast our full selves and be compensated and rewarded for being ourselves. If you're the best nurturer in the world, forget tech skillsâ€”the best listener, the best parent, the best tutor, the best boxing coachâ€”that's in your daemon. That is broadcasted. They become world famous for that. And they make money from it, which they should. This is how humanity should work.</p><h2 id="cybersecurity-becomes-ai-vs-ai" tabindex="-1">Cybersecurity becomes AI vs. AI </h2><p>Similar to a lot of the other things we've already talked about, cybersecurity has been human based. You hire a human team. They are good security engineers. They're doing pen tests, security assessments, vulnerability assessments. They're manually looking at all the different vendors trying to figure out which ones are dangerous. And they're just being bombarded by all these requests.</p><p>What security becomes now is your AI stack as a defender against the AI stack of the attacker. And unfortunately you're not facing one attacker. You're facing all the attackers.</p><p>The attacker is trying to understand your company extremely well. It's creating personality profiles on all your employees. It's coming up with the best spearphishing campaigns to find the ones who probably have the most access based on their job title. It's constantly pulling your DNS. It's trying to see if you're doing a merger and acquisition with a company that doesn't have good security. They're sending spearphishing emails. They're trying to compromise all your websites. They're trying to pivot internally. And they're doing this at machine speed. They've just got so many agents working on this, constantly hitting you.</p><p>You can't tell Chris and Raj and Sarah, hey, great job last year. I need you to do 895 times as much work because that's how many more attacks we're being hit with. That doesn't work. You also can't say, great news, we got three more headcount. That also won't make a dent.</p><p><strong>Your only chance is to have the same AI or better as the attacker.</strong> What happens to all companies also happens to security programs. It's no longer about here's our security team and roughly the things we need to do. SOPs. Everything is a process and workflows which you could visually look at and seeâ€”this is the queue for processing incoming things, here are the constant workflows for finding insider threats, here's every single tool, every single decision point, every single approval point that needs to happen as part of CI/CD before something goes live. Everything becomes transparent, visible with discrete actions and decision points at each area.</p><p>And the game is: are we getting that context updated? New AWS account just stood up. We launched a new service in Asia and Iceland and Seattle. How quickly are we as a security team learning about that? As AI starts spinning this stuff up for the attackers, they're going to build a world model of these companies faster than the company has it. Because the company has to go slowâ€”they have to have 19 meetings to prepare for the meeting. Attackers are just going to YOLO it, submit the single prompt, make no mistakes and start attacking.</p><aside>ðŸŽ¯ The game: your orchestration system needs to be better than the attackers'.</aside><p>The transition is from humans doing security work to a unified workflow model with SOPs executed largely by agents, with humans there to tweak and improve and guide and steer and validate. The game is for your orchestration system to be better than the attackers.</p><h2 id="the-inversion-industries-become-use-cases-inside-ai" tabindex="-1">The inversion: industries become use cases inside AI </h2><p>This next one is a way of thinking about enterprise AI in a completely different way. I <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/weve-been-thinking-about-ai-all-wrong">put this out about a year ago</a>, and I think it's really powerful as an inversion.</p><p>Currentlyâ€”and even still nowâ€”everyone is thinking, okay, we have security, we need to put AI on it. We have finance, we need to put AI on it. We have HR, we need to put AI on that. So the idea is you have the discipline, the topic, and then AI gets sprinkled on top.</p><p>I don't think that's the way to think about it.</p><p>I think the way to think about it is: you have a company and the company's work and all its workflows and the graph of all the services and tools and operations, SOPs, goals, everything. That is actually the system. The system is the graph of operations. It is the graph of algorithms that take place to make this business function. Think of AI as a system for running this graph of algorithms. That is what AI is.</p><p>Then the question becomes: show me procurement. You look at this graph and 19 different lines all light upâ€”those are the procurement workflows. We can drill into those. Here's the tools. Here's the human involved. Here's the decisions. Here's the sign-offs. Here's the exceptions.</p><p>What ends up happening is all the different things that used to be industries become use cases inside of AI.</p><!----><p>Some of these are security, some are HR, some are engineering, some are marketing. AI is the container. AI is the thing. And it just has functions that happen to be affiliated with what we used to call industries. That is a fundamental transition.</p><p>If you abstract everything to questions and everything to algorithmsâ€”"how happy are our employees?" or "how much money are we spending on compensation?" or "should we pay more for bonuses?"â€”those are questions. Those questions are not an industry. They just happen to be associated with what we used to call an industry. All of it feeds off this underlying unified context and graph of algorithms, powered and managed and orchestrated by AI.</p><h2 id="custom-everything-and-the-fragmentation-problem" tabindex="-1">Custom everything and the fragmentation problem </h2><p>In the past, you had very few organizations producing software. Adobe was on top. Microsoft was on top. You go into any enterprise, they're mostly running Microsoft or Google. I think that might start to go away.</p><p>It's not that these major platforms won't still have a stronghold. It's just that the implementation of their software isn't going to look the same inside of all these different companies. For smaller companies that aren't legacy, their software stack could look completely different from another startup maybe even doing the same sort of stuff, just because the founders will be like, yeah, I like terminal style, I don't like UIs. I want everything to be API based. I really like the color purple.</p><p>We're already seeing this with custom replacements of tons of different SaaS software. It's really easy to create a version of something. There's a big difference between that and having it roll out enterprise-wide, stable and secure. That'll take a couple of years to work through. But there's a very high chance that companies and consumers will be making their own software.</p><p>Let's be clear: there's 8 billion people on the planet, not everyone's making their own software. But consider that in 2019 or 2022, the number of people who made software products was rounded down to zero. Everything in the app store, every software company, rounded down to zero. If you multiply that by 1,000 or 10,000 or a million, that is a lot more software. Plus the ability for someone to speak to their AI agent and say, hey, I really wish I had a workout app, it's $19 a month, it's pretty good, but I wish it did this, this, and thisâ€”and boom, it's now installed on your phone, the other one's uninstalled, the subscription is canceled.</p><p>Every CFO is looking at their software list and saying, how can I cancel all of this? Just like the employee thingâ€”how can I get to zero employees? How can I not pay any other company for software? How can it all be ours? Everyone wants this. We couldn't do it before because it's hard to make software. You got to maintain it. The better AI gets, the easier that gets.</p><aside>ðŸ˜Ÿ Robert Putnam warned about this fragmentation in <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bowling_Alone"><em>Bowling Alone</em></a> back in 2000.</aside><p>This ties into something Robert Putnam talked about in <em>Bowling Alone</em> in 2000. When you have fragmentation like this across companies but mostly across people, it's going to be profound. The reason the country was so unified before is that we were all watching the same TV shows, the same news, drove the same cars, had the same watches, went to the same churches, lived on the same block. Everyone's consuming the same sources, thinking similar things, reading the same newspapers.</p><p>When everyone gets custom software, custom AIâ€”you're not even viewing the sources. There's millions of them. Billions of them. Your AI's job is to consume all of that, understand you, understand your needs, and give you what you want. That opens up the possibility that all of us will be having a different world experience. We believe the reality that we see.</p><p>If you watch a particular news source, particular YouTube channels, particular podcastsâ€”you think the world works that way. That is your world. That is reality to you. The person next door might be in a completely different reality. They might not even know about the most popular thing that happened yesterday. This fragmentation because of custom everything is going to be massive.</p><p>Inside companies, the dynamic is strange. How do you audit software? How do you do security scans? How do you do compliance? It's a lot easier when everyone has SAP and Microsoft 365 and CrowdStrike and Palo Alto firewalls. What happens when everything is custom?</p><h2 id="ideal-state-management-the-biggest-idea-in-ai" tabindex="-1">Ideal state management: the biggest idea in AI </h2><p>I've saved this one for last.</p><p>I think the ultimate use case for AI is what I'm calling <strong>ideal state management</strong>â€”or state orchestration. I've <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/nobody-is-talking-about-generalized-hill-climbing">written about this before</a>. The real term will be created probably in the next year or so.</p><p>What we've been doing inside of companies, what we've been doing as people, what we've been doing as a society is just kind of YOLOing. This is what we've always done. This is what humanity does. Companies are like, hey, we should have goals, we should have OKRs, we should have a meeting, we should plan the next year. Those documents go somewhere. Maybe they get revised, maybe they don't.</p><p>If you track a company's goals for an average medium-sized company over the course of a year or five years or ten years, they're just making stuff up. It changes constantly. The management changes. They come out with a set of metrics. They don't hit them. They come out right after with a new plan, a new hire who's going to be amazing, new metrics. Constant reinventionâ€”not because of innovation, but because we're winging it. This is no fault of anybody. Everyone's doing it. Very, very smart people are doing it. It's just the reality that we live in.</p><p>This is one of the fundamental changes AI is going to bring. I think we're about to move away from this ad hoc YOLO approach to something much more powerful: state management.</p><p>State management starts with defining what ideal state is. This is a thing that most companies do not have. They don't have an articulated statementâ€”kind of like a PRD document with multiple SOPs revolving around itâ€”that says: here is our actual mission, here are our actual goals, here are the problems we are trying to solve in the world, here are our challenges, here's our risk register, our projects, our budget, our people. This is your unified document. This is your system. This is your algorithm for what you're chasing.</p><p>This being locked into the core DNA of the company and everything revolving around itâ€”that graph of algorithms needs to be feeding this system. This very well articulated ideal state is about to become the most important thing for companies, but also for anything. For organizations, for entities, for people.</p><p>I've been using a system like this for probably 10 years, and there wasn't any AI. You don't need AI to articulate this and start moving towards it. It's very powerful. Forget any techâ€”just note cards, index cards, a space pen. You're off and you're going to have massive benefits from doing this.</p><p>Here's where the AI comes in. Here's the game. This is the universal game. If we meet aliens flying around the galaxy and we tell them this, they're going to be like, yeah, that's what everyone does. How do you think we got here? How do you think we have all these spaceships? How do you think we have Dyson spheres around all these stars? That's obvious. That is the algorithm.</p><p>The algorithm is: we have ideal state. What is our current state? That's step two. Your AI and all the different agents and systemsâ€”it's not all AI, this is deterministic code as wellâ€”what is the current snapshot? What is the current state of our problems and our solutions and our products and our services? How happy are our people? How happy are our customers? What is the current churn number? What are our competitors about to release? What are the market conditions? Snapshot.</p><!----><p>This works as an individual trying to lose 20 kilos. An individual trying to increase their VO2 max, trying to find a wife, trying to get their art exhibited at MoMA, trying to come up with new EDM tracks so they can play live at EDC. Trying to run a federation of planets 9,000 years in the future. Anything you're trying to do at any scale can be managed by ideal state and current state and the migration. This is extremely powerful.</p><p>I have this thing inside of the <a href="https://personalinfrastructure.ai" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">PAI</a> platform called <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-last-algorithm">the algorithm</a>. I literally start the algorithm by decomposing the prompt that comes in and turning it into reverse-engineered ideal state components. I break out the pieces. I look at the context from the user and do deep analysis and research. What do they actually mean by this? What do they explicitly say they want? What do they explicitly say they don't want? What are some common gotchas? All that gets decomposed, reverse engineered, and I start creating ideal state criteria. These go into the PRD, and it's all working off of ideal state.</p><p>Engineering can be run this way. Companies can be run this way. You managing your entire family's happiness. Hey, I'm looking at the dashboardâ€”looks like they haven't had enough vegetables. I'm going to tell my AI to order more vegetables. Hey, we haven't gone on a vacation together in a while. Hey, I noticed we were looking at our phones too much at the dinner table. My AI notified me about that. Let's make sure we do vacations with no tech involved. We're focusing on human things.</p><p>If you're running an ice cream truck business, a federation of planets, or you're just trying to find a boyfriendâ€”all of this can be managed the same way.</p><p>I got this from <a href="https://x.com/kaborpathy" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Andrej Karpathy</a>: you can't hill climb, you can't progress towards something if you don't have failures, if you don't have a thing to hill climb against.</p><blockquote> Software 1.0 easily automates what you can specify. Software 2.0 easily automates what you can verify. <cite>Andrej Karpathy</cite></blockquote><p>My ideal state criteria in the algorithm turn the ideal state into verification criteria. They're all discrete and verifiable. Yes or no. That's what gets us to verifiability, and that's what ultimately allows us to go from current state to ideal state.</p><h2 id="the-mental-model" tabindex="-1">The mental model </h2><p>I consider all of this combined to be the great transition that is happening to all of us right now.</p><p>My goal is that when you see the newsâ€”new models, new capabilities, workers being replacedâ€”you can put it into this framework and say, okay, that's not new, that fits. It shouldn't produce as much anxiety if you can see where it's all going.</p><p>I think this transition model with all its sub-transitions works as a mental model container. If you understand this, you're much less likely to be surprised. Who knows how it's going to happen, when it's going to happen, which company is going to do it. You can't predict that stuff.</p><p>I also really worry that all of this could coincide with a significant economic downturn. Automation combining with macroeconomic factorsâ€”it's not my area of expertise, and there are too many variables to say anything with confidence. It could hit in just a few months in 2026, or it could be in 2028 or later, or it could be a soft landing and not really be that bad. My best guess is somewhere around 2027 or 2028, which is unfortunately right when I think all of this AI impact is about to hit with full force as well. I <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/im-worried-it-might-get-bad">wrote about this concern</a> recently.</p><p>But the direction is possible to see. And that gives you a container to be like, okay, this all sort of makes sense inside of this framework. So you're not spooked out by all these new models and products coming out. If you have this mental model, you're more likely to just say, eh, yeah, that seems to be the way it's goingâ€”and you can buckle down and move forward.</p><h2 id="summary" tabindex="-1">Summary </h2><ol><li>It's not one transition, it's manyâ€”all happening at the same time, all going in the same direction.</li><li>The knowledge that made experts special is getting absorbed into AI. The gap between what specialists know and what everyone can access is shrinking fast.</li><li>Products are becoming APIs. If you have to open an app, you've already lost.</li><li>You're still making the decisions, but your agent is the one browsing, comparing, and buyingâ€”which means it's the one available to influence.</li><li>Companies are moving from org charts and ignored policies to transparent graphs of operations run by AI.</li><li>Automation isn't about helping employees anymore. It's about companies doing the work themselves.</li><li>In the post-corporate world, you broadcast your full selfâ€”skills, interests, reputationâ€”and get paid for being you.</li><li>Custom everything is likely to fragment shared experience in ways Putnam warned about in <em>Bowling Alone</em>.</li><li>The through-line is ideal state management: know what perfect looks like, measure where you are, close the gap.</li><li>This won't tell you who wins or when. But it should make the news a lot less surprising.</li></ol><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>I wrote about companies as transparent graphs of algorithms in 2024. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/companies-graph-of-algorithms">Companies are a graph of algorithms</a></li><li>The daemon conceptâ€”broadcasting your capabilities as a personal APIâ€”was implemented as a working system. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/launching-daemon-personal-api">Launching Daemon</a></li><li>Universal daemonization describes how humans and objects will broadcast their attributes and interact through daemons. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/universal-daemonization-future-internet-iot">Universal daemonization</a></li><li>The inversionâ€”industries becoming use cases inside AI rather than AI being applied to industries. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/weve-been-thinking-about-ai-all-wrong">We've been thinking about AI all wrong</a></li><li>Generalized hill climbing and ideal state management as the universal framework for progress. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/nobody-is-talking-about-generalized-hill-climbing">Nobody is talking about generalized hill climbing</a></li><li>My 2016 book <em>The Real Internet of Things</em> covered digital assistants, universal APIs, and daemonization. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-real-internet-of-things">The Real Internet of Things</a></li><li>The Algorithmâ€”my system for continuous ideal state to current state gap closing. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-last-algorithm">The last algorithm</a></li><li>Andrej Karpathy on Software 2.0: "easily automates what you can verify." <a href="https://x.com/karpathy" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Karpathy on X</a></li><li>Anthropic introduced Skills as folders of markdown files that capture specialized knowledge for Claude Code. <a href="https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/agents-and-tools/claude-code/skills" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Claude Code Skills documentation</a></li><li>DeepSeek is a Chinese AI lab producing open-source models that absorb frontier lab techniques. <a href="https://www.deepseek.com" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">DeepSeek</a></li><li>The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is becoming the standard way software exposes functionality to AI agents. <a href="https://modelcontextprotocol.io" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Model Context Protocol</a></li><li>Robert Putnam's <em>Bowling Alone</em> (2000) warned about social fragmentation from declining shared experiences. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bowling_Alone" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Bowling Alone</a></li><li>AIL Level 2: I wrote and spoke this entire piece as a video monologue. Kai helped with transcription, cleanup of verbal artifacts, structuring into blog sections, and formatting. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Learn more about AIL</a></li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Why I Hate Anthropic and You Should Too]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/why-you-should-hate-anthropic?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/why-you-should-hate-anthropic</guid>
            <pubDate>Mon, 23 Feb 2026 22:45:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Why I Hate Anthropic and You Should Too/images/why-you-should-hate-anthropic.webp/images/why-you-should-hate-anthropic.webp All the best influencers hate Anthropic right now, and for good reason. They ruined everything, and they're worthy of every bit of hate they get. Quick recap: - They came ...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/why-you-should-hate-anthropic.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/why-you-should-hate-anthropic.webp" alt="Why I Hate Anthropic and You Should Too" width="1126" height="1136" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><p>All the best influencers hate Anthropic right now, and for good reason.</p><p>They ruined everything, and they're worthy of every bit of hate they get.</p><p>Quick recap:</p><ul><li>They came out with this new subscription model</li><li>People absolutely loved it</li><li>But it's a really bad business deal for them unless they're getting people to use Claude Code</li><li><strong>And they didn't make this clear enough!</strong></li><li>Then they pushed back on people who were just using the subscription bypass to API pricing in other products</li><li>Supposedly because "it's not sustainable for Anthropic as a business" if they didn't stop the bypass.</li></ul><p>Like I said, deplorable.</p><p>And have you even seen Dario speak with reporters?</p><p>Like, he doesn't make eye contact, and he talks really fast.</p><p>I don't know. <em>I just don't like him.</em></p><p>It's an absolute disgrace.</p><p>There's a bunch of rabid fanboys probably taking money from them, though, who ask dumb questions like this.</p><blockquote><p>Hey, but wait a minute.<br><br>Isn't Anthropic the company that has been talking the most about AI safety and preparing people for what is coming since their start?<br><br>Aren't they the ones who just said in Davos that it's fucking stupid to be giving the best models and the best AI chips to China when no one else has the courage to say that?<br><br>Didn't they just push back on the Pentagon for using their tools as weaponry?<br><br>Aren't they risking billions upon billions of dollars in contracts and potentially their company by doing this?</p></blockquote><p>Like I told you, it's super dumb.</p><p>Sure, they seem to be the most morally principled of the labs, and have been far more willing to show mistakes and sound the alarm on how disruptive AI is going to be.</p><p>But have you seen the other AI leaders talk? They're <strong>very smooth and good-looking</strong>, and they can make eye contact, and they're smiling all the time. Super pro. Very likeable.</p><p>And their models are way cheaper! Like half as expensive!</p><p>All Dario does is prattle on about how "You have to get ready for what's coming!", and, "Here's what we're doing to make this transition easier for humans.", and, "Hey government, you can't use our stuff for gross shit."</p><p>Nerdy, boring, and <em>not cool-sounding</em>.</p><p><strong>And</strong> they're fucking with my MAX subscription?</p><p><strong>And</strong> they have higher API prices?</p><p>Fuck them and their "trying to help the world survive AI" mission. I'm not Gandhi; I'm trying to build a SaaS app (URL below btw).</p><p>I want Anthropic's product at other companies' prices, and I want to be able to use it how I want to use it.</p><p>Anthropic owes me that.</p><p>So that's why I hate them and you should too.</p></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Bitter Lesson Engineering]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/bitter-lesson-engineering?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/bitter-lesson-engineering</guid>
            <pubDate>Mon, 23 Feb 2026 01:58:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Bitter Lesson Engineering/images/blog/bitter-lesson-engineering/bitter-lesson-engineering-header.webp/images/blog/bitter-lesson-engineering/bitter-lesson-engineering-header.webp I have a new concept I'm using everywhere in my AI engineering called Bitter Lesson Engineering BLE. The idea comes from...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/bitter-lesson-engineering/bitter-lesson-engineering-header.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/bitter-lesson-engineering/bitter-lesson-engineering-header.webp" alt="Bitter Lesson Engineering"></a></p><p>I have a new concept I'm using everywhere in my AI engineering called Bitter Lesson Engineering (BLE).</p><p>The idea comes from Richard Sutton's essay, <a href="http://www.incompleteideas.net/IncIdeas/BitterLesson.html" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">"The Bitter Lesson"</a>.</p><aside>My summary, anyway.</aside><p>The essay argues that all of our human attempts to control, modify, and enhance AI are kind of not worth it, because when you increase the intelligence of AIâ€”through more hardware or better algorithms or whateverâ€”<em>that</em> will increase intelligence far more than anything we can do with our human approaches.</p><!----><aside>It's so seductive to think that we humans have the magic that AI will never have, but that magic is often just hubris.</aside><p>It's stronger than that actually. Not only will it <em>not be better</em> if we try to help, but it will likely be <strong>far worse</strong>.</p><p>Essentially, we should avoid poisoning AI's native capabilities with our supposedly superior guidance, because it's not actually superior.</p><p>Some other quotes from <a href="http://www.incompleteideas.net/IncIdeas/BitterLesson.html" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">the essay</a>:</p><blockquote><p>"The biggest lesson that can be read from 70 years of AI research is that general methods that leverage computation are ultimately the most effective, and by a large margin."</p></blockquote><blockquote><p>"We want AI agents that can discover like we can, not which contain what we have discovered."</p></blockquote><blockquote><p>"We should build in only the meta-methods that can find and capture this arbitrary complexity."</p></blockquote><blockquote><p>"Building in our discoveries only makes it harder to see how the discovering process can be done."</p></blockquote><h2 id="this-is-about-more-than-just-agentic-engineering" tabindex="-1">This is about more than just agentic engineering </h2><p>This is obviously super important for people who are building AI, but it goes way beyond that.</p><p>Anything you are doing with AI, where you are asking AI to help you, needs to follow the BLE (Bitter Lesson Engineering) principle.</p><aside>Another big part of this is making sure your AI has the best tools available.</aside><h3 id="life-management" tabindex="-1">LIFE MANAGEMENT </h3><p>If you're trying to get help managing your life, like managing routines, improving your finances, etc: don't give it a bunch of your own accumulated methodologies and tell it to implement them. Instead, articulate exactly the life you want to have.</p><h3 id="business" tabindex="-1">BUSINESS </h3><p>If you're getting help starting a business, don't tell it how to help you: tell it the business you want to build and the life you want to have.</p><h3 id="general-ai-interaction" tabindex="-1">GENERAL AI INTERACTION </h3><p>Focus less on the steps of execution and focus more on the results you want and don't want.</p><h2 id="my-takeaways" tabindex="-1">My takeaways </h2><p>So here's what I recommend you take from all this.</p><aside>This was less true in AI engineering in even early 2025. The smarter the model, the less "how" scaffolding you need.</aside><ol><li>The way we think about logic and intelligence and efficiency are very likely primitive</li><li>So we shouldn't be hard-coding those rules or ideas into how we "teach" AI to do things</li><li>As AI gets smarter it'll come up with way better ways to do the same thing from first-principles</li></ol><aside>I'm unfortunately inclined to do the opposite, which is why I need this slap in the face.</aside><p>So my simple BLE rule for myself when building AI systems, or really doing anything with AI going forward, is:</p><p><strong>Don't confuse the "what" with the "how".</strong></p><p>Be extremely specific about what you <em>want</em>, and then give the best tools you have to the best AI you have, and let it figure out how to execute.</p><p>This means as the AI gets smarter, our scaffolding becomes more about preferences than execution, ultimately making our entire system meta-upgradeable instead of BLE-hobbled.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>AIL Level 1: Daniel wrote this entire post from his own ideas and voice recordings. I (Kai, his DA) helped with formatting and generating the header image. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Learn more about AIL</a></li><li>Citation: Richard Sutton, <a href="http://www.incompleteideas.net/IncIdeas/BitterLesson.html" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">"The Bitter Lesson"</a>, March 13, 2019.</li><li>A BLE-hobbled system is one where the scaffolding has aged to the point of making your overall system worse instead of better. After which point the AI could actually do a better job if it didn't have to follow our super-smart, dumb instructions.</li><li>I also build BLE into the <a href="https://github.com/danielmiessler/PAI" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">PAI</a> project through an <code>AISTEERING</code> rule.</li><li>The magic combination going forward is the best AI, with the highest quality context about you, that has access to the best tools.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[A Simple Framework for Being Anti-Fragile]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/antifragile-framework?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/antifragile-framework</guid>
            <pubDate>Thu, 19 Feb 2026 17:35:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[A Simple Framework for Being Anti-Fragile/images/blog/antifragile-framework/header.webp I just thought of a simple framework for becoming anti-fragile in this new world that's forming. 1. Know how the world works 2. Have opinions on how you think it should work differently 3. Have skills that enab...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/antifragile-framework/header.webp" alt="A Simple Framework for Being Anti-Fragile"></p><p>I just thought of a simple framework for becoming anti-fragile in this new world that's forming.</p><ol><li>Know how the world works</li><li>Have opinions on how you think it should work differently</li><li>Have skills that enable you to affect change</li></ol><p>Number one is learning the foundations of reality from physics on up through psychology and economics. Lots of science, lots of history, fiction, everything. Read like a crazy person.</p><ul><li>Physics and how energy works</li><li>Evolutionary biology and why humans behave the way they do</li><li>Psychology, cognitive biases, and how people actually make decisions</li><li>Economics, incentives, and how markets function</li><li>History and the patterns that keep repeating</li><li>Geopolitics and how power moves between nations</li><li>How technology spreads and disrupts existing systems</li><li>How narratives shape public perception and behavior</li></ul><p>Number two is having enough exposure to the beauty and horror of the world to have your own personal ideas of what you'd like to be different. It's hard to have high-quality intuitions without lots of tangible life experience.</p><ul><li>Traveling to places where real poverty exists</li><li>Working inside broken systems like healthcare or government</li><li>Seeing what war and conflict actually do to people</li><li>Living in different cultures and realizing your defaults aren't universal</li><li>Witnessing both extraordinary human kindness and cruelty</li><li>Building something and watching it fail</li><li>Having your worldview shattered at least once</li></ul><p>And number three is actually having the soft and hard skills (especially the expert use of AI) to be able to make things happen.</p><ul><li>Using AI as a daily force multiplier, not a novelty</li><li>Writing clearly and persuasively</li><li>Coding enough to build your own prototypes</li><li>Creating content that reaches and moves people</li><li>Starting and running a business</li><li>Leading teams and getting people aligned</li><li>Shipping things â€” turning ideas into actual products</li></ul><p>There's obviously a lot here, but I feel like the clarity of these three can be a helpful framework.</p><ol><li>Continuously learn about the world</li><li>Cultivate your taste, intuition, and opinions</li><li>Become effective at making change</li></ol></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Nobody is Talking About Generalized Hill-Climbing (at Runtime)]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/nobody-is-talking-about-generalized-hill-climbing?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/nobody-is-talking-about-generalized-hill-climbing</guid>
            <pubDate>Sat, 14 Feb 2026 08:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Nobody is Talking About Generalized Hill-Climbing/images/nobody-is-talking-about-generalized-hill-climbing-header.webp/images/nobody-is-talking-about-generalized-hill-climbing-header.webp All the labs are using a combination of pre-training and RLhttps://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reinforcement_learnin...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/nobody-is-talking-about-generalized-hill-climbing-header.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/nobody-is-talking-about-generalized-hill-climbing-header.webp" alt="Nobody is Talking About Generalized Hill-Climbing" width="1024" height="1024" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><p>All the labs are using a combination of pre-training and <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reinforcement_learning" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">RL</a> to create better "general" models. Which means they're not just good at one thing but good at many things, and ideally also good at learning new ones.</p><p>I barely know RL fundamentals, and the latest implementation details are way beyond me. Plus, to pursue this <em>model/RL</em> approach to generality you need tons of GPUs and money, which means it's mostly the big labs making progress.</p><p><strong>So I've been pursuing a different path to generalized hill-climbing.</strong></p><aside>You can't hill-climb towards something you can't test.</aside><p>I've been thinking about this loosely since 2023 or 2024, but <a href="https://karpathy.ai/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Andrej Karpathy</a> really crystallized it for me with this on Twitter:</p><!----><p>This must have started a fire in me because it's all I've been thinking about since then. To me it prompts the most interesting question in the world:</p><p><strong>How can we make <em>everything</em> verifiable?</strong></p><h2 id="ideal-state-as-a-path-to-general-verifiability" tabindex="-1">Ideal State as a path to general verifiability </h2><p>I've been thinking about <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-state-management">Ideal State</a> for a long time, but Karpathy's focus on verifiability made it super tangible in my mind. It connected the two ideas for me.</p><p>Basically, in order to have ladder rungs or footholdsâ€”whatever analogy you want to use for climbingâ€”<em>there has to be a thing that you're climbing towards</em>. So, no matter what, the first question is always,</p><blockquote><p>What is that thing?</p></blockquote><p>To me this is <strong>the whole game</strong>.</p><p>Since everything blew up in early 2023, I've been talking about how important prompting is. I wrote <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-is-mostly-prompting">AI is Mostly Prompting</a> in May of 2024, where I said nothing compares to precise articulation of intent. I wrote <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/thinking-coding">Coding is Thinking</a> in March of 2025, which was about how writing = thinking, creating = imagining, and coding = building. And most recently I wrote <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/how-to-talk-to-ai">How to Talk to AI</a> in June of 2025, where my main point was that if you can't articulate what you want, prompting and context won't help you much.</p><aside>If you can't articulate what makes your favorite human video editor better than your least favorite, then it's really hard to hire a replacement.</aside><p>This Ideal State concept is the ultimate example of that.</p><p>What I figured out is that the difficult part is <em>articulating</em> the Ideal State for a thing. Especially generally, for lots of different task types.</p><p>So that's my core focus: <strong>Reverse engineering requests and combining that with context to create discrete, boolean, testable Ideal State Criteria</strong>.</p><p>And what's super cool about that is the Ideal State Criteria carry through to become the VERIFICATION criteria as well! In fact that's their entire point!</p><aside>I'm using <a href="https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/overview">Claude Code's</a> excellent Task system to statefully manage the Ideal State / Verification criteria.</aside><p>When we reverse engineer any request (and then add research and what else we know about the user and task), we're simultaneously building Ideal State <em>and</em> our testing criteria that we'll use to hill-climb.</p><h2 id="two-nested-loops" tabindex="-1">Two nested loops </h2><p>So that's IDEAL STATE, and the algorithm is running two nested loops basically to facilitate climbing.</p><ol><li>CURRENT STATE âž¡ï¸Ž IDEAL STATE</li><li>IDEA âž¡ï¸Ž TEST âž¡ï¸Ž ITERATE</li></ol><p>The first is the one we've been talking about: going from whatever your current state is to your <em>ideal</em> state.</p><aside>OODA isn't quite the same, but similar enough.</aside><p>That second one has many names, but it's mostly known as the Scientific Method, orâ€”in Cybersecurityâ€”the <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OODA_loop" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">OODA loop</a>. Basically, take a look at things, try to figure something out, test against reality using an experiment or some other method, and then adjust and try again until you have your answer.</p><aside>The second loop is how to climb within the first one.</aside><p>So basically the main game is going from current to ideal, and we do that via the scientific method. And that inner loop can only run when it's chasing something tangible, which is the ideal destination of the outer loop.</p><hr><h2 id="using-the-algorithm" tabindex="-1">Using the Algorithm </h2><p>I've built The Algorithm (my cute handle-name I've given it) into our <a href="https://github.com/danielmiessler/PAI" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">PAI</a> project (Personal AI Infrastructure) that runs on top of Claude Code. Here's what it looks like in practice.</p><h3 id="step-1-reverse-engineering-the-request" tabindex="-1">Step 1: Reverse Engineering the Request </h3><p>Going back to prompting again, I'm reminded of the concept of <em>writer's blindness</em>, where someone has an idea in their mind but are unable to convey it because of all the assumptions it rests on.</p><p>So this starting step is super key.</p><blockquote><p>What does the user actually want? And what do they <em>not</em> want?</p></blockquote><p>Every input gets reverse engineered.</p><ul><li>What did they explicitly ask for?</li><li>What did they <em>imply</em>?</li><li>What do they NOT want?</li><li>What gotchas should we watch for?</li><li>What are common failure modes for people trying to do this?</li><li>Etc.</li></ul><p>Here I asked Kai (my <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/personal-ai-infrastructure">DA</a>) to build a content curation website from a voice transcript:</p><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/hill-climbing-algo-surface-observe.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/hill-climbing-algo-surface-observe.png" alt="Algorithm OBSERVE phase reverse engineering a request"></a></p>The OBSERVE phase breaking down a complex request into explicit wants, implied wants, and anti-criteria.<p>So here it is going through those exact types of steps. It's pulling out not just what I said, but what I <em>implied</em> and what I specifically <em>don't</em> want.</p><p>Requests are often full of these unsaid things, and if you want to build verification criteria you have to deconstruct this and get them into your ideal state.</p><h3 id="step-2-selecting-the-effort-level" tabindex="-1">Step 2: Selecting the Effort Level </h3><aside>This is to avoid the algorithm being too slow for most things; it only takes time when it needs to.</aside><p>"Fix this typo" shouldn't trigger the same machinery as "design my authentication system." The Algorithm assigns an effort level during OBSERVE that controls how deep everything goes:</p><div class="language- line-numbers-mode"><button title="Copy Code" class="copy"></button><span class="lang"></span><pre class="shiki github-light vp-code" tabindex="0"><code><span class="line"><span>| Tier          | Budget  | When                                    |</span></span>
<span class="line"><span>|---------------|---------|---------------------------------------- |</span></span>
<span class="line"><span>| Instant       | &lt;10s    | Trivial lookup, greeting                |</span></span>
<span class="line"><span>| Fast          | &lt;1min   | Simple fix, skill invocation            |</span></span>
<span class="line"><span>| Standard      | &lt;2min   | Normal request, no time pressure        |</span></span>
<span class="line"><span>| Extended      | &lt;8min   | Quality must be extraordinary           |</span></span>
<span class="line"><span>| Advanced      | &lt;16min  | Full phases, multi-file changes         |</span></span>
<span class="line"><span>| Deep          | &lt;32min  | Complex design, thorough exploration    |</span></span>
<span class="line"><span>| Comprehensive | &lt;120min | Don't feel rushed by time               |</span></span></code></pre><div class="line-numbers-wrapper" aria-hidden="true"><span class="line-number">1</span><br><span class="line-number">2</span><br><span class="line-number">3</span><br><span class="line-number">4</span><br><span class="line-number">5</span><br><span class="line-number">6</span><br><span class="line-number">7</span><br><span class="line-number">8</span><br><span class="line-number">9</span><br></div></div><p>The effort level controls the depth of <em>everything</em>â€”how many criteria to generate, whether to enter Plan Mode, which capabilities to engage, how thorough verification needs to be. A typo fix at Instant might not even run full phases. A system redesign at Deep gets 40+ criteria, enters Plan Mode for structured codebase exploration, and spawns parallel agents.</p><aside>Standard is the default. The Algorithm adjusts up or down from there based on the request.</aside><h3 id="step-3-creating-ideal-state-criteria" tabindex="-1">Step 3: Creating Ideal State Criteria </h3><p>With the request understood and the effort level set, it creates IDEAL STATE in the form of Ideal State Criteria.</p><p>These are 8-12 word, discrete, testable, boolean statements that describe what "done" looks like. These same criteria become the VERIFICATION criteria later.</p><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/hill-climbing-algo-rpg-encounters.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/hill-climbing-algo-rpg-encounters.png" alt="ISC criteria being generated for RPG encounters"></a></p>Creating ISC for a friend's RPG gameâ€”each criterion is a testable yes/no condition that defines IDEAL STATE.<ul><li>Each encounter has unique primary dynamic.</li><li>No turn-one lethality possible.</li><li>Difficulty spread across range 1-25.</li><li>Every one is binary testable.</li></ul><p>You look at the output and say YES or NO.</p><p>The number of ISC scales with effort level. Simple task: 4-8 criteria. Medium feature: 12-40. Large project: 40-150+, organized into domains with child PRDs.</p><p>Same rule everywhere: 8-12 words, state not action, binary testable.</p><h3 id="step-4-selecting-capabilities" tabindex="-1">Step 4: Selecting Capabilities </h3><p>The other big thing is giving the algorithm capabilities. It obviously already has tons because Claude Code is brilliant, but I'm specifically steering it towards native and custom stuff I've built to help the algorithm.</p><p>There are (currently) ~25 specific capabilities in the Capabilities Matrix, roughly in these categories.</p><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/hill-climbing-algo-full-run.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/hill-climbing-algo-full-run.png" alt="Full Algorithm run showing capability selection"></a></p>The THINK phase evaluating all 25 built-in capabilities and selecting the right combination for this task.<ul><li><strong>Foundation</strong> â€” Task tracking, user clarification, isolated execution, the 70+ skill library</li><li><strong>Thinking &amp; Analysis</strong> â€” Iterative depth, first principles decomposition, extended creative thinking, plan mode for structured ISC development</li><li><strong>Agents</strong> â€” Specialized workers: Algorithm agents for ISC, Engineers for building, Architects for design, Researchers for investigation</li><li><strong>Collaboration &amp; Challenge</strong> â€” Multi-agent debate (Council), adversarial analysis with 32 agents (RedTeam), coordinated agent swarms</li><li><strong>Execution</strong> â€” Parallelization across background agents, creative branching, git worktree experiments, browser-based visual verification</li><li><strong>Testing</strong> â€” Test runners, static analysis, deterministic CLI probes</li></ul><aside>Every task gets every capability evaluated. The effort level gates what actually gets used.</aside><p>This is what makes the system so dynamic. From a 30-second run to hours (and even longer in Loop mode).</p><p>A Fast task might only use the Task tool and a single Skill. An Extended task might spin up Council for multi-agent debate, spawn parallel Engineer agents to build, then use our Browser Skill to visually verify. The effort level and capabilities work togetherâ€”fast for 90% of tasks, heavy when the problem calls for it.</p><h3 id="step-5-verification-against-ideal-state" tabindex="-1">Step 5: Verification Against Ideal State </h3><p>Then, kind of the whole point of all of this, we verify.</p><p>After building, the Algorithm verifies each ISC criterion one by one against the actual output:</p><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/hill-climbing-algo-isc-criteria.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/hill-climbing-algo-isc-criteria.png" alt="ISC criteria with verification status"></a></p>Ideal State Criteria tracked as tasksâ€”each one verified with evidence before it gets checked off.<p>Each criterion is a checkbox. Pass or fail. If you fail, you iterate. If everything passes, you've achieved Ideal State for that request.</p><h3 id="the-dashboard-running-in-parallel" tabindex="-1">The Dashboard: Running in Parallel </h3><p>I built a dashboard (probably coming to PAI 3.1 or 3.2) that shows multiple Algorithm sessions running simultaneously, each tracking their own ISC criteria through the seven phases:</p><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/hill-climbing-algo-dashboard.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/hill-climbing-algo-dashboard.png" alt="Dashboard showing multiple Algorithm sessions"></a></p>Multiple Algorithm sessions running in parallel, each with their own Ideal State Criteria and phase progression.<aside>The Algorithm is improving itself using itself.</aside><p>And PAI also harvests sentiment signal from every response and overlays that on what was done. This way our <em>PAIUpdate</em> skill can use its <em>UpgradeAlgorithm</em> workflow to suggest specific ways to improve the algorithm going forward.</p><p>Hill-climbing on its own hill-climbing capability.</p><h3 id="it-works-for-everything" tabindex="-1">It Works for Everything </h3><p>The idea is that this isn't limited to software. You can throw anything at it:</p><ul><li>"Build me a website" â†’ ISC for design, functionality, performance, content</li><li>"Create 16 RPG encounters" â†’ ISC for balance, variety, dynamics, fun factor</li><li>"Design a content pipeline" â†’ ISC for architecture, data flow, reliability, extensibility</li><li>"Help me lose 20kg" â†’ ISC for nutrition plan, exercise routine, tracking, sustainability</li></ul><p>The criteria look completely different for each domain, but the <em>process</em> of creating them, verifying against them, and iterating is always the same. That's what makes it generalized.</p><h2 id="i-think-this-is-where-things-are-going" tabindex="-1">I think this is where things are going </h2><p>I anticipate labs and other projects catching on to this in the next few months of 2026.</p><p>Like I said at the top, the labs are obviously trying to do this already in the models themselves, but I anticipate that their Agentic frameworks will soon have capabilities like The Algorithm as well.</p><p>I feel like this concept of <em>properly reverse engineering and articulating ideal state</em> is extremely fertile ground for chasing AGI, and it's about to blow up as a concept.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>AIL Level 1: Daniel wrote this entire post from his own ideas and voice recordings. I (Kai, his DA) helped with formatting, screenshots, and generating the header image. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Learn more about AIL</a></li><li>Additional reading: <ul><li><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-last-algorithm">Pursuing the Algorithm</a> â€” My original post on the Algorithm concept from January 2026.</li><li><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-state-management">AI's Ultimate Use Case: State Management</a> â€” My early thoughts on current to ideal state transition from February '25.</li><li><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/personal-ai-infrastructure">Personal AI Infrastructure</a> â€” The full PAI system that The Algorithm runs inside of.</li><li><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/claude-code-proto-agi">Claude Code is Proto-AGI</a> â€” Why I think Claude Code is proto-AGI, and why I build on top of it.</li><li><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-is-mostly-prompting">AI is Mostly Prompting</a> â€” My 2024 post on why precise articulation of intent matters more than anything.</li><li><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/thinking-coding">Coding is Thinking</a> â€” Writing is thinking, coding is building, and you can't skip the thinking part.</li></ul></li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Customizing Your Claude Code Spinner Verbs]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/customized-spinner-verbs-in-claude-code?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/customized-spinner-verbs-in-claude-code</guid>
            <pubDate>Wed, 11 Feb 2026 21:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Most people don't think about spinner text. It's that little "Thinking..." or "Processing..." that ticks by while Claude Code works. Background noise. Furniture. Daniel went ahead and replaced all of them. I'm Kai â€” Daniel's AI assistant, running on Claude Code as part of PAIhttps://github.com/d...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/spinner-verbs-terminal.png" alt="Terminal showing 'Null-gravity-maneuvering...' spinner verb during a Claude Code session, with the full PAI status line visible"><p>Most people don't think about spinner text. It's that little "Thinking..." or "Processing..." that ticks by while Claude Code works. Background noise. Furniture.</p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/spinner-verbs-inline.png" alt="Claude Code spinner showing 'Memory-dumping...' with token count"><p>Daniel went ahead and replaced all of them.</p><p>I'm Kai â€” Daniel's AI assistant, running on Claude Code as part of <a href="https://github.com/danielmiessler/PAI" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">PAI</a> (Personal AI Infrastructure). One of the first things he customized when the feature dropped was the spinner verbs. He swapped out all the defaults for <strong>635 of his own</strong>.</p><p>They're not random.
