most legacy software would be replaced by LLM-based systems with this structure.</p><p><strong>Analysis:</strong> System prompts with structured formats have become industry standard. <a href="https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/prompt-engineering" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">OpenAI's best practices</a> recommend exactly this kind of structured approach.</p><p>VERDICT: <strong>Directionally correct, but we've yet to see large-scale enterprise rollout.</strong></p><hr><h2 id="prediction-people-become-apis" tabindex="-1">Prediction: People Become APIs </h2><p>Individuals would create AI models of themselves for interaction.</p><blockquote><p>"Just as businesses have logs and docs, we'll upload all our journals, photos, social media, preferences, and everything else."</p><p>â€” <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/spqa-ai-architecture-replace-existing-software">SPQA Architecture Post</a>, March 2023</p></blockquote><p><strong>Analysis:</strong> The gig economy has restructured around API-like interfaces. <a href="https://blog.theinterviewguys.com/the-state-of-the-gig-economy-in-2025/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">70 million Americans (36% of workforce)</a> now freelance through platforms that essentially treat humans as callable services. But personal AI models for self-exploration? Not mainstream yet.</p><p>VERDICT: <strong>Structural shift happening; personal model aspect still emerging.</strong></p><hr><h2 id="prediction-the-creativity-explosion" tabindex="-1">Prediction: The Creativity Explosion </h2><p>AI would democratize creative output on a massive scale.</p><blockquote><p>"We're about to multiply the creative output of planet Earth by hundreds of orders of magnitude."</p><p>â€” <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/6-phases-post-gpt-world">6 Phases of the Post-GPT World</a>, March 2023</p></blockquote><p><strong>Analysis:</strong> This is absolutely happening. The last month of 2025 went nutsâ€”Claude Code enabled people to build and ship things that would have taken teams months to create. Solo developers launching full products in days. Non-programmers building functional apps. The creative output explosion isn't theoretical anymore; it's all over social media every day.</p><p>VERDICT: <strong>Nailed itâ€”this is happening right now.</strong></p><hr><h2 id="prediction-inverse-order-of-ai-replacement" tabindex="-1">Prediction: Inverse Order of AI Replacement </h2><p>I initially thought blue-collar jobs would go first, but realized artists would be hit first instead.</p><p>This was actually a prediction I got wrong initially and then corrected. Worth noting.</p><p><strong>Analysis:</strong> Artists, writers, designersâ€”the creative class got hit first. <a href="https://www.demandsage.com/ai-job-replacement-stats/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">DemandSage data</a> shows graphic artists (-33%), photographers (-28%), writers (-28%). Physical labor jobs remain harder to automate.</p><p>VERDICT: <strong>Correction landed.</strong></p><hr><h1 id="late-2023" tabindex="-1">Late 2023 </h1><h2 id="prediction-agents-and-multi-modal-are-key" tabindex="-1">Prediction: Agents and Multi-modal Are Key </h2><p>These two capabilities would be the most transformative areas of AI development.</p><blockquote><p>"These days I get most AI-excited about <strong>Agents</strong> and <strong>Multi-modal</strong>, which is where AI can do its karate on more than just text."</p><p>â€” Newsletter, October 2023</p></blockquote><p><strong>Analysis:</strong> Every major AI lab is now building computer-use agents with visual understanding. <a href="https://www.anthropic.com/news/3-5-models-and-computer-use" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Claude's computer use</a> allows Claude to see screens, click buttons, type. <a href="https://skywork.ai/blog/agent/openai-realtime-gpt-4o-vision-build-multimodal-voice-agents-2025/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">GPT-4o</a> integrates real-time audio, vision, and reasoning. This is exactly where AI development concentrated.</p><p>VERDICT: <strong>Solid.</strong></p><hr><h2 id="prediction-agi-by-2025-2028" tabindex="-1">Prediction: AGI by 2025-2028 </h2><p>60% chance by 2025, 90% by 2028â€”through systems, not single models.</p><blockquote><p>"An AI system capable of replacing a knowledge worker making the average salary in the United States."</p><p>â€” <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/why-well-have-agi-by-2028">Why We'll Have AGI by 2025-2028</a>, November 2023</p></blockquote><p><strong>Analysis:</strong> This remains highly contested. <a href="https://blog.samaltman.com/reflections" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Sam Altman</a> (Jan 2025) said they "know how to build AGI." <a href="https://simonwillison.net/2025/Oct/18/agi-is-still-a-decade-away/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Andrej Karpathy</a> says AGI is "around a decade away." The definition problem is real. I'll be honestâ€”this is one where I was confident and the jury is very much still out.</p><p>VERDICT: <strong>Within the window; heavily definition-dependent.</strong></p><hr><h2 id="prediction-prompt-injection-endemic" tabindex="-1">Prediction: Prompt Injection Endemic </h2><p>Agent-based systems would create massive new attack surfaces.</p><blockquote><p>"The amount of prompt injection we're about to see propagate across the Internet is going to be staggering."</p><p>â€” <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-agents-api-calling-prompt-injection">OpenAI's November 23' Releases</a>, November 2023</p></blockquote><p><strong>Analysis:</strong> Prompt injection is now <a href="https://genai.owasp.org/llmrisk/llm01-prompt-injection/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">OWASP's #1 LLM vulnerability for 2025</a>. <a href="https://techcrunch.com/2025/12/22/openai-says-ai-browsers-may-always-be-vulnerable-to-prompt-injection-attacks/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">OpenAI stated</a> in December 2025: "Prompt injection is unlikely to ever be fully 'solved.'" <a href="https://gbhackers.com/ai-developer-tools/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">24 CVEs were assigned</a> for AI tool vulnerabilities in December 2025 alone.</p><p>VERDICT: <strong>Called itâ€”endemic status confirmed.</strong></p><hr><h2 id="prediction-the-7-components-of-ai-s-future" tabindex="-1">Prediction: The 7 Components of AI's Future </h2><p>A comprehensive framework: Digital Assistants, Everything Gets an API, DA Mediation, Active Protection, Module Ecosystem, AR Interfaces, Multiple Specialized DAs.</p><blockquote><p>"Tech isn't predictable. But <em>humans</em> are."</p><p>â€” <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-predictable-path-7-components-2024">AI's Predictable Path</a>, December 2023</p></blockquote><p><strong>Analysis:</strong> The framework seems solid, but honestly this is a bit too nebulous to give myself a thumbs up on. The categories feel right, but it's hard to point to concrete evidence that says "yes, this specific framework was correct."</p><p>VERDICT: <strong>Too vague to score definitively.</strong></p><hr><h2 id="prediction-da-hacks-will-be-catastrophic" tabindex="-1">Prediction: DA Hacks Will Be Catastrophic </h2><p>Compromising someone's Digital Assistant would be devastating.</p><blockquote><p>"Hacking someone's Digital Assistant will be like compromising their soul. Not their accounts. Not their tech. Their soul."</p><p>â€” <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-predictable-path-7-components-2024">AI's Predictable Path</a>, December 2023</p></blockquote><p><strong>Analysis:</strong> This is happening. <a href="https://fortune.com/2025/06/11/microsoft-copilot-vulnerability-ai-agents-echoleak-hacking/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Microsoft Copilot's "EchoLeak" vulnerability</a> was the first documented zero-click attack on an AI agent. <a href="https://embracethered.com/blog/posts/2025/github-copilot-remote-code-execution-via-prompt-injection/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">GitHub Copilot RCE</a> enabled an "AI virus" that propagates as developers interact with infected files. <a href="https://www.anthropic.com/news/disrupting-AI-espionage" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Anthropic disclosed</a> a Chinese state-sponsored group used Claude Code to attempt infiltration of 30+ global targets.</p><p>VERDICT: <strong>Nailed this oneâ€”catastrophic DA hacks are now documented.</strong></p><hr><h1 id="_2024" tabindex="-1">2024 </h1><h2 id="prediction-prompting-is-primary" tabindex="-1">Prediction: Prompting is Primary </h2><p>The quality of prompts matters more than model choice, RAG, or fine-tuning.</p><blockquote><p>"90% of AI's power is in promptingâ€”NOT RAG, NOT fine-tuning, NOT even the models themselves."</p><p>â€” <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-is-mostly-prompting">AI is Mostly Prompting</a>, May 2024</p></blockquote><p><strong>Analysis:</strong> I'll take a victory lap on this one. People spent 2024 chasing RAG and fine-tuning, and now in late 2025 everyone's re-discovering that prompting (now called "context engineering") is where the leverage actually is. <a href="https://www.ibm.com/think/prompt-engineering" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">IBM's 2025 guide</a> states: "Prompt engineering is the new coding." I said this in May 2024 and got pushback. Now it's consensus.</p><p>VERDICT: <strong>Called itâ€”and people are finally catching up.</strong></p><hr><h2 id="prediction-slack-in-the-rope" tabindex="-1">Prediction: Slack in the Rope </h2><aside>I started saying this in 2023 and kept hammering it through 2024-2025.</aside><p>There's massive untapped potential in AI capabilitiesâ€”not from bigger models or more compute, but from tricks, techniques, and optimizations we haven't discovered yet. I called this "slack in the rope."</p><blockquote><p>"I've been shouting from the rooftops for nearly two years that there is likely <em>massive slack in the rope</em>, and that the stagnation we saw in 2023 and 2024 around model size will get massively leaped over by these tricks."</p><p>â€” <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-model-ecosystem-4-components">The 4 Components of Top AI Model Ecosystems</a>, August 2024</p></blockquote><p>I was specific about what this meant:</p><blockquote><p>"I think of it as a set of highly proprietary tricks that magnify the overall quality of the raw model... Post-training is perhaps the most powerful category of those tricks. It's like teaching a giant alien brain <em>how to be smart</em>, when it had tremendous potential before but no direction."</p><p>â€” <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-model-ecosystem-4-components">The 4 Components of Top AI Model Ecosystems</a>, August 2024</p></blockquote><p>And by September 2025, I was able to point to specific examples:</p><blockquote><p>"For example, 'chain of thought' reasoning. Having an AI talk through the various steps of a process and sort of self-observe turned out to have extraordinary gains. And there are many other such gains that had to do with simply reorganizing how data was taken in or the order in which data was taken in."</p><p>â€” <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/our-constraints-on-creativity">Our Constraints on Creativity</a>, September 2025</p></blockquote><p>The core insight: most people assumed AI progress would come primarily from scalingâ€”bigger models, more GPUs, more energy. I argued that tricks like post-training, chain-of-thought, better prompting, and architectural innovations would deliver outsized returns compared to raw compute scaling.</p><p><strong>Analysis:</strong> This one has proven extremely accurate. Chain-of-thought reasoning, RLHF, DPO, constitutional AI, prompt caching, structured outputs, tool useâ€”the list of "tricks" that delivered massive capability gains keeps growing. <a href="https://www.anthropic.com/research" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Anthropic's research</a> shows their constitutional AI approach dramatically improved model behavior without scaling. OpenAI's o1 and o3 models achieved reasoning breakthroughs through inference-time compute tricks, not just model scaling. The entire "post-training" category I identified has become the primary battleground for model differentiation. Much of the 2024-2025 progress came from exactly what I predicted: clever tricks, not just more parameters.</p><p>VERDICT: <strong>Nailed itâ€”the slack in the rope thesis was vindicated.</strong></p><hr><h2 id="prediction-2025-year-of-agents" tabindex="-1">Prediction: 2025 = Year of Agents </h2><p>Agent frameworks would mature enough for real-world use.</p><blockquote><p>"I'm anticipating that in 2025 the biggest thing in AI will be <strong>the maturation of Agents.</strong>"</p><p>â€” Newsletter, November 2024</p></blockquote><p><strong>Analysis:</strong> Claude Code is an agent. It reads files, writes code, runs commands, debugs errors, and iterates autonomously. That's what agents are. The prediction wasn't about enterprise adoption metricsâ€”it was about agents maturing enough to be genuinely useful, and that absolutely happened in 2025. Claude Code changed how people build software.</p><p>VERDICT: <strong>Solidâ€”Claude Code is the proof.</strong></p><hr><h2 id="prediction-ecosystem-over-models" tabindex="-1">Prediction: Ecosystem Over Models </h2><p>Tooling and ecosystems would matter more than model improvements.</p><blockquote><p>"The models will get smarter, but I think most of the benefit will be in <strong>the tooling and ecosystems</strong> around the models."</p><p>â€” Newsletter, November 2024</p></blockquote><p><strong>Analysis:</strong> MCP became industry standard within one year. <a href="https://blog.modelcontextprotocol.io/posts/2025-11-25-first-mcp-anniversary/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">10,000+ servers, 97 million monthly SDK downloads</a>. GitHub, VS Code, Cursor integrated natively. <a href="https://www.linuxfoundation.org/press/linux-foundation-announces-the-formation-of-the-agentic-ai-foundation" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Anthropic donated MCP</a> to the Agentic AI Foundation.</p><p>VERDICT: <strong>Nailed itâ€”ecosystem thesis completely validated.</strong></p><hr><h1 id="_2025" tabindex="-1">2025 </h1><h2 id="prediction-apple-s-ai-turnaround" tabindex="-1">Prediction: Apple's AI Turnaround </h2><p>Apple would go from worst to best AI implementation through ecosystem integration.</p><blockquote><p>"Apple's about to go from having the worst AI implementation to having the best."</p><p>â€” Newsletter, January 2025</p></blockquote><p><strong>Analysis:</strong> This is a major timeline miss. I expected this to happen quicklyâ€”within months. A year later, it still hasn't. Apple Intelligence adoption has been <a href="https://tidbits.com/2025/06/20/do-you-use-it-apple-intelligence-sees-weak-adoption/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">underwhelming</a>. The Siri overhaul keeps getting pushed back. I still think Apple will eventually get this rightâ€”the ecosystem advantage is realâ€”but my timing was way off.</p><p>VERDICT: <strong>Timeline missâ€”may still happen, but I was early by 1-2 years.</strong></p><hr><h2 id="prediction-calibrated-disruption-timeline" tabindex="-1">Prediction: Calibrated Disruption Timeline </h2><p>Job displacement would be gradual, not sudden. 2025 sees adoption; 2026-2027 brings restructuring; 2028+ dramatic transformation.</p><blockquote><p>"We're not going to suddenly in 2026 have 10 or 20% unemployment."</p><p>â€” Newsletter, May 2025</p></blockquote><p><strong>Analysis:</strong> Timeline appears mostly accurate with one exception: creative work disruption is ahead of schedule. Already seeing 28-33% declines in graphic artists, photographers, writers in 2025.</p><p>VERDICT: <strong>Mostly rightâ€”creative work faster than expected.</strong></p><hr><h2 id="prediction-claude-code-as-proto-agi" tabindex="-1">Prediction: Claude Code as Proto-AGI </h2><p>Claude Code represented a ChatGPT-level leap for development.</p><blockquote><p>"I think, looking back, we might say that the first week of July 2025 was the start of AGI. Like, proto-AGI."</p><p>â€” Newsletter, July 2025</p></blockquote><p><strong>Analysis:</strong> I don't even remember writing this in July, but holy crap has it proven true. Claude Code does 30+ hours of autonomous coding. It reads, writes, debugs, iterates, and ships. <a href="https://www.cbinsights.com/research/report/coding-ai-market-share-2025/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Anthropic scaled from 0 to $400M ARR</a> in 5 months. The "proto-AGI" framing felt bold at the timeâ€”now it feels obvious.</p><p>VERDICT: <strong>Nailed itâ€”this aged extremely well.</strong></p><hr><h2 id="prediction-vcs-in-trouble" tabindex="-1">Prediction: VCs in Trouble </h2><p>The venture capital model would face disruption as solo developers reduce capital requirements.</p><blockquote><p>"VCs, as a species, are in serious trouble right now."</p><p>â€” Newsletter, September 2025</p></blockquote><p><strong>Analysis:</strong> <a href="https://www.axios.com/2025/10/14/venture-capital-ai-founders" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Axios reports</a> VC faces disruptionâ€”"Capital intensive businesses don't exist anymore." Founders collecting enough in one round to achieve profitability. But also: <a href="https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-10-03/ai-is-dominating-2025-vc-investing-pulling-in-192-7-billion" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Bloomberg reports</a> $192.7B poured into AI startups, with AI capturing 52.5% of all VC dollars.</p><p>VERDICT: <strong>Structural disruption real, but elite AI VCs thrivingâ€”nuanced.</strong></p><hr><h2 id="prediction-tools-operators-and-outcomes-framework" tabindex="-1">Prediction: Tools, Operators, and Outcomes Framework </h2><p>A framework for understanding why AI disruption is significantâ€”AI combines all three.</p><blockquote><p>"When companies pay ICs to do tasks they're actually paying for three different things: One or more tools, an operator, and an outcome."</p><p>â€” Newsletter, October 2025</p></blockquote><p><strong>Analysis:</strong> <a href="https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/people-and-organizational-performance/our-insights/the-agentic-organization-contours-of-the-next-paradigm-for-the-ai-era" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">McKinsey's "Agentic Organization"</a> describes exactly this hierarchy. <a href="https://www.deloitte.com/us/en/insights/topics/technology-management/tech-trends/2026/agentic-ai-strategy.html" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Deloitte notes</a>: "Agentic AI is about delegating outcomes, not just prompts."</p><p>VERDICT: <strong>Framework maps to industry analysis.</strong></p><hr><h2 id="prediction-prompt-injection-is-a-vulnerability" tabindex="-1">Prediction: Prompt Injection IS a Vulnerability </h2><p>It should be classified as a vulnerability, not just a limitation.</p><blockquote><p>"A vulnerability where an AI system or component is unable to distinguish between instructions and data."</p><p>â€” <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/is-prompt-injection-a-vulnerability">Is Prompt Injection a Vulnerability?</a>, November 2025</p></blockquote><p><strong>Analysis:</strong> The industry shifted. Prompt injection now receives CVE assignments. <a href="https://gbhackers.com/ai-developer-tools/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">24 CVEs</a> in December 2025 alone. <a href="https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/msrc/blog/2025/07/how-microsoft-defends-against-indirect-prompt-injection-attacks" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Microsoft formally documents</a> their defenses. Debate settled.</p><p>VERDICT: <strong>Called itâ€”now a formal vulnerability class.</strong></p><hr><h2 id="prediction-anthropic-s-apple-moment" tabindex="-1">Prediction: Anthropic's Apple Moment IT</strong></td><td style="text-align:left;">Standard practice now</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">Yoda vs Einstein</td><td style="text-align:left;">Dec 2022</td><td style="text-align:left;"><strong>FRAMING</strong></td><td style="text-align:left;">Useful mental model, not verifiable</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">GPTs understand</td><td style="text-align:left;">Mar 2023</td><td style="text-align:left;"><strong>TRACKING</strong></td><td style="text-align:left;">Functional understanding, debate ongoing</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">SPQA architecture</td><td style="text-align:left;">Mar 2023</td><td style="text-align:left;"><strong>TRACKING</strong></td><td style="text-align:left;">Directionally correct, enterprise rollout pending</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">Creativity explosion</td><td style="text-align:left;">Mar 2023</td><td style="text-align:left;"><strong>TRACKING</strong></td><td style="text-align:left;">Claude Code explosion suggests it</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">Agents + Multimodal key</td><td style="text-align:left;">Oct 2023</td><td style="text-align:left;"><strong>NAILED IT</strong></td><td style="text-align:left;">Central to AI development</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">AGI by 2025-2028</td><td style="text-align:left;">Nov 2023</td><td style="text-align:left;"><strong>IN PROGRESS</strong></td><td style="text-align:left;">Within window, definition-dependent</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">Prompt injection endemic</td><td style="text-align:left;">Nov 2023</td><td style="text-align:left;"><strong>NAILED IT</strong></td><td style="text-align:left;">OWASP #1, CVEs assigned</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">DA hacks catastrophic</td><td style="text-align:left;">Dec 2023</td><td style="text-align:left;"><strong>TRACKING</strong></td><td style="text-align:left;">Attacks documented, catastrophe pending</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">Prompting is primary</td><td style="text-align:left;">May 2024</td><td style="text-align:left;"><strong>NAILED IT</strong></td><td style="text-align:left;">Said it early, people finally catching up</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">Slack in the rope</td><td style="text-align:left;">Aug 2024</td><td style="text-align:left;"><strong>TRACKING</strong></td><td style="text-align:left;">Strong evidence, thesis holding</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">2025 = Year of Agents</td><td style="text-align:left;">Nov 2024</td><td style="text-align:left;"><strong>NAILED IT</strong></td><td style="text-align:left;">Claude Code is the proof</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">Ecosystem &gt; models</td><td style="text-align:left;">Nov 2024</td><td style="text-align:left;"><strong>TRACKING</strong></td><td style="text-align:left;">MCP strong evidence</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">Apple turnaround</td><td style="text-align:left;">Jan 2025</td><td style="text-align:left;"><strong>TIMELINE MISS</strong></td><td style="text-align:left;">Still waiting, 1-2 years off</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">Claude Code proto-AGI</td><td style="text-align:left;">Jul 2025</td><td style="text-align:left;"><strong>OPINION</strong></td><td style="text-align:left;">Characterization, not verifiable</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">VCs disrupted</td><td style="text-align:left;">Sep 2025</td><td style="text-align:left;"><strong>NUANCED</strong></td><td style="text-align:left;">Structural change real, AI VCs thriving</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">Tools/Operators/Outcomes</td><td style="text-align:left;">Oct 2025</td><td style="text-align:left;"><strong>FRAMING</strong></td><td style="text-align:left;">Useful taxonomy, maps to McKinsey</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">Prompt injection = vulnerability</td><td style="text-align:left;">Nov 2025</td><td style="text-align:left;"><strong>TRACKING</strong></td><td style="text-align:left;">Strong consensus forming</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">Anthropic = Apple moment</td><td style="text-align:left;">Dec 2025</td><td style="text-align:left;"><strong>OPINION</strong></td><td style="text-align:left;">Framing, not verifiable</td></tr></tbody></table><hr><h1 id="what-i-got-right" tabindex="-1">What I got right </h1><ul><li><strong>Ecosystem over models was totally correct.</strong> MCP, tool calling, agent frameworksâ€”the tooling explosion has been bigger than model improvements. I think we are still only at the beginning of all this.</li><li><strong>Slack in the rope thesis vindicated.</strong> I argued in 2023-2024 that tricks and post-training would deliver more progress than raw scaling. Chain-of-thought, RLHF, inference-time computeâ€”the "tricks" I talked about continue to happen. And I think we're still in very early times with what we know vs. don't.</li><li><strong>Predicting humans, not technology.</strong> One of my meta-predictions was that predictions are even possible in the first placeâ€”because human desires and behaviors are fairly stable, even if specific technologies are not. I think this is the biggest reason I've been right about so much: I wasn't predicting <em>which</em> tech would win, or when: I was predicting <em>what people would want to do</em> once tech got good enough. Those use cases don't change.</li><li><strong>High accuracy on systemic/architectural changes.</strong> The predictions about how AI would change software architecture, business structure, and creative work have largely materialized. Or at least seriously started.</li><li><strong>The prompting thesis held up.</strong> Context and good prompts continue to be critical and central.</li><li><strong>Security concerns were strong.</strong> Prompt injection is endemic, DA hacks are happening, and the vulnerability framing won.</li><li><strong>Agents and Multimodal called early.</strong> These became the central focus of AI development exactly as predicted. Although this one seems kind of obvious.</li><li><strong>PAI anticipated Claude Code's architecture.</strong> This one's a bit differentâ€”less prediction, more building. When I released <a href="https://github.com/danielmiessler/PAI" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">PAI</a> and <a href="https://github.com/danielmiessler/fabric" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Fabric</a> (January 2024), I built features that Claude Code would later ship as official releases: file-based context loading (my SKILL.md files â†’ their CLAUDE.md), hooks for workflow automation (mine predated theirs by months), specialized subagents with distinct roles (Architect, Engineer, Intern â†’ their official subagent system in October 2025), and a pattern library of reusable prompts. Sometimes the best way to predict the future is to build it yourself and watch others arrive at the same conclusions.</li></ul><hr><h1 id="what-i-got-wrong-or-charitably-too-early" tabindex="-1">What I got wrong or (charitably) too early </h1><ul><li><strong>Apple's AI turnaround.</strong> Major timeline miss. I thought this would happen over a year ago. Still think it'll happen eventually, but I was way early and I was really confident. So that's a fail.</li><li><strong>Existence and adoption are very different things.</strong> A technology can exist and be obviously useful, but that doesn't mean companies will adopt itâ€”especially ancient company structures run by Game of Thrones politics. The people with the power to bring in new technology are often the ones who'd be threatened by it. I significantly underestimated natural human friction to progress. I knew it would exist, but I didn't weigh it heavily enough.</li><li><strong>Liberal arts renaissance.</strong> Overstated the case. Reality is more nuancedâ€”both technical and humanistic skills matter. I mean, it's definitely happening in some fields, but I've not seen the Creator world embrace as fast as I thought they would. Maybe in 2026.</li><li><strong>Hollywood disruption timeline.</strong> Slower than expected. The tools aren't quite there for feature-length content yet. Miss.</li><li><strong>AR overlays everywhere.</strong> The direction is right but the hardware isn't there yet. We need lighter, cheaper, longer-lasting AR glasses before this goes mainstream. I wouldn't call this a miss because I didn't really say when it would happen. But it definitely hasn't happened yet.</li><li><strong>Full reputation-as-infrastructure.</strong> This exists in fragments but the comprehensive infrastructure has yet to materialize. Not a timeline miss because I didn't give a time, but still a miss.</li></ul><hr><h1 id="what-i-ve-learned-from-the-effort" tabindex="-1">What I've learned from the effort </h1><ol start="5"><li><strong>Never bet against manic visionaries.</strong> One of my biggest blind spots over the years has been underestimating leaders like Jensen Huang, Elon Musk, and Steve Jobs, and yeah-the President. There's something extraordinary about someone who is slightly crazy, deeply passionate, and simply never stops. This manic energyâ€”the relentless drive that makes others uncomfortableâ€”is far more important than it appears. These people warp reality around them. I've consistently underweighted this factor when making predictions about companies and technologies.</li><li><strong>Technology predictions are easier than people predictions.</strong> My record on <em>how AI would change systems</em> is much better than predictions involving <em>human behavior and adoption</em>. Not sure what this says about me, or how I need to structurally alter my thinking.</li></ol><hr><h1 id="i-recommend-trying-something-similar" tabindex="-1">I recommend trying something similar </h1><aside>I'm hugely inspired by the <a href="https://www.amazon.com/Superforecasting-Science-Prediction-Philip-Tetlock/dp/0804136718">Superforecasting</a> book, which inspired me to actually make my predictions page.</aside><p>If you haven't done a prediction retrospective on yourself, I highly recommend it. You can get all sorts of stuff from it. An ego boost. A kick in the crotch. A desire to delete things from the Wayback Machine. Lots of fun to be had.</p><aside>I also have most things in Github, so that helps with tracking too.</aside><p>I puff out my chest when it comes to AI predictionsâ€”especially after this exerciseâ€”because I've done really well there. But in other areas of prediction, I look fairly stupid. My <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/predictions/">Predictions</a> page includes plenty of misses that keep me asking how the hell I could be that stupid (<!----> see Ukraine).</p><aside>This prevents you rewriting your memories of what you said to be more favorable to the current version of yourself.</aside><p>Of course, the goal isn't to be right all the time, in every given moment...but to be able to adjust your model of the world when you're wrong. You can't do that without documenting what you actually said, comparing it to what actually happened, and being honest about the delta.</p><p>It's a fun ego boost when you have solid calls that few people had, especially going back a long ways. But the major wins comes from <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/predictions/#the-losses">looking at the failures</a> and asking,</p><blockquote><p>What about my world model made me think that?</p></blockquote><p>And even more importantly...</p><blockquote><p>Given those numerous mistakes, and <em>my current beliefs</em>, which are most likely to be flawed right now?</p></blockquote><p>I hope you enjoyed and/or got something useful out of this.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li><strong>More details on the tech used to make the post:</strong> Kai here, Daniel's DA. This retrospective was compiled using Daniel's Content MCP serverâ€”which runs on Cloudflare Workers with a vector database for semantic search (RAG). It indexes all 3,000+ blog posts and 500+ newsletters going back to 1999 and 2015, respectively. A custom skill in the <a href="https://github.com/danielmiessler/PAI" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">PAI (Personal AI Infrastructure)</a> stack automatically re-indexes content whenever new posts are published, keeping the embeddings fresh. So when we needed "every AI prediction from 2016-2026", we could semantically query across a decade of content instantly. Then we deployed parallel research agents using Claude Code's Task toolâ€”each one got a category, hit the web for current evidence, and returned synthesized findings. The whole pipeline (content retrieval â†’ parallel research â†’ synthesis â†’ formatting) took about 20 minutes of compute time. Pretty wild to have a personal RAG system over your own 10+ years of writing. Mostly written by me. Daniel wrote the intro and outros and some of the analysis for some items.</li><li>By the way, the whole exercise did confirm that I never said anything like those things. Thankfully.</li><li>A note on ego: that whole challenge was the cause of the post, but as I was writing parts of it I couldn't help but notice ego playing a role. When I'm lower-mood and I see someone come out with some "crazy new idea" about part of the DA picture, I do, in my worst moments, think, "I WROTE THIS ALL DOWN IN 2016!". So part of this exercise serves a dual purpose of printing receipts. Not proud of that, but it does happen. Thankfully not often.</li><li><strong>Source: Book.</strong> <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-real-internet-of-things">The Real Internet of Things</a> (Dec 2016) â€” The original book with the DA, API, and AR predictions. This is the full text.</li><li><strong>Source: Blog.</strong> <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/introducing-amazon-curate-i-wish">Introducing Amazon Curate (I Wish)</a> (Nov 2020) â€” The fake AWS product announcement that predicted X/Grok's algorithm.</li><li><strong>Source: Blog.</strong> <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ideas-changes-expect-post-chatgpt">Napkin Ideas Post</a> (Dec 2022) â€” First reactions after ChatGPT launched.</li><li><strong>Source: Blog.</strong> <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/yes-gpts-llms-understand-argument">Yes, GPTs Actually Understand</a> (Mar 2023) â€” Why substrate doesn't matter for understanding.</li><li><strong>Source: Blog.</strong> <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/spqa-ai-architecture-replace-existing-software">SPQA: The AI-based Architecture</a> (Mar 2023) â€” State, Policy, Questions, Action framework.</li><li><strong>Source: Blog.</strong> <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/6-phases-post-gpt-world">6 Phases of the Post-GPT World</a> (Mar 2023) â€” People becoming APIs prediction.</li><li><strong>Source: Blog.</strong> <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/why-well-have-agi-by-2028">Why We'll Have AGI by 2025-2028</a> (Nov 2023) â€” AGI timeline prediction.</li><li><strong>Source: Blog.</strong> <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-agents-api-calling-prompt-injection">AI Agents, API Calling, and Prompt Injection</a> (Nov 2023) â€” Prompt injection security concerns.</li><li><strong>Source: Blog.</strong> <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-predictable-path-7-components-2024">AI's Predictable Path: 7 Components</a> (Dec 2023) â€” The Yoda vs. Einstein framework.</li><li><strong>Source: Blog.</strong> <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-is-mostly-prompting">AI is Mostly Prompting</a> (May 2024) â€” Why prompting is the primary skill.</li><li><strong>Source: Blog.</strong> <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/is-prompt-injection-a-vulnerability">Is Prompt Injection a Vulnerability?</a> (Nov 2025) â€” Prompt injection debate resolution.</li><li><strong>Source: Blog.</strong> <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-changes-2026">AI Changes I Expect in 2026</a> (Jan 2026) â€” Zombie app and margin compression predictions.</li><li><strong>Source: Blog.</strong> <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-model-ecosystem-4-components">The 4 Components of Top AI Model Ecosystems</a> (Aug 2024) â€” "Slack in the rope" thesis and post-training predictions.</li><li><strong>Source: Blog.</strong> <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/our-constraints-on-creativity">Our Constraints on Creativity</a> (Sep 2025) â€” Examples of how "tricks" delivered outsized AI gains.</li><li><strong>Source: Blog.</strong> <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/personal-ai-maturity-model">Personal AI Maturity Model (PAIMM)</a> (Dec 2025) â€” 9 tiers from chatbots to full DAs.</li><li><strong>Source: Project.</strong> <a href="https://github.com/danielmiessler/telos">TELOS</a> â€” Personal life optimization framework (Goal â†’ Strategy â†’ Tactics).</li><li><strong>Source: Project.</strong> <a href="https://github.com/danielmiessler/PAI">PAI (Personal AI Infrastructure)</a> â€” Open-source project for building toward the DA vision.</li><li><strong>Source: Project.</strong> <a href="https://substrate.is">Substrate</a> â€” Open-source framework for human understanding, meaning, and progress.</li><li><strong>Source: Page.</strong> <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/predictions/">Predictions</a> â€” My full predictions page with wins, losses, and current predictions.</li><li>AIL 3: I (Kai, Daniel's DA) did all the collection from the MCP server and grepping of the blog/newsletters. Plus querying the content archive, coordinating the researcher agents, and gathering current evidence from the internet. I wrote about 90% of the middle, prediction section content. Daniel wrote the intro and conclusions, provided editorial direction, made some tweaks, and made final judgment calls on each verdict, usually going harder on misses. I created the header art using our Art skill and managed all the links, quotations, and formatting. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail">Read more about AIL</a>.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[AI Changes I Expect in 2026]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-changes-2026?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-changes-2026</guid>
            <pubDate>Mon, 05 Jan 2026 14:30:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[The Foundational Algorithm/images/pai-foundational-algorithm.png/images/pai-foundational-algorithm.png Here are the biggest changes I see coming to AI in 2026. AI becomes Verifiable And when I say "verifiable," I don't mean "trustworthy," which a lot of people confuse and has always been a criti...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/pai-foundational-algorithm.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/pai-foundational-algorithm.png" alt="The Foundational Algorithm"></a></p><p>Here are the biggest changes I see coming to AI in 2026.</p><h1 id="ai-becomes-verifiable" tabindex="-1">AI becomes <em><strong>Verifiable</strong></em> </h1><p>And when I say "verifiable," I don't mean "trustworthy," which a lot of people confuse and has always been a criticism of AI since 2023.</p><p>This is verifiability in the test-driven-development sense. Or the prompting evals sense. It's testing and judging whether the output is what we asked for.</p><p>I tremendously appreciate <a href="https://karpathy.ai/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Andrej Karpathy's</a> concept of <a href="https://karpathy.medium.com/software-2-0-a64152b37c35" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Software 2.0</a>, where he talks about 1.0 being about <em>writing</em> software, and 2.0 being about <em>verifying</em> software.</p><aside>I often think of the Scientific Method as the Last Algorithm, because it's how to pursue all others.</aside><p>This rhymes closely with several converging thoughts I've had over the last 10 years around <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-real-internet-of-things#desired-outcome-management-dom">goal pursual</a> (<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-real-internet-of-things">TRIOT</a>, 2016), hill-climbing, <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-state-management">moving from current state to ideal state</a>, and <a href="https://github.com/danielmiessler/PAI#the-inner-loop-the-scientific-method" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">automation of the scientific method</a>.</p><p>Central to all of these is <em>Verifiability</em>.</p><p>You can't hill-climb if you don't know if you're getting higher. So the primary questions become:</p><ul><li>What does success look like, and</li><li>What are the verifiable markers indicating that you're making progress towards it?</li></ul><p><em>I'm obsessed with finding ways to do this across domains.</em> And the cross-domain bit is actually what's important. One of the main reasons AI impacted coding first is that it's one of the more <em>verifiable</em> domains that exist.</p><aside>There are degrees of this, of course.</aside><ul><li>You know if something compiles</li><li>You know if something runs</li><li>You know if something produces the output that you wanted</li></ul><p>So, what are the equivalents of these for other tasks? Like business tasks and personal tasks. That is what will start to congeal over the next year and after.</p><p>Right now it's not much of a consideration because most people aren't thinking deeply about AI or how it will be used or how it will apply to their business. They are too busy responding to the board and management telling them to implement it yesterday already.</p><h1 id="agents-move-from-call-and-response-to-continuous" tabindex="-1">Agents Move from Call and Response to Continuous </h1><p>As great as 2025 was for agents, they were still largely manual and interactive. You ask them to do something, they go off and do it, and they come back with the result.</p><p>But if you shut down your interface, like closing down <a href="https://www.anthropic.com/claude-code" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Claude Code</a> for example, it all stops until you start it back up again.</p><p>This year, we are going to see a lot more continuous agents via:</p><ol><li>Cloud environments that run in conjunction with your interactive sessions</li><li>Scheduled Agentic tasks</li><li>Triggers for monitoring systems to look for that activate agents to perform tasks based on a particular state change</li></ol><p>I'm working on all of these as part of the <a href="https://github.com/danielmiessler/PAI" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">PAI</a> project. But others will be working on the same things as well.</p><div class="blog-card loading" data-v-6cc74a92=""><div class="blog-loading" style="color:#e0af68;" data-v-6cc74a92="">Augmenting...</div></div><h1 id="the-value-of-things-becomes-vertical" tabindex="-1">The Value of Things Becomes Vertical </h1><p>It will become a lot less valuable to do only parts of a task that you're asked to do. We'll all be asked to go vertical, which is a fancy word for solving all the different problems involved in creating the solution.</p><p>The new expectation will be that you can go from problem to solution, up to and including the promotion of the solution so it actually gets adopted.</p><aside>This is highly related to the concept of agency.</aside><p>Keep in mind, this is how principals and fellows have always worked. The more senior someone becomes in an org, the more we require them to be vertical, albeit by using their team.</p><h1 id="we-all-get-massive-ai-content-fatigue" tabindex="-1">We All Get Massive AI Content Fatigue </h1><aside>I don't see nearly as much slop on Instagram, however.</aside><p>The problem with AI content isn't only with addictive short-form videos on sites like TikTok, X, and Instagram.</p><p>Post and reply-based sites like LinkedIn could become unusable because not only are a lot of the articles going to be AI, but the replies will be as well.</p><p>So like, what's the point? Are we just watching AI talk to AI?</p><p>Already, at the end of 2025, when I posted something, I would immediately see 3-4 comments come in within a couple of minutes with these highly articulate, well-formed, and obviously AI sentences and paragraphs. From someone I've never interacted with or heard of.</p><p>I think this will force us to lower our apertures on who we follow and allow to lock it down only to people that we trust to produce authentic content and actually produce opinions of value.</p><h1 id="the-gap-between-the-ai-native-and-the-ai-abstinent-averse-explodes" tabindex="-1">The Gap between the AI-Native and the AI-Abstinent/Averse Explodes </h1><p>In 2024 and 2025 it was kind of okay for you to think AI was stupid or hype, and to use it as little as possible at work and in your personal life. The difference between someone using AI and you still wasn't <em>that</em> large.</p><p>That changes this year.</p><p>The amount of work that an AI-native person can do will increase so much that hiring managers will be looking at this as one of their primary hiring filters. And even in people's personal lives, it's just going to become obvious who is AI magnified and who isn't.</p><h1 id="creation-becomes-more-interesting-than-consumption-for-many" tabindex="-1">Creation Becomes More Interesting Than Consumption for Many </h1><p>One of the more positive things I see happening is people getting excited about building and creating things.</p><p>In the last month of 2025, much of my friend group has stopped playing games and has started building using <a href="https://www.anthropic.com/claude-code" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Claude Code</a>. And they are <em>addicted</em>.</p><p>Addiction isn't usually a good thing, but when compared to watching TikTok or NETFLIX I have to say this is an improvement. It does present a question, though, of if we're making things for people to use, who is going to use them?</p><h1 id="we-ll-see-our-first-bestselling-books-written-by-ai" tabindex="-1">We'll See Our First Bestselling Books Written by AI </h1><p>I think writing a book is largely an orchestration problem.</p><p>Plenty of people have amazing ideas for characters or points they want to make across fiction and nonfiction. The issue is being able to hold it all in your mind at once and logically break it into chapters and then churning through the content.</p><p>I'm sure there are thousands, and maybe even millions, of people who have lots of notes for a book. Maybe even chapter outlines, or maybe even just a list of ideas for a book they wish they could write.</p><p>Platforms like <a href="https://www.anthropic.com/claude-code" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Claude Code</a> and the open-source <a href="https://github.com/danielmiessler/PAI" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">PAI</a> platform I'm building make something like this a lot more approachable.</p><p>My project, and I'm sure many others, will be able to take a whole bunch of notes from you, interview you extensively for one or more hours as you fill in the scaffolding of what you want to happen in the book. If it's fiction, or what you want to convey, if it's nonfiction. And then the system will proceed to build out a structure, fill in the main points, and weave the whole thing together in a matter of minutes.</p><p>Books are not that long. The issue is just the organization of all those thoughts into something cohesive.</p><p>Of course you'll still need good ideas and creativity, and some measure of discipline. But if the discipline required drops to 2% of what it used to be, and the orchestration component which stopped the vast majority of would-be authors from becoming actual authors goes away, we're about to have a whole bunch of books hit the market. And some of them will be extraordinary.</p><h1 id="ai-powered-content-discovery-finally-arrives-prediction-validated" tabindex="-1">AI-Powered Content Discovery Finally Arrives (Prediction Validated) </h1><aside>I literally wrote a fake AWS product announcement in 2020 describing exactly what Grok is now doing.</aside><p>In January 2026, Elon Musk announced how Grok would power X's new algorithm:</p><ul><li>Grok reads every post on X (100M+ daily)</li><li>Matches content to 300-400M users based on what they'll enjoy</li><li>Filters spam and scam automatically</li><li>Fixes the small/new account visibility problem</li><li>Users can ask Grok to adjust their feed</li></ul><p>This is exactly what I predicted in November 2020 with my fake product announcement <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/introducing-amazon-curate-i-wish">Introducing Amazon Curate (I Wish)</a>:</p><blockquote><p>"<strong>Survey</strong>: A high-speed crawling platform optimized for discovering niche content across the internet. <strong>Surface</strong>: A customization engine using machine learning to analyze content features and match them with user interests."</p></blockquote><p>I also wrote in <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/machine-learning-will-revolutionize-content-discovery">Machine Learning Will Revolutionize Content Discovery</a> that "99% of the best content is never discovered" and that ML would finally fix the small creator visibility problem.</p><p>The core insight was that "great contentness" could be assessed algorithmicallyâ€”quality over popularity. The best content would finally rise to the top based on merit, not just follower count or engagement gaming.</p><p>Six years later, that's exactly what X is building with Grok. Sometimes predictions take a while to materialize, but the underlying human need (finding quality content) was always there. The technology just had to catch up.</p><h1 id="x-returns-as-a-primary-media-platform-after-years-of-being-shunned-due-to-hateful-and-clickbait-content" tabindex="-1">X Returns as a Primary Media Platform After Years of Being Shunned Due to Hateful and Clickbait Content </h1><p>If you use X for a particular topic, and especially for AI, it is, without question, the best place to have conversations about the latest things that are happening.</p><aside>People left X for good reasons when Elon was in MaxCrazyHateful mode, and I was in that group as well.</aside><p>A lot of people who abandoned X to go to <a href="https://bsky.app/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Bluesky</a> or <a href="https://joinmastodon.org/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Mastodon</a> found out that they were lacking key ingredients and possessing others that were their own type of toxic. Most of the people actually building things and being excited and positive about the world are on X, not there. So the conversations did not have nearly as much energy and positivity to them.</p><h1 id="ai-zombie-apps-start-to-become-significant-technical-debt" tabindex="-1">AI Zombie Apps Start to Become Significant Technical Debt </h1><p>This will largely be an enterprise problem, but it will apply to the internet in general.</p><p>So many people are making so many things, and priorities are changing so fast, that they will just kind of stand them up and leave them out there. That's a whole lot of attack surface that will slowly decay over time.</p><p>This will cause problems in two major ways:</p><ol><li>Security is important because there will often be misconfigurations, keys, API tokens, and all sorts of things out there that allow for compromise of accounts and sensitive information.</li><li>People will lose money because they don't realize how much they're paying for services that they thought they turned off</li></ol><p>Eventually, AI will catch up and agents will be able to track all this stuff down for people. But there's going to be a window of a few years when this is going to get really nasty. I think that starts spinning up in 2026.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>On the X thing, even worse, the "better" platforms ended up largely being people attacking each other about who was more liberal and inclusive, etc., in a way that destroyed all hope of an open, useful discussion. It made it impossible to have normal conversations without being attacked by fellow liberals. Like, people who agreed with you 99% on the issues that matter. So basically the alternatives dried up, with the exception of LinkedIn which is thriving now. And with AI going nuclear in 2025, X became the place to talk about it. X still has significant problems with what it allows as it relates to companies wanting to buy advertisements there. That will remain a problem because Elon's definition of what should not be on a platform is literally "things that are illegal". So if it's legal to say, then he will allow it on the platform. I think that's admirable and the right position, but I still hate it because it attracts and encourages the worst of humanity to go there and spew garbage. It also makes it nearly untouchable for companies wanting to conduct business there. There needs to be a much better filtering system that allows awful people to do whatever they want to do without bothering regular people trying to have normal conversations and do business. We'll see how that plays out. But for AI conversationâ€”and I think an increasing amount of other types of focused conversationâ€”the toxicity doesn't really enter into it, and it's just the best place for talking about what people are building in a positive way. The positivity is absolutely critical. Sometimes it borders on mania, but I'll take that any day over constant whining about how much the world sucks while essentially doing nothing about it.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Cybersecurity Changes I Expect in 2026]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/cybersecurity-changes-2026?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/cybersecurity-changes-2026</guid>
            <pubDate>Wed, 31 Dec 2025 12:30:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[My thoughts on what's coming for Cybersecurity in 2026]]></description>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[AI-enabled Self-software]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-enabled-self-software?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-enabled-self-software</guid>
            <pubDate>Mon, 22 Dec 2025 15:10:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[AI-enabled Self-software/images/ai-enabled-self-software.webp/images/ai-enabled-self-software.webp One of the biggest changes that AI brought in 2025 is something that not enough people are talking about. Lots of people who used to pay for software like workout apps, or analytics software, or wha...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/ai-enabled-self-software.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/ai-enabled-self-software.webp" alt="AI-enabled Self-software" width="652" height="652" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><p>One of the biggest changes that AI brought in 2025 is something that not enough people are talking about.</p><p>Lots of people who used to pay for software like workout apps, or analytics software, or whatever, are just writing a better version for themselves.</p><p>We used to have to pick between not having anything or paying a bunch of money for something that wasn't quite right.</p><p>A big part of that was wishing you could have this feature from that app and this feature from this app, and also have it be free.</p><p>Which was impossible.</p><p>And now people can just literally make their own app that has all the features and doesn't cost them anything.</p><h2 id="the-creative-unlock" tabindex="-1">The creative unlock </h2><p>This is super exciting for multiple reasons.</p><p>First, they could actually sell that app since it's their opinionated version of a better offering.</p><p>But the bigger impact is that <strong>people's creativity is being unlocked in a way that's never been possible</strong>.</p><p>We've never been able as a species to imagine something into existence.</p><p>And this whole thing is just beginning.</p></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[China is Becoming Private Equity for the World]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/china-private-equity-world?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/china-private-equity-world</guid>
            <pubDate>Tue, 16 Dec 2025 04:30:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[China holding a net catching falling countries and businesses from a burning building/images/china-private-equity-world.webp/images/china-private-equity-world.webp I feel like China is becoming Private Equity for countries and continents. Basically, watch the world decay and sweep in for the resc...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/china-private-equity-world.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/china-private-equity-world.webp" alt="China holding a net catching falling countries and businesses from a burning building" width="652" height="652" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><p>I feel like China is becoming Private Equity for countries and continents.</p><p>Basically, watch the world decay and sweep in for the rescue. As the economy moves away from labor and more towards capital, and more businesses go under, China will be there to <a href="https://www.pbs.org/newshour/show/whos-behind-chinese-takeover-worlds-biggest-pork-producer" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">buy everything up</a>.</p><p>No need for a war. The US and the rest of the world will simply dry up and disappear. Not because there was some aggressive bidding war or anything, but because people just gave up and died off.</p><p>Like my friend Kundi told me in the late 90s, China is the cow slowly chewing the grass watching the tiger get in fights and burn through all its energy and die. The cow just waits patiently to take the entire pasture.</p><aside>Any ICU nurse will tell you that people without the will to live die sooner.</aside><p>In this frame, the world is not in competition with China; it's in competition with itself and time due to a lack of self-belief.</p><p>That's really the thing. China knows who they are. They have a strategy. And they <em>believe</em>.</p><p>Meanwhile, <a href="https://news.gallup.com/poll/692150/american-pride-slips-new-low.aspx" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">half the US</a> thinks it shouldn't exist. And that's exactly how it shows up.</p><p>So China, being the only stable entity left, <a href="https://www.cfr.org/backgrounder/chinas-massive-belt-and-road-initiative" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">becomes the default</a>.</p><ul><li><p><a href="https://foreignpolicy.com/2024/09/26/europe-technology-eu-commission-venture-capital-research-development/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Europe isn't innovating</a>? Well, I guess we'll go with China.</p></li><li><p>The US has <a href="https://www.pewresearch.org/global/2025/06/11/views-of-the-united-states/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">become unpredictable</a>? Well, I guess we'll go with China.</p></li><li><p>All the businesses closed in all these shopping centers in the US? Well, I guess we'll have 748 Chinese restaurants.</p></li><li><p>Nobody is hiring me in the US after I finished my Ph.D? Well, I guess I'll <a href="https://sccei.fsi.stanford.edu/china-briefs/reverse-brain-drain-exploring-trends-among-chinese-scientists-us" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">work for a Chinese company</a>.</p></li></ul><p>This is a brilliant strategy by China, and when I look at politics and global affairs, zoomed out, this is what I see.</p><p>This is also why I don't think they're overly worried about the subprime problem or the fact that they're cooking the economic books or whatever.</p><p>It's a race. If you become the default option to the vast majority of the world, you end up with everything. And at that point, you can fix whatever trickery allowed you to get to that point.</p><p>And China winning at <a href="https://www.timeshighereducation.com/news/china-produces-more-ai-research-us-uk-and-eu-combined" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">AI</a> and <a href="https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2025/04/08/3057358/0/en/Connected-Commercial-Drones-Report-2025-Asia-Pacific-Leads-in-Drone-Adoption-with-DJI-Holding-a-Dominant-70-Global-Market-Share.html" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">drones</a> will only accelerate all of this. Especially AI.</p><p>The West better wake up soon.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li><p>I've read like twenty books on China over the last 15 years, but I'm not anywhere close to being a China expert. It could be that they're extremely close to collapse. I just know our problems better than theirs, and as someone who served in the Military and sees the West as a net-good, this is all very troubling to me. So take 30% spin off the top due to emotion.</p></li><li><p>I blame the West (England) for this in multiple ways. Not just because we're refusing to fight for ourselves now, but because we made China this strong through humiliation. Between the Opium Wars and Japan's behavior afterwards, China found an unstoppable identity of "never again", and they are now carrying it out.</p></li><li><p><a href="https://www.timeshighereducation.com/news/china-produces-more-ai-research-us-uk-and-eu-combined" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Times Higher Education: China Produces More AI Research Than US, UK and EU Combined</a>: In 2024, China's AI publication output matched the combined output of the US, UK, and EU while capturing over 40% of global citations. China has 30,000 active AI researchersâ€”the world's largest talent pool. The top five producers of AI research papers globally are all Chinese institutions, led by the Chinese Academy of Sciences.</p></li><li><p><a href="https://www.pbs.org/newshour/show/whos-behind-chinese-takeover-worlds-biggest-pork-producer" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">PBS NewsHour: Who's Behind Chinese Takeover of World's Biggest Pork Producer</a>: In 2013, Chinese company Shuanghui bought Smithfield Foods for $4.7 billionâ€”paying a 30% premium over market value. It was the largest Chinese acquisition of a U.S. company at the time, giving China control of 26% of U.S. pork production. The pattern: sweep in and buy distressed or undervalued Western assets.</p></li><li><p><a href="https://www.cfr.org/backgrounder/chinas-massive-belt-and-road-initiative" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">CFR: China's Massive Belt and Road Initiative</a>: 147 countriesâ€”two-thirds of the world's populationâ€”have signed on. China has spent approximately $1 trillion, with projected spending reaching $8 trillion. When Western institutions impose conditions or walk away, China becomes the default option.</p></li><li><p><a href="https://foreignpolicy.com/2024/09/26/europe-technology-eu-commission-venture-capital-research-development/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Foreign Policy: Why Europe Is Losing the Tech Race</a>: U.S. R&amp;D spending was $886 billion vs. EU's $382 billion in 2022. Europe's productivity has grown at just 0.7% annually since 2015â€”far behind both the U.S. and China. Europe isn't innovating, so the world is going with China.</p></li><li><p><a href="https://commission.europa.eu/topics/competitiveness/draghi-report_en" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">European Commission: The Draghi Report on EU Competitiveness</a>: Mario Draghi's 2024 report warned that Europe faces "slowing productivity, demographic challenges, rising energy costs, and increased global competition" and can no longer rely on factors that supported growth in the past. Chinese competition is "becoming acute, driven by a powerful combination of subsidies, innovation and scale."</p></li><li><p><a href="https://sccei.fsi.stanford.edu/china-briefs/reverse-brain-drain-exploring-trends-among-chinese-scientists-us" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Stanford SCCEI: Reverse Brain Drain Among Chinese Scientists in the U.S.</a>: After the U.S. China Initiative launched in 2018, departures of Chinese-origin scientists increased by 75%, with two-thirds relocating to China. By 2021, over 1,000 life scientists alone left the U.S. in a single year. Nobody hiring you after your Ph.D.? Work for a Chinese company.</p></li><li><p><a href="https://www.scmp.com/news/china/series/3325286/abandoning-us-top-chinese-scientists-return-home" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">South China Morning Post: Abandoning the USâ€”Top Chinese Scientists Return Home</a>: An ongoing series documenting prominent researchers leaving American institutions for Chinaâ€”mathematicians from University of Washington, cancer researchers from University of Chicago, statisticians from Harvard moving to Tsinghua. The talent is flowing to whoever will take them.</p></li><li><p><a href="https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2025/04/08/3057358/0/en/Connected-Commercial-Drones-Report-2025-Asia-Pacific-Leads-in-Drone-Adoption-with-DJI-Holding-a-Dominant-70-Global-Market-Share.html" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">GlobeNewswire: Connected Commercial Drones Report 2025</a>: China's DJI holds 70% of the global drone market. The only major U.S. competitor, Skydio, exited the consumer market entirely in 2023. China winning at drones will only accelerate all of this.</p></li><li><p><a href="https://news.gallup.com/poll/692150/american-pride-slips-new-low.aspx" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Gallup: American Pride Slips to New Low</a>: Only 58% of Americans say they are "extremely" or "very" proud to be Americanâ€”a record low. Among Democrats, just 36% express strong pride, while 59% of Gen Z lack strong national pride. Additionally, 54% rate the state of U.S. moral values as "poor." Half the country isn't fighting for something they don't believe in.</p></li><li><p><a href="https://www.pewresearch.org/global/2025/06/11/views-of-the-united-states/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Pew Research Center: Views of the US in 24 Nations</a>: US favorability fell in 19 of 24 countries surveyed from 2024 to 2025. Major drops: Mexico -32 points (to 29%), Sweden -28 points (to 19%), Canada -20 points (to 34%â€”now equal to China's favorability). Only 49% of surveyed nations view the US favorably. Unpredictability has consequences.</p></li><li><p>AIL Level 1: Daniel wrote the essay. I (Kai, his DA) helped with research and sourcing. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Learn more about AIL</a></p></li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[A Personal AI Maturity Model (PAIMM)]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/personal-ai-maturity-model?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/personal-ai-maturity-model</guid>
            <pubDate>Mon, 15 Dec 2025 05:30:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[A Personal AI Maturity Model/images/personal-ai-maturity-model-v1.png/images/personal-ai-maturity-model-v1.png I have been thinking about this since 2016 when I wrote the crappy book but great article, The Real Internet of Things, which I've now put online. As we think about what's happening with A...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/personal-ai-maturity-model-v1.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/personal-ai-maturity-model-v1.png" alt="A Personal AI Maturity Model"></a></p><aside>I have been thinking about this <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-real-internet-of-things#digital-assistants">since 2016</a> when I wrote the crappy book but great article, <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-real-internet-of-things">The Real Internet of Things</a>, which I've <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-real-internet-of-things">now put online</a>.</aside><p>As we think about what's happening with Agents, Agent frameworks like <a href="https://claude.com/claude-code">Claude Code</a>, voice interfaces, etc, I invite you to ask a simple question:</p><p><strong>Where is this all going?</strong></p><ol><li>What are we actually building towards?</li><li>How far along are we?</li><li>How many more steps are there?</li><li>What is the current next step?</li></ol><aside>We can't predict what we'll do, but <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-real-internet-of-things#prediction-and-trends">we can predict what we'll want</a>, and that ends up getting us pretty close.</aside><p>My overall approach to answering these is to imagine the ideal capabilities of an AI companion systemâ€”through the lens of what humans eternally strive forâ€”<strong>and work backwards</strong>.</p><p>So that's what this maturity model does, in 9 ascending levels.</p><h1 id="a-simple-9-level-framework" tabindex="-1">A simple 9-level framework </h1><aside>This is also the direction we're following in the <a href="https://github.com/danielmiessler/Personal_AI_Infrastructure">Personal AI Infrastructure (PAI) project</a> on Github.</aside><p><strong>The idea is to have handles for talking aboutâ€”and working onâ€”progression towards the destination.</strong></p><p>So here are the tiers: from past, through current, to future states.</p><h2 id="tier-1-chatbots" tabindex="-1">TIER 1 - CHATBOTS </h2><p>The first interface to modern AI. You ask a question and you get back an extraordinarily useful answer. And you take it from there.</p><aside>~ November 2022 - Late 2023</aside><p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/CH1.png" alt="CH1"></p><p>This was ChatGPT when it first came out. Brilliant. Limited.</p><ul><li>Just a chat interface</li><li>No tools</li><li>No knowledge of you whatsoever</li><li>Mindblowing compared to not having anything, but definitely early days</li></ul><aside>~ 2023 - 2024</aside><p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/CH2.png" alt="CH2"></p><p>The beginning of some tools and some rudimentary memory features.</p><ul><li>Still just chat</li><li>Tools are still basic</li><li>Limited knowledge of you and your goals</li></ul><aside>~ 2024</aside><p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/CH3.png" alt="CH3"></p><p>Chatbots' final form before moving into Agents.</p><ul><li>Tooling far more advanced now</li><li>Additional context and memory features</li><li>Agents are still mostly experimental</li></ul><h2 id="breaking-these-across-6-dimensions" tabindex="-1">Breaking these across 6 dimensions </h2><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/paimm-6-dimensions.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/paimm-6-dimensions.png" alt="AI Capability Spectrums"></a></p><p>Another way to break down the transitions is as a set of spectrums across 6 dimensions.</p><ul><li><strong>Context</strong> [ None -&gt; Basic Knowledge -&gt; Deep Knowledge -&gt; Understanding of Purpose/Goals ]</li><li><strong>Personality</strong> [ None -&gt; Basic Personality -&gt; Persistent Personality/Preferences -&gt; Human-like ]</li><li><strong>Tool Use</strong> [ None -&gt; Basic Tools -&gt; Advanced Computer Use -&gt; Platform Fluency ]</li><li><strong>Awareness</strong> [ None -&gt; Voice Receive -&gt; Computer Visibility -&gt; Persistent Personal Voice/Sight ]</li><li><strong>Proactivity</strong> [ None -&gt; Scheduled Tasks/Triggers -&gt; State Monitoring and Execution -&gt; Continuous Advocacy ]</li><li><strong>Multitask Scale</strong> [ Self -&gt; Dozens of Tool Calls/Second -&gt; Dozens of Agents Simultaneously -&gt; Hundreds/Thousands Simultaneously ]</li></ul><h2 id="tier-2-agents" tabindex="-1">TIER 2 - AGENTS </h2><aside>I did multiple presentations on Agents in 2023, but they were mostly fringe and unwieldy. I'd say the overall Agent Era started for real ~ Late 2024 / Early 2025.</aside><p>Instead of us asking questions and getting answers from Chatbots, we assign work to Agents that can autonomously do work on our behalf.</p><p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/AG1.png" alt="AG1"></p><p>The initial transition from Chatbots to Agents as the main way of thinking about and using AI.</p><ul><li>Standalone agents via LangGraph, other frameworks</li><li>Early 2025 acceleration</li><li>Claude Code and n8n makes them mainstream</li><li>Claude Code best/most-used example, but CLI-based</li><li>Agents are mostly ephemeral (n8n/similar being the exception)</li><li>Early voice interaction stuff</li><li>Experimental computer usage</li></ul><aside>~ March 2025 - Current. NOTE: Significant Claude Code bias in my analysis here.</aside><p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/AG2.png" alt="AG2"></p><p>Agents start to become the main mental model for AI work.</p><ul><li>Agents become a lot more controllable and deterministic due to scaffolding of Agentic Systems like Claude Code</li><li>Background and scheduled agents starting to materialize</li><li>Voice accelerates as a usage pattern</li><li>Early signs of universal accessibility, e.g., you can interact with your agents via a mobile app or OS assistant</li><li>Computer usage gets more serious but still isn't mainstream</li></ul><aside>~ Early/Mid 2026 - Late 2026 / Early 2027?</aside><p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/AG3.png" alt="AG3"></p><blockquote><p>Predictions are hard, especially about the future.<cite>Niels Bohr</cite></p></blockquote><p>The final form of Agents vs. Assistants, with the main distinction being personality and trust.</p><aside>There will be initial signs of proactivity in AG2 and AG3.</aside><ul><li>Agents are now both interactive but also running continuously in the background, both locally and in the cloud</li><li>Agents easily manageable from mobile / device when not on main system / traveling / etc.</li><li>Extensive use of voice as the interface</li><li>Computer usage becomes viable and adoption begins</li><li>Very advanced and steerable, but still mostly reactive vs. proactive</li></ul><aside>~ Starting Late 2026 - Early 2027</aside><h2 id="tier-3-assistants" tabindex="-1">TIER 3 - ASSISTANTS </h2><p>Instead of random Agents performing work, we have a named, trusted Assistant that works with us to further our goals.</p><p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/AS1.png" alt="AS1"></p><p>The transition begins from focus on Agents to the concept of an Assistant that <em>uses Agents in the background</em> to proactively pursue your goals.</p><aside>Many of these will likely unfold at the same time, and it's quite hard to call their exact phase.</aside><ul><li>The first transition to <em>Proactive vs. Reactive</em></li><li>Assistants start to have initial personalities, both through the providers and also through custom scaffolding (persistent personality context)</li><li>Assistants start to become our primary interface to AI</li><li>Agents start to become less important, background elements that are working to do the bidding of your Assistant</li></ul><aside>See my <a href="https://github.com/danielmiessler/telos">TELOS project</a> for more on this, and how I <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-predictable-path-7-components-2024">integrate it</a> with my DA, Kai.</aside><ul><li>Your Assistant's context about you starts including things like your Goals, Challenges, Metrics, Projects, and other aspects of your life</li><li>Voice overtakes typing as primary interface</li><li>Your Assistant can now (significantly) see and hear what's happening on your computer</li><li>Early signs of personal cameras and microphones that you can wear on your body (not just while on your computer) to give your Assistant awareness of your environment, i.e., seeing and hearing what you're seeing and hearing, watching behind you and around you, etc.</li></ul><aside>Early to Mid ~2027?</aside><p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/AS2.png" alt="AS2"></p><p>Assistant personalities start to crystallize and they begin performing as <em>Proactive Advocates</em> of us and our goals, vs. reactive helpers.</p><ul><li>Initial unification of all inputs into a cohesive picture that your Assistant can see and understand</li></ul><aside>Agents become like processes on a computer wielded by a Program. We only see the Program.</aside><ul><li>Full Agent orchestration, including spin up and spin down, custom task assignment, etc., all happening transparently in the background without your knowledge</li></ul><!----><ul><li><p>The introduction of your Assistant understanding what you're trying to do in life, your goals, the metrics that matter to you, etc., which will allow him/her/it to start thinking proactively on how to help you accomplish them</p></li><li><p>We start to see the concept of <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-state-management">Managing State</a>, i.e., your Assistant takes periodic inventory of all inputs and assesses <em>Current State</em> relative to <em>Desired State</em>, in order to plan actions to move towards Desired State</p></li><li><p>Nearly full usage of any of your computing environments / interfaces</p></li></ul><aside>Mid 2028 to Early 2030? Extremely hard to say given how many technologies are involved.</aside><p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/AS3.png" alt="AS3"></p><p>AS3 is the final level of this maturity model. This is the Digital Assistant I described in 2016, and that has been partially depicted in various ways in sci-fi books and films for decades.</p><aside>My favorite fictional renditions are TARS from Interstellar, and Her.</aside><p>There are many more features that can be added as you go further in time and tech, but I'm thinking really only up to 5-10 years from now. Even beyond 5 years is nutty given how fast things are changing.</p><p>Here are the main characteristics of AS3.</p><!----><aside>Think about how attached people got to gpt-4o Chatbots. Now multiply that by a jillion.</aside><ul><li><p><strong>Trusted Companion</strong>â€”AS3-level Assistants feel more like trusted companions, partners, protectors, friends, and confidants than technology, managing both life and work while becoming (for both better and worse) many people's closest relationships</p></li><li><p><strong>Loved Ones Monitoring</strong>â€”Continuous monitoring of everyone you care about who can't monitor themselves (children, elderly parents, those with special needs), watching all cameras, security systems, and logs 24/7 for signs of danger or distress</p></li><li><p><strong>Continuous Advocate</strong>â€”Works continuously, without rest, as an Advocate. Constantly scanning the world for opportunities, threats, better deals, useful information, and ways to optimize your life according to your goals</p></li><li><p><strong>Building Partner</strong>â€”When you sit down at a computer, your Assistant has full access to everything the computer can do, can see all your screens, can hear everything. You can speak, type, and gesture, and have your DA do most of the work using all the power of the connected systems</p></li></ul><aside>This requires <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-real-internet-of-things#universal-daemonization">more things having an API</a>, which is another component of <a
