href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-real-internet-of-things">TRIOT</a>.</aside><ul><li><p><strong>Environmental Customization</strong>â€”Automatically adjusts environments you enter into such as lighting, temperature, music, displays, and ambient settings in any space you enter based on preferences, current mood, time of day, and what you're trying to accomplish</p></li><li><p><strong>Enhanced Perception</strong>â€”Will grant superhuman-like senses through available feeds: seeing through walls via building cameras, hearing specific conversations across rooms by filtering audio, accessing available mics, zooming into distant objects, accessing thermal vision and other sensory augmentation</p></li></ul><aside>This will be most enabled/magnified by <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-real-internet-of-things#augmented-reality">AR interfaces</a>, which is another component of TRIOT.</aside><ul><li><p><strong>Active Protection</strong>â€”Rewrites abusive messages before you see them, summarizes manipulative communications to extract what's really being asked, removes extremist propaganda from feeds, fact-checks claims in real-time during conversations, performs live character analysis on people you're interacting with</p></li><li><p><strong>Universal Authentication</strong>â€”Handles all authentication continuously through multi-factor streams (voice, gait, location, behavior patterns), automatically enrolling new devices into your ecosystem with proper security settings and managing permissions across all connected systems</p></li><li><p><strong>Deep Understanding</strong>â€”Deep understanding of your full context and history as a person: your upbringing, your relationship with your parents and family, your education, your traumas (optional, of course), your journal, your goals, your aspirations, etc. All in service of better helping you become who you are trying to become</p></li></ul><h1 id="some-vignettes" tabindex="-1">Some vignettes </h1><p>Sometimes the best way to tell is to show, so here are some examples of what it'll be like to use an AS3 level Assistant.</p><h2 id="protecting-you-and-your-loved-ones" tabindex="-1">Protecting You and Your Loved Ones </h2><ul><li><p><strong>Your mom hasn't moved from her bedroom in three hours past wake time</strong>â€”Your DA calls her, alerts the neighbor and emergency services with her location and medical history</p></li><li><p><strong>Walking at night, your DA monitors 47 nearby cameras and notices concerning behavior ahead</strong>â€”"Take the next right, safer route, you'll still make it on time"</p></li></ul><h2 id="outsourcing-research" tabindex="-1">Outsourcing Research </h2><ul><li><strong>You mention wanting a new couch</strong>â€”Six minutes later your DA interrupts: "Found the perfect one, your roommate loves it, on sale tomorrow at 4am for $1,100 less." You: "Order it." Your DA: "Done. Delivery Thursday between 2-4pm, I've already cleared your calendar."</li></ul><h2 id="detecting-and-filtering-influence-campaigns" tabindex="-1">Detecting and Filtering Influence Campaigns </h2><ul><li><strong>Propaganda campaign targeting your 16-year-old son, and marketing campaign trying to get you to dislike a certain product</strong>â€”Your DA: "Heads up, there's a coordinated propaganda campaign targeting teens in your area. I've been filtering it from your son's feeds. Also detected astroturfing trying to tank Brand X's reputation. Want the analysis or just the cleaned feed?" You: "Just keep it clean." Your DA: "Done."</li></ul><h2 id="freelance-work" tabindex="-1">Freelance Work </h2><ul><li><strong>You do bug bounties on the side, and a new program just opened</strong>â€”While you're eating dinner, Kai messages you: "New program just launched. I'm doing recon right now and already found something juicy. Just submitted the report. Team's response time is fast, so you might hear back before dessert."</li></ul><h2 id="monitoring-mental-state-and-energy" tabindex="-1">Monitoring Mental State and Energy </h2><ul><li><strong>You've been doing negative self-talk for the past hour, energy levels dropping</strong>â€”Your DA notices the pattern and adjusts your lighting to warmer tones, starts playing your "getting unstuck" playlist. Then: "Hey, I've noticed you're being pretty hard on yourself today. You've actually shipped three major features this week. Want to take a walk? I can reschedule your 3pm."</li></ul><h2 id="routine-management-and-optimization" tabindex="-1">Routine Management and Optimization </h2><ul><li><strong>Your entire morning needs coordinating</strong>â€”Optimal wake time, coffee started, news queued, 9am meeting moved to 10am, vitamins ordered, bills paid, 127 spam emails dismissed. Fifteen minutes in, zero decisions made</li></ul><h2 id="business-context" tabindex="-1">Business Context </h2><h3 id="career-growth-employee" tabindex="-1">Career Growth (Employee) </h3><ul><li><p><strong>You're heads-down on a project that isn't connected to your promotion goals</strong>â€”Your DA: "Hey, you've spent 23 hours this week on the legacy migration, but it's not on your promo doc and your manager doesn't know about it. Meanwhile, the API redesignâ€”your Q1 key resultâ€”hasn't moved in two weeks. Want me to draft a quick status update for Sarah showing the migration work, or should we carve out time tomorrow for the API project?"</p></li><li><p><strong>Quarterly business review coming up</strong>â€”Your DA has been monitoring company OKRs, your team's goals, and your personal deliverables. "Your presentation is 80% done, but I noticed you're missing the cost-savings analysis leadership cares about. I pulled the numbers from last quarter and drafted a slide. Also, the CEO mentioned supply chain resilience three times in last week's all-handsâ€”I added a section connecting your project to that theme. Review when you're ready."</p></li></ul><h3 id="team-leadership-manager" tabindex="-1">Team Leadership (Manager) </h3><ul><li><p><strong>You manage a team of eight and need to stay on top of everything</strong>â€”Your DA monitors each team member's projects, blockers, and career goals. "Quick heads up: Marcus has been blocked on the vendor API for four days and hasn't escalated. Jenny's utilization is at 120% while Tom's at 60%â€”might want to rebalance. Also, your 1:1 with Priya is in an hour and she's been researching internal transfer policies. Might be worth asking how she's feeling about growth opportunities."</p></li><li><p><strong>Budget planning season</strong>â€”Your DA: "I've mapped all current projects against your Q2 budget allocation. You're 15% over on contractor spend but 20% under on tooling. Three projects have no clear tie to the department's OKRsâ€”want me to flag those for the prioritization meeting? Also, here's a one-pager showing how your team's work ladders up to the VP's top three priorities. Useful ammunition."</p></li></ul><h3 id="executive-company-leadership" tabindex="-1">Executive (Company Leadership) </h3><ul><li><p><strong>You're a VP responsible for a business unit</strong>â€”Your DA maintains continuous awareness of company goals, team performance, project status, budget utilization, and competitive threats. "Board meeting prep: Revenue is tracking 8% ahead, but two key hires fell through and the roadmap is slipping. Your biggest risk right now is the enterprise deal with Acmeâ€”their CISO raised concerns about our SOC 2 timeline. I've drafted talking points and a mitigation plan. Also, competitor X just announced a feature that undercuts our Q3 launch. Want the analysis now or after your 10am?"</p></li><li><p><strong>Strategic planning and threat modeling</strong>â€”Your DA: "Based on current spend patterns, market signals, and the threat model we built last quarter, here's what I'm seeing: 60% of security budget is on perimeter defense but our actual incidents are coming from supply chain and insider threats. I've drafted a reallocation proposal. Also, three of your five strategic initiatives are competing for the same engineering resourcesâ€”someone's going to lose. Want me to model the trade-offs before Thursday's leadership sync?"</p></li></ul><h2 id="tactical-vs-strategic-goal-monitoring" tabindex="-1">Tactical vs. Strategic Goal Monitoring </h2><ul><li><strong>Quarterly review time</strong>â€”Your DA: "We shipped 47 features and closed $280K in consulting this quarter, but we're off track on your 2026 goals. Your Q1 target was launching the AI Security Fundamentals course and signing three enterprise partnerships. We're at 12% course completion and zero partnerships. Here's the fix: transition Acme Corp and TechStart to Ryan as referrals, block Tuesdays/Thursdays for course recording starting next week, and I'll schedule intro calls with the four target companies from your January strategy docâ€”Microsoft, Google, Anthropic, and OpenAI."</li></ul><p>The power of AS3-level Assistants comes from their combination of continuous awareness, proactive action, and deep understanding of your goals and context.</p><p>This shifts the relationship from tool to partnerâ€”one that actively works to make you safer, healthier, more focused on what matters, and more effective at becoming who you want to be.</p><h1 id="summary" tabindex="-1">Summary </h1><ol><li>There's nothing wrong with the various companies and builders stumbling randomly towards something that ends up looking like Digital Assistants. That's fun too. I just prefer knowingâ€”at least roughlyâ€”where it's heading and where we are along the path.</li><li>The idea that such a thing <em>is predictable at all</em> is based on my belief/conjecture that tech (and the future more generally) are <em>not</em> predictable, but <em>human desires are</em>. And that they're mostly stable over time. So if we know people consistently want more safety, more connection, more capability, etc...you can stochastically anticipate this is what will get built and selected for. From there I start with what an ultimate form of that might look like, and work backwards.</li><li>Combining that with how modern AI has progressed since late 2022, I see the rough evolution of personal AI as: <em>Chatbots -&gt; Agents -&gt; Assistants</em>. Chatbots are basic call-and-response, leaving all the work to the user. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/raid-ai-definitions#agents">Agents</a> are autonomous workers who can do tasks on their own. And Assistants are what we're actually building towards, i.e., competent companions that make us safer, healthier, happier, etc., and generally more effective at whatever we're trying to do in life and work.</li><li>This model basically argues that 1) it is actually <em>possible</em> to know (roughly) where we are going, and that 2) it's actually <em>useful</em> to know this because it serves the purpose of <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sensemaking" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Sensemaking</a>. It gives order to the seemingly random, noisy tech developments along the way. And most importantly, for builders like us, it provides focus and direction on what to create next, and why.</li></ol><p>I hope it's useful to you.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>I am 85% happy with Version 1.0 of this model, but there will most definitely be features/dimensions I will want to fix/add in the near future. I expect a v2.0 before July 2026, and maybe smaller releases before that. Please <a href="mailto:daniel@danielmiessler.com?Subject=The Personal AI Maturity Model">reach out</a> with any comments of what you think I missed or can improve on. If I include them, I'll credit you here in the notes.</li><li>Another thing to mention which I talked about in my <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-predictable-path-7-components-2024">Predictable Path AI post</a> is that all this capability is guaranteed to come with a wide range of downsides. There are too many to cover here, and I talk about them in the other post, but anything that's this powerful will be abused. First of all, a compromise of the system that controls this for someone will be catastrophic. They can be manipulated through it. Their friend/partner/DA can be deleted, altered, or erased in the way that Alzheimer's or death does. Then there are the possibilities of paying poorer people to install shims into their DAs that allow or push certain narratives and not others. And then there's the prospect of the government mandating access to the primary DA providers to monitor, inject, and otherwise control what's being seen by its principal. All this stuff is very possible and could happen. I focus on the positive because that's what I'm trying to build. But you can't be in security for nearly 30 years and not know that it can also go down this path.</li><li>One aspect I've talked about elsewhere is that you may need <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-predictable-path-7-components-2024#_7-we-ll-have-additional-role-based-das-performing-specific-tasks-it-ll-be-a-collective-of-ais-managed-by-your-primary-da">multiple DA roles</a> because it will be weird to have your "best buddy" be your coach and your therapist and your doctor and your flirty partner at the same time, so those will likely be different DA personas, possibly/likely with your main DA as primary.</li><li>I have a whole sister model to this for Corporate vs. Personal AI, which I've talked a bunch about in some of the posts linked below.</li><li>Additional Reading: "<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-real-internet-of-things">The Real Internet of Things</a>." 2016. The original conception of Digital Assistants as the endpoint of personal AI.</li><li>Additional Reading: "<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-predictable-path-7-components-2024">AI's Predictable Path: 7 Components</a>." 2023. The architectural framework underlying modern AI systems and how TELOS and Kai fit into the complete trajectory of AI development.</li><li>Additional Reading: "<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ais-next-big-thing-is-digital-assistants">AI's Next Big Thing is Digital Assistants</a>." 2023. More on the endpoint of this progression.</li><li>Additional Reading: "<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-agents-api-calling-prompt-injection">AI Agents, API Calling, and Prompt Injection</a>." 2023. Security considerations as we build these systems.</li><li>Additional Reading: "<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/spqa-ai-architecture-replace-existing-software">SPQA: AI Architecture Replacing Software</a>." 2023. The underlying architecture pattern enabling this evolution.</li><li>Additional Reading: "<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/raid-ai-definitions">RAID (Real World AI Definitions)</a>." 2024. Practical definitions of AI terms including Agents, AGI, ASI, and other key concepts.</li><li>Additional Reading: "<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/personal-ais-will-mediate-everything">Personal AIs Will Mediate Everything</a>." 2024. Why personal AI assistants will become our primary interfaces to the digital world.</li><li>Additional Reading: "<a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-state-management">AI's Ultimate Use Case: Current to Desired State</a>." 2025. More on the state management concept referenced in AS2.</li><li>Credits: Thanks to <a href="https://x.com/Jhaddix">Jason Haddix</a> and <a href="https://linkedin.com/in/sasazdjelar">SaÅ¡a Zdjelar</a> most of all for their constant support and feedback when I was writing the book in 2016, and for being an endless stream of ideas, discussion, and help up through today on these topics. And also to <a href="https://www.linkedin.com/in/clintgibler/">Clint Gibler</a> for always being up for frequent phone calls and long walk discussions about this stuff. And to <a href="https://x.com/mattjay">Matt Johansen</a> for a constant source of mutual ideas and encouragement.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[We Need a New Type of Cybersecurity Product]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/new-type-cybersecurity-product?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/new-type-cybersecurity-product</guid>
            <pubDate>Fri, 12 Dec 2025 17:14:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[I think we in Cybersecurity have largely failed to show its value to the busines over the last decades. And I think we need a completely new type of product to address it. And before you get excited about a pitch or a product launch, I'm not interested in creating this product outside of custom imp...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p>I think we in Cybersecurity have largely failed to show its value to the busines over the last decades. And I think we need a completely new type of product to address it.</p><p>And before you get excited about a pitch or a product launch, I'm not interested in creating this product outside of custom implementations for my consulting customers. My reason for writing this is that <strong>I want other companies to make this</strong>. And more broadly, to pivot to this approach going forward.</p><p>But first, what's the actual issue?</p><aside>I know many exceptions to this, Where the pitch is excellent and directed at the right people, but it's the rare exception.</aside><p>Our problem is communication. I think we are communicating the wrong things to the wrong people inside the organization. Not always, but generally.</p><p><strong>Instead of providing an interface to the busines that makes them feel safe, and assures them that their money has been well-spent, we're chaotically documenting <em>the activities</em> we're doing for them.</strong></p><p>If I had to capture it in a single push it would be something like:</p><p>Cybersecurity Program Products should communicate safety. The word "Security" itself is actually Latin for seâ€”"without", and "cura"â€”worry. <em>Without worry</em>. It's literally the thing we're supposed to be providing.</p><p>CPP products should provide an interface that, upon viewing them, the viewer will experience a sense of calm about the state of their customers' and business's data and infrastructure.</p><p>The products can't outright <em>say</em> this. They have to show it through a combination of narrative and evidence presentation that invokes the feeling within the viewer.</p><h2 id="some-ideas-around-components" tabindex="-1">Some ideas around components </h2><aside>Some of these components exist already and others need to be created.</aside><ul><li>A focus on presenting the right level of narrative and data, at the right time, to the right people. AI (<em>deep sigh</em>) can of course help extensively with dynamic, audience-targeted narrative and data presentation</li><li>An always-updated top-down narrative of the Security Program's goals, metrics, challenges, strategies, budget, team, projects, and timelines. E.g.:</li></ul><blockquote><p>We are currently pursuing <em>these</em> goals, which we track by <em>these</em> metrics, and we're facing these challenges, which we're overcoming with these strategies, which is why we're doing these projects, which is how we're spending this budget, using this team, all of which is yielding these results...</p></blockquote><ul><li>Clear articulation of what attackers are currently trying to do, which they can't because we're doing X and Y (dynamically updated with (<em>another sigh</em>) AI of course)</li><li>Clear articulation of what it would likely cost if those attackers were successful (dynamically updated with data / evidence from news / industry)</li><li>Primary use of short, clean narrative describing our state, what attackers are currently doing, and how we're proactively and actively countering those efforts</li><li>Clear linking of how we're spending our money to the programs that are providing that proactive security.</li></ul></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Anthropic's Vision Advantage is a Lot Like Apple's from the 2010s]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/anthropics-vision-advantage?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/anthropics-vision-advantage</guid>
            <pubDate>Thu, 11 Dec 2025 12:43:21 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Anthropic's vision advantage/images/anthropic-vision-advantage.webp/images/anthropic-vision-advantage.webp OpenAI and Google have more than a model problem when competing with Anthropic. Anthropic right now feels like Apple in the 2010s with the iPhone. And Opus 4.5 is like their iPhone. The tri...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/anthropic-vision-advantage.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/anthropic-vision-advantage.webp" alt="Anthropic's vision advantage" width="652" height="652" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><p>OpenAI and Google have more than a model problem when competing with Anthropic.</p><p>Anthropic right now feels like Apple in the 2010s with the iPhone. And Opus 4.5 is like their iPhone.</p><p>The trick is that it's not really about the model, or the phone. It's the ecosystem.</p><p>Apple dominated not just because they had better hardware, but because every part of the ecosystem worked seamlessly across different hardware offerings.</p><p>Claude Code is kind of like the Apple Ecosystem from the 2010s.</p><p>Companies think they're competing with Opus, but they're actually competing with Claude Code's vastly superior user empathy, context management, and a deep understanding of the goal of this AI tech.</p><p>Again, very similar to Apple. Apple created a creative, artistic ecosystem where the tech got out of the way and let people make and work.</p><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/anthropic-vision-iceberg.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/anthropic-vision-iceberg.webp" alt="The ecosystem iceberg" width="652" height="652" loading="lazy"></a></p><p>Anthropic and the Claude Code team are figuring out what work people are actually trying to do, and then working backwards from that.</p><p>It's vision-led. And rumors are that Dario is a lot like Jobs as well. Religiously passionate about the mission, and a steamroller when it comes to pursuing it.</p><p>So as everyone is thinking about what model OpenAI is about to release, don't fall into that trap. It's not the models.</p><p>It's the alignment of what people actually do in their lives with a cohesive ecosystem that helps them do it.</p><p>For Anthropic that's embodied by Claude Code right now, and I've yet to see any vision that comes close.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>A smart reader pointed out that they're both all-in on trust as well. They both put a ton of effort into not only trying to do the right thing, but making sure people know that they are. My personal theory is that this caution around Siri Prompt Injection risk is why Apple's been so slow to move on AI that takes advantage of their ecosystem.</li><li>AIL Level 2: Daniel wrote the content as always. I (Kai, his DA) helped with formatting and created the DNA helix and iceberg diagrams. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Learn more about AIL</a>.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[The Bubble Is Labor]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/real-bubble-is-human-labor?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/real-bubble-is-human-labor</guid>
            <pubDate>Mon, 08 Dec 2025 17:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[We've forgotten that companies have never wanted to hire anyone in the first place]]></description>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Thoughts on Doctorow's 'Reverse Centaurs' AI Talk]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/thoughts-on-doctorow-ai-essay?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/thoughts-on-doctorow-ai-essay</guid>
            <pubDate>Mon, 08 Dec 2025 00:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[A passionate figure shakes fist at indifferent corporate machinery/images/blog/doctorow-reverse-centaur/header.webp/images/blog/doctorow-reverse-centaur/header.webp This is another brilliantly written and highly misguided essay by Cory Doctorowhttps://pluralistic.net/2025/12/05/pop-that-bubble/u-w...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/doctorow-reverse-centaur/header.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/doctorow-reverse-centaur/header.webp" alt="A passionate figure shakes fist at indifferent corporate machinery" width="652" height="652" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><p>This is another brilliantly written and highly misguided <a href="https://pluralistic.net/2025/12/05/pop-that-bubble/#u-washington" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">essay by Cory Doctorow</a>. It demonstrates a complete lack of understanding of what AI actually is and where it's going. And provides the worst possible guidance as a result.</p><p>He refers to AI as "auto-complete", and thinks that any job replacement from AI will be reversed once the bubble pops and AI is revealed as incompetent.</p><blockquote><!--[--> all it does is calculate the most probable word to go next <cite>Cory Doctorow</cite><!--]--></blockquote><p>This is very much like Krugman saying in the 90s that nobody will use the internet.</p><p>It's funny because I cheered many times during the read because he obviously cares about humans, which is my main concern as well. But this is why it's important to understand the fundamentals of what you're discussing beyond a surface level.</p><p>People arguing from emotion, and then influencing others, without understanding the reality on the ground cause a lot of harm. This essay will cause a lot of harm, as many others have, and many AI haters with large followings have as well.</p><p>And to be clear, I don't consider people who just talk about caution with AI to be haters. I tell people to be cautious about it as well. Anybody who is logical and cares about people is urging caution. With what you give to AI, with how you use it, with not becoming over-dependent on it, etc. I could fill a book with reasons to be cautious when using AI.</p><p>That's not what I'm talking about with AI hate. AI haters are those who have an underlying thread throughout their comments which is communicating one simple thing: "It's a scam. It will blow over. And there is no point in thinking deeply about it or how it will affect your life because soon it will be as important as NFTs or crypto. Or the .com bubble."</p><blockquote><!--[--> when crypto dies, what it will leave behind is bad Austrian economics and worse monkey JPEGs. <cite>Cory Doctorow</cite><!--]--></blockquote><p>Basically, they are telling their followers, "Don't worry about it. You don't need to get ready. You do not need to learn this as some fundamental new skill." It really is like telling people in the 1980s and 90s: "Don't worry about computers, they will blow over." Except many times worse.</p><p>Everyone I know who has this position actually cares about people. So they're not being immoral by doing this. They are wrong about what AI is, what it's capable of, and where it's heading, and because of that, they're giving out advice that is harmful. It's as simple as that.</p><h2 id="the-reverse-centaur" tabindex="-1">The Reverse Centaur </h2><p>I will say that I loved Doctorow's "Reverse Centaur" analogy. I think it is phenomenal and I think it captures one of the main concerns I have about how AI will affect humans and their work. Or, more accurately, it's describing a mechanism through which that will happen.</p><p>The concept contrasts two arrangements:</p><ul><li><strong>Centaur</strong>: A human head directing a machine bodyâ€”you benefit from automation</li><li><strong>Reverse Centaur</strong>: A machine head on a human bodyâ€”the machine directs you, extracting your labor</li></ul><p>Amazon delivery people used to be people who drove a car. Then they'll become people who drive a mostly automated car. As it gets more advanced, there will be an automated car that uses people just a little bit. And then eventually it'll just be an automated car driven by a robot. No people required.</p><blockquote><!--[--> an Amazon delivery driver, who sits in a cabin surrounded by AI cameras, that monitor the driver's eyes and take points off if the driver looks in a proscribed direction <cite>Cory Doctorow</cite><!--]--></blockquote><p>So basically three-quarters through this arc, you have AI that needs humans just a little bit. And I also loved his related point where the human gets blamed for mistakes and that's the little bit part where they're involved.</p><blockquote><!--[--> The radiologist's job isn't really to oversee the AI's work, it's to take the blame for the AI's mistakes. <cite>Cory Doctorow</cite><!--]--></blockquote><p>Definitely a great point, and quite nasty.</p><h2 id="the-real-problem" tabindex="-1">The Real Problem </h2><p>It's his conclusions, which again are based on his weak understanding of where AI is now, what it's capable of, and the path that it seems to be on, that's the problem.</p><p>He sees this pivot to AI and the firing of workers as this horrible mistake that's going to disrupt lives and cause untold problems in the labor force. And that when it finally corrects itself, when the AI bubble bursts, it will be very hard to hire the people back.</p><p>He imagines the aftermath will look like this:</p><blockquote><!--[--> We'll have a bunch of coders who are really good at applied statistics. We'll have a lot of cheap GPUs...And we'll have the open source models that run on commodity hardware <cite>Cory Doctorow</cite><!--]--></blockquote><p>His argument is that corporations are making a bet on AI that will fail, and they're selling it to investors with pitches like:</p><blockquote><!--[--> you fire 9/10s of your radiologists, saving $20m/year, you give us $10m/year, and you net $10m/year <cite>Cory Doctorow</cite><!--]--></blockquote><p>That's not the problem. The problem is the jobs are going away permanently. Most jobs, especially after robots enter the picture. I really wish he would use his incredible intellect and creativity to focus on that reality instead of the fantasy that AI will just blow over as a scam.</p><p>The outcomes are completely different, and far more importantly, the advice to give people now is far different.</p><h2 id="the-wrong-fight" tabindex="-1">The Wrong Fight </h2><p>We shouldn't be telling people to fight against the AI narrative and the AI hype and the corporations who are trying to use AI to get rid of jobs. It's fucking ridiculous.</p><p>What do you think a fucking corporation is? Its only purpose is to make money using as few resources as possible. Do you realize how many more people they would have to hire if they didn't use computers? There would be millions more jobs if computers didn't exist. The moment that they can accomplish their goals spending zero resources, that is exactly what a corporation is supposed to do.</p><p>This mistake of well-meaning people, like Doctorow, is adding morality to this. It is not the job of people who make things to hire others to help them. The hiring of other people to help them is a thing that happens when they have no choice. AI is now offering that choice. That is the way to look at this.</p><p>The question for us is not, "How can we stop this?" "How can we stop these corporate tycoons who are ruining everything?" That's unbelievably child-like thinking.</p><h2 id="the-right-questions" tabindex="-1">The Right Questions </h2><p>Questions we should be asking are things like:</p><ol><li>How can we get better at being human?</li><li>What should society look like when humans don't have to work?</li><li>What are the skills and capabilities that matter to us as individuals? How can we become stronger at them?</li><li>What does the economy look like when the classic Capital/Labor balance no longer exists because labor can now be done by capital?</li><li>What does a new economy look like that is human-based? Based on each person not being a corporate worker, but instead being a human individual offering insights, nurturing, perspective, judgment, art, and creativity?</li><li>What does it look like to exchange value using those things as currency instead of a fiat system given to us by capital that we then spend to buy things that we laborers create?</li></ol><p>These are extremely hard questions that I can barely even start to think about. It's completely uncharted. Net new. Frightening and wonderful.</p><p>It would be nice to have people like Doctorow and Gary Marcus and others to help think about these questions instead of trying to fucking put their hand up and stop computers and the internet. You're wasting your breath and hurting people in the process. Please switch your attention to helping people get ready for a post-corporate-worker and post-labor reality.</p></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[I Built Two Claude Code Features a Week Before Anthropic Released Them]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/i-built-two-claude-code-features-before-anthropic-released-them?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/i-built-two-claude-code-features-before-anthropic-released-them</guid>
            <pubDate>Sun, 07 Dec 2025 08:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Two Claude Code Features I Built Before Anthropic/images/blog/claude-code-features-before-anthropic/header-combined.webp/images/blog/claude-code-features-before-anthropic/header-combined.webp I'm not the type who brags, but I have to brag about this. I guess it's not really bragging. It's more lik...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/claude-code-features-before-anthropic/header-combined.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/claude-code-features-before-anthropic/header-combined.webp" alt="Two Claude Code Features I Built Before Anthropic"></a></p><p>I'm not the type who brags, but I have to brag about this.</p><p>I guess it's not really bragging. It's more like validation.</p><p>Anyway.</p><p>I'm basically in love with Claude Code for multiple reasons, I have been for months. You know that by now. I've built a whole <a href="https://github.com/danielmiessler/PAI" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">open source project</a> around it and have constructed my entire AI ecosystem on top of it.</p><p>And now this thing has happened twice:</p><p><strong>Twice I've built a complete, full-featured system into Kai (my personal AI infrastructure), and then less than a week later, Anthropic actually released the same functionality into Claude Code itself.</strong></p><h2 id="the-two-features" tabindex="-1">The Two Features </h2><hr><h2 id="feature-1-universal-file-based-context-ufc" tabindex="-1">Feature 1: Universal File-based Context (UFC) </h2><p>Before Claude Code existed, I was already building what I called the <strong>Universal File-based Context</strong> system. Basically just using the file system to manage AI context and history.</p><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/claude-code-features-before-anthropic/ufc-system.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/claude-code-features-before-anthropic/ufc-system.webp" alt="UFC System Architecture"></a></p><h3 id="the-problem" tabindex="-1">The Problem </h3><p>Every AI conversation was ephemeral. Context disappeared between sessions. I'd have the same conversations over and over because the AI had no memory of what we'd built together.</p><h3 id="what-i-built" tabindex="-1">What I Built </h3><p>Universal file system-based context. It had three parts:</p><ol><li><strong><code>~/.claude/context/</code></strong> - The brain. System prompt, user preferences, active project state</li><li><strong><code>~/.claude/prompts/</code></strong> - Task-specific prompts that load based on what I'm doing</li><li><strong><code>~/.claude/history/</code></strong> - Automatic capture of sessions, learnings, research, and decisions</li></ol><p>Hooks would automatically inject the right context at session start and capture the good stuff at session end.</p><h3 id="what-anthropic-later-released" tabindex="-1">What Anthropic Later Released </h3><p>On October 16, 2025, Anthropic released <a href="https://claude.com/blog/skills" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Agent Skills</a>â€”a complete system for extending Claude with modular capability packages. But it wasn't just "here's a folder structure." They built a sophisticated architecture:</p><p><strong>Progressive Disclosure (3 Levels):</strong></p><ul><li><strong>Level 1 - Metadata:</strong> Skill name and description pre-loaded in the system prompt, so Claude knows what's available without loading everything</li><li><strong>Level 2 - Core Instructions:</strong> The full <code>SKILL.md</code> file loads only when Claude determines the skill applies to the current task</li><li><strong>Level 3+ - Nested Resources:</strong> Additional files load dynamically only as neededâ€”making context effectively unbounded</li></ul><p><strong>Dynamic Loading:</strong> Rather than loading all skill content upfront, Claude uses filesystem tools to request specific files by name. This is exactly the "just-in-time context" pattern I'd been building with hooksâ€”load context when relevant, not all at once.</p><p><strong>Code Execution Integration:</strong> Skills can bundle scripts (Python, Bash) that Claude executes as tools. Their engineering blog describes this as providing "deterministic reliability that only code can provide" for operations better suited to code than token generation.</p><p>The parallels to what I built:</p><ul><li>My <code>~/.claude/prompts/</code> task-specific loading â†’ Their progressive disclosure</li><li>My hooks injecting context at session start â†’ Their dynamic loading system</li><li>My <code>~/.claude/context/</code> directory â†’ Their <code>SKILL.md</code> architecture</li></ul><p>I spent all this time building something I thought would be useful. Then about a week later, turns out they'd been working on the same thing and released a better, more native version of it. Made me so happy.</p><hr><h2 id="feature-2-dynamic-skill-loading" tabindex="-1">Feature 2: Dynamic Skill Loading </h2><p>Then just a few days ago, Anthropic released a blog called <a href="https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/advanced-tool-use" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Advanced Tool Use</a>.</p><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/claude-code-features-before-anthropic/architecture.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/claude-code-features-before-anthropic/architecture.png" alt="Context-Aware Routing System"></a></p><p>I had <em>just</em> built something similar and taken it out of production because I thought it was overkill.</p><h3 id="what-i-built-1" tabindex="-1">What I Built </h3><p>I built a <strong>persistent state management system</strong> that maintained a state file updated after every action. The architecture worked like this:</p><ol><li><strong>State File:</strong> A dedicated file tracked the current session contextâ€”what we were working on, what the goal was, what had been accomplished</li><li><strong>Haiku Summarization:</strong> After each action, Haiku (fast and cheap) would quickly summarize the session state, so the system always had a compressed understanding of "what are we doing right now"</li><li><strong>User Prompt Submit Hook:</strong> On every user message, the hook would check the state file to understand the current contextâ€”so I could say "push this" and it would know exactly what "this" meant</li><li><strong>No Full Session Reads:</strong> Because Haiku maintained the summary, the system never had to re-read the entire conversation history to understand context</li></ol><p>The whole point was to keep Claude locked in on exactly what we were doing. Later in a Claude Code sessionâ€”especially after multiple compactionsâ€”even Claude can start to lose the plot. This system was designed to be an external memory that never forgot the task purpose.</p><p>I ended up only using it for a couple of days and then swapping it out because I thought it was excessive scaffoldingâ€”too heavy for the benefit. I figured a better model or better native scaffolding would eventually solve the problem without all that machinery.</p><h3 id="what-anthropic-published" tabindex="-1">What Anthropic Published </h3><p>Then they published <a href="https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/advanced-tool-use" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">this engineering blog</a> on Advanced Tool Use. The key pattern they describe is the <strong>Tool Search Tool</strong>â€”a meta-tool that lets Claude discover capabilities on-demand instead of loading everything upfront.</p><p>The specific technique: Tools are marked with <code>defer_loading: true</code> to make them discoverable on-demand. Claude initially sees only the Tool Search Tool itself plus a few high-priority tools. Everything else gets loaded only when needed.</p><p>I ran my upgrade skill against this blog and it said, "Hey, you should implement this pattern." So I didâ€”creating extremely abridged versions of skills at startup that save context, then dynamically loading full skill content only when Claude determines it's relevant. You can see the architecture in the diagram above.</p><p>The results from their testing:</p><blockquote><p>"Instead of loading all tool definitions upfront, the Tool Search Tool discovers tools on-demand. Claude only sees the tools it actually needs for the current task."</p></blockquote><p>And the token savings?</p><blockquote><p>"This represents an <strong>85% reduction in token usage</strong> while maintaining access to your full tool library."</p></blockquote><p>They also found that Opus 4.5's accuracy improved from 79.5% to 88.1% with this patternâ€”fewer tokens meant less confusion, not less capability.</p><hr><h2 id="what-this-all-means" tabindex="-1">What This All Means </h2><p>So basically in both cases I had an idea that I thought would be super useful for Claude Code, and I implemented it. Then a couple of days laterâ€”or in the case of UFC about a week laterâ€”it turns out the Claude Code team was building this the whole time and they release a better version.</p><p>On one hand you're like, "oh they made it better." But on the other hand I'm like, very happy that it validates that I'm thinking about this whole context game correctly.</p><p>I've been talking about this since 2023. The scaffolding of a system is going to be incredibly important. I wrote a whole post about <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-model-ecosystem-4-components">the 4 components of a good AI system</a> where I talked about context being so critical.</p><p>I feel like Anthropic gets this more than anyone, and especially the Claude Code team.</p><p>All this to really say that I just feel proud of myself for the fact that I seem to be thinking along the same lines as the Claude Code team, and in my own limited way I might even be a step ahead.</p><p>Sorry for the self-congratulations, but I just feel really excited about this.</p><p>See you in the next one.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>ðŸ¤– <strong>AIL Level 2:</strong> Daniel dictated this post. Kai added links, images, and formatting. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Learn more about AIL</a>.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Autonomous Cars or No? Stunning Data on Autonomous Car Safety Benefits]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/autonomous-cars-safety-data?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/autonomous-cars-safety-data</guid>
            <pubDate>Tue, 02 Dec 2025 20:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Dr. Jonathan Slotkin, a neurosurgeon and co-founder of Scrub Capital, published an excellent piece in the NYThttps://www.nytimes.com/2025/12/02/opinion/self-driving-cars.html today about autonomous car safety. DANIEL: Opening commentary here The human cost When that much energy enters a skull, n...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p>Dr. Jonathan Slotkin, a neurosurgeon and co-founder of Scrub Capital, published <a href="https://www.nytimes.com/2025/12/02/opinion/self-driving-cars.html" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">an excellent piece in the NYT</a> today about autonomous car safety.</p><p>[DANIEL: Opening commentary here]</p><h2 id="the-human-cost" tabindex="-1">The human cost </h2><blockquote>When that much energy enters a skull, no operation can turn it back.<cite>Dr. Jonathan Slotkin</cite></blockquote><p>[DANIEL: Comment on prevention vs. treatment]</p><h2 id="the-data" tabindex="-1">The data </h2><p>Waymo's safety performance data from 100 million driverless miles across 4 U.S. cities (through June 2025):</p><ul><li><strong>91% fewer serious-injury-or-worse crashes</strong> compared to human drivers on the same roads</li><li><strong>80% fewer injury-causing crashes</strong> compared to human drivers</li><li><strong>96% lower rate of injury-causing crashes at intersections</strong></li></ul><p>[DANIEL: React to these numbers]</p><h2 id="the-human-driving-baseline" tabindex="-1">The human driving baseline </h2><ul><li><strong>39,000 Americans</strong> died in motor vehicle crashes last year</li><li><strong>10,000 crash victims</strong> arrive at emergency rooms daily</li><li>Crashes are the <strong>#2 leading cause of death</strong> for children and young adults</li><li><strong>$1 trillion+ combined annual</strong> economic and quality-of-life toll from crashes</li></ul><p>[DANIEL: Comment on how we accept this as normal]</p><h2 id="the-adoption-threshold" tabindex="-1">The adoption threshold </h2><blockquote>We do not need everyone to use self-driving cars to realize profound safety gains.<cite>Dr. Jonathan Slotkin</cite></blockquote><p>If <strong>30% of cars were fully automated</strong>, could prevent approximately <strong>40% of crashes</strong> overall.</p><p>[DANIEL: Comment on partial adoption being enough]</p><h2 id="why-autonomous-vehicles-are-safer" tabindex="-1">Why autonomous vehicles are safer </h2><blockquote>A system that follows rules, avoids distraction, sees in all directions and prevents high-speed conflicts will avert deadly collisions much more often.<cite>Dr. Jonathan Slotkin</cite></blockquote><p>[DANIEL: Comment on the obvious logic here]</p><h2 id="the-documented-waymo-incidents" tabindex="-1">The documented Waymo incidents </h2><p>All serious incidents involving Waymo vehicles were caused by <strong>human drivers</strong>, not Waymo system failures:</p><ul><li>High-speed crash pushing another car into stopped Waymo vehicle</li><li>Red-light runner hitting Waymo and other vehicles</li><li>Motorcyclist hit by Waymo, then fatally struck by hit-and-run driver</li></ul><p>Minor incidents:</p><ul><li>Vehicle looped parking lot roundabout for five minutes</li><li>Software recall after hitting utility pole at low speed</li></ul><p>[DANIEL: Comment on the irony of humans causing the accidents]</p><h2 id="political-resistance" tabindex="-1">Political resistance </h2><p><strong>Washington, D.C.:</strong> Postponed key autonomous vehicle deployment report despite 18 months of successful testing.</p><p><strong>Boston:</strong> City Council considering mandatory "human safety operator" requirement in every autonomous vehicle.</p><p>[DANIEL: Comment on emotional vs. data-driven policy]</p><h2 id="the-accountability-difference" tabindex="-1">The accountability difference </h2><p>Autonomous vehicles must report <strong>ALL contacts</strong> causing injury or property damage over $1,000, while human drivers don't report the majority of accidents despite many involving injuries.</p><p>[DANIEL: Final thoughts on transparency and accountability]</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li><p>This post references Dr. Jonathan Slotkin's opinion piece "Don't Fear Self-Driving Cars. They Save Lives." published in The New York Times on December 2, 2025. <a href="https://www.nytimes.com/2025/12/02/opinion/self-driving-cars.html" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">NYT Opinion</a></p></li><li><p>AIL Level 2: Daniel wrote all commentary and analysis. I (Kai, his DA) helped with extracting quotes and structuring the draft. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Learn more about AIL</a></p></li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[How to Fix an Unbearably Slow iCloud Drive]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/fix-slow-icloud?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/fix-slow-icloud</guid>
            <pubDate>Tue, 02 Dec 2025 04:36:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[iCloud Drive Fix - Clearing the FileProvider database cache/images/fix-slow-icloud-diagram.png/images/fix-slow-icloud-diagram.png I was recently going insane trying to figure out why everything on my Mac was DeathGod slow. - Apps were taking forever to open - Finder was unresponsive - The whole ...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/fix-slow-icloud-diagram.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/fix-slow-icloud-diagram.png" alt="iCloud Drive Fix - Clearing the FileProvider database cache"></a></p><p>I was recently going insane trying to figure out why everything on my Mac was DeathGod slow.</p><ul><li>Apps were taking forever to open</li><li>Finder was unresponsive</li><li>The whole system felt like it was about to die</li></ul><p>I was actually in the process of moving things out of iCloud, which was taking literal days. And I was thinking I needed to replace my Mac altogether.</p><p>Then I did another 37 AI/google searches and found some hints. Here's what I ended up going with.</p><h1 id="the-problem-and-the-fix-that-worked-for-me" tabindex="-1">The problem and the fix that worked for me </h1><p>The problem is basically these giant cache files of contents on iCloud. They live in places like this:</p><aside>ðŸ“ Where * is some long series of characters.</aside><div class="language-bash line-numbers-mode"><button title="Copy Code" class="copy"></button><span class="lang">bash</span><pre class="shiki github-light vp-code" tabindex="0"><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">cd</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> ~/Library/Application</span><span style="color:#005CC5;">\ </span><span style="color:#032F62;">Support/FileProvider/</span><span style="color:#005CC5;">*</span><span style="color:#032F62;">/database</span></span></code></pre><div class="line-numbers-wrapper" aria-hidden="true"><span class="line-number">1</span><br></div></div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/icloud-fileprovider-directory.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/icloud-fileprovider-directory.png" alt="FileProvider directory showing UUID folders"></a></p>What mine looked like<h2 id="what-to-do" tabindex="-1">What to do </h2><!----><ol><li>Open Terminal and go to the FileProvider directory:</li></ol><div class="language-bash line-numbers-mode"><button title="Copy Code" class="copy"></button><span class="lang">bash</span><pre class="shiki github-light vp-code" tabindex="0"><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">cd</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> ~/Library/Application</span><span style="color:#005CC5;">\ </span><span style="color:#032F62;">Support/FileProvider/</span></span></code></pre><div class="line-numbers-wrapper" aria-hidden="true"><span class="line-number">1</span><br></div></div><ol start="2"><li>Open it in Finder so you can see what you're working with:</li></ol><div class="language-bash line-numbers-mode"><button title="Copy Code" class="copy"></button><span class="lang">bash</span><pre class="shiki github-light vp-code" tabindex="0"><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#6F42C1;">open</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> .</span></span></code></pre><div class="line-numbers-wrapper" aria-hidden="true"><span class="line-number">1</span><br></div></div><ol start="3"><li><p>Go into the folder with the long UUID string (like in the screenshot above)</p></li><li><p>Go into the <code>database</code> folder inside that</p></li><li><p>Delete everything inside the <code>database</code> folder:</p></li></ol><aside>ðŸ’¡ You can also just delete the files with Finder if you prefer.</aside><div class="language-bash line-numbers-mode"><button title="Copy Code" class="copy"></button><span class="lang">bash</span><pre class="shiki github-light vp-code" tabindex="0"><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#6F42C1;">rm</span><span style="color:#005CC5;"> -rf</span><span style="color:#005CC5;"> *</span></span></code></pre><div class="line-numbers-wrapper" aria-hidden="true"><span class="line-number">1</span><br></div></div><ol><li>Restart your Mac</li></ol><h1 id="what-to-expect" tabindex="-1">What to expect </h1><p>First of all, I had a 44 GB file in one of mine.</p><p><em>44 gigabytes</em>.</p><p>No wonder the system couldn't do anything. It couldn't read the cache file describing what was in the folder, let alone being able to do anything with it.</p><p>When you delete those files, it forces iCloud to regenerate its local database from scratch. I didn't lose any data, and things started working within just a couple of minutes.</p><p>Very soon after doing this and restarting, you should see your computer basically come back to life.</p><p>Hope it works for you!</p></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Is Prompt Injection a Vulnerability?]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/is-prompt-injection-a-vulnerability?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/is-prompt-injection-a-vulnerability</guid>
            <pubDate>Tue, 25 Nov 2025 23:16:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[The Same Vulnerability Pattern/images/is-prompt-injection-vulnerability-header.png I want to respond to my buddy Joseph Thacker's blog posthttps://josephthacker.com/ai/2025/11/24/prompt-injection-isnt-a-vulnerability.html about whether Prompt Injection is a vulnerability. I'm in the "Yes" camp. ...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/is-prompt-injection-vulnerability-header.png" alt="The Same Vulnerability Pattern"></p><p>I want to respond to my buddy Joseph Thacker's <a href="https://josephthacker.com/ai/2025/11/24/prompt-injection-isnt-a-vulnerability.html" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">blog post</a> about whether Prompt Injection is a vulnerability.</p><div class="blog-preview-card" data-v-7df9dc61=""><a href="https://josephthacker.com/ai/2025/11/24/prompt-injection-isnt-a-vulnerability.html" class="blog-preview-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" data-v-7df9dc61=""><div class="blog-preview-thumbnail-hero" data-v-7df9dc61=""><img src="https://josephthacker.com/assets/images/pi_not_a_vuln.jpeg" alt="Prompt Injection Isn't a Vulnerability (Most of the Time)" class="blog-preview-thumbnail" data-v-7df9dc61=""><div class="blog-preview-overlay" data-v-7df9dc61=""></div><div class="blog-preview-source" data-v-7df9dc61="">josephthacker.com</div></div><div class="blog-preview-content" style="background:rgba(245, 245, 248, 0.95);" data-v-7df9dc61=""><div class="blog-preview-content-left" data-v-7df9dc61=""><h3 class="blog-preview-title" data-v-7df9dc61="">Prompt Injection Isn't a Vulnerability (Most of the Time)</h3><p class="blog-preview-description" data-v-7df9dc61="">Prompt injection is almost never the root cause of AI vulnerabilitiesâ€”the actual issue is what the model is permitted to do</p></div><div class="blog-preview-content-right" data-v-7df9dc61=""><div class="blog-preview-date" data-v-7df9dc61="">Nov 23, 2025</div></div></div></a></div><p>I'm in the "Yes" camp. I think Prompt Injection <em>is</em> a vulnerability...<em>assuming you have it</em>.</p><p>First, I use this as my definition.</p><!----><p>And here the "system or component" could be an a model, an agent, an application, etc. I make this distinction because the consumption, parsing, and processing of inputs in any applicationâ€”and especially AI-based onesâ€”can be quite complex.</p><p>Joseph's argument says is basically:</p><blockquote><p>I think we need to change how we talk about prompt injection. A lot of security folks have treated it like it's always a stand-alone vulnerability that can be fixed (including me), but I've changed my mind and I'm going to convince you to do the same!<br><br>Prompt injection is very often a delivery mechanism rather than a vulnerability. And the lack of clarity around this is causing a lot of confusion in the handling of AI Vulnerability reports. It's costing bug bounty hunters money (including me and my friends!) and causing developers to mis-prioritize fixes. So my hope is that this post will help clear things up.<cite><a href="https://josephthacker.com/ai/2025/11/24/prompt-injection-isnt-a-vulnerability.html" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Joseph Thacker</a></cite></p></blockquote><p>And his main claim:</p><blockquote><p>My main claim is that (around 95% of the time) the actual vulnerability is what we allow the model to <em>do</em> with the malicious output triggered by prompt injections. In those cases, the root cause is what can be achieved with the prompt injection, and not the injection itself (which may be unavoidable).<cite><a href="https://josephthacker.com/ai/2025/11/24/prompt-injection-isnt-a-vulnerability.html" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Joseph Thacker</a></cite></p></blockquote><p>I find this argument compelling, and I have flirted with it in the past myself. But ultimately, I do not think it's correct.</p><p>Ultimately, what he's saying is that bounties and companies pay for *impacts, not for vulnerabilities.</p><h1 id="do-vulnerabilities-need-to-be-fixable" tabindex="-1">Do vulnerabilities need to be fixable? </h1><p>One question this whole thing raises is whether or not something is a vulnerability if it can't be fixed.</p><p>It's crucial to understand that Joseph is primarily talking about the bug bounty use case where hunters are flooding company bounty submission processes with "Prompt Injection" vulnerabilities without being able to show why it matters.</p><p>The COMPANY:</p><blockquote><p>Ok, but what can you do with it?</p></blockquote><p>The HUNTER:</p><blockquote><p>Nothing that I know of, but Prompt Injection is really bad.</p></blockquote><p>From this perspective, it's easy to see Joseph's point. Basically, "If you can't demonstrate a problem, it's not a problem."</p><p>I strongly resonate with this argument, but I think it's missing something that I can demonstrate with an analogy.</p><h1 id="prompt-injection-and-the-pope" tabindex="-1">Prompt Injection and The Pope </h1><p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/pope-prompt-injection-analogy.png" alt="Control Parallels: Pope Security and AI Defense"></p><p>The Pope has to interact with crowds. It's just something he has to do.</p><p>And the problem with crowds is that <strong>you can't tell the good people from the bad just by looking at them.</strong></p><p>In this analogy, the fact that the Pope has to get close to crowds is just like applications needing to take input from users. And the fact that you can't tell who in the crowd is good or bad just by looking at them is the Prompt Injection vulnerability.</p><h2 id="the-mapping-of-components" tabindex="-1">The mapping of components </h2><p>This is actually a great analogy for a number of reasons.</p><p>First, it's a nearly impossible thing to solve because you can't look at somebody and know what's inside their heart and mind. And second, there are many layers of defense that you can apply that significantly reduce the risk.</p><ul><li>You can control access to the area by having people go through metal detectors</li><li>You can monitor the crowd using with security guards on foot near the Pope</li><li>You can put the Pope in a bullet-proof transparent box so it's much harder to stab or shoot him</li></ul><h1 id="why-the-classification-as-vulnerability-matters" tabindex="-1">Why the classification as vulnerability matters </h1><p>Being seasoned to endless semantic debates, I hesitate to hing arguments on categorization or labels. But I think there's a strong reason to consider the issue a vulnerability.</p><p>If we consider a Prompt Injectionâ€”or the inability to understand the danger of a crowdâ€”to be a type of Risk Background Noise, we stop looking for solutions.</p><!----><p>We disengage our creative, problem-solving minds and can end up accepting more risk than necessary as a result.</p><p>But if we consider it a vulnerability, we continue looking for the AppSec equivalents of metal detectors and Popemobiles.</p><h1 id="prompt-injection-as-attack-and-mechanism" tabindex="-1">Prompt Injection as attack and mechanism </h1><p>So that's my argument for why it's a vulnerability, and why we should consider it one.</p><p>But part of the confusion comes from the fact that Prompt Injection can fill multiple roles in day-to-day security discussion.</p><ol><li>It can be a <strong>technique / mechanism</strong>: <br><br>"They used Prompt Injection to launch a Data Exfiltration attack..."</li><li>It can be an <strong>attack</strong>: <br><br> "They poisoned the application's Agent via Prompt Injection..."</li><li>And it can be a <strong>vulnerability</strong>: <br><br> "The application is vulnerable to Prompt Injection..."</li></ol><p>But for the reasons above, I would say #3 is the actual primitive, and #1 and #2 are practical language handles for discussing it in the real world.</p><h1 id="a-brief-aside-on-the-prompt-injection-vs-jailbreaking-debate" tabindex="-1">A brief aside on the "Prompt Injection vs. Jailbreaking" debate </h1><p><a href="https://simonwillison.net/2022/Sep/12/prompt-injection/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Simon Willison</a>, who actually coined the term "Prompt Injection," draws his dividing line along:</p><ul><li>Prompt Injection is the confusion of content and instructions</li><li>Jailbreaking is attempting to bypass security</li></ul><p>I think those are quite clean, and they mesh with my discussion above.</p><p>My fellow security expert and friend, <a href="https://x.com/jhaddix" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Jason Haddix</a> thinks this is too low-level, and prefers a more subset-type definition:</p><!----><p>I think Jason's definition is practically superior in some ways due to its simplicity, but I don't think it's high-resolution enough to take specific action on. Which is why I think the strict definitions are more useful to those with the problem.</p><p>Basically, the whole point of laying out whether something is a vulnerabilityâ€”and whether you have it or notâ€”is to create a plan for eliminating (or at least reducing) the risk it presents. And it's hard to do that if it's not specific enough.</p><p>This is Joseph's argument too, but I think the fact that you can mitigate risk from the vuln without eliminating it completely warrants the continued use of the Vulnerability label.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol start="4"><li>I asked Sam Altman directly if he thought we would be able to solve Prompt Injection any time soon, and he said he thought it would require a fundamental advancement in computer science. And I agree.</li><li>Thacker, Joseph. "Prompt Injection Isn't a Vulnerability (Most of the Time)." <em>josephthacker.com</em>, 24 Nov. 2025, <a href="https://josephthacker.com/ai/2025/11/24/prompt-injection-isnt-a-vulnerability.html" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">https://josephthacker.com/ai/2025/11/24/prompt-injection-isnt-a-vulnerability.html</a>.</li><li>Willison, Simon. "Prompt injection attacks against GPT-3." <em>simonwillison.net</em>, 12 Sep. 2022, <a href="https://simonwillison.net/2022/Sep/12/prompt-injection/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">https://simonwillison.net/2022/Sep/12/prompt-injection/</a>. The post where Willison coined the term "prompt injection."</li><li>Willison, Simon. "Prompt injection and jailbreaking are not the same thing." <em>simonwillison.net</em>, 5 Mar. 2024, <a href="https://simonwillison.net/2024/Mar/5/prompt-injection-jailbreaking/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">https://simonwillison.net/2024/Mar/5/prompt-injection-jailbreaking/</a>.</li><li>AIL Level 1: Daniel wrote all the arguments and I, his DA Kai, helped Red Team the ideas and did the art. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Learn more about AIL</a>.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Thoughts on Prompt Injection OPSEC]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/thoughts-on-prompt-injection-opsec?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/thoughts-on-prompt-injection-opsec</guid>
            <pubDate>Mon, 24 Nov 2025 22:54:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Prompt Injection OPSEChttps://danielmiessler.com/images/prompt-injection-opsec-header.png I want to respond to this blog posthttps://disesdi.substack.com/p/ai-red-teaming-has-an-opsec-problem that's arguing that prompt injection strings are essentially zero-days that we should not share with attack...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/prompt-injection-opsec-header.png" alt="Prompt Injection OPSEC"></p><p>I want to respond to <a href="https://disesdi.substack.com/p/ai-red-teaming-has-an-opsec-problem" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">this blog post</a> that's arguing that prompt injection strings are essentially zero-days that we should not share with attackers.</p><div class="blog-preview-card" data-v-7df9dc61=""><a href="https://disesdi.substack.com/p/ai-red-teaming-has-an-opsec-problem" class="blog-preview-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" data-v-7df9dc61=""><div class="blog-preview-thumbnail-hero" data-v-7df9dc61=""><img src="https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F097324f0-9ee7-4067-9f88-d003734499d0_960x540.jpeg" alt="AI Red Teaming Has An OPSEC Problem" class="blog-preview-thumbnail" data-v-7df9dc61=""><div class="blog-preview-overlay" data-v-7df9dc61=""></div><div class="blog-preview-source" data-v-7df9dc61="">disesdi.substack.com</div></div><div class="blog-preview-content" style="background:rgba(232, 218, 200, 0.95);" data-v-7df9dc61=""><div class="blog-preview-content-left" data-v-7df9dc61=""><h3 class="blog-preview-title" data-v-7df9dc61="">AI Red Teaming Has An OPSEC Problem</h3><p class="blog-preview-description" data-v-7df9dc61="">Don't buy, invest in, or pay for a course about 'AI red teaming' until you read this</p></div><div class="blog-preview-content-right" data-v-7df9dc61=""><div class="blog-preview-date" data-v-7df9dc61="">Nov 23, 2025</div></div></div></a></div><p>I'll start by saying that the author seems genuinely concerned that harm is being done, so I appreciate the piece from that standpoint. We need more people in this debate, not fewer, and I appreciate anyone in the arena.</p><p>But I don't think the fundamental claims are correct, and there seem to be competing sources of criticism beyond just the public safety concern.</p><h1 id="security-theater" tabindex="-1">Security Theater? </h1><p>We'll start with this one, which seems quite extreme.</p><blockquote><p>Not only is "AI red teaming" mostly security theater, but it actually makes systems <em>more</em> vulnerable.<br><br>How?<br><br>Because of the game theoretic constraints introduced by the mathematical realities of the underlying system architectures.</p></blockquote><p>First, a lot of things can be said about the AI Red Teaming / Security space, but I don't think Security Theater is one of them.</p><p>We know <a href="https://www.reuters.com/technology/chatgpt-sets-record-fastest-growing-user-base-analyst-note-2023-02-01/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">AI adoption is happening faster than any previous tech</a>. Time to 100 million users:</p><ul><li><strong>ChatGPT:</strong> 2 months</li><li><strong>TikTok:</strong> 9 months</li><li><strong>Instagram:</strong> 2.5 years</li></ul><p>As UBS analysts put it: "In 20 years following the internet space, we cannot recall a faster ramp in a consumer internet app." And <a href="https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/quantumblack/our-insights/the-state-of-ai" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">McKinsey reports</a> that 78% of organizations now use AI in at least one business functionâ€”up from 55% in 2023.</p><p>This is resulting in an extraordinary number of new applicationsâ€”enterprise apps, startup apps, hobby applications, and everything in between.</p><p>Many of these applications have AI front-ending the system in the form of one or more agents, or at least some sort of AI as part of the flow that processes inputs.</p><aside>There can obviously be products and services that are trying to grift off of this, but that's true for any legitimate problem.</aside><p>This is an acute and time-sensitive security challenge. The fact that AI Pentesting and Red Teaming and overall security services exist is not, to me, in any way security theater.</p><h1 id="_2-what-does-this-mean" tabindex="-1">2. What does this mean? </h1><p>Then we have this to back up the security theater claim.</p><blockquote><p>...because of the game theoretic constraints introduced by the mathematical realities of the underlying system architectures.</p></blockquote><p>That reads to me like someone trying to stop me from debating them because they're smarter than me.</p><p>But they ask for grace, so let's give it to them.</p><blockquote><p>Disclosing the attacksâ€“like promptsâ€“that were successful against any system does nothing to make anyone safer. In fact, it makes things considerably worse. The key to understanding why lies in two important facts: First, these attacks are not patchable. There is no security fix.</p></blockquote><p>Even if something can't be fixed completely doesn't mean there isn't value in defenders understanding how attacks are carried out in the real world.</p><p>There are mitigations. There are controls. Even if they're not foolproof.</p><blockquote><p>Hardening against one prompt or even 50,000 prompts likely leaves a nearly infinite amount of variances unpatched.</p></blockquote><p>Yes, but <em>no</em> security is absoluteâ€”regardless of domain. The question is whether we're tangibly reducing risk by learning how the attacks are done and putting defenses in place.</p><h1 id="_3-this-argument-sounds-familiar" tabindex="-1">3. This argument sounds familiar </h1><p>Then we have this bit, which is getting to the main issue I have with the post.</p><blockquote><p>Prominent "AI red teams" put libraries of prompts right out on the open internet. Proudly. For anyone to use.</p></blockquote><p><strong>This is the same argument that people made against releasing Metasploit and similar tools.</strong></p><p>Basically, "If we talk about how we get attacked, it will just help attackers."</p><aside>The research is not as good as a dedicated team of professional human researchers. But it seems fairly broad and unbiased.</aside><p>I had my AI system go and do <a href="https://github.com/danielmiessler/Substrate/blob/main/research/offensive-security-tools-net-effects-november-2025/red-team-analysis.md" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">a whole bunch of research</a> on this, and while the results were mixed, the data definitely pointed in the direction of information helping defenders more than attackers.</p><p>So it seems to me that the burden is on the author to describe why prompt injection is specifically different than other types of offensive technique sharing.</p><p>Like I said above, I don't think the fact that it's unpatchable is the answer there. Because controls can still mitigate a lot of the risk.</p><h1 id="_4-attackers-already-know" tabindex="-1">4. Attackers already know </h1><p>Here they are arguing that they are using the knowledge from the published prompts and building their own system.</p><p>My argument around this has been since 2023 that it's going to get easier and easier to do these attacks the better AI models and scaffolding becomes.</p><blockquote><p>And our paper shows that these attacks will never stop popping up. The prompt gives you a start. Math gives you the plan. Infinity is now manageable, and time is on your side ... You pick one prompt, and iterate. That's it. ... That "defended" prompt just became the key that lets an attacker into the entire system.</p></blockquote><p>Potentially true, yes.</p><p>But it's not as if the attacker was completely lost and had no idea how to do this using AI themselves.And then the AI red teaming community released the prompts, and now they are able to do the attacks.</p><p>Anybody who's wanting to launch these attacks, especially at scale against sophisticated defenders, has already known for a long time how to create an engine that combines attack strings in a combinatorial and/or iterative way that can be automated.</p><p>And it's only getting easier and easier to make better and better automated AI attack engines like these. At this point I and many others can one-shot a prompt that builds such a system. We're not telling most attackersâ€”especially the good onesâ€”anything they don't already know.</p><h1 id="_5-coming-right-out-with-it" tabindex="-1">5. Coming right out with it </h1><p>And now temperature increases on the language.</p><blockquote><p>If you maintain a public repo of prompts you use to test, you are endangering every client you ever had.</p></blockquote><p>First off, there's a difference between public prompts and the prompts that have worked against customers. Or at least there should be.</p><aside>These I would consider more in the category of zero-day, or if not zero-day then customer data.</aside><p>I am in this space myself and I have many friends who do AI Security Assessments multiple times a month. They are all very careful to keep specific prompts that worked against actual customers out of anything they release publicly.</p><p>They might share the techniques, or the cateogory, or talk about it if it's a new class of attack or something, but they're not copying and pasting actual customer attack strings to Github.</p><h1 id="_6-over-the-line" tabindex="-1">6. Over the line </h1><p>And finally:</p><blockquote><p>If you paid for an AI red team to assess your AI security, they are likely your biggest AI security liability.</p></blockquote><p>I guess given the context above, the author is basically saying that if you have a security company that you presumably trusted enough to bring in in the first place, if you have them look at your AI systems, they're going to take the exact prompts they used and publish them directly online.</p><p>That is an extraordinary claim to make, and as I said above, I know it not to be true for myself and my friends who do this work.</p><h1 id="_7-something-else-entering-the-conversation" tabindex="-1">7. Something else entering the conversation </h1><blockquote><p>You, a celebrity â€œAI red teamerâ€ did all my work for me.</p></blockquote><p>And here we see some definite noise in the signal they're trying to transmit.</p><aside>Or at least the signals are mixed enough to dampen the security argument.</aside><p>This is starting to feel a lot more like an attack on one or more specific individuals and their "celebrity", rather than a security discussion.</p><h1 id="my-take" tabindex="-1">My take </h1><p>If I were to steelman the argument being made, which I am again thankful they brought up...I would say something like:</p><p><strong>Prompt injection strings are more like zero-day exploits than Metasploit modules because there's no absolute patch for them. And attackers can move much faster than defenders.</strong></p><p>I think this is a (nearly) decent argument because the security community already agrees that zero-day exploits should not be released immediately to the public, even if researchers and/or defenders have them in hand.</p><p>So then the burden is on the counter-argument to say why prompt injection strings are not 0days.</p><p>But I don't think the analogy holds for a few key reasons:</p><aside>I'm giving rough numbers here. There is no established data on this yet.
