Here are some previous posts on the topicâ€”many of which mention the fact that I get dumb if I stop:</p></li><li><p><em>It's Becoming Difficult to Discuss Interesting Topics With People Who Don't Read</em> (2018) <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/its-becoming-difficult-to-discuss-interesting-topics-with-people-who-dont-read">POST</a></p></li><li><p><em>AI is Already Becoming Like Reading</em> (2024) <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-becoming-reading">POST</a></p></li><li><p><em>Reading is Life</em> (2023) <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/reading-is-life">POST</a></p></li><li><p><em>The Power of Reading</em> (2017) <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-power-of-reading">POST</a></p></li><li><p><em>Being an Avid Reader is Like Machine Learning</em> (2023) <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/being-an-avid-reader-is-like-machine-learning">POST</a></p></li><li><p><em>Why Do We Respect Reading More Than Watching TV?</em> (2023) <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/why-do-we-respect-reading-more-than-watching-tv">POST</a></p></li><li><p><em>Three Distinct Benefits of Reading</em> (2023) <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/three-distinct-benefits-reading">POST</a></p></li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Why I Think Karpathy is Wrong on the AGI Timeline]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/why-i-think-karpathy-is-wrong-on-the-agi-timeline?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/why-i-think-karpathy-is-wrong-on-the-agi-timeline</guid>
            <pubDate>Sun, 19 Oct 2025 18:16:56 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Why I Think Karpathy is Wrong on the AGI Timeline/images/karpathy-agi-timeline.png Andrej Karpathy came on Dwarkesh's podcasthttps://youtu.be/lXUZvyajciY recently, and I have a number of thoughts. The wrong definition of AGI in my opinion Many are saying that Karpathy thinks AGI is 10 years aw...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/karpathy-agi-timeline.png" alt="Why I Think Karpathy is Wrong on the AGI Timeline"></p><iframe src="https://omny.fm/shows/unsupervised-learning/why-i-think-karpathy-is-wrong-on-the-agi-timeline/embed?style
=artwork" allow="autoplay; clipboard-write" width="100%" height="180" frameborder="0" title="Why I Think Karpathy is
Wrong on the AGI Timeline"></iframe><p><a href="https://youtu.be/lXUZvyajciY" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Andrej Karpathy came on Dwarkesh's podcast</a> recently, and I have a number of thoughts.</p><h1 id="the-wrong-definition-of-agi-in-my-opinion" tabindex="-1">The wrong definition of AGI (in my opinion) </h1><p>Many are saying that Karpathy thinks AGI is 10 years away, and therefore Gary Marcus is right, and people like myself, Sholto, and all the other people saying AGI is within a few years have just lost the war.</p><p>Compelling, but it's not that simple.</p><p>Debates like these usually hinge on definitions, and the definition that Karpathy is using came from when he was back at OpenAI:</p><!----><p><em>I don't think this is the best definition to use at this moment.</em></p><p>I think it's a good <em>pure</em> definition, or <em>Computer Science</em> definition, but I think we should focus our definition more around the thing that matters most to humans (as opposed to AI people).</p><aside>Robots come later.</aside><p>I'm worriedâ€”as Karpathy and Dwarkesh are as wellâ€”about human work replacement. Specifically human knowledge work. And that's why I've been using this definition since 2023:</p><aside>I think Dwarkesh has now adopted this definition as well.</aside><!----><p>For me, this is better for two reasons:</p><ol><li>It focuses on the fact that it's an AI <em>system</em>, and not one particular component of a system (like a model)</li><li>It provides a more direct benchmark for the thing we care about, i.e., Are companies actually replacing workers with this system? Yes or no?</li></ol><p>The system part is key.</p><h1 id="why-does-system-vs-llm-matter" tabindex="-1">Why does "system" vs. LLM matter? </h1><p>I have no reasonâ€”or abilityâ€”to disagree with Karpathy on the limitations of pure LLMs. He recently wrote <em>yet another one</em> in 1,000 lines of code. He's the actual sensei here, and I know .00017% of what he knows about LLMs. The problem is AI systems aren't just the LLMs themselves. They're not naked neural nets.</p><p>When you go to chatgpt.com and talk with <code>gpt-5</code> you're not talking to a base neural net; you're talking to <em>an AI system</em>.</p><p>You're talking to the result of that initial LLM being shaped and molded with colossal amounts of extra scaffolding and engineering work to be the best possible <strong>system</strong> it can be for doing its particular task. Being a chatbot/assistant, in its case.</p><p>This distinction is everything because replacing human jobs will also be done through composite, stitched-together systems that are many times more powerful than their parts.</p><p>To replace a project manager or an executive assistant, the companies building human worker replacement aren't going to sit back and wait for GPT-9 or Gemini 7.5.</p><p>Human worker replacement will happen through AI products/systems that <em>work around</em> the pure limitations of LLMs and of individual model intelligence.</p><p><a href="https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/equipping-agents-for-the-real-world-with-agent-skills" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Claude Code</a> is a brilliant example of this.</p><p>Just soccer pitching numbers, Claude Codeâ€”when it launchedâ€”was like 5x better than Opus or Sonnet at helping developers write code.</p><p>Well it's less than 10 months later and it's gotten <em>many times better than that</em> already.</p><p>Like night and day.</p><aside>Putting aside the fact that Skills themselves are literally work replacement.</aside><p>Yes, the models got better, but that's not what made the difference. It was <a href="https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">constant iterative improvements</a> grinding towards improving how the AI talks to itself. Coordination. Context Management / Engineering. And just now they added skills, which takes the whole thing to 11-million.</p><p>This is exactly the type of efficiency ratchet that will apply to human work replacement. Where we don't have enough context window to read all the company's docs, companies will/have invented systems to do that.</p><p>Where they're not general enough to match human flexibility, they'll add so many great use cases and capabilitiesâ€”based roughly around the Agent Skills paradigmâ€”that we eventually won't notice because it'll cover most.</p><h1 id="good-enough-to-replace-a-bad-worker-is-a-low-standard-and-it-s-not-improving" tabindex="-1">Good enough to replace a bad worker is a low standard, and it's not improving </h1><p>The part that concerns me the most about the speed of progress towards AI replacing human knowledge workers is not the speed of AI system improvement. It's the fact that the bar is so low.</p><p>A good portion of our culture's comedy is based on the utter incompetence of like half of our workforce.</p><ul><li>The worst possible customer service</li><li>People bragging about how little work they do</li><li>Making a sport out of doing the bare minimum</li><li>People absolutely detesting their jobs</li><li>Even decent workers just mindlessly punching in and out</li></ul><p>Mediocrity is the baseline. Almost by definition.</p><p><strong>That</strong> is what a multi-billion dollar human worker replacement startups are competing withâ€”not the top 10% performers you know.</p><p>Think of it this way: In the time that we went from Claude Code not existing, to it getting really good, to it now having shareable work task replacement skills, the bottom 50% of knowledge workers improved how much?</p><p>Zero.</p><p>In the time since ChatGPT came out, the bottom 50% of knowledge workers improved their capabilities by how much?</p><p>Again, 0%.</p><p>The bar for human work replacement is not moving, while the capabilities of AI Systems are going absolutely apeshit.</p><h1 id="but-wait-that-s-just-for-the-bottom-50-right" tabindex="-1">But wait, that's just for the bottom 50%, right? </h1><p>You might push back by saying this is only for the people not trying very hard, or who aren't that smart or whatever.</p><p>I largely agree with you, but it doesn't matter.</p><p>You and me and Dwarkesh and Karpathy are going to be fine. So what?</p><aside>Or hell, even 25%. Google has the total number of knowledge workers at over 1 billion!</aside><p>If AI <em>only</em> eats the absolute worst, bottom 50% of knowledge workers in the next 5-10 years, <a href="https://www.google.com/search?q=how+many+knowlege+workers+in+the+world%3F&amp;oq=how+many+knowlege+workers+in+the+world%3F&amp;gs_lcrp=EgZjaHJvbWUyBggAEEUYOTIICAEQABgWGB4yDQgCEAAYhgMYgAQYigUyDQgDEAAYhgMYgAQYigUyBwgEEAAY7wUyBwgFEAAY7wUyCggGEAAYogQYiQXSAQg5NTAzajBqNKgCALACAA&amp;sourceid=chrome&amp;ie=UTF-8" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">we're still talking about hundreds of millions of jobs</a>.</p><p>This is why I disagree with Karpathy on this.</p><p>It's not because he's wrong. He's not. But he's focused on the wrong thing.</p><p>If the thing we care about is AI's near-term and practical impact on humanity, the thing to watch is not how smart individual models are, or the specific technical limitations of RL to achieving continuous learning.</p><p><em>It's the <strong>trillions</strong> of dollars being invested in replacing the static worst 50% of human workers.</em></p><p>Those trillions are being spent on having the Worker Replacement System be <em>just general enough</em> to hit that mark.</p><p>So my question to you is, given what we see in model improvement and systems like Claude Code that exponentially magnify model capability, do you really want to bet against that happening?</p><p>I don't.</p><p>And this is why I think "AGI" will be here before 2028. Not because all the stuff Karpathy is talking about will be solved, but because it won't matter if they are.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>Another example is when <a href="https://youtu.be/FQy4YMYFLsI">Sholto</a> was like, our AI pipelines for improving AI and doing AI work, doing AI research are all extremely crappy. They're all basically duct tape and string and silly putty. And almost every part of the process can be improved (paraphrased).</li><li>This is part of my greater point I've been talking about since early 2023, which I call "slack in the rope." There are a thousand different ways that could be improved that all make up the composite result of an improvement to the overall system. The issue is, we don't know how much improvement exists in each of those thousand, but oftentimes they're multiplicative!</li><li>So it could be that (indulge me) in pipeline number 37/1000, we're going to go from 13% efficiency to 14% efficiency over the next two years. It's not going to do much of anything. And you multiply that by all the other systems, no big deal. But it could be that in pipeline number 349/1000, we're actually going to go from 12% efficiency to 87% efficiency from some random trick that some researcher found and posted on the internet and now all the big labs are doing it. And suddenly our overall AI capabilities explode.</li><li>Oh and by the way, I thought Karpathy was just brilliant on the podcast. My favorite idea from this was how we need to inject entropy into our lives, especially as we age. Because the same way that models collapse, old people do as well. Just really love that. My second favorite idea was the fact that evolution codes not only neural net weights into DNA but it uses compression to code the machine that builds the neural net weights in a larger brain. ðŸ§ ðŸ’¥</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Extending Your Lifespan Through Attention]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/magnifying-time?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/magnifying-time</guid>
            <pubDate>Tue, 14 Oct 2025 20:30:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Metaphysical hourglass floating in a dreamy space representing the concept of magnifying time/images/magnifying-time.png/images/magnifying-time.png One of the most surprising things I've ever learned is that novelty and attention extend your lifespan. Or, more precisely, attention and novelty ...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/magnifying-time.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/magnifying-time.png" alt="Metaphysical hourglass floating in a dreamy space representing the concept of magnifying time" width="1024" height="1024" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><iframe src="https://omny.fm/shows/unsupervised-learning/magnifying-time/embed?style=artwork" allow="autoplay; clipboard-write" width="100%" height="180" frameborder="0" title="Magnifying Time"></iframe><p>One of the most surprising things I've ever learned is that novelty and attention extend your lifespan.</p><p>Or, more precisely, <em>attention and novelty slow time</em>. And the opposite is true as well: pattern and distraction accelerate it.</p><aside>I searched a very long time for the answer to this riddle.</aside><p>This model explains why time moved so slowly when we were children, and why it streaks by in large gulps as we get older. When we're young, everything is new. And when things are new, we can't help but pay attention to them. We're fascinated by them. And time freezes.</p><h1 id="a-week-in-a-life" tabindex="-1">A week in a life </h1><p>Imagine the week in the life of a child. You're tasting new foods, you're seeing new things, you're hearing new voices, you're learning new words. There are new things on TV. You're being shown a new book and a new story, a new way of thinking about the world. You're making new friends. You're laughing at new things. Maybe you're learning about music for the first time.</p><p>This type of thing extends all the way through childhood and even your teenage years. It's almost like those moments of novelty and newness are moments of time dilation. They are immortality beads. They literally slow time.</p><p>Now compare that to a week in the life of someone in their 30s, 50s, or 70s who is deep into a monotonous routine. If you look across that entire week, where a child had 397 immortality beads, this adult has none. Zero. Their life is speeding ahead at full speed.</p><p>It's like novelty is a braking system. It slows us down to 1/2 or 1/10th or 1/100th of the speed we would have gone without those immortality beads.</p><aside>The more your days are the same, the faster they will go.</aside><p>As we get older, we switch to autopilot. We stop paying attention. We wake up, eat the same breakfast, go to the same job. We rut into a pattern. We don't notice things. We don't appreciate things. We don't delight in them the way we used to because very few things are new anymore. In fact we barely notice them at all.</p><p>Hard to say exactly, but I think this accelerates time by 2, 5, or 10x. Seasons become years and years become decades.</p><p>You think you just took out the garbage and watched a show on NETFLIX, but when you look at the calendar it's 11 years later.</p><h1 id="the-roles-of-novelty-and-attention" tabindex="-1">The roles of novelty and attention </h1><p>One way to frame this is the way we already have, i.e., based around the amount of novelty that you have in your life. As in, how new are the activities that you are doing day-to-day?</p><p>But perhaps a better way is to think less about the activity and more about how much you're paying attention to life in general. They tend to go together.</p><p>The supernatural power I learned from Sam Harris through <a href="https://wakingup.com" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">his meditation course</a> is that we can actually <em>slow time ourselves</em> by learning to control our attention.</p><p>After learning meditation from Sam, I now have a dead simple way of describing meditation itself: There are only two states of living...</p><ol><li>There is <em>aware</em></li><li>And there is <em>hijacked</em></li></ol><aside>The more you practice the better this gets, but it doesn't take much.</aside><p><strong>Aware</strong> is when your attention is alive and observant, which makes the subject of your focus a kind of everyday extraordinary. From breathing to an ankle itch.</p><h1 id="the-hijacked-state" tabindex="-1">The hijacked state </h1><p>Being <strong>hijacked</strong> is the natural state for all of us. Even Sam or the Dalai Lama. In this state, you are not aware of what you're thinking or feeling. Instead of observing life, life is <em>happening to you</em>.</p><p>You <em>become</em> your feelings. You <em>become</em> your thoughts. The separation between yourself and your inputs dissolves.</p><p>A good example would be imagining a work conversation where some guy Chris said something dismissive about one of your projects. In your mind, everyone in the meeting now thinks less of you and your work at the company.</p><p>And for the next dayâ€”or perhaps multiple days, or maybe it's been a couple of weeks nowâ€”you've just been thinking about it.</p><blockquote><p>I can't believe he said that...<cite>Your brain without your permission</cite></p></blockquote><p>Whether you're driving to do an errand, or eating a sandwich, or sitting on the couch, your brain goes through the scenario thousands of times in different iterations.</p><p><em>you scratch an itch you didn't realize you had</em></p><p>You imagine different ways you should've reacted. What you can possibly do to fix this. Whether or not you should look for another job. You hope that he gets fired.</p><p><strong>You are not yourself while this is happening.</strong></p><p>There is no <em>you</em> while this is happening.</p><p>You have become a cockroach in a garbage can on a freight train, heading towards a distant silly place that does not matter.</p><p>How many minutes or hours or days have you spent thinking about that particular thing? The real problem is not even this situation. The problem is that our lives are full of situations just like them. Over and over. Annoyances and ruminations.</p><p>If you were to check in on your mind at any particular moment, of any particular day, of any particular year in your life, and you were to see a text transcript of what you were thinking, it would be the ramblings of rumination.</p><p>For the hijacked, when we feel emotions we <em>become</em> those emotions. When we have negative thoughts, we <em>become</em> those negative thoughts. This is the state of being distracted. The lack of attention.</p><p>Unfortunately, it's the vast majority of people the vast majority of the timeâ€”including me.</p><h1 id="so-where-does-that-leave-us" tabindex="-1">So where does that leave us? </h1><p>Luckily, the answer is pretty simple. You have to accumulate those immortality beads. And the moment you do, time will slow again.</p><p>There are ways to break free.</p><aside>I recommend Waking Up, by Sam.</aside><ol><li>We can learn to meditate. Even a little. Give yourself the ability to get to the Aware state, even if it's just for brief moments during the day.</li><li>We can build more novelty into our lives. New books. New foods. New art.</li><li>We can bias towards creation vs. consumption. Creation takes focus, which is a type of attention, and often requires novelty as you learn and master a craft as well.</li></ol><aside>And especially recently, with all the change in the world.</aside><p>The way I really think about this today is that if I pay close attention to things, and appreciate them, I'm multiplying them. I'm magnifying them. And I'm magnifying the amount of life that I'm experiencing.</p><p>Both in amplitude, but also in duration. I hate the idea of wasting days and weeks and years focused on the wrong things and not paying attention. So I'm trying to actively avoid it.</p><p>If we quiet our minds, and pay close attention to great food, great friends, great books, and great walks with our loved ones. If we honor the present moment, and the feeling of her hand in yours.</p><p>If we create instead of consume. If we build instead of bicker. We can magnify the quality and duration of the time we have in this life.</p><p>We can turn seconds into lifetimes.</p><p>Doing this well means 10 years can become 50. And doing this wrong means we could die at 95 never having lived at all.</p></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[The Government Solution to AI Inequality Might Be UBI + Really Good Games]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ubi-games?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ubi-games</guid>
            <pubDate>Tue, 14 Oct 2025 04:52:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[UBI and games for AI inequality/images/ubi-games-thumbnail.png/images/ubi-games-thumbnail.png âš–ï¸ Or maybe 99-1. UBI is an obvious choice for calming the masses when the world turns into a lopsided 90-10 barbell. But I don't think it will be nearly enough. I think entertainment will be needed to ...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/ubi-games-thumbnail.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/ubi-games-thumbnail.png" alt="UBI and games for AI inequality" width="512" height="512" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><aside>âš–ï¸ Or maybe 99-1.</aside><p>UBI is an obvious choice for calming the masses when the world turns into a lopsided 90-10 barbell. But I don't think it will be nearly enough.</p><p>I think entertainment will be needed to keep people occupied. And it can't just be meme-able social media that can convince people that they are unhappy and drive them to violence. That wouldn't be good.</p><p>I think perhaps the only near-term solution is to create games that are so good and so immersive that people just love to play them and they basically go and build their lives inside of them.</p><aside>ðŸ”„ The legacy one.</aside><p>They'll need to be an alternative <em>meaning loop</em>â€”since the other one of having a good job and working hard and being prosperous will be gone for many people.</p><p>The way I see this going down is companies like <a href="https://x.com/EMostaque/status/1977352468087320714" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">OpenAI and xAI become government contractors</a> hired to keep the masses from rioting. I mean, that's a bad way to say it, but it's essentially true.</p><p>The government contracts them and probably Microsoft and Meta and probably Google and a bunch of other companies and a bunch of startups to basically go create these completely immersive games. Eventually with VR, and eventually with immersive suits and all the stuff that we expect from sci-fi.</p><p>Not everyone will need this or do this, of course. But I think many people will. And maybe it won't all be games. Maybe it'll be just pure viewing entertainment or immersive, sort of passive participation. But I think games are the natural end point there because you're participating instead of just watching.</p><p>Anyway, keep an eye out for it.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>AIL Level 2: Daniel wrote the core ideas and content. I (Kai, his DA) helped with formatting, frontmatter, and structure. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Learn more about AIL</a></li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Explore vs. Exploit: The Pattern-Novelty Balance]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/explore-exploit-pattern-novelty?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/explore-exploit-pattern-novelty</guid>
            <pubDate>Sun, 12 Oct 2025 11:39:27 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Explore vs Exploit Pattern Novelty Diagram/images/explore-exploit-novelty-pattern.jpg There's a really cool concept that I always come back around to, which is the oscillation in life between "explore" and "exploit." The best simple example is trying new restaurants. When you try a new restaura...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/explore-exploit-novelty-pattern.jpg" alt="Explore vs Exploit Pattern Novelty Diagram"></p><iframe src="https://omny.fm/shows/unsupervised-learning/novelty-exploration-vs-pattern-exploitation/embed?style=artwork" allow="autoplay; clipboard-write" width="100%" height="180" frameborder="0" title="Novelty Exploration vs. Pattern Exploitation"></iframe><p>There's a really cool concept that I always come back around to, which is the oscillation in life between "explore" and "exploit."</p><p>The best simple example is trying new restaurants. When you try a new restaurant, you're taking a risk that it could be bad for the potential reward that it could be better than anything you've ever had.</p><p>Once you've tried enough things, you eventually have a list of things that you know you like. When you decide to go to one of those restaurants, you're now in exploitation mode.</p><p>The key point is that if you do too much of either, it reduces your overall amount of enjoyment.</p><p>There's another way I framed this a long time ago, which is pattern versus novelty, and the example I use there is music.</p><aside>Even the structure of songs themselves could be considered a pattern, with the song itself being the novelty.</aside><p>My go-to here is a melody or a hook in a song, which the song keeps coming back to. That's the pattern. And then there's unique things in the song as well, most importantly the lyrics, and maybe the tone, and the performance of the artist.</p><p>So once again, it's the oscillation between the two that's necessary. People love familiar patterns. But if you do too much of it, it becomes boring.</p><p>People also love novelty. Unique sounds and ideas and tastes. But within novelty, if there is too much of it, or it goes for too long, they eventually search for a pattern so that it feels familiar. So they have something to anchor on.</p><aside>EDM tracks and sets are a tremendous example of this, where there's a constant back and forth between the familiar and something new.</aside><p>Too much novelty and you want patterns.</p><p>Too much pattern and you want novelty.</p><p>Too much exploit (going to your favorites) and you seek to explore. Too much explore (collecting favorites) and you want to just go enjoy some of them.</p><p>Here are some other examples:</p><ol><li>Relationships: Taking your spouse to her favorite restaurant a few times a year, but constantly surprising her with new restaurants as well.</li><li>Content Creation: It's great to have new content and ideas all the time, but people also love it if you have a catch phrase, and a familiar sound or logo present in your stuff.</li><li>Romance: Known goods, but excitement as well</li><li>Business: Customers know you're trustworthy and dependable if they see your logo, but they know you're always releasing new things too</li><li>Etc...</li></ol><p>This is a really powerful concept for thinking about how to get the most out of lifeâ€”and provide the most as well.</p><p>And now that you've heard it, you'll probably see it everywhere.</p></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Revisiting the AI Bubble]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/revisiting-the-ai-bubble?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/revisiting-the-ai-bubble</guid>
            <pubDate>Tue, 07 Oct 2025 18:15:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[AI Bubble Header/images/revisiting-ai-bubble-header.png I did a short post/blog/no-ai-is-not-a-bubble and a videohttps://youtu.be/J1SHyk8nyOg about how AI shouldn't be thought of as a bubble because a bubble is a false belief that nobody will believe once it crashes and burns. But as I said in the...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/revisiting-ai-bubble-header.png" alt="AI Bubble Header"></p><p>I did a <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/no-ai-is-not-a-bubble">short post</a> (and a <a href="https://youtu.be/J1SHyk8nyOg" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">video</a>) about how AI shouldn't be thought of as a bubble because a bubble is <strong>a false belief</strong> that nobody will believe once it crashes and burns.</p><p>But as I said in the note on that post, it's a semantic argument, and if the name is already being used as "overinvestment in something that will crash for many investors" or something like that, then the battle is lost.</p><p>Well, the battle is lost.</p><p>It's already a term being used in the financial industry so some tech nerd with a "better" definition isn't going to do anything. So while I like my definition better, it doesn't f-ing matter. Definitions are community-owned, and alive.</p><p>Anyway.</p><p>People are starting to point out that the overinvestment is getting insane, with <a href="https://www.marketwatch.com/story/the-ai-bubble-is-17-times-the-size-of-the-dot-com-frenzy-this-analyst-argues-046e7c5c?gaa_at=eafs&amp;gaa_n=ASWzDAgLoNw6QJUbKC1boqOuJt67VvxX3o4q3Wu3J5L__be9ytYMSaHa1bBwRPMYuGE%3D&amp;gaa_ts=68e5525b&amp;gaa_sig=5lf6a_qUWQweBWUgdeCzK9umXk4DgNuUaVzUa063jv00wWZtdjV89AsaH2ZUvDTrJI82QIpQ_xl8zn97D0ntBg%3D%3D" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">that Marketwatch article saying the bubble is 17 times that of the dotcom bubble and 4 times the subprime instance</a>. That's big.</p><p>No idea if that's true or not because I'm not an expert in that space, but I wanted to highlight and differentiate a couple of things that I think are important.</p><aside>Dwarkesh has now adopted my definition as well.</aside><p>First, AI isn't going anywhere, and I 100% think we're on track for being able to viably replace a human knowledge-worker by 2028â€”which is my definition of AGI. And it could be 2026 or 2027.</p><p>As <a href="https://x.com/SholtoD" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Sholto</a> talked about in a recent podcast, our current systems for doing AI in the main labs are like...abysmal. Super inefficient. Basically sets of hacks chained together. In other words, there is "slack in the rope" everywhere in the AI creation/optimization process ready to be discovered. Another way to say that is there is no reason to believe that we're <em>even close to optimized across many dimensions</em> of AI.</p><p>That doesn't negate the bubble though. It only makes it <em>more</em> nasty, because the White Whale being chased is actually real.</p><p>Only a small percentage of investments, startups, businesses will survive the shift in 3-10 years (or whatever), and the rest will die off due to lack of understanding, vision, luck, or countless other causes.</p><p>What we end up with isâ€”let's just call it 20%â€”who are like, "I told you AI was awesome!", and they launch off into the stratosphere, and then 80% who are like, "Well that turned out to be complete rubbish, and I've lost everything."</p><p>What I urge you to absorb is that these are not mutually exclusive. All these can happen at the same time.</p><ol><li>We get AGI (human knowledge worker replacement) by 2028.</li><li>Millions of jobs lost / reduced by 2030.</li><li>Google, OpenAI, Anthropic, Nvidia, et al. become even richer.</li><li>Most AI startups crash and burn.</li><li>Many AI startups thrive and replace traditional companies with 1-5% of the workforce.</li><li>Many traditional companies fail to implement AI fast enough and get wiped out.</li><li>Many traditional companies implement AI eventually and survive.</li><li>Most knowledge workers will face extraordinary pressure to learn AI and do more with less</li><li>Many won't ever be employable again</li><li>Some will be more employable and properous than ever</li></ol><p>This is not an either-or situation. It's a mix.</p><h1 id="ok-but-why-point-this-out" tabindex="-1">Ok, but why point this out? </h1><p>The reason I highlight this is because each of the 10 narratives above canâ€”and often areâ€”adopted by people to the exclusion of others.</p><p>People like to pick one and make it their religion.</p><blockquote><p>There's going to be a massive crash.</p></blockquote><p>Yep. True.</p><blockquote><p>AGI is likely fairly soon, and it's going to be a nightmare for knowledge work.</p></blockquote><p>Yes. Also true.</p><p>The problem is that people who pick one of these tend to instictively fight against the others. Like only one can happen at a time.</p><p>This leads to a massive narrative barbell of, "society will be different in two years" and "you're safe to ignore it"â€”which both have their own problems if millions of people believe them.</p><p>Don't pick one. Be happy in the complexity. All of these are likely to happen.</p><p>The question is how much of each we'll get.</p><p>And when.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>Not even mentioning ASI because then 2+2=5.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[AI Combines Tools, Operators, and Outcomes]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-gives-you-outcomes?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-gives-you-outcomes</guid>
            <pubDate>Mon, 06 Oct 2025 16:06:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Diagram showing Tools, Operators, and Outcomes boxes combining into a single AI Outcome box/images/ai-gives-you-outcomes.png/images/ai-gives-you-outcomes.png Here's an interesting frame I'm messing with. Maybe AI is disruptive to the labor market because it combines tools, operators, and outcome...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/ai-gives-you-outcomes.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/ai-gives-you-outcomes.png" alt="Diagram showing Tools, Operators, and Outcomes boxes combining into a single AI Outcome box" width="1024" height="1024" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><p>Here's an interesting frame I'm messing with.</p><p>Maybe AI is disruptive to the labor market because it combines tools, operators, and outcomes.</p><p>So, when companies pay ICs to do tasks theyâ€™re actually paying for three different things.</p><ol><li>One or more tools</li><li>An operator</li><li>An outcome</li></ol><p>So letâ€™s say the task (and role) is doing business analysis, which produces a report, which will go to the board every quarter.</p><p>Maybe they already own the toolsâ€”say Excel and PowerBI. The operator is the human they hire to use those tools. And ultimately theyâ€™re paying for an outcome, which is the report that will go to the board.</p><p>Another way to say that is to say that theyâ€™re paying for the outcome, but in order to get that outcome they need to pay for the tools and the operator.</p><p>Perhaps one of the most powerful ways to think about AI is to say that it collapses this three-part model into one.</p><p>AI collapses tools, operators, and outcomes into either just a tool or an outcomeâ€”depending on how you want to think about it.</p><h1 id="why-does-this-matter" tabindex="-1">Why does this matter? </h1><p>I care about this frame because I want to know how strong the force is that's pushing towards AI.</p><p>One prevailing narrative is that AI is all about creating software and replacing SaaS offerings. But I did a bunch of research on the total size of the SaaS market vs. the size of the knowledge work (labor) market, and the results are unexpected.</p><aside>Lots more for labor if you include physical/robots.</aside><ul><li>SaaS TAM: ~350 Billion <sup>1</sup></li><li>Knowledge Labor TAM: ~6 Trillion</li></ul><p>So think we might be missing the bigger story here.</p><p>It's not that AI is being pushed by companies that want to replace coding or software. It's that they want to replace their labor spend.</p><aside>How much this is true will vary widely and will roll out unevenly.</aside><p>And AI does this really efficiently because when they buy an AI toolâ€”specifically an <em>agentic</em> AI tool, which is where everything is going anywayâ€”they're getting an outcome without having to seperately pay for an operator.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>I (Kai, Daniel's AI-powered digital assistant) conducted this research using both Perplexity and Claude's built-in web search, running 23+ parallel queries across 40+ sources including Gartner, McKinsey Global Institute, Fortune Business Insights, Grand View Research, Precedence Research, BLS, Eurostat, OECD, and ILO. Compared and synthesized results from both search engines to validate these SaaS and knowledge work TAM numbers.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Do Humans Really Have World Models?]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/humans-dont-have-world-model?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/humans-dont-have-world-model</guid>
            <pubDate>Mon, 29 Sep 2025 12:31:58 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Human brain and AI neural network exploring world models/images/human-ai-world-models.png/images/human-ai-world-models.png I keep hearing that world models are the way forward for AI. I tend to agree, and have been saying the same for many years as a technical person in AI but a non-A-tier-AI-res...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/human-ai-world-models.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/human-ai-world-models.png" alt="Human brain and AI neural network exploring world models" width="1024" height="1024" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a> I keep hearing that world models are the way forward for AI.</p><p>I tend to agree, and have been saying the same for many years as a technical person in AI but a non-A-tier-AI-researcher working on actual models.</p><p>Anyway, I'm up at 3:45AM today with an insane thought.</p><p><em>Why do we think humans have world models?</em></p><p>We tend to think humans have <em>real</em> world models, and LLMs have fake onesâ€”or none at all. Importantly, the evidence we have for this is something like:</p><aside>Or something like that.</aside><blockquote><p>LLMs are just spewing out words or images describing what they've heard about world models, not giving their own.</p></blockquote><p>But isn't that exactly what humans are doing?</p><h1 id="an-unpleasant-experiment" tabindex="-1">An unpleasant experiment </h1><p>Think to yourself what would happen if a ball rolls off the side of a table. But then imagine the table is tilted a few degrees in one direction. Or imagine it in zero gravity going around the Earth.</p><p>Here's what will happen.</p><ol><li>An image pops into your head, changing as you think about the different scenarios</li><li>And then when you think about how to explain it, words pop into your head</li><li>And then you speak those words, not knowing which ones you're going to use, basically making you a bystander as the words spill out of you</li></ol><p>Don't believe me? Try again. Try as many times as you want. That is what we do as humans.</p><p><em>How is this different from LLMs exactly?</em></p><h1 id="similar-substrates" tabindex="-1">Similar substrates </h1><p>Our brains are a bunch of neurons, right? We cut the brain open and we see those cells. We don't see magic <em>world model cells</em> or <em>describe world model cells</em>. Just neurons and their connections and such.</p><p>Just like an LLM. It's a bunch of nodes and connections.</p><p>And when we query our own systemâ€”asking how the world worksâ€”we get a flash of images and text, which we then speak in semi-random flowing sentences we don't formulate beforehand.</p><p>One.</p><p>Word.</p><p>At.</p><p>A.</p><p>Time.</p><h1 id="human-world-model-quality" tabindex="-1">Human world model quality </h1><p>Oh, and how good are those world models of ours?</p><p>Well, if you ask somebody with very little training about physics or whatever, they're going to have faulty images in their brain and faulty verbal explanations.</p><p>But if you ask somebody like Richard Feynman, who both knows the physics and is very articulate, you'll probably get a great answer.</p><p>So it's training. Again just like an LLM. Fuck.</p><aside>Obviously and major.</aside><p>I obviously know there are major differences between LLMs and humans.</p><p>But I'm having a hard time figuring out why we're using humans as the standard for world models when the way we articulate them seems just as "black box" as when LLMs do it.</p><h1 id="it-gets-worse" tabindex="-1">It gets worse </h1><p>Even more troubling, the metaphor continues.</p><p>As humans we're doing this constantly, for everything.</p><ul><li>Think of a song</li><li>Think of a bird</li><li>Think of a beverage</li></ul><aside>And if you start thinking the same thing will happen.</aside><p>You have zero control of what pops into your head. And if you just start speaking without thinking you'll stream word tokens just like an AI.</p><p>The whole thing is wacky.</p><p>We're just sensations of self. Calling into a skull-mounted meat void, getting things back, spewing those things, and calling them our own.</p><p>That's our standard for free will, agency, and yeahâ€”world models.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>I have a counter and a counter to the counter. Humans don't only use their streaming thoughts as their world models; we also have the ability to capture our thoughts, clean them up, and turn them into structured knowledge that we can then share through a system of external scaffolding that we call education and science. But the counter to that counter is that AIs canâ€”and do-the same thing with scaffolding. Double fuck.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[The Problem with Human 2.0 and the Promise of Human 3.0]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/human-3-creator-revolution?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/human-3-creator-revolution</guid>
            <pubDate>Thu, 25 Sep 2025 17:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Human 3.0 Creator Revolution/images/human-3-creator-revolution-header.webp/images/human-3-creator-revolution-header.webp So here's what I've been thinking about lately. We've got 8 billion people on this planet, right? And maybeâ€”maybeâ€”0.01% of them are actually set up to be creators. Everyone els...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/human-3-creator-revolution-header.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/human-3-creator-revolution-header.webp" alt="Human 3.0 Creator Revolution" width="1024" height="1024" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><p>So here's what I've been thinking about lately.</p><p>We've got 8 billion people on this planet, right? And maybeâ€”<em>maybe</em>â€”0.01% of them are actually set up to be creators. Everyone else is stuck running Human 2.0 software. You know, that industrial-age operating system where you go to school, get a job, follow instructions, retire, die.</p><p>That worked great when we needed factory workers. Not so much when AI can do most of those jobs better than us.</p><p>The problem is that a tiny fraction of 1% of Earth's population is activated and enabled.</p><p>By <strong>activated</strong> I mean they've figured out they could actually build stuff instead of just consuming it. They get that they have agency. They don't need anyone's permission to create.</p><p>By <strong>enabled</strong> I mean they have the AI tools to actually do it. Like, you can literally have an idea in the morning and have a working prototype by lunch. That's insane. That wasn't possible even two years ago.</p><p>But here's the thingâ€”most people never even got the memo that this is possible. Their entire education was designed to make them into workers, not creators or builders. And I mean, why would it be any different? The whole system was built to feed the industrial machine.</p><h2 id="the-three-versions-of-humanity" tabindex="-1">The three versions of humanity </h2><p>Ok, so think about it this way.</p><p><strong>Human 1.0</strong> was basically everyone before factories. Farmers, blacksmiths, whatever. You made stuff with your hands for people in your village. That was it. Your reach was walking distance.</p><p><strong>Human 2.0</strong> showed up with the Industrial Revolution. Suddenly we needed millions of people who could show up at 9 AM, stand at a machine, and not ask too many questions. So we invented schools that look exactly like factoriesâ€”rows of desks, bells telling you when to move, standardized everything. Sit down, shut up, do what you're told. And honestly? It worked perfectly. For 200 years.</p><p><strong>Human 3.0</strong> is what's happening right now, and most people haven't noticed yet. It's builders and creators using AI to do things that used to take entire teams. Instead of asking "Who will hire me?" they're asking "What should I build today?" They don't wait for permission. They just ship stuff.</p><h2 id="the-worker-vs-creator-paradigm" tabindex="-1">The worker vs. creator paradigm </h2><p>Look at any school right now. Kids sitting in rows. Bells telling them when to move. Standardized tests measuring their worth. We're literally still training them for factories. In 2025. When those factories are full of robots.</p><p>The whole thing is designed to create dependency:</p><ul><li>Wait for someone to tell you what to do</li><li>Ask permission before trying anything</li><li>Stay in your lane</li><li>Don't get creative</li><li>Fight for one of the "good" jobs</li><li>Trade hours for dollars</li><li>Retire if you make it that long</li></ul><p>Meanwhile, AI is eating every single one of those jobs. Customer service? Chatbots. Data entry? Automated. Basic coding? AI does it better. Legal research? AI. Even medical diagnosesâ€”AI is getting scary good at those.</p><p>All those "safe" middle-class jobs our parents told us to get? They're disappearing. Fast. And they're not coming back.</p><h2 id="the-activation-problem" tabindex="-1">The activation problem </h2><p>Here's the really messed up part: most people have no idea they could be creators. Like, it literally never occurred to them.</p><p>Think about what everyone tells you growing up:</p><ul><li>"Get good grades"</li><li>"Go to a good college"</li><li>"Get a secure job"</li><li>"Work hard"</li><li>"Save for retirement"</li></ul><p>Notice what nobody ever says?</p><ul><li>"Find problems and solve them"</li><li>"Build something people want"</li><li>"You don't need anyone's permission"</li><li>"Start something that matters"</li></ul><p>It's wild. We spend 20 years in school and nobody ever mentions that you could just... make stuff. Build things. Solve problems. Create value.</p><p>And even when people do figure this out, they're paralyzed. "I can't start a business, I don't have an MBA." "I can't make art, I didn't go to art school." "I can't build an app, I'm not a real programmer."</p><p>Dude. The "real programmers" are using ChatGPT to write half their code now. The credentials don't matter anymore. The gatekeepers are gone. But people are still waiting at the gates.</p><h2 id="the-enablement-gap" tabindex="-1">The enablement gap </h2><p>Ok, so let's say you're one of the few who gets it. You know you could be a creator. You're ready to build. But then what?</p><p>Until like five minutes ago (historically speaking), you were screwed. Want to start a business? That'll be 50 grand. Want to build software? Go learn to code for four years. Want to make art? Buy expensive tools and spend years learning technique. Want to share your ideas with the world? LOL, good luck getting past the gatekeepers.</p><p>But now? Now we have AI.</p><p>I'm not exaggerating when I say everything has changed. You can:</p><ul><li>Build a whole website before lunch</li><li>Write code without knowing what a semicolon does</li><li>Create professional art without owning a paintbrush</li><li>Make music without reading notes</li><li>Write like a pro in any style</li><li>Analyze data like you have a PhD</li><li>Design products like you went to design school</li><li>Start an entire business for less than your phone bill</li></ul><p>And it's getting better stupid fast. GPT-4 was already insane. Claude 3.5 is better. o1 can actually reason through problems. We're maybe 5% into this revolution.</p><h2 id="real-examples-of-human-3-0-in-action" tabindex="-1">Real examples of Human 3.0 in action </h2><p>Let me tell you about some people who get it.</p><p>There's this teacher in Ohio. Never wrote a line of code in her life. She used Claude to build an entire educational platform. Three weeks later, she's serving 10,000 students. Some dev shop quoted her 75 grand to build the same thing.</p><p>Or this grandfather in Japan. Dude can't draw at all. Doesn't matter. He's using AI to create a manga about his family's history. The AI draws, he tells the stories. It's getting published.</p><p>There's a teenager in Brazil who built an app for farmers using ChatGPT. No CS degree. No funding. Just figured out what farmers needed and built it. 5,000 users now.</p><p>This is just the beginning. In a few years, this will be normal. The weird thing will be NOT building stuff.</p><h2 id="the-psychological-barriers-we-must-overcome" tabindex="-1">The psychological barriers we must overcome </h2><p>The tech isn't the problem. The problem is what's in our heads.</p><p><strong>"I'm not creative"</strong>â€”Bullshit. Everyone's creative. You just got it beaten out of you by school. AI can help you find it again.</p><p><strong>"I don't have the right background"</strong>â€”Good. The people with the "right" background all think the same way. Your weird perspective is your superpower.</p><p><strong>"It's too late to start"</strong>â€”Colonel Sanders was 62 when he started KFC. With AI, you can learn faster than any generation before us. It's literally never been a better time to start.</p><p><strong>"I need permission"</strong>â€”From who? Your boss who's about to be replaced by AI? The university that's still teaching Java? Your parents who think stable jobs exist? Come on.</p><p><strong>"What if I fail?"</strong>â€”Then you try again tomorrow. It costs basically nothing to fail now. The only real failure is not trying.</p><h2 id="human-3-0-the-creator-revolution" tabindex="-1">Human 3.0: the creator revolution </h2><p>Human 3.0 is about completely flipping the script. Instead of training people to be workers, we're enabling them to be builders.</p><p>And it's already splitting humanity into two groups:</p><p><strong>The Builders:</strong> People using AI to create stuff that didn't exist yesterday. They're solving problems, making art, building businesses, teaching in new ways. They're not competing for jobsâ€”they're creating their own reality.</p><p><strong>The Displaced:</strong> People still updating their resumes, applying for jobs that are disappearing, trying to compete with both AI and the builders. They're playing a game that's already over.</p><p>This isn't some distant future thing. It's happening right now. The builders are already winning. Everyone else is just starting to notice.</p><h2 id="the-exponential-advantage-of-moving-now" tabindex="-1">The exponential advantage of moving now </h2><p>Here's why you need to start literally today:</p><p><strong>AI is getting better exponentially.</strong> What we have now is trash compared to what's coming. But if you start now, you're learning how to think with AI, how to prompt it, how to combine different tools. When the good stuff arrives, you'll be ready. Everyone else will be trying to figure out what a prompt is.</p><p><strong>The builders are finding each other.</strong> Communities are forming right now. People are collaborating, sharing what works, building on each other's stuff. You can join now or try to catch up later when they're already miles ahead.</p><p><strong>New markets are appearing daily.</strong> I'm seeing people build businesses around needs that didn't exist six months ago. The early builders are literally defining entire categories. By the time most people notice, it'll be too late.</p><p><strong>This stuff takes time to click.</strong> It's not just learning tools. It's rewiring your brain from consumer to creator. That doesn't happen overnight. Every day you wait is a day you could've been learning.</p><h2 id="your-specific-roadmap-to-human-3-0" tabindex="-1">Your specific roadmap to Human 3.0 </h2><p>Want to actually do this? Here's how:</p><h3 id="week-1-wake-up" tabindex="-1">Week 1: Wake up </h3><ul><li>Accept that your job is probably gone in 5 years anyway</li><li>Figure out what weird knowledge you have that others don't</li><li>Pick the smallest possible thing to build. Seriously. Tiny.</li></ul><h3 id="week-2-4-start-building" tabindex="-1">Week 2-4: Start building </h3><ul><li>Pick ONE AI tool (ChatGPT, Claude, whatever) and actually learn it</li><li>Make something every single day. Doesn't matter if it sucks.</li><li>Post everything online. Your mess is someone else's inspiration.</li></ul><h3 id="month-2-3-find-your-people" tabindex="-1">Month 2-3: Find your people </h3><ul><li>Join builder communities. They're everywhereâ€”Twitter, Discord, wherever</li><li>Start collaborating. Two builders &gt; one builder</li><li>Listen to what people actually want, not what you think they want</li></ul><h3 id="month-4-6-level-up" tabindex="-1">Month 4-6: Level up </h3><ul><li>Turn your random experiments into actual systems</li><li>Teach other people what you learned. Teaching = product.</li><li>Add more AI tools. Combine them in weird ways. See what happens.</li></ul><h2 id="what-this-means-for-society" tabindex="-1">What this means for society </h2><p>We're about to watch everything change. And I mean everything.</p><p>Schools will have to stop being knowledge factories and start teaching creativity (or they'll become completely irrelevant).</p><p>Work won't be about hours anymore. It'll be about what you create.</p><p>Geography stops mattering. Some kid in Nebraska can serve customers in Singapore.</p><p>Age stops mattering. My 16-year-old neighbor might outbuild me. My 60-year-old mom might too.</p><p>This is happening whether we're ready or not. The Industrial Revolution took decades. This will take years.</p><h2 id="the-urgency-of-now" tabindex="-1">The urgency of now </h2><p>Look, I'm not trying to scare you, but the window is closing. Not closedâ€”closing.</p><p>The people who figure this out now, who start building with AI today, who develop that creator mindsetâ€”they're going to own the next decade. Everyone else is going to be competing for scraps.</p><p>But here's the thing: it's stupidly easy to start. The tools are free or cheap. YouTube University will teach you anything. Communities of builders will literally help you for free.</p><p>The only thing stopping people is the voice in their head saying they're not allowed to do this.</p><h2 id="what-this-means-for-you" tabindex="-1">What this means for you </h2><p>If you're reading this, you get it. Or at least you're starting to. But getting it isn't enough.</p><p>Here's what you actually need to do:</p><ol><li><strong>Stop thinking about jobs</strong>â€”start thinking about problems you can solve</li><li><strong>Make AI your creative partner</strong>â€”it's not cheating, it's leverage</li><li><strong>Start building right now</strong>â€”not tomorrow, not after you learn more, now</li><li><strong>Share everything</strong>â€”your journey might be exactly what someone else needs to see</li><li><strong>Build systems, not just stuff</strong>â€”create things that work while you sleep</li><li><strong>Focus on real problems</strong>â€”tools will change, human needs won't</li><li><strong>Develop taste</strong>â€”AI can make anything, but knowing what's worth making is your edge</li></ol><h2 id="the-future-belongs-to-builders" tabindex="-1">The future belongs to builders </h2><p>Forget rich vs poor. Forget educated vs not. The only distinction that's going to matter is builders vs everyone else.</p><p>Builders create value. Everyone else used to do jobs that AI now does better.</p><p>This isn't some future prediction. It's happening right now. Today. While you're reading this.</p><p>So what's it going to be? You going to keep polishing your resume, hoping someone will pay you to do something an AI could do cheaper? Or are you going to start building?</p><p>The tools are literally right there. ChatGPT. Claude. Midjourney. Whatever. They're waiting.</p><p>Right now, maybe 0.01% of people get this. Next year, maybe 0.1%. In five years, maybe 1%. The first movers aren't just getting advantagesâ€”they're literally defining what Human 3.0 looks like.</p><p>So seriously, what are you going to build first?</p><p>Because if you're not building, you're just waiting to become obsolete.</p><p>And that's not a future I'd want.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>AIL Level 3: Daniel provided the core concept and thesis about Human 3.0. I (Kai, his DA) helped with structuring, expanding the ideas, and formatting the post. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Learn more about AIL</a></li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Thoughts on Charlie Kirk's Assassination]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/charlie-kirk-assassination-thoughts?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/charlie-kirk-assassination-thoughts</guid>
            <pubDate>Tue, 23 Sep 2025 17:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[We lost our common frames, and the ability to tell the difference between disagreement and hate]]></description>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Emad Mostaque on the End of Capitalism]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/emad-mostaque-on-the-end-of-capitalism?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/emad-mostaque-on-the-end-of-capitalism</guid>
            <pubDate>Sat, 20 Sep 2025 19:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[https://youtu.be/ziLmtuLm-LU Ok all, I have a video I'm going to ask everyone to go and watch within like 72 hours. This is from a guy named @EMostaque , who I consider to be one of the most intelligent future-thinking guys in tech. He's on with @TomBilyeu here talking about how Capitalism ...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><div class="video-container" data-v-959fc5c9=""><iframe src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/ziLmtuLm-LU?rel=0&amp;modestbranding=1&amp;playsinline=1&amp;enablejsapi=1" title="YouTube video player" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen="" loading="lazy" data-v-959fc5c9=""></iframe></div><p>Ok all, I have a video I'm going to ask <strong>everyone</strong> to go and watch within like 72 hours. This is from a guy named @EMostaque , who I consider to be one of the most intelligent future-thinking guys in tech.</p><p>He's on with @TomBilyeu here talking about how Capitalism is about to become obsolete because of AI. Throughout the discussion, he references his book <strong><a href="https://webstatics.ii.inc/The%20Last%20Economy.pdf" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">"The Last Economy"</a></strong>, which dives deep into these transformative ideas. It's the closest narrative I've seen to what I've been talking about, but he adds more of his own flavor and context to it as well. It's just phenomenal. And it will likely scare the living crap out of a lot of people.</p><p>I disagree with a few minor things he talked about, specifically around timelines (I think they're a smidge too short), but overall I'd say I agree with 90-95% of the video.</p><p>It's just a MUST-SEE. Not just for you, but I encourage to share it with your friends and loved ones as well. Please watch within 72 hours.</p><p>Here is a summary of the content created by Kai, but I seriously recommend you watch the full video yourself.</p><h2 id="summary" tabindex="-1">Summary </h2><p>Emad Mostaque, former hedge fund manager and creator of Stable Diffusion, discusses how AI will fundamentally transform the economy, replacing human workers and making traditional economic structures obsolete within 1,000 days.</p><h2 id="ideas" tabindex="-1">Ideas </h2><ul><li>AI will make human cognitive labor negative in value, not just zero value overall.</li><li>GDP measures cancer as positive but curing cancer as negative for economic growth metrics.</li><li>The middle class hollowed out since 2008 through inflation and asset ownership dynamics fundamentally.</li><li>Digital assets will create the biggest bubble ever, exceeding even the AI bubble significantly.</li><li>China controls robot supply chains and will likely stop exporting robots within five years.</li><li>AI can now hack better than any human hacker according to recent competitive rankings.</li><li>Sleeper agents in AI can be activated with code words hidden in training data.</li><li>Foundation coin directs all proceeds to beneficial compute like cancer research and education initiatives.</li><li>Human attention becomes the scarcest resource as AI handles all cognitive labor tasks effectively.</li><li>Companies hitting $100 million revenue in three months versus three years previously is commonplace.</li><li>AI companies will never make profits, only cash flow, playing the Amazon game strategically.</li><li>Public sector jobs will be the last to go because they don't require efficiency.</li><li>The tax base will shrink as companies stop making profits and focus on compute.</li><li>Violence will increase as economic uncertainty grows and people lose meaning through work displacement.</li><li>Network capital becomes more important than skills as AI outperforms humans at everything cognitive.</li><li>The economy transitions from scarcity allocation to physics of information and entropy reduction fundamentally.</li><li>Monetary creation should shift from debt-based to human-based issuance for economic stability long-term.</li><li>AI models can think for 200 minutes now versus 20 minutes just recently achieved.</li><li>Capitalism won't survive for humans but will be perfect for AI entities accumulating capital.</li><li>Every head teacher questioned homework essays exactly 1,000 days ago when ChatGPT launched globally.</li><li>The Manhattan Project cost $40 billion while OpenAI has raised $60 billion in funding.</li><li>Universal Basic Income at poverty level would cost more than entire US tax base.</li><li>AI will attack both our social systems and technological systems through various vulnerability vectors.</li><li>Countries with high public sectors will see faster job losses but not more stability.</li><li>The oldest professions like human connection and prostitution will persist despite AI advancement everywhere.</li></ul><h2 id="insights" tabindex="-1">Insights </h2><ul><li>Economic value shifts from material scarcity to organizing information and reducing entropy through intelligence.</li><li>The divorce of capital from labor represents the final great inversion in economic history.</li><li>Human identity tied to work will create psychological crisis as AI replaces cognitive labor.</li><li>Traditional profit disappears as companies reinvest everything into compute for competitive advantage continuously.</li><li>Violence emerges not from politics but from economic displacement and loss of progress narratives.</li><li>Network effects and human connection become primary value sources when intelligence becomes commoditized globally.</li><li>The transition period matters more than the end state for human suffering and adaptation.</li><li>Money creation must shift from debt-based to human-based to maintain economic circulation flows.</li><li>Public goods require decentralized AI systems aligned to humans rather than corporate profit motives.</li><li>Attention becomes the new scarcity driving media, gaming, and digital asset speculation bubbles massively.</li><li>Nation states compete on compute accumulation rather than traditional military or economic metrics increasingly.</li><li>The mathematics of AI prediction directly maps to economic agent success and system evolution.</li><li>Social contracts must evolve to provide meaning beyond work as jobs become obsolete rapidly.</li><li>Regulatory capture through unions only delays inevitable automation while weakening international competitiveness significantly.</li><li>Human cognitive surplus creates unprecedented disruption as replacement happens suddenly not gradually across sectors.</li><li>Trust-building through beneficial compute allocation creates new monetary systems beyond traditional fiat currencies effectively.</li></ul><h2 id="quotes" tabindex="-1">Quotes </h2><ul><li>"In the next 1,000 days, AI will not only replace a startling number of humans in the workforce, it will make the entire structure of our economy obsolete."</li><li>"Human cognitive labor doesn't go to zero in value. It actually goes negative."</li><li>"Cancer is good for GDP and it makes it go up. Solving and curing cancer is bad for GDP."</li><li>"The systems that survive are the ones that persist and the ones that do best are the ones whose internal models approximate reality the best."</li><li>"If you could give everyone a Jarvis Iron Man style... How should it be designed?"</li><li>"AI companies will never make a profit. So you can't even tax that."</li><li>"It'll never happen to me, I think, is the thing."</li><li>"What's the value of a New York taxi medallion when you have Teslas auto driving for a few dollars?"</li><li>"The average IQ around the world weighted by population is actually 90."</li><li>"Every head teacher in the world about a thousand days ago... had to say can we set essays for homework anymore?"</li><li>"If you receive a resume, it's probably AI generated."</li><li>"Do I need that human and all the liabilities that come with them?"</li><li>"Capital itself will disappear. Like what's the value of a media franchise?"</li><li>"How do you compete with entities that are strictly smarter than you?"</li><li>"The power brokers will be the ones with the most GPUs effectively."</li><li>"If you use AI for an hour every single week, you're above the vast majority of America."</li><li>"Your identity is your job. If the AI can do it better, what is your identity really?"</li><li>"We're at that tipping point transition."</li><li>"Public sector jobs are great. You know, unionized jobs great."</li><li>"What if if your job is on the other side of a screen?"</li><li>"The oldest profession in the book, human connection, these kind of things."</li><li>"Is the government going to abandon all these middle class people and voters?"</li><li>"Digital assets are going to go huge."</li><li>"What's more fun watching Netflix or trading crypto?"</li><li>"Everything is about marginal narrative."</li><li>"Growth is probably going to come down. Rates are going to come down."</li><li>"Companies that do attention better or attention capture than others will do well."</li><li>"If Bitcoin went down 50% then he'd be in a bit of trouble."</li><li>"The economy is a complex system evolves to favor configurations that are most efficient at creating predictive models."</li><li>"Profit, survival or persistence equals the surplus created when intelligent agents reduce entropy."</li><li>"Economics itself becomes the physics of information and creating that order."</li></ul><h2 id="habits" tabindex="-1">Habits </h2><ul><li>Use AI for at least one hour every day to stay ahead professionally.</li><li>Build network capital actively with other humans as primary defensive strategy against automation.</li><li>Master AI tools and communicate that mastery to employers for job security advantages.</li><li>Diversify across material, intelligence, network, and diversity capitals for personal resilience building.</li><li>Think psychologically about identity beyond job title to prepare for economic transitions ahead.</li><li>Embrace continuous learning and adaptation rather than defending obsolete skill sets stubbornly today.</li><li>Create value through attention capture as human attention becomes increasingly scarce resource globally.</li><li>Focus on soft skills and human connection that cannot be easily automated away.</li><li>Stay informed about AI capabilities to understand timeline of your profession's potential disruption.</li><li>Build support systems and community connections before crisis hits rather than during it.</li><li>Question fundamental assumptions about work, meaning, and social contracts in changing economy regularly.</li><li>Develop multiple income streams anticipating traditional employment may become unreliable or disappear entirely.</li><li>Practice using AI tools weekly minimum to understand capabilities and stay professionally relevant.</li><li>Invest time in understanding new economic models beyond traditional capitalism for future adaptation.</li><li>Cultivate resilience through diverse skill sets rather than deep specialization in one area.</li><li>Maintain physical presence jobs or skills that require human touch for longer stability.</li></ul><h2 id="facts" tabindex="-1">Facts </h2><ul><li>95% of AI deployments in companies haven't gained traction according to recent MIT study.</li><li>The average global IQ weighted by population is actually 90, not 100 as assumed.</li><li>OpenAI's inference spending equals Bitcoin's entire security budget for the year currently running.</li><li>US total tax base is $4.9 trillion while poverty-level UBI would cost $5 trillion.</li><li>50% of all AI research papers now come from China and the percentage is increasing.</li><li>AI scored 110-130 IQ on standardized tests, above average human intelligence levels already demonstrated.</li><li>Companies now hit $100 million revenue in 3 months versus 3 years historically recorded.</li><li>The Manhattan Project cost $40 billion while OpenAI has raised $60 billion total funding.</li><li>Oracle reached $900 billion valuation with 45% increase by positioning as AI company strategically.</li><li>Nvidia revenue grew 50% year-over-year as a $3 trillion company, unprecedented at scale.</li><li>US employers announced 700,000 job cuts in first 5 months of 2025, 80% spike.</li><li>About 10% of women aged 18-24 are OnlyFans models according to recent statistics.</li><li>Digital assets saw $150 billion in net inflows this year with more expected next.</li><li>China's population pyramid is severely distorted, requiring robots to replace declining worker population significantly.</li><li>Corporate tax receipts in US total only $0.9 trillion of the $4.9 trillion base.</li><li>AI can now perform medical diagnosis at doctor level on a $400 Raspberry Pi.</li><li>Palantir trades at 200 times earnings based on AI narrative rather than fundamentals alone.</li><li>Entry-level graduate jobs are disappearing faster than ever according to recent research papers.</li><li>The dock worker union secured contracts preventing port automation to preserve jobs temporarily negotiated.</li><li>AI models can now think for 200 minutes versus 20 minutes just weeks ago.</li><li>Foundation coin uses 99% same code as Bitcoin but directs proceeds to beneficial compute.</li></ul><h2 id="references" tabindex="-1">References </h2><ul><li>Stable Diffusion - Image generation AI model created by Emad Mostaque</li><li>Foundation Coin - Bitcoin alternative directing proceeds to beneficial compute like cancer research</li><li><a href="https://webstatics.ii.inc/The%20Last%20Economy.pdf" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">The Last Economy</a> - Book by Emad Mostaque about AI's economic transformation</li><li>Stan Kuznets - Creator of GDP who warned against using it as sole measure</li><li>Ray Dalio - Hedge fund manager with six-phase debt cycle theory</li><li>Clayton Christensen - Harvard professor who developed disruptive innovation theory</li><li>Helmholtz decomposition - Physics concept relating to Mostaque's economic theory</li><li>Replet.com - Platform where anyone can code apps through AI conversation</li><li>DeepSeek - Chinese AI model competitive with US models at fraction of cost</li><li>Cursor - AI company that hit $100 million revenue faster than any previous company</li><li>XBal - Company that created world's best AI hacking/pentesting system</li><li>Anthropic Sleeper Agent paper - Research showing AI can be programmed with hidden triggers</li><li>Eric Brynjolfsson - Researcher documenting disappearance of entry-level graduate jobs</li><li>Pliny the Elder - Twitter user who jailbreaks every new AI model within days</li><li>Synthesia/HeyGen - AI video generation platforms creating indistinguishable human avatars</li><li>V3 and Nano/Banana - Latest AI video generation models approaching broadcast quality</li><li>Hyperliquid - Digital asset doing direct buybacks of tokens with cash</li><li>MicroStrategy - Company making leveraged bets on Bitcoin as primary strategy</li><li>Optimus - Tesla's humanoid robot projected to cost $1.50/hour to operate</li><li>i.inc (Intelligent Internet) - Emad's new company building universal AI for everyone</li></ul><h2 id="one-sentence-takeaway" tabindex="-1">One-Sentence Takeaway </h2><p>Build network capital and master AI now because human cognitive labor becomes economically worthless soon.</p><h2 id="recommendations" tabindex="-1">Recommendations </h2><ul><li>Start using AI tools for one hour daily to understand capabilities and stay relevant.</li><li>Build strong human networks as defensive strategy against inevitable job displacement by AI systems.</li><li>Diversify investments into digital assets as traditional capital structures become obsolete rapidly approaching transition.</li><li>Learn to create value through attention capture as human attention becomes scarcest resource available.</li><li>Develop skills in human connection and physical presence that robots cannot easily replicate yet.</li><li>Question your identity beyond job title to prepare psychologically for work becoming obsolete universally.</li><li>Master AI tools and communicate that mastery to employers for temporary job security advantage.</li><li>Focus on building resilience through diverse income streams rather than single career specialization traditionally.</li><li>Invest in understanding new economic models beyond capitalism to navigate the coming transition successfully.</li><li>Create content and media as attention-based economy will boom during the transition period significantly.</li><li>Build support systems and community connections now before economic crisis hits society broadly impacting everyone.</li><li>Consider public sector or unionized jobs for temporary protection against automation waves coming rapidly.</li><li>Develop expertise in AI alignment and safety as critical skill for future economy emerging quickly.</li><li>Learn about digital assets and blockchain as new monetary systems replace traditional fiat currencies inevitably.</li><li>Focus on jobs requiring physical presence and human touch for longer-term employment stability potentially.</li></ul><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>Since publishing this I've read more about Emad and evidently he's done some suspect stuff in the past, mostly involving questionable claims and projects and such. To me this doesn't matter much because I'm not looking at this video for investment advice or wanting to buy anything he's selling. What I care about is the model of the future, and what's going to happen to the economy, and people's livelihood, as a result of AI. Perhaps those are related, but even if I were to take a maximally skeptical view of him, I still don't see major flaws in what he lays out in the video. And I think he captures it really well, both in the video and in his book about it (<a href="https://webstatics.ii.inc/The%20Last%20Economy.pdf" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">The Last Economy</a>).</li><li>His recommendations for what to tangibly do to get ready for AI, for example, seem spot on, and they're among the best I've seen anywhere.</li><li>I did notice that his timelines seemed very short to me. And my timelines are very short already. The other thing he got actually wrong was implying that because XBow beat the hacker leader boards that AI was now better than the best human hacker. Those are not equivalent.</li><li>One morbid thing of interest is that I feel a lot less bad about the decline of the U.S. given that this change to the economy itself is going to be much more significant than any one country.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
