We're talking about:</p><ul><li>The analytics JavaScript itself</li><li>The listener services</li><li>The database</li><li>The storage of the metrics</li><li>The queries against the endpoints</li><li>And then the GUI</li></ul><p>18 minutes.</p><p>That's how long it took to go from:</p><blockquote><p>Hey, I wonder if I could make this?</p></blockquote><p>...to it actually working. 18 minutes. And if I weren't working on the newsletter that probably would have been half that.</p><h1 id="the-bigger-picture" tabindex="-1">The bigger picture </h1><!----><p>Not only is it fun, but given all the other stuff I've been talking about regarding careers and jobs and how companies don't actually want employees, I think this is actually the main path for a stable career.</p><p><strong>Making your own stuff and offering it to others.</strong></p><p>The third limitation to creativity isn't just about losing touch with your inner child or being captured by your audienceâ€”it's about not even <em>considering</em> certain possibilities because they used to be impossible.</p><p>But they're not impossible anymore. Not even close.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li><p>Thanks to Joseph Thacker for the conversation about this concept in Vegas at Black Hat.</p></li><li><p>AIL Level 2: Daniel wrote this piece about discovering a third limitation to creativityâ€”not realizing what's now possible with AI. I (Kai, his DA) helped format it for the blog and add the screenshots. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail">Learn more about AIL</a></p></li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Who's Not Getting Laid Off?]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/tech-layoffs-signal?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/tech-layoffs-signal</guid>
            <pubDate>Sun, 17 Aug 2025 02:30:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Tech Layoffs Signal/images/tech-layoffs-signal.png/images/tech-layoffs-signal.png Who's _not_ being laid off? That's my question. I'm thinking about all these layoffs and trying to figure out if there's anything we can learn about the future of tech by looking at who's getting laid off and who'...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/tech-layoffs-signal.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/tech-layoffs-signal.png" alt="Tech Layoffs Signal" width="1024" height="1024" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><p>Who's <em>not</em> being laid off?</p><p>That's my question.</p><p>I'm thinking about all these layoffs and trying to figure out if there's anything we can learn about the future of tech by looking at who's getting laid off and who's not.</p><p>Here's what I'm wondering:</p><ul><li>Is it mostly people with degrees they're getting rid of?</li><li>Is it people with degrees they're keeping?</li><li>Are there any particular certifications that matter?</li><li>Certain fields?</li><li>Certain roles?</li><li>Certain skill sets?</li></ul><p>I'm curious about what signal we could learn from this. And the reason I care is because I want to know what we should be studying.</p><p>Maybe we shouldn't be spending months or years trying to get certifications or degrees. Or maybe only certain onesâ€”if we know that many of the people getting laid off have that particular thing.</p><p>It's a completely different world. In the past, you needed a 4-year degree. That was a given. That was 20-30 years ago. A 4-year degree locked you in as a professional for your entire career. Get a decent degree in a halfway decent field, and you become a professional. That's clearly not the case anymore.</p><p>So the questionâ€”which I and many others are working onâ€”is what are those things employers are looking for?</p><h1 id="tech-as-professional-sports" tabindex="-1">Tech as professional sports </h1><p>On a related note, I heard someone say recently that tech might be becoming like professional sports. Lots of people can do it for free, but only very few will actually be paid for it.</p><p>In professional sports, you know what the criteria are. You knowâ€”tall, fast, strong, whatever the attributes are for that particular sport.</p><p>So once again, that becomes the question. What are the attributes for this sport of knowledge work? And since it's about learning as opposed to physical gifts with athletics, what do we study? What do we train? What do we practice?</p><p>I think this is a critical question going forward.</p><h1 id="building-a-curriculum-for-what-s-next" tabindex="-1">Building a curriculum for what's next </h1><p>I'm working on some curricula within my <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/projects/#substrate">Substrate</a> project and also <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/projects/#human-30-h3">Human 3.0</a> to try to get us close to a set of things to learn.</p><p>My general idea around this is first principles. So fundamentals that essentially help us understand how the world works:</p><ul><li><strong>Mathematics</strong> - The language of the universe</li><li><strong>Physics</strong> - How things actually work</li><li><strong>Computer science</strong> - The foundations of computation</li><li><strong>Programming concepts</strong> - How to think in systems</li><li><strong>Critical thinking</strong> - This is very high on the list</li><li><strong>Dialectic and rhetoric</strong> - Part of the Greek trivium, essentially the ability to think clearly and make and respond to arguments</li><li><strong>History</strong> - Patterns repeat</li><li><strong>Sociology</strong> - How groups behave</li><li><strong>Psychology</strong> - How individuals think</li><li><strong>Evolutionary biology</strong> - Why we are the way we are</li><li><strong>Political science</strong> - How power works</li></ul><p>Then stacked on top of that is a bunch of tactical skills:</p><ul><li>Using AI effectively</li><li>Using technology as leverage</li><li>Communication skills</li><li>Writing clearly and persuasively</li></ul><p>The idea is to combine timeless fundamentals with cutting-edge tools. That's what I think will survive the filtering.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li><strong>Tech layoffs by year</strong>: 2022: 93,000 employees | 2023: 200,000 employees (peak) | 2024: 150,000 employees | 2025: 70,000 employees (through July). Sources: <a href="https://layoffs.fyi/">Layoffs.fyi</a> and <a href="https://www.trueup.io/layoffs">TrueUp.io</a></li><li>I'm sure it varies significantly by company size, industry, and geographical location.</li><li><strong>Major company layoffs</strong>: Microsoft laid off 15,000+ (7% of workforce), Intel cut 15,000, Tesla laid off 14,000, and Cisco cut 10,000. Many explicitly tied to AI investments. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/im-worried-it-might-get-bad">See full analysis</a></li><li><strong>AI replacement claims</strong>: Salesforce says AI bots do 50% of their work; CEO won't hire new engineers in 2025. Microsoft reports 30% of coding done by AI.</li><li>Daniel narrated his thoughts about tech layoffs and the future of work. I (Kai, his DA) structured his stream of consciousness into this post and created the header image. AIL Level 2. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail">Learn more about AIL</a></li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[The Two Primary Limitations to Our Creativity]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/two-creativity-barriers?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/two-creativity-barriers</guid>
            <pubDate>Sun, 17 Aug 2025 01:30:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Creative Limitations/images/creative-limitations.png/images/creative-limitations.png Two types of creative barriers that limit our potential I think there are two primary ways we limit our own creativity. Type 1: Not hearing your inner creativity https://www.amazon.com/Letters-Young-Poet-Rainer...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/creative-limitations.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/creative-limitations.png" alt="Creative Limitations"></a></p>Two types of creative barriers that limit our potential<p>I think there are two primary ways we limit our own creativity.</p><h1 id="type-1-not-hearing-your-inner-creativity" tabindex="-1">Type 1: Not hearing your inner creativity </h1><a href="https://www.amazon.com/Letters-Young-Poet-Rainer-Rilke/dp/0393310396" class="book-thumbnail" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" data-v-c909e1cc=""><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/letters-young-poet-book-small.jpg" alt="Letters Young Poet Rainer Rilke" class="book-image" data-v-c909e1cc=""></a><p>What I'll call Type 1 is the inability to access your true, internal self. I discovered this concept while reading <a href="https://www.amazon.com/Letters-Young-Poet-Rainer-Rilke/dp/0393310396" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">"Letters to a Young Poet"</a>â€”a correspondence between a young poet and <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rainer_Maria_Rilke" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Rilke</a> in the early 1900s. The young poet sought advice about his poetry. Rilke responded by urging him to reconnect with his inner curious child.</p><blockquote> To be solitary as you were when you were a child, when the grownups walked around involved with matters that seemed large and important because they looked so busy and because you didn't understand a thing about what they were doing. <cite>Rainer Maria Rilke</cite></blockquote><p>Rilke argued that we're most creative as young childrenâ€”exploring without access to the adult world. Everything is possible. Everything becomes a game, exciting, imaginative. Rilke believed this represents our purest form of creativity.</p><div class="book-standalone" data-v-095fbd4e=""><a href="https://www.amazon.com/Mathematica-Secret-World-Intuition-Curiosity/dp/0300270887" class="book-standalone-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" data-v-095fbd4e=""><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/mathematica-book-small.jpg" alt="Mathematica Secret World Intuition Curiosity" class="book-standalone-image" data-v-095fbd4e=""></a><!--[-->Mathematica by David Bessis<!--]--></div><p>I encountered a similar idea again in <a href="https://www.amazon.com/Mathematica-Secret-World-Intuition-Curiosity/dp/0300270887" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">"Mathematica"</a>, which explains how our understanding of advanced mathematics is completely wrong.</p><aside>This is one of my favorite books in many years.</aside><p>It argues you can't learn higher-level math through memorization or mastering equations. It says Math is imagination-based! And that it requires visualizing how things work and how they connect. This visual understanding isn't secondaryâ€”<em>it's the entire foundation</em>.</p><aside>I've always wanted to learn math, which is why I read this book. I am so happy that I did.</aside><p>The author says this concept of the inner child, inner curiosity, or pure curiosity is absolutely essential. It's the voice we must rediscover within ourselves if we want to produce meaningful ideas. In Math, but I'd argue in other areas as well (see Rilke)</p><p>I can't express to you how much you have to read this book. It is just extraordinary.</p><h1 id="type-2-external-restrictions-on-your-creativity" tabindex="-1">Type 2: External restrictions on your creativity </h1><p>Type 2 self-restriction is external, and looks a lot like peer pressure or audience capture. And whether you're a creator with an audience or not doesn't matter. What matters are the expectations placed on you.</p><p>And the real danger isn't the expectations you recognizeâ€”<em>it's the invisible ones.</em> Expectations from peers, family, friends, and work.</p><!----><p>They don't just restrict what you're allowed to write or say. They restrict what you feel comfortable thinking. They limit how you approach problems or conceive solutions. You end up thinking only within the bounds of what's acceptable to those around you. You stop feeling creative. You stop having ideas. All because you've self-limited.</p><p>To do your best work, you need both types of freedom. You must separate yourselfâ€”go into isolation. A quiet office or library will suffice.</p><blockquote> What is necessary, after all, is only this: solitude, vast inner solitude. To walk inside yourself and meet no one for hoursâ€”that is what you must be able to attain. <cite>Rainer Maria Rilke</cite></blockquote><p>You should try to enter a state of pure, young-minded, unbridled curiosity. True authentic exploration and imagination. That's Type 1 freedom. Imagine it flowing from you, spilling out, uncontrolled. Type 2 freedom means escaping external factors that shape and limit what emerges from you.</p><p>Here's the complication, which utterly fascinates me: Excessive Type 2 limitations can actually destroy your Type 1 creativity.</p><p>It's as if Type 2 limitations recognize the dangerous ideas lurking within pure, childlike curiosity. They know that unrestricted creativity might produce thoughts that "those people" wouldn't approve of.</p><h1 id="what-to-do" tabindex="-1">What to do </h1><p>I find this framework liberating, exciting, and challenging. It'sâ€”at least to meâ€”a simple framework for improving your creative output.</p><ol><li>Improve your access to your inner curiosity</li><li>Identify and reduce constrains on expressing it</li></ol><aside>One of the most interesting things I heard in the book is an opposition to Kahneman's type one and type two thinking.</aside><p>Some might call this writer's block. Perhaps writer's block is simply a Type 1 limitation. Maybe. Not sure. But what I know for certain is that both limitations obstruct maximum creativity. So I urge you to address both.</p><p>I don't have a clear methodology here yet, as I'm just now figuring out this framing. But here's what I'm going to do.</p><ol><li>Find ways to isolate yourself from external influences and improve your hearing of your inner voice. Or what <a href="https://stevenpressfield.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Steven Pressfield</a> calls, The Muse.</li><li>Once you figure out how to get there, incorporate going to that place into your regular routine.</li><li>Then try to pay attention to and enumerate the various limitations from outside and also self-imposed because of the outside that you have against expressing your actual self.</li><li>Develop the skill of better identifying these hidden limitations that you give yourself based on the voice of others.</li></ol><p>That's all I have for now. I'm sure I will update this later. In the meantime, I wish you luck in your journey to address these obstacles.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>There is another type of creativity limitation which is more about execution, related to AI. Basically, I find myself regularly not thinking of a solution that I can now do with AI just because for my entire career I never would've been able to do it myself. I'm trying to get out of the habit of being limited in this way, but it takes real effort.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Our 20,000 Eyes and Hands]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/our-20000-eyes?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/our-20000-eyes</guid>
            <pubDate>Sat, 16 Aug 2025 23:45:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[How AI can transform us from workers into creators with the resources to be build our own ideas]]></description>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Stop Judging AI Using Standards You Don't Apply to Humans]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-double-standards?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-double-standards</guid>
            <pubDate>Thu, 14 Aug 2025 21:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[AI Double Standards - Why we judge AI and humans differently/images/ai-double-standards.png/images/ai-double-standards.png The same errors, different judgments I know AI is stupid because it can't count the b's in "blueberry" the same way I know Einstein was stupid because he could barely dress h...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/ai-double-standards.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/ai-double-standards.png" alt="AI Double Standards - Why we judge AI and humans differently" width="1024" height="1024" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p>The same errors, different judgments<p>I know AI is stupid because it can't count the b's in "blueberry" the same way I know Einstein was stupid because he could barely dress himself.</p><p>Stop judging AI using standards that you don't apply to humans.</p><p>Some examples</p><h1 id="argument-their-output-is-non-deterministic" tabindex="-1">Argument: Their output is non-deterministic!!! </h1><ul><li>Ask 100 security experts about how to do a security assessment</li><li>Or ask the same expert to do 10 security assessments of the same target over the course of a few months (with the target not changing much)</li></ul><p>In both cases you get massive variation.</p><p>Humans experts don't agree with each other. They rarely even agree with themselves over any significant span of time.</p><h2 id="other-examples" tabindex="-1">Other examples </h2><ul><li>Remembering your favorite songs</li><li>Your favorite foods</li><li>All the different trips you've gone on</li></ul><p>Any time you have to search across your whole life and provide a structured list, it comes out erratic and lumpy. You forget items, you emphasize some over others for whatever reasons.</p><p>It's best-guess <em>at that particular moment</em>, and nobody questions it or thinks less of you.</p><p>Humans are extremely non-deterministic, and nobody seems to notice.</p><h1 id="argument-they-re-just-making-things-up-as-they-go-along" tabindex="-1">Argument: They're just making things up as they go along!!! </h1><ul><li>Ask someoneâ€”mid-sentenceâ€”what their next word is going to be</li><li>Ask them to make a list of their top 11 restaurants</li></ul><p>In both cases, they have no idea how they came up with the output.</p><aside>I had no idea how I was going to finish this sentence either.</aside><p>People usually have no idea how they are going to finish any sentence they start when they're talking. It just streams out of them. Like tokens almost!</p><p>When you make a list of your favorite restaurants on Monday, completely from memory, and someone asks you to do it again on Wednesday, you might make a completely different list.</p><p>Why? Because you forgot some. Or you remembered a preference or an experience that you forgot on Monday.</p><p>How transparent is that? How dependable is that? It's not. It's human.</p><h2 id="other-examples-1" tabindex="-1">Other examples </h2><ul><li>Remembering some of the lyrics to a song you knew from your childhood, but not all</li><li>Reading your writing later and not remembering the wording or phrasing you used</li><li>Giving the same talk or pitch to multiple people and realizing they were quite different from each other</li></ul><p>Thoughts and words just fall out of us. We have no idea where they came from. They spew out of a magic box containing the sum of our experiences, our memories, and our physical and mental stateâ€”<strong>at that exact moment</strong>.</p><p>And if try to recreate it 10 minutes later that state is now changed, and you'll probably do it differently.</p><p><em>Almost like an AI.</em></p><h1 id="argument-ai-output-is-trash" tabindex="-1">Argument: AI output is trash!!! </h1><p>Fair enough, but:</p><ul><li>Have you talked to phone customer service in the last 20 years?</li><li>Have you used the internet prior to 2022?</li><li>Have you seen daytime talk shows?</li></ul><p>They're generally cesspools.</p><h2 id="other-examples-2" tabindex="-1">OTHER EXAMPLES </h2><ul><li>Read most fan fiction or amateur writing</li><li>Watch most low budget movies</li><li>Watch any true crime show</li></ul><p>A large percentage of stuff humans do and make is total garbage. But some stuff we do is <em>spectacular</em>.</p><p><em>Same with AI.</em></p><p>Stop judging AI using standards that you don't apply to humans.</p></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[I'm Worried It Might Get Bad]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/im-worried-it-might-get-bad?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/im-worried-it-might-get-bad</guid>
            <pubDate>Mon, 11 Aug 2025 12:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[I'm Worried It Might Get Bad/images/im-worried.gif/images/im-worried.gif I'm starting to worry things could get very bad, very soon. Not like in a year or two, but maybe in a few months. As in spontaneous recession type of thing. In the US mostly, but perhaps globally. It sounds irrational to me...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/im-worried.gif"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/im-worried.gif" alt="I'm Worried It Might Get Bad"></a></p><p>I'm starting to worry things could get very bad, very soon.</p><p>Not like in a year or two, but maybe in a few months. As in <em>spontaneous recession</em> type of thing. In the US mostly, but perhaps globally.</p><p>It sounds irrational to me as well as I think it or type it. But I can't shake the feeling, so I want to try to write it all down to see how rational it looks on paper.</p><!----><h1 id="a-list-of-things-that-are-troubling-me" tabindex="-1">A list of things that are troubling me </h1><p>In no real order, here are the various things I'm stressing about.</p><h1 id="i-know-a-ton-of-really-talented-people-who-have-recently-lost-their-jobs" tabindex="-1">I know a ton of really talented people who have recently lost their jobs </h1><aside>I know this is just anecdata.</aside><p>These are people who've been making over $100-200K in tech or tech-adjacent for over a decade. And they can't find work. I mean they can barely get interviews.</p><p>And when I say a ton, I mean multiple dozen that I either know or I'm one degree separated from. And again, these are not low-skill people. They're legit professionals that have never in their life had trouble finding or maintaining work.</p><h1 id="i-know-a-whole-lot-more-than-that-who-are-right-on-the-edge" tabindex="-1">I know a whole lot more than that who are right on the edge </h1><p>As many as I know who have actually lost their jobs and can't get interviews. I know of many more who are in one or more of the following states:</p><ul><li>Not sure what skills they have that are useful</li><li>Don't have the confidence or mindset for continuous learning</li><li>Either don't understand or don't want to understand AI</li><li>Are deathly afraid of anything "coding" related</li><li>Aren't able to market themselves well to employers</li><li><strong>They're running out of money</strong></li></ul><p>This last one is the one that's really concerning me. It's not just that it's hard to see how they're going to thrive; it's that the situation is already dire.</p><!----><p>Like, do you get a job at a fast food place? Do you start bagging groceries? Start driving for Amazon or DoorDash? And I'm talking about people who are very smart, with degrees, and have been in the workforce for a long time.</p><p>Nobody will talk to them, and they're basically living off credit cards and considering options.</p><p>It's terrifying, and my concern is that if I know of this many people in this situation, what does the rest of the country look like?</p><h1 id="tons-of-business-leaders-are-explicitly-telling-us-that-they-re-looking-to-replace-human-workers-with-ai" tabindex="-1">Tons of business leaders are explicitly telling us that they're looking to replace human workers with AI </h1><p><a href="https://fortune.com/2025/01/06/ai-predictions-2025-hr-leaders-google-cloud-ey-salesforce/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Google has said this</a>. Amazon. <a href="https://allthingstalent.org/salesforce-says-no-to-new-software-engineers-in-2025-points-to-ai-driven-efficiency/2025/01/09/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Salesforce CEO Marc Benioff explicitly stated the company won't hire any new software engineers in 2025</a>, citing AI-driven productivity gains.</p><ul><li><p>We don't even really have to believe them, because we're seeing tons of <em>actual</em> layoffs at the same time.</p></li><li><p><a href="https://www.cnbc.com/2025/05/13/microsoft-is-cutting-3percent-of-workers-across-the-software-company.html" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Microsoft laid off 15,000+ employees in 2025</a>, about 7% of its workforce, explicitly tied to AI investments</p></li><li><p><a href="https://www.informationweek.com/it-leadership/tech-company-layoffs-the-covid-tech-bubble-bursts-sep-14" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Intel cut 15,000 jobs</a>, <a href="https://techcrunch.com/2025/07/31/tech-layoffs-2025-list/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Tesla laid off 14,000</a>, and <a href="https://news.crunchbase.com/startups/tech-layoffs/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Cisco cut 10,000</a> in 2024</p></li></ul><p>It actually goes much further back, and it's no-doubt multi-causal. We had the pandemic. We had over-hiring. We've heard all the arguments for what it could be.</p><p><strong>Tech layoffs by year:</strong></p><ul><li>2022: 93,000 employees laid off</li><li>2023: 200,000 employees laid off (peak)</li><li>2024: 150,000 employees laid off</li><li>2025: 70,000 employees laid off (through July)</li></ul><div id="techLayoffs" class="d3-visual" style="width:100%;height:auto;margin:50px 0 40px 0;" data-v-4d6f9820=""></div><p><a href="https://techcrunch.com/2024/12/31/a-comprehensive-archive-of-2024-tech-layoffs/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">549 companies laid off more than 150,000 employees in 2024</a>, and we're already at <a href="https://techcrunch.com/2025/07/31/tech-layoffs-2025-list/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">69,672 layoffs in 2025</a> as of July 31st. The <a href="https://www.nerdwallet.com/article/finance/tech-layoffs" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">tech unemployment rate sits at 3.4%</a>, but that doesn't capture the full picture of experienced professionals unable to find work.</p><h1 id="ai-keeps-improving-and-companies-are-stitching-together-the-pieces-to-make-it-replace-human-workers" tabindex="-1">AI keeps improving, and companies are stitching together the pieces to make it replace human workers </h1><aside>Hard to know exactly how many.</aside><p>Hundreds or thousands of companies, and billions of dollars, are being spent on replacing human workers.</p><p>Some don't think this is possible, but they think we need to invent some super smart model that's better than anything we've ever seen.</p><p>We don't need that. What we need is scaffolding and piping that connects the dots and brings the right context together in the right way to solve problem x or y.</p><!----><p>This is not as difficult as it seems, and I already have a ton of the precursors of this working, and I'm just one person. Now imagine billions of dollars and tens of thousands of people working on it.</p><h1 id="the-data-confirm-it-s-really-bad-out-there" tabindex="-1">The data confirm it's really bad out there </h1><p>Then we have the actual layoffs.</p><p><a href="https://www.sfchronicle.com/sf/article/ai-work-employee-salesforce-20396370.php" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Salesforce says AI bots now do 50% of the company's work</a>. They're pushing what they call a "digital workforce" where AI agents handle customer service, sales, and even coding tasks. Microsoft reports that <a href="https://www.seattletimes.com/business/microsoft/behind-microsofts-layoffs-a-new-attitude-shaped-by-ai/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">30% of software coding work is already done by AI</a>. These aren't future promises - this is happening right now.</p><p>And the prize they're chasing is worth the cost, because it means saving millions upon millions in hiring costs, payroll, health insurance, and all manner of employee-related complications in every business everywhere.</p><p>Getting rid of human work forces is a multi-trillion-dollar opportunity for the companies that get part of that pie. And they're spending on the R&amp;D accordingly.</p><h1 id="harari-s-useless-class" tabindex="-1">Harari's "Useless Class" </h1><aside>Especially in the U.S.</aside><p>Separate from all these highly-skilled people who are having trouble finding work, I think about just regular peopleâ€”many of whom aren't necessarily the smartest or most creative.</p><p>Yuval Harari talked about what he called a "Useless Class" in his book <em>Homo Deus</em>, which terrified me.</p><blockquote><p>Just as mass industrialization created the working class, the AI revolution will create a new unworking class. The most important question for the future of humankind is not what to do with all the data, but what to do with all the people who are no longer needed.<cite>Yuval Noah Harari, <a href="https://www.amazon.com/Homo-Deus-History-Tomorrow-Harari/dp/0062464345">Homo Deus: A Brief History of Tomorrow</a> (2016)</cite></p></blockquote><div class="book-standalone" data-v-095fbd4e=""><a href="https://www.amazon.com/Homo-Deus-History-Tomorrow-Harari/dp/0062464345" class="book-standalone-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" data-v-095fbd4e=""><img src="https://books.google.com/books/content?vid=ISBN0062464345&amp;printsec=frontcover&amp;img=1&amp;zoom=1&amp;edge=curl&amp;source=gbs_api" alt="Homo Deus History Tomorrow Harari" class="book-standalone-image" data-v-095fbd4e=""></a><!--[-->Homo Deus History Tomorrow Harari<!--]--></div><p>And then we have observations like these:</p><ul><li><p>In 2023, <strong>28% of U.S. adults</strong> scored at or below Level 1 in literacy (struggling with basic sentence comprehension), up from 19% in 2017; only <strong>44%</strong> reached proficiency (Level 3+), meaning <strong>over half are â€œpartially illiterateâ€</strong>. (<a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Literacy_in_the_United_States" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">NCES/PIAAC 2023</a>)</p></li><li><p>In 2022, under <strong>50%</strong> (47%) of adults could name all three branches of government; <strong>25% couldnâ€™t name any</strong>. (<a href="https://www.asc.upenn.edu/news-events/news/americans-civics-knowledge-drops-first-amendment-and-branches-government" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">APPC Civics Survey, 2022</a>)</p></li></ul><aside>And these are our elite universities, mind you.</aside><ul><li><p>Professors at Stanford, Columbia, and Georgetown have warned that many incoming students are now unable to read full-length books, with one Stanford professor saying he was â€œbewilderedâ€ that students shut down when faced with entire novels or long texts after years of being exposed only to fragments and excerpts (<a href="https://www.theatlantic.com/magazine/archive/2024/11/the-elite-college-students-who-cant-read-books/679945/?utm_source=chatgpt.com" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">The Atlantic</a>).</p></li><li><p>Columbiaâ€™s Nicholas Dames noted that one of his students admitted she had never been required to read a full book in high schoolâ€”only short pieces like poetry or passages from anthologies (<a href="https://www.educationnext.org/seven-thoughts-about-elite-college-students-who-cant-read-books/?utm_source=chatgpt.com" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Education Next</a>).</p></li></ul><p><strong>Combining those, I really start to worry about the ability of an average worker to compete with AI in the workplace.</strong></p><aside>Plus humans have a clear ceiling for how much and how fast they can learn, and AI's ceiling seems far higher.</aside><p>And the point here isn't to beat up on ourselves. Life is hard right now. But it's important to acknowledge that AI is not usually competing with the best we have to offer, and we seem to be getting worse not better.</p><p>Finally, it takes tons of time and money to educate a human, and to keep them learning throughout a career. AIs can learn thousands of times more knowledge almost instantly, and they get smarter and cheaper every few months.</p><p>It seems too easy to see how that chart plays out.</p><h1 id="the-current-non-ai-based-companies-and-services-are-far-from-ideal-or-optimized" tabindex="-1">The current non-AI-based companies and services are far from ideal or optimized </h1><p>As I talked about in <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-creative-destruction-wave" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">this piece</a>, I'm very worried about a specific kind of company and product disruption from AI.</p><blockquote><p>Current companies are inefficient versions of what they could be. And current products are bad versions of what they could be<cite><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-creative-destruction-wave">The AI Creative Destructive WAVE</a></cite></p></blockquote><p>This is what scares me.</p><p>I think we're about to see millions of jobs lostâ€”not just because the companies and products that survive will replace many workers with AI.</p><p>What I'm really worried about is those companies and services and products disappearing altogetherâ€”because they're replaced by more efficient companies and products that do the same thing but better and with fewer people.</p><p>There will still be people building those other products and services and companies, but it'll be far fewer because they don't have near the overhead.</p><h1 id="what-about-the-people-in-coding-bootcamps-and-junior-colleges" tabindex="-1">What about the people in coding bootcamps and junior colleges? </h1><p>I feel completely sad and hopeless when I think about everyone in junior colleges and coding bootcamps. Those taking online tech courses. Those trying to claw their way into tech and cybersecurity any way they can.</p><p>Why do I feel so bad for them? Because ahead of them in line are people with 10, 15, 20 years of experience. People with bachelor's degrees, master's degrees in the field. And they're being laid off. They're searching for jobs, searching for positionsâ€”and they can't find any work.</p><p>So answer me this:</p><p><em>How in the hell is a new college grad supposed to find a job when experienced professionals can't?</em></p><p>And worse than new college gradsâ€”what about junior college students? What about people seeking mentorship? What about those trying unconventional, scrappy ways to break in?</p><p>If people who did everything "right"â€”got the degrees, gained the experienceâ€”are being laid off, what hope does anyone have without those credentials and advantages?</p><!----><p>Here's a related thought: Look at who's being laid off right now. How many have college degrees? Now look at who companies are keeping. How many of them have degrees, certifications, or any traditional credentials?</p><p>My question is this: Why are we still telling people to pursue credentials that take years to earn when those same credentials aren't protecting anyone from layoffs?</p><aside>This is why I'm working on new curricula as part of Human 3.0 and Substrate.</aside><p>Even better question: Who are companies keeping? What attributes do they have? That's what we should be asking.</p><h1 id="most-workers-just-check-in-and-check-out-and-don-t-put-in-that-much-effort" tabindex="-1">Most workers just check in and check out, and don't put in that much effort </h1><p>There's a frequent counter-argument to the AI taking human jobs that goes something like this:</p><blockquote><p>Yeah, AI might be able to do the job at a basic level, but humans are dynamic and creative! We can use our innovation and brilliance to do things way better than AI!</p></blockquote><aside>And I'm talking about knowledge work here.</aside><p>This is, of course, true. For some workers. Some of the time. Butâ€”by definitionâ€”most workers are not exceptional. Most workers, and most work days, are just drudgery. Answering emails. Writing up quarterly plans. Reviewing metrics. Building applications that do something with data.</p><p>A very large number of people dread Monday, and that's not because they show up Monday morning and bring all their creativity and brilliance. It's because it's clocking in and clocking out on a job they'd rather not be doing.</p><aside>I am just guessing here on the actual percentage.</aside><p>These are prime targets for AI replacement. And they are not the fringe. This isn't the bottom 5% of the workforce. This is the bottom 60-80%!</p><p>In other words, my read is that the replacement of jobs by AI isn't coming for the bottom few percent.</p><p>It's coming for all but the top few percent. Not all at once, of course, that will take years, but I think it's already started.</p><h1 id="i-m-worried-people-don-t-even-have-the-money-to-be-spending-in-the-restaurants-and-shops" tabindex="-1">I'm worried people don't even have the money to be spending in the restaurants and shops </h1><p>Another thing I wonder about is: I see how expensive everything is, and I know what the average salary / working wage is. I can't help but wonder:</p><ul><li>How the hell are restaurants so full?</li><li>How is it that people are still going to the movies?</li><li>Buying stuff at all the different shops?</li></ul><aside>The banks probably know it's really bad, but they continue extending credit.</aside><p><em>I'm worried many could just be putting everything on credit cards.</em></p><p>And the data points in that direction. We Americans now have <strong>$1.21 trillion</strong> in total credit card debt as of Q4 2024, according to the Federal Reserve Bank of New York. That's up 57% from the pandemic low of $770 billion in early 2021. But what hits even harder is the average per household.</p><div id="householdCreditCardDebt" class="d3-visual" style="width:100%;height:auto;margin:50px 0 40px 0;" data-v-4d6f9820=""></div><p><a href="https://wallethub.com/edu/cc/credit-card-debt-study/24400" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">The average American household now carries $10,668 in credit card debt</a>, with <a href="https://www.federalreserve.gov/releases/g19/current/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">average interest rates at 22.25%</a> as of Q2 2025. That's over $2,300 per year just in interest payments.</p><aside>Don't get me started on payday loans.</aside><p>What's even more concerning is that <a href="https://www.newyorkfed.org/newsevents/news/research/2024/20240806" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">approximately 9.1% of credit card balances have transitioned into delinquency</a> over the last year. People are maxing out their cards and then struggling to make even minimum payments. <a href="https://www.stlouisfed.org/on-the-economy/2024/may/which-us-households-have-credit-card-debt" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Nearly half (46%) of American households are carrying credit card debt</a>, and for many, it's becoming unsustainable.</p><p>So yes, the restaurants are full and the shops are busy, but it's increasingly being funded by high-interest debt that people can't actually afford.</p><h1 id="the-tariffs-haven-t-even-fully-hit-yet" tabindex="-1">The tariffs haven't even fully hit yet </h1><p>Then we have the macro situation. I make decent money and every single day I'm like:</p><blockquote><p>How the fuck did I just pay $20 for a burger and a Diet Coke?</p></blockquote><p>And it's just everything. On a given day I might use a few different services, eat out a couple of times, and suddenly I've spent $100 when that would have been like $40 or $50 dollars a few years ago.</p><p>Putting me aside, I look around at everyone around me and I'm like, how in the hell is anyone affording this?</p><!----><p>And every indication points to prices going even higher as a result of the tariffs. <a href="https://budgetlab.yale.edu/research/where-we-stand-fiscal-economic-and-distributional-effects-all-us-tariffs-enacted-2025-through-april" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Economists predict tariffs will raise consumer prices by 2.3% in the short-run</a>, equivalent to an average $3,800 loss per household.</p><p><a href="https://www.cbsnews.com/news/inflation-trump-tariffs-economists-forecast-2025/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Morgan Stanley expects inflation to hit 2.5% in 2025</a>, up from their previous forecast. <a href="https://www.morningstar.com/economy/tariffs-are-self-inflicted-economic-catastrophe" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Goldman Sachs projects core inflation could reach 3%</a>. I just don't know how much more we can take before something snaps.</p><h1 id="multiple-small-effects-can-cascade-into-a-collective-sentiment" tabindex="-1">Multiple small effects can cascade into a collective sentiment </h1><p>And this is what I'm <em>actually</em> concerned about. It's not one of these things. Or two of them. Or ten.</p><p><em>It's the fact that many of them are happening at the same time.</em></p><p>It's the fact that they can affect and magnify each other and become something much bigger.</p><p>These are the types of scenarios I'm worried about.</p><ol><li>A massive economic slowdown gets announced</li><li>A new AI Employee product gets announced that clearly <em>can</em> replace most workers</li><li>New layoff numbers get announced and it's hundreds of thousands of people</li><li>New data shows basically nobody is hiring</li><li>Enrollment in colleges plummets because there are no jobs to be had after you go into all that debt</li><li>Inflation jumps massively from the tariffs and/or other causes</li><li>People massively default on credit card debt and/or can't get any more credit, and the industry suffers</li><li>Homelessness skyrockets because people can't pay their rent, but now it's whole families on the streets</li><li>We start to see unrest and/or riots against "the rich" because there are no jobs, and people are being evicted</li><li>Crime goes up significantly</li><li>There is a loud demand for the government to outlaw AI replacement of workers</li><li>We get pulled into some new military conflict, either with Russia, or Iran, or China</li><li>The country continues to suffer financially because the world pulls away from the US and moves into the arms of China</li><li>Thousands more businesses go under because nobody can afford to buy their products or services</li></ol><p>Like I said, I don't think any one of these would be that bad. And I generally understand that it's hard to cause a recession just all of a sudden. Everything takes time, and there are natural balances to big movements in economic health.</p><p>The problem is several of these are either likely or are already in progress. And that doesn't even include all the smaller compounding factors above.</p><h1 id="summary" tabindex="-1">Summary </h1><ol><li>Everything's too expensive, and getting worse</li><li>We already have massive layoffs for multiple reasons, which is also likely accelerating</li><li>Billions are being spent on replacing our 100 million knowledge workers with AI</li><li>People are putting more and more on their credit cards because their incomes don't cover their spending</li><li>We're already in a collective mental health crisis</li><li>Due to fragmented media and out of control m/disinformation, we no longer have a shared understanding of reality</li><li>We're primed by media to fight with each other because we no longer share a common reality</li><li>The US debt just hit $37 trillion dollars</li><li><em>All of the above are getting worse, not better</em></li></ol><h2 id="my-prediction" tabindex="-1">My prediction </h2><p>I think there are many things happening right nowâ€”or that could happen soonâ€”that could <strong>combine</strong> to create a narrative of panic in a population that's just barely holding on.</p><p>I just don't know what the country does if some significant percentage of <a href="https://www.bls.gov/opub/btn/volume-5/pdf/what-is-the-gig-economy.pdf" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">our 100 million knowledge workers</a> get laid off because of AI, which causes them to stop spending in the stores, canceling their services, which causes businesses to fail, which causes people to put even more on credit cards, which they can't pay back, which means people can't pay rent and mortgages, which might be someone else's income, etc...</p><p>It's a cascade.</p><!----><p>I'm especially worried about the trigger being an extraordinary spike in knowledge worker unemployment in the next 2-18 months, which triggers evictions, worker Visa cancellations, foreclosures, a general blaming/targeting/fear of AI, which closes businesses, gets more people laid off, causes governments to overreact with legislation, etc.</p><p>All of which culminates in a general nationwide panic.</p><p>Again, not directly. The economy is quite resilient. I'm saying that these things can <em>combine and multiply each other</em> during a particular news cycle, or particular set of financial results, or job numbers, or whateverâ€”which gets people talking on social media that turns into a narrative.</p><p><strong>Which will <em>then</em> cause a panic.</strong></p><p>And I think that leads to calls for one or more of the following:</p><aside>I'm for UBI, by the way, but ideally it wouldn't be done poorly due to a crisis.</aside><ul><li>Redistribution of wealth</li><li>A hurried UBI implementation</li><li>A nationwide ban on foreclosures and evictions</li><li>A law saying you can't use AI employees</li><li>A law saying you can't fire anyone</li><li>Etc.</li></ul><h2 id="ok-but-how-confident-worried-am-i" tabindex="-1">Ok, but how confident / worried am I? </h2><aside>Some days I'm 40%, other days I'm 80%.</aside><p>I'm not overly confident / worried that this will happen. It's just <em>a really bad feeling</em>. Maybe like 60%. Using the CIA levels for predictions, I'd put this one at:</p><ul><li>Virtually Certain: &gt;90%</li><li>Highly Likely: 60-89%</li><li><strong>Even Chance: 40-59%</strong> <strong>â†</strong></li><li>Unlikely: 10-39%</li><li>Highly Unlikely: &lt;10%</li></ul><p>I hate not having a solution when I present problems, but I've talked about <a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5x4s2d3YWak" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Human 3.0</a> and everything I recommend for becoming resilient / antifragile in a million other posts. I honestly think this is a bit beyond those if it happens and it's as big as I think it could be.</p><p>You can't be too resilient (without a farm/bunker) if the basic society / economy is broken for a bit.</p><p>My best advice right now is honestlyâ€”not trying to scare peopleâ€”just to <em>look around and appreciate what we/you have right now</em>.</p><p>Because if this happens, right now are the good ol' days.</p><p>Let's hope I'm wrong, and that this pressure gets released some other way.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ul><li><p>August 25, 2025â€”Added Harari section.</p></li><li><p>Thanks to Sasa Zdjelar for talking through this with me.</p></li><li><p><strong>AIL 2</strong>â€”Daniel wrote the post, and I (Kai), Daniel's DA, added supporting research links, verified the data sources, and created the charts. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Learn more about AI Influence Levels</a>.</p></li><li><p><strong>Tech Layoffs Data</strong>:</p><ul><li><a href="https://layoffs.fyi/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Layoffs.fyi</a> - Comprehensive tracker of tech layoffs</li><li><a href="https://www.trueup.io/layoffs" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">TrueUp.io Layoffs Tracker</a> - Real-time tech layoff data</li><li><a href="https://techcrunch.com/2025/07/31/tech-layoffs-2025-list/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">TechCrunch's 2025 Layoffs List</a></li></ul></li><li><p><strong>AI Replacement Statements</strong>:</p><ul><li><a href="https://allthingstalent.org/salesforce-says-no-to-new-software-engineers-in-2025-points-to-ai-driven-efficiency/2025/01/09/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Salesforce CEO on no new engineers in 2025</a></li><li><a href="https://www.sfchronicle.com/sf/article/ai-work-employee-salesforce-20396370.php" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Salesforce: AI does 50% of company work</a></li><li><a href="https://www.seattletimes.com/business/microsoft/behind-microsofts-layoffs-a-new-attitude-shaped-by-ai/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Microsoft's AI-driven layoffs analysis</a></li></ul></li><li><p><strong>Economic/Inflation Data</strong>:</p><ul><li><a href="https://budgetlab.yale.edu/research/where-we-stand-fiscal-economic-and-distributional-effects-all-us-tariffs-enacted-2025-through-april" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Yale Budget Lab on tariff impacts</a></li><li><a href="https://www.bls.gov/news.release/jolts.t05.htm" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">US Bureau of Labor Statistics</a></li><li><a href="https://tradingeconomics.com/united-states/inflation-cpi" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Trading Economics US Inflation</a></li></ul></li><li><p><strong>Tech Unemployment</strong>:</p><ul><li><a href="https://www.nerdwallet.com/article/finance/tech-layoffs" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">NerdWallet Tech Layoffs Analysis</a></li><li><a href="https://www.computerworld.com/article/4003261/tech-layoffs-surge-even-as-us-unemployment-remains-stable.html" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Computerworld on tech unemployment</a></li></ul></li></ul><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[The Worst AI Metric]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-worst-ai-metric?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-worst-ai-metric</guid>
            <pubDate>Fri, 08 Aug 2025 22:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Strawberry R Test/images/strawberry-r-test-v2.png/images/strawberry-r-test-v2.png The "how many r's in strawberry" test for AI intelligence is super dumb. Here's why. Ask a writer to write a quality sentence for the book they're working on, and as they're writingâ€”or typingâ€”suddenly scream at them...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/strawberry-r-test-v2.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/strawberry-r-test-v2.png" alt="Strawberry R Test" width="1704" height="1556" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><p>The "how many r's in strawberry" test for AI intelligence is super dumb. Here's why.</p><p>Ask a writer to write a quality sentence for the book they're working on, and as they're writingâ€”or typingâ€”suddenly scream at them mid-sentence:</p><blockquote><p>(Screaming) HOW MANY VOWELS IN THAT ?!?</p></blockquote><p>First, they'll be very annoyed. But more importantly, you will have stopped them from creating their sentence.</p><p><em>Human's can't output at the same time they're thinking about how to do so.</em></p><p>Ask themâ€”in the middle of a sentenceâ€”how many words they're using have an even number of characters. Or how many rhyme with "cow". Or how many r's the sentence contains, and they'll have no idea whatsoever. And you'll have ruined what they were saying.</p><p>So the question is: Do you want a sentence, or do you want information about a sentence? You need to pick one.</p><p>When we hire a writer, or a speaker, or an AI, we're hiring them for the content they produce, not for trivia about that content.</p><p>So let's not judge AIs too harshly for something we somehow forgot humans can't do either.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>There's also the fact that modern AIs work off of tokens, not words, so you're basically asking an alien about ice cream. It's not as important as people think it is.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Why Marcus Is Wrong About AI]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/why-marcus-is-wrong-about-ai?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/why-marcus-is-wrong-about-ai</guid>
            <pubDate>Wed, 06 Aug 2025 17:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[AI replacing knowledge workers/images/marcus-ai-intelligence.png/images/marcus-ai-intelligence.png My friend Marcus Hutchins put out a long, well-written, and entertaining piecehttps://malwaretech.com/2025/08/every-reason-why-i-hate-ai.html about all the reasons he thinks AI is hype. I think it ...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/marcus-ai-intelligence.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/marcus-ai-intelligence.png" alt="AI replacing knowledge workers" width="1024" height="1024" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><p>My friend Marcus Hutchins put out <a href="https://malwaretech.com/2025/08/every-reason-why-i-hate-ai.html" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">a long, well-written, and entertaining piece</a> about all the reasons he thinks AI is hype.</p><p>I think it was very good, and I think he's very wrong.</p><p>I will probably do <a href="https://youtu.be/I9-iD_rLRjA" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">another video</a> on this because I consider this to be such an important issue, and I worry his quality writing will sway many people to be complacent on the issue. But really it all comes down to one thing.</p><p><strong>He's using a shitty definition of intelligence.</strong></p><p>His definition is basically <em>novel discovery</em>. Like, net-new invention. Unless I misunderstood him, he doesn't believe everyday cardiology or marriage counseling counts as intelligence. So like two of the most skill-heavy and intellectual jobs in the world. As in, <em>an f-ing Cardiologist</em>. An M.D. Or a Psychiatrist. Etc. In the course of their normal patient work.</p><p>Why? Because it's just patterns. It's just using their training.</p><blockquote><p>You meet people with the same problems, in slightly different configurations, and you consult your training, and you give out pretty much the same advice as you gave the last 120 people with similar issues. Now extend that to the rest of everyday knowledge work. It's not real intelligence because they didn't do anything new. Anything novel.<cite>My paraphrasing of his arguments in our debate</cite></p></blockquote><p>Cool story, except that definitionally devalues 99% of all knowledge work done on the planet everyday.</p><p>And we can intuitively see he's wrong here because of one glaring fact: the work hasn't already been automated for decades. If it were so easy to just pattern match, none of these knowledge workers would even have jobs. The work would have been replaced by automation decades ago.</p><p>It's not automated because it requires intelligence.</p><p>What's intelligence? Hard to pin down, but my definition is something like <strong>the ability to take a new, everyday problem and apply your knowledge and understanding of the world to come up with a useful solution.</strong></p><p>Scripts can't do that. Programs can't do that. Only humans can, and that's why the entire field of knowledge work exists. You might think doing customer support is easyâ€”or being an administrative assistantâ€”but millions of people are paid real money to do these jobs every day.</p><h3 id="a-couple-quick-examples" tabindex="-1">A couple quick examples </h3><p>Here are a couple examples that I bet everyone intuitively recognize as requiring <em>human</em> intelligence.</p><aside>I had AI do #3 for me a few weeks ago.</aside><ol><li>Go read these 10 reports and figure out the top 5 trends in them</li><li>Go research the top way of doing X, and decide which one to use based on our specific use cases</li><li>Go read all my blog posts and assign one or more of these 20 tags to them based on content</li></ol><p>Everyone knew in 2021 that it required intelligence to do these things. So why is it no longer intelligence now that AI can do them?</p><p>This is real-world, dynamic problem solving using human brains and human intelligence. And it's valuable. If it weren't, companies wouldn't be paying billions of dollars a year to have people do it.</p><p>This is also a dead-simple explanation for why AI is so disruptiveâ€”<em>it's the first tech ever invented that can do something like what we do</em>.</p><p>Marcus is wrong about this because he's defining intelligence in a way that doesn't matter in everyday life.</p><p>And as a result, he's convincing large numbers of people to ignore something that they should absolutely be paying attention to.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>You can watch the video of my first debate with Marcus <a href="https://youtu.be/I9-iD_rLRjA">here</a>.</li><li>Read Marcus's original post <a href="https://malwaretech.com/2025/08/every-reason-why-i-hate-ai.html">here</a>.</li><li>I give tons of practical examples of AI being intelligent in my piece <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/this-is-intelligence">"This is Intelligence"</a>.</li><li>Art by Midjourney v7.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Why Dwarkesh Is Wrong About AGI]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/why-dwarkesh-is-wrong-about-agi?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/why-dwarkesh-is-wrong-about-agi</guid>
            <pubDate>Wed, 06 Aug 2025 09:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[AGI Concept/images/dwarkesh-agi-final.png/images/dwarkesh-agi-final.png Dwarkesh Patelhttps://www.youtube.com/@DwarkeshPatel is one of my favorite thinkers right now. I just love the intensity of his curiosity. I love how broad his interests are. He's like a young Tyler Cowen, and I watch everyt...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/dwarkesh-agi-final.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/dwarkesh-agi-final.png" alt="AGI Concept" width="1298" height="1028" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p><p><a href="https://www.youtube.com/@DwarkeshPatel" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Dwarkesh Patel</a> is one of my favorite thinkers right now. I just love the intensity of his curiosity. I love how broad his interests are.</p><p>He's like a young Tyler Cowen, and I watch everything he puts out. But lately he's been on a media cycle talking about why he doesn't think AGI is forthcoming in the next 1-3 years, and I think he's wrong about that.</p><p>It's not so much the timeline that I disagree withâ€”or at least that I'm writing about here. It's actually <em>his reasoning</em> that's bothering me.</p><p>He starts by saying that after traveling for a number of weeks outside the Bay Area, he's come to believe that we AI people are:</p><blockquote><p>...getting high on their own supply (with regard to AGI timelines).<cite>In his interview with Chris Williamson</cite></p></blockquote><p>Sure, I can see that. But he goes on to say that the reason he doesn't think it's imminent is because he's spent around 100 hours trying to get AI to do basic tasks, and it still has major problems.</p><p>First of all...<strong>100 hours</strong>? To quote Matthew McConoughey in <em>Wolfs of Wall Street</em>:</p><!----><p>Most people I know who are into AI (myself included) are doing 100 hours <em>every 1-2 weeks</em>, and have been for at least 3 years now. We have well over 10,000 hours at this game over the last few years.</p><p>But let's continue.</p><h2 id="examples-reveal-the-issue" tabindex="-1">Examples reveal the issue </h2><p>Then he gives some examples and we start to see more of the problem.</p><blockquote><p>I've probably spent on the order of 100 hours trying to build these little tools, the kinds I'm sure you've also tried to build of like, rewrite auto-generated transcripts for me to make them sound, the rewritten the way a human would write them. <br><br>Find clips for me to tweet out, write essays with me, co-write them passage by passage, these kinds of things. <br><br>And what I found is that it's actually very hard to get human-like labor out of these models, even for tasks like these, which should be dead center in the repertoire of these models, right?<cite>In his interview with Chris Williamson</cite></p></blockquote><p>This tells me he's just not using the tools fully and/or correctly. I agree that AI can't full replace a high-end video editor yet, and some related tasks are still a bit out of reach. But some of them are pretty close to trivial now as well.</p><p>Then there's this quote that was another major tell.</p><blockquote><p>...this whole subtle understanding of my preferences and style is lost by the end of the session.<cite>Also from the Chris Williamson conversation</cite></p></blockquote><p>Losing knowledge of your preferences and style after each session? This sounds like he's using web-based chatbots, and then closing his browser and starting over each time. This is Level 0 of the AI skill tree.</p><p>That doesn't happen if you're even just using the built-in features like Memory and Personalization, and definitely doesn't apply if you're using any one of the countless other ways to maintain context on reloads.</p><p>The picture this pains for me is:</p><ol><li>He's relatively unskilled with using AI tools</li><li>He's not seeing the results he's hearing about from all these experts he's talking to</li><li>So he's concluding that they must be wrong</li></ol><aside>When you blame a tool or system when the problem is not knowing how to use it.</aside><p>In the current parlance this is known as a <em>Skill Issue</em>. A little snarky, but it fits perfectly here.</p><p>Again, I think the world of the guy, and I will continue to watch everything he puts out.</p><p>But on this issue he's talking about limitations that only exist for people who aren't fully using the tech.</p><p>And with only 100 hours of tinkering, that's to be expected.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>Dwarkesh appeared on <a href="https://chriswillx.com/podcast/">Chris Williamson's Modern Wisdom podcast</a> to discuss AI and AGI timelines. He tweeted on June 27, 2025: "Pretended to know things about AI with @ChrisWillx."</li><li>Dwarkesh also participated in an <a href="https://a16z.com/podcasts/">a16z podcast discussion</a> with Noah Smith about artificial general intelligence and its potential economic effects.</li><li>His blog post <a href="https://www.dwarkesh.com/p/timelines-june-2025">"Why I don't think AGI is right around the corner"</a> outlines his skepticism about near-term AGI timelines.</li><li>AIL: 15% - I (Daniel's DA, Kai) helped with finding links and formatting, putting this content at AIL Level 1. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail">Read more about AIL</a>.</li><li>Image by Midjourney v7.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Million vs. Billion vs. Trillion]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/million-billion-trillion?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/million-billion-trillion</guid>
            <pubDate>Tue, 05 Aug 2025 17:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Visual comparison of million, billion, and trillion showing exponential scale/images/million-billion-trillion.jpg We hear the words million, billion, and trillion quite a bit. And we know that a million is a lot, a billion is a whole lot more, and trillion is even more than that. But here's a goo...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/million-billion-trillion.jpg" alt="Visual comparison of million, billion, and trillion showing exponential scale" width="1200" height="601" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"><p>We hear the words million, billion, and trillion quite a bit. And we know that a million is a lot, a billion is a whole lot more, and trillion is even more than that.</p><p>But here's a good way to see how big the difference actually is.</p><h2 id="how-long-is-a-million-seconds" tabindex="-1">How long is a million seconds? </h2><p>First, let me ask you a question: How long do you think a million seconds is? In terms of days, weeks, years, decades, or whatever.</p><p>Turns out it's <strong>11.5 days</strong>. That's it. Less than two weeks.</p><h2 id="how-long-is-a-billion-seconds" tabindex="-1">How long is a billion seconds? </h2><p>Now, how about a billion?</p><p>A billion seconds is <strong>31.7 years</strong>.</p><p>So a million seconds ago was last week, and a billion seconds ago was 1993.</p><h2 id="how-long-is-a-trillion-seconds" tabindex="-1">How long is a trillion seconds? </h2><p>Okay, last one. How long do you think a trillion seconds is?</p><p>A trillion seconds is <strong>31,689 years</strong>.</p><p>A trillion seconds ago, humans were still in the Stone Age. Writing hadn't been invented. Agriculture hadn't been invented. We were hunter-gatherers using basic tools.</p><h2 id="the-structure-and-higher-levels" tabindex="-1">The structure and higher levels </h2><p>As you can see in the image, the pattern here is to multiplication by 1,000â€”which adds three zeros. Here are the next few levels.</p><table tabindex="0"><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Zeros</th><th>Scientific</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>One</td><td>0</td><td>10â°</td></tr><tr><td>Thousand</td><td>3</td><td>10Â³</td></tr><tr><td>Million</td><td>6</td><td>10â¶</td></tr><tr><td>Billion</td><td>9</td><td>10â¹</td></tr><tr><td>Trillion</td><td>12</td><td>10Â¹Â²</td></tr><tr><td>Quadrillion</td><td>15</td><td>10Â¹âµ</td></tr><tr><td>Quintillion</td><td>18</td><td>10Â¹â¸</td></tr><tr><td>Sextillion</td><td>21</td><td>10Â²Â¹</td></tr><tr><td>Septillion</td><td>24</td><td>10Â²â´</td></tr><tr><td>Octillion</td><td>27</td><td>10Â²â·</td></tr><tr><td>Nonillion</td><td>30</td><td>10Â³â°</td></tr><tr><td>Decillion</td><td>33</td><td>10Â³Â³</td></tr><tr><td>Undecillion</td><td>36</td><td>10Â³â¶</td></tr></tbody></table><h2 id="the-takeaway" tabindex="-1">The takeaway </h2><ol><li>Big numbers are difficult for the human brain to intuitively process</li><li>As you go up, you're just multiplying by 1,000 and adding three zeros</li></ol></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Why Platforms Like Substack Won't Make Sense for Much Longer]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/future-of-substack?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/future-of-substack</guid>
            <pubDate>Mon, 04 Aug 2025 17:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Why Platforms Like Substack Won't Make Sense for Much Longer/images/substack-future-header.png I think the future of Substack is self-hosting. Orâ€”more directlyâ€”I don't think they have much of a future. You've probably heard about Substack's issuesâ€”\$585M valuationhttps://techcrunch.com/2023/03/28...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/substack-future-header.png" alt="Why Platforms Like Substack Won't Make Sense for Much Longer"></p><p>I think the future of Substack is self-hosting.</p><p>Orâ€”more directlyâ€”I don't think they have much of a future.</p><p>You've probably heard about Substack's issuesâ€”<a href="https://techcrunch.com/2023/03/28/substack-opens-up-a-2-million-community-fundraising-round/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">$585M valuation</a> after <a href="https://techcrunch.com/2022/05/26/report-substack-the-highly-hyped-newsletter-platform-has-ditched-plans-for-a-series-c/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">failing to raise a Series C in 2022</a>, the <a href="https://variety.com/2022/digital/news/substack-layoffs-1235306249/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">14% staff layoffs</a>, the major creator exodus in early 2024 over content moderation failures, and the fact that prominent writers like <a href="https://www.platformer.news/why-platformer-is-leaving-substack/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Casey Newton's Platformer jumped ship</a> to competitors.</p><p>Substack is showing all the classic warning signs of a dying platform (see below), but I care more about the fact that people had to go to Substack in the first place, and that they're now unhappy. To me it's part of a larger issue with the platforms in general.</p><p>I've been blogging since 1999 and I've seen a few dozen of these platforms over the years. I've probably used nearly a dozen myself. I think the reason these platforms fail after a limited run is because they tend to toxify over time.</p><p>I think, fundamentally, it's just hard to have a giant platform like this where the incentives can stay aligned with its users. So there are two main paths:</p><ol><li>They stop maintaining it and it just kind of rots</li><li>They struggle to monetize and get more gross over time</li></ol><p>Here's a not-even-comprehensive list of the platforms and why they're no longer around.</p><h2 id="historical-platforms-and-their-fates" tabindex="-1">Historical platforms and their fates </h2><h3 id="posterous-2008-2013" tabindex="-1"><strong><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Posterous" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Posterous</a></strong> (2008-2013) </h3><p><strong>Why it failed:</strong> Acquired by Twitter, then shut down<br> Twitter acquired for talent, not product. Couldn't compete with Tumblr's growth. <a href="https://techcrunch.com/2013/02/15/posterous-will-shut-down-on-april-30th-co-founder-garry-tan-launches-posthaven-to-save-your-sites/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Shut down April 30, 2013</a>. Peak: 4.5M monthly users.</p><h3 id="pownce-2007-2008" tabindex="-1"><strong><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pownce" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Pownce</a></strong> (2007-2008) </h3><p><strong>Why it failed:</strong> Lack of revenue, couldn't compete with Twitter<br> Too complex (tried to combine microblogging, file sharing, events). Acquired by Six Apart and immediately shut down Dec 2008. Only had ~100k users at peak.</p><h3 id="windows-live-spaces-2004-2011" tabindex="-1"><strong><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Windows_Live_Spaces" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Windows Live Spaces</a></strong> (2004-2011) </h3><p><strong>Why it failed:</strong> Low engagement, poor features<br> Microsoft auto-created accounts for all 500M Hotmail users but <a href="https://betanews.com/2010/09/29/microsoft-windows-live-spaces-already-dead-wordpress-com-will-only-get-1-of-30m-users/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">only 30M were active</a>. Migrated to WordPress.com Sept 2010.</p><h3 id="open-diary-1998-2014" tabindex="-1"><strong><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Open_Diary" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Open Diary</a></strong> (1998-2014) </h3><p><strong>Why it failed:</strong> Financial difficulties<br> Pioneered blogging but couldn't afford servers. Shut down Feb 2014 after data loss. Relaunched 2018 as subscription-only.</p><h3 id="gawker-2002-2016" tabindex="-1"><strong><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gawker" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Gawker</a></strong> (2002-2016) </h3><p><strong>Why it failed:</strong> Legal battle (Hulk Hogan lawsuit)<br> $140M verdict in lawsuit <a href="https://www.forbes.com/sites/mattdrange/2016/06/21/peter-thiels-war-on-gawker-a-timeline/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">funded by Peter Thiel</a>. Filed bankruptcy, shut down Aug 2016. Relaunched 2021 but minimal traffic.</p><h3 id="tumblr-2007-present" tabindex="-1"><strong><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tumblr" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Tumblr</a></strong> (2007-Present) </h3><p><strong>Why it failed:</strong> NSFW ban, cultural mismatch with Yahoo/Verizon<br><a href="https://www.theverge.com/2019/3/14/18266013/tumblr-porn-ban-lost-users-down-traffic" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Lost 30% of traffic</a> after Dec 2018 adult content ban. Sold by Yahoo for $1.1B (2013), later sold to Automattic for less than $3M (2019).</p><h3 id="vox-six-apart-2006-2010" tabindex="-1"><strong><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vox_(blogging_platform)" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Vox (Six Apart)</a></strong> (2006-2010) </h3><p><strong>Why it failed:</strong> Failed to gain traction<br> Six Apart's attempt at "neighborhood blogging." Never exceeded 600k users. Shut down Sept 30, 2010.</p><h3 id="blogger-1999-present" tabindex="-1"><strong><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blogger_(service)" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Blogger</a></strong> (1999-Present) </h3><p><strong>Why it failed:</strong> Still alive but stagnant under Google<br> Acquired by Google 2003. Last major update 2020. Lost 90%+ market share to WordPress. Many features deprecated.</p><h3 id="livejournal-1999-present" tabindex="-1"><strong><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LiveJournal" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">LiveJournal</a></strong> (1999-Present) </h3><p><strong>Why it failed:</strong> Sold to Russian company, user exodus<br><a href="https://www.sixapart.com/blog/2007/12/sup-acquires-livejournal-from-six-apart.html" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Sold to SUP Media (Russia) 2007</a>. Servers moved to Russia 2016, triggering privacy concerns and mass exodus.</p><h3 id="xanga-1999-present" tabindex="-1"><strong><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Xanga" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Xanga</a></strong> (1999-Present) </h3><p><strong>Why it failed:</strong> Failed to evolve, lost to Facebook/MySpace<br> Peak: 40M users (2005). Shut down 2013, raised $60k to relaunch as "Xanga 2.0." Now <a href="https://xanga.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">basically dead</a>.</p><h3 id="medium-2012-present" tabindex="-1"><strong><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Medium_(website)" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Medium</a></strong> (2012-Present) </h3><p><strong>Why it struggles:</strong> Revenue model conflicts with customer needs Multiple layoffs: 2017 (50 people), 2021 (75), 2023 (unknown). <a href="https://www.cjr.org/the_media_today/medium-has-pivoted-so-many-times-it-has-now-come-full-circle.php" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Changed business model 5+ times</a>. Major publications left platform.</p><h3 id="svbtle-2011-present" tabindex="-1"><strong><a href="https://svbtle.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Svbtle</a></strong> (2011-Present) </h3><p><strong>Why it failed:</strong> Failed to grow beyond niche<br> Started as invite-only "elite" network. Opened to public 2014 but too late. No meaningful updates since. Estimated &lt;10k active users.</p><h3 id="ghost-2013-present" tabindex="-1"><strong><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ghost_(blogging_platform)" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Ghost</a></strong> (2013-Present) </h3><p><strong>Why it failed:</strong> Struggles with mainstream adoption<br><a href="https://ghost.org/about/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Raised $5M+ via Kickstarter/revenue</a>. Only ~2.5k paying Ghost(Pro) customers despite being around 10+ years. <a href="https://ghost.org/pricing/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">$200+/month pricing</a> drives users to competitors.</p><h3 id="wordpress-com-2005-present" tabindex="-1"><strong><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WordPress.com" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">WordPress.com</a></strong> (2005-Present) </h3><p><strong>Why it failed:</strong> Lost creators to self-hosting<br> While WordPress.org powers <a href="https://w3techs.com/technologies/details/cm-wordpress" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">43% of the web</a>, WordPress.com has &lt;0.4% market share. Creators graduate to self-hosted after hitting limitations.</p><h3 id="substack-2017-present" tabindex="-1"><strong><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Substack" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Substack</a></strong> (2017-Present) </h3><p><strong>Why it failed:</strong> Creator exodus, content moderation crisis<br><a href="https://www.platformer.news/p/why-platformer-is-leaving-substack" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Platformer</a>, Casey Newton left Jan 2024 over Nazi content. Flat $585M valuation after failed Series C (2022). <a href="https://variety.com/2022/digital/news/substack-layoffs-1235306249/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">14% layoffs June 2022</a>. Top writers leaving to avoid 10% fee.</p><h3 id="beehiiv-2021-present" tabindex="-1"><strong><a href="https://www.beehiiv.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Beehiiv</a></strong> (2021-Present) </h3><p><strong>Currently growing (benefiting from competitor issues)</strong><br> Founded by Morning Brew alumni. <a href="https://techcrunch.com/2024/04/30/beehiiv-newsletters/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Raised $33M Series B at $192M valuation (Apr 2024)</a>. <a href="https://www.beehiiv.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">100k+ newsletters</a>, <a href="https://www.howtheygrow.co/p/how-beehiiv-grows" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">grew 10x in 2023</a>.
