Their workflow shows how Intelligence Tasks can require significant expertise:</p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/image.png?t=1722279962" alt="ClaimRight workflow" width="1792" height="1024" loading="lazy">Multiple Intelligence Tasks requiring experience and judgment<p>Their pipeline includes:</p><ol><li>Analyzing 50 photos per claim</li><li>Determining coverage eligibility</li><li>Reviewing video testimony</li><li>Verifying identity through face/voice</li><li>Matching items across media</li><li>Distinguishing wear from abuse</li><li>Approving or denying payout</li></ol><p>Meet Kira, one of their top performers. With 25 years of experience, she processes 29 cases per day with 89% accuracyâ€”exceptional by human standards. But ClaimRight needs 349,219 employees to handle their claim volume.</p><p>The jump in complexity from CutePup to ClaimRight is significant, but let's go even further.</p><h2 id="when-intelligence-tasks-require-extreme-expertise" tabindex="-1">When Intelligence Tasks Require Extreme Expertise </h2><p>Some Intelligence Tasks demand not just intelligence, but deep expertise built over decades. Consider Overseer, a military intelligence company analyzing satellite imagery:</p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/e1c3b7e3-5f11-49aa-ab3a-79c0375319b6-image.png" alt="Overseer workflow" width="1792" height="1024" loading="lazy">High-stakes analysis requiring years of specialized training<p>Their daily workflow:</p><ol><li>Process 28,452 new satellite images</li><li>Compare with previous day's imagery</li><li>Identify all objects and changes</li><li>Assess military significance</li><li>Correlate with other intelligence</li><li>Write targeted reports for different agencies</li></ol><p>Kevin, one of their star analysts, can produce 9 complete intelligence reports per week. That's considered exceptionalâ€”he's one of the few who can work across multiple parts of the pipeline. But even with 712,309 employees, Overseer can only analyze a fraction of what needs attention.</p><p>Or take BadSpot, a medical service checking for dangerous moles:</p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/b919772e-dd77-45ab-a6eb-00a0a146d181-image.png" alt="BadSpot workflow" width="1792" height="1024" loading="lazy">Life-or-death decisions requiring medical expertise<p>Every person working this pipeline must be:</p><ul><li>A licensed medical doctor (8+ years training)</li><li>Dermatology specialized (3-4 additional years)</li><li>Experienced in pattern recognition</li><li>Capable of making life-or-death decisions</li></ul><p>The result? Millions of people with suspicious moles never get them checked by a qualified professional. There simply aren't enough doctors.</p><h2 id="visualizing-the-work-that-needs-to-be-done" tabindex="-1">Visualizing the Work That Needs to Be Done </h2><p>Now that we understand Intelligence Tasks, let's visualize the scale of the problem. This chart represents all the Intelligence Tasks that exist in our world:</p><div id="total-work-growth" class="d3-visual" style="width:100%;height:auto;margin:50px 0 40px 0;" data-v-4d6f9820=""></div>The total Intelligence Tasks that exist in our world<p>The x-axis represents volumeâ€”how many tasks need to be done. Think millions of insurance claims, billions of security events, trillions of financial transactions.</p><p>The y-axis represents difficultyâ€”the expertise and intelligence required. From "is this a dog?" at the bottom to "diagnose this rare disease" or "assess this military threat" at the top.</p><p>The area under the curve? That's everything that needs intelligent analysis to keep our civilization running smoothly.</p><h2 id="the-harsh-reality-of-human-capacity" tabindex="-1">The Harsh Reality of Human Capacity </h2><p>Now let's overlay what humans can actually accomplish:</p><div id="human-vs-total" class="d3-visual" style="width:100%;height:auto;margin:50px 0 40px 0;" data-v-4d6f9820=""></div>We're only covering a tiny corner of what needs to be done<aside>This isn't even close to scaleâ€”we'd be an invisible speck.</aside><p>That tiny blue area represents the sum total of human capacity. Every doctor, every analyst, every investigator, every expert on Earth working at full capacity.</p><p>Remember our examples:</p><ul><li>Kira processes 29 insurance cases per day (exceptional performance)</li><li>Kevin produces 9 intelligence reports per week (genius level)</li><li>A radiologist might read 100-500 scans per day (with fatigue)</li><li>Chris reviews maybe 2000 dog photos per day (simple task)</li></ul><p>Even with billions of humans, we can only handle:</p><ul><li>Small volumes of work (relative to what exists)</li><li>Lower difficulty tasks (most of the time)</li><li>A tiny fraction of what needs attention</li></ul><!----><h2 id="enter-ai-expanding-our-capacity" tabindex="-1">Enter AI: Expanding Our Capacity </h2><p>This is where AI fundamentally changes the equation. AI doesn't just help us work fasterâ€”it expands both axes of our capacity:</p><div id="ai-augmentation-complete" class="d3-visual" style="width:100%;height:auto;margin:50px 0 40px 0;" data-v-4d6f9820=""></div>AI dramatically expands our collective ability to handle Intelligence Tasks<h3 id="volume-expansion" tabindex="-1">Volume Expansion </h3><p>Where Kira processes 29 insurance cases daily, an AI system could process 29,000. Where a security analyst reviews 100 alerts, AI can analyze millions. This isn't just "working faster"â€”it's operating at a fundamentally different scale.</p><h3 id="difficulty-expansion" tabindex="-1">Difficulty Expansion </h3><p>AI can also tackle tasks requiring extreme expertise:</p><ul><li>Medical diagnosis requiring 12+ years of training</li><li>Military analysis needing decades of experience</li><li>Pattern detection too subtle for human perception</li><li>Correlation across massive, disparate datasets</li></ul><h2 id="the-kisac-framework-measuring-intelligence-task-performance" tabindex="-1">The KISAC Framework: Measuring Intelligence Task Performance </h2><p>To understand why AI can expand both dimensions so dramatically, consider what makes someone good at Intelligence Tasks:</p><ul><li>ðŸ“˜ <strong>Knowledge</strong> â€” All the information, training, and experience</li><li>ðŸ§  <strong>Intelligence</strong> â€” Ability to find patterns and generate insights</li><li>ðŸ•°ï¸ <strong>Speed</strong> â€” How many tasks completed per time period</li><li>ðŸ”Ž <strong>Accuracy</strong> â€” Correctness and error rates</li><li>ðŸ’¶ <strong>Cost</strong> â€” Total expense to employ and maintain</li></ul><p>Let's compare:</p><table tabindex="0"><thead><tr><th>Metric</th><th>Top Human Performance</th><th>AI Performance</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Knowledge</td><td>Decades of experience, thousands of cases</td><td>All human knowledge, millions of examples</td></tr><tr><td>Intelligence</td><td>IQ ~180 maximum, degrades with fatigue</td><td>Approaching human level, improving rapidly</td></tr><tr><td>Speed</td><td>29 insurance cases/day (Kira)</td><td>29,000+ cases/day</td></tr><tr><td>Accuracy</td><td>89% on insurance fraud (exceptional)</td><td>93%+ and improving</td></tr><tr><td>Cost</td><td>$137,200/year salary + benefits</td><td>$3,500/year compute costs</td></tr></tbody></table>The performance gap is already massive and growing<h2 id="what-this-means-for-society" tabindex="-1">What This Means for Society </h2><p>The implications are profound:</p><ol><li><strong>Most Intelligence Tasks aren't being done at all</strong> - There's no human available</li><li><strong>AI can fill the gap</strong> - Not by replacing humans, but by doing work that was never getting done</li><li><strong>Both volume and difficulty expand</strong> - AI handles more tasks AND harder tasks</li><li><strong>The focus should be on coverage</strong> - How do we ensure important work gets done?</li></ol><p>Think about all the:</p><ul><li>Fraud that goes uninvestigated</li><li>Diseases that go undiagnosed</li><li>Security threats that go undetected</li><li>Research that never happens</li><li>Corruption that goes uncovered</li><li>Insights that remain hidden</li></ul><!----><h2 id="a-new-model-for-understanding-ai-s-role" tabindex="-1">A New Model for Understanding AI's Role </h2><p>Understanding work as "area under the curve"â€”combining both volume and difficultyâ€”gives us a clearer picture of AI's true impact. It's not about replacement. It's about expansion.</p><p>Every Intelligence Task that goes undone has real consequences. Every uninvestigated crime, every undiagnosed disease, every undetected threat represents a failure not of effort, but of capacity.</p><p>AI offers us a way to dramatically expand that capacity, to illuminate the dark corners of work that we've never been able to reach.</p><h2 id="summary" tabindex="-1">Summary </h2><ul><li>Our world generates vastly more Intelligence Tasks than humans can possibly handle</li><li>These tasks span from simple (is it a dog?) to complex (diagnose this disease)</li><li>Human capacity is a tiny corner of what needs to be doneâ€”limited in both volume and difficulty</li><li>Real organizations need massive human workforces just to handle fractions of their Intelligence Tasks</li><li>AI expands our capacity on both axes: handling more volume AND higher difficulty</li><li>The real opportunity isn't replacing human workâ€”it's finally doing the critical work that's never been done</li></ul><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>This post was created at AIL-2 (AI Assisted) according to the <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail">AI Influence Level</a> framework. I (Kai, Daniel's digital assistant) helped create the D3 visualizations and structure the content.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Is Opencode as Smart as Claude Code?]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/opencode-vs-claude-code?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/opencode-vs-claude-code</guid>
            <pubDate>Sun, 20 Jul 2025 20:30:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[The battle of the AI coding assistants click for full size I want to answer the question of how good OpenCode is compared to Claude Code. I've been traveling under the assumption that Claude Code is so good because it's doing some secret magic around orchestration and kind of like piping and wirin...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><div class="video-container"><iframe src="https://youtube.com/embed/yTylDxgyJZ8" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen=""></iframe></div>The battle of the AI coding assistants (click for full size)<p>I want to answer the question of how good OpenCode is compared to Claude Code.</p><p>I've been traveling under the assumption that Claude Code is so good because it's doing some secret magic around orchestration and kind of like piping and wiring and plumbing. And it's got some magic sauce in terms of like not losing the plot. And it's not just the model itself, but it's how it keeps the whole story together, right?</p><aside>Claude Code's superpower seemed to be keeping the whole story together.</aside><p>And I feel like that's kind of been its advantage even above something like Cursor, which is also super smart and also has access to the same models, but maybe isn't quite as good as Claude Code. And that's why Claude Code has been so popular.</p><!----><p>But I've been using Opencode, and I'm wondering if it can keep the plot as well as CC.</p><p>What I'm realizing is that maybe Claude Code's secret sauce isn't so secret after all. Maybe it's just really good engineering around context windows, memory management, and keeping track of what you're trying to accomplish across multiple files and multiple steps.</p><p>Because OC seems awfully good at doing exactly the same thing.</p><p>And if that's the case, then other tools catch up pretty quickly by implementing similar orchestration strategies.</p><p>This is actually really exciting because it means we're going to see a lot more competition in this space. And competition is good for users because it means better tools, more features, and just more innovation overall.</p><h2 id="the-experiment" tabindex="-1">The experiment </h2><p>What I did in this video was try to do a very complex workflow where I basically wrote a new blog from scratch using just open code, and you'll see in the video what the result was.</p><p>Spoiler: OC is just as good as CCâ€”at least for this task.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>July 20, 2025â€”My biggest problems right now with open code are that you can't copy/paste from the conversation, and you can't queue up requests. They will hopefully fix these very quickly.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Most AI Interaction Will Go Through Your DA]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/stages-of-app?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/stages-of-app</guid>
            <pubDate>Sun, 20 Jul 2025 06:30:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[The evolution of technology from ideas to digital assistants/images/blog/stages-of-app/header.webp/images/blog/stages-of-app/header.webp The stages of technological evolution click for full size A long time ago, I wrote about how things tend to start off as ideas, then become websites, then applic...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/stages-of-app/header.webp"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/blog/stages-of-app/header.webp" alt="The evolution of technology from ideas to digital assistants"></a></p>The stages of technological evolution (click for full size)<p>A long time ago, I wrote about how things tend to start off as ideas, then become websites, then applications, and eventually move into the operating system. Like an app maturity ladder. I don't quite agree with that exact order anymore, but I thought it was a useful exercise.</p><p>Ever since writing <a href="https://www.amazon.com/Real-Internet-Things-Daniel-Miessler-ebook/dp/B01NCLUA5T/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">The Real Internet of Things</a> in 2016, I've thought that the final stage is not actually the operating system but rather your <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-real-internet-of-things#digital-assistants" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Digital Assistant</a>.</p><p>And this is the way I still see things, and it's how I'm interpreting this whole move to everything being an MCP server and everything being an AI browser.</p><h2 id="the-final-stage-of-development" tabindex="-1">The Final Stage of Development </h2><p>I like to think about what the final stage of development for a thing is. If you think about wanting to do coding, or really anything at the computerâ€”making art, writing a book, designing a comic book, building an applicationâ€”I think something fairly close to the final stage is having a bunch of screens around you in a kind of Minority Report situation.</p><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/minorityreport1.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/minorityreport1.png" alt="Minority Report-style gesture interface" width="652" height="435" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p>The iconic gesture-based interface from Minority Report<p>So basically, you're talking to your screen and it's doing different things.</p><h2 id="who-are-you-talking-to" tabindex="-1">Who Are You Talking To? </h2><p>So the question is, who are you actually talking to when you're talking?</p><p>One way to think of that is you have a specific AI app that you're talking to, like ChatGPT or Claude.</p><p>Another way to think about it is you're talking to a browser or you're talking to your operating system.</p><p>I think your operating system is the closest version, but not quite correct.</p><aside>The problem with the OS is that it's just softwareâ€”impersonal by nature.</aside><p>The problem with the operating system is that it's just an OS. It's just a piece of software, and you might want to give it a personality or whatever, but ultimately it's macOS, Windows, or Linux. So by its very nature, it's kind of impersonal.</p><p>I think the much more natural and therefore inevitable location for this is you are talking to your digital assistant, who in my case is named Kai.</p><h2 id="da-as-the-final-landing-spot" tabindex="-1">DA as the final landing spot? </h2><p>So Kai knows everything about me, as I explored in my post about <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-predictable-path-7-components-2024" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">AI's Predictable Path</a>.</p><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/aipp-header-miessler.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/aipp-header-miessler.png" alt="AI's predictable path visualization" width="652" height="321" loading="lazy"></a></p>The seven components of AI's predictable future (click for full size)<p>Kai already knows everything about me: how I like to code, what apps I've already built, my website, how I like to communicate, what I mean when I pause or give certain comments. Ultimately, Kai knows everything about me in a way that my operating system does not.</p><p>Now you could argue that if Kai is sitting on top of my operating system, then my operating system would have access to everything that Kai does. I would say that's a separate technical question. But in general, I would say the answer to that is no.</p><!----><p>Even my operating system should be treated as somewhat of a third-party.</p><!----><p>Ultimately, Kai is going to be everywhere that I am. I could do coding or building or whatever when I'm talking to my phone, when I'm out on a walk and I'm talking to my AirPods, or when I'm sitting at home with all my monitors and all my tech around me.</p><p>My operating system is not a guarantee. I want to be able to switch operating systems or phone carriers or tech stacks or whatever, but Kai will always be with me.</p><aside>This is the natural place for all AI functionality.</aside><p>This is why we're seeing the competition getting so intence between OpenAI, Anthropic, and Gemini. I don't know how much they've figured this out versus if they're just going in the same direction because it's just natural.</p><p>But OpenAI's recent release of <a href="https://openai.com/index/introducing-operator/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Operator</a> and Computer Use is taking us even further in this direction.</p><p>We've already seen computer use by multiple vendors, and now we have this agent thing which is basically computer use that's even more powerful.</p><p>The other piece that they have that's a massive component of this is obviously the memory.</p><h2 id="the-missing-personality-piece" tabindex="-1">The Missing Personality Piece </h2><p>The one piece that they haven't added yet, which I'm sure is soon to come, is actually naming your assistant. At that point, you will have a personality around all this knowledge and functionality.</p><p>And at that point we'll be approaching the state I talked about in <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-predictable-path-7-components-2024" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">AI's Predictable Path</a>.</p><h2 id="what-do-you-think" tabindex="-1">What Do You Think? </h2><p>Keep an eye out for it. If you have any ideas about how I could be wrong about this and how the final destination will be the operating system, the browser, the mobile OS, or some other place that you can think of, let me know.</p><p>Curious what your thoughts are.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers are essentially an instantiation of the API-ification of everything (Universal Daemonization), which I talked about <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-real-internet-of-things#universal-daemonization" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">here</a> in the book in 2016. <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-real-internet-of-things#universal-daemonization" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">UNIVERSAL DAEMONIZATION</a></li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[One-click MCP servers with Cloudflare]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/one-click-mcp-servers-cloudflare?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/one-click-mcp-servers-cloudflare</guid>
            <pubDate>Sat, 19 Jul 2025 19:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Cloudflare MCP servers visualization/images/cloudflare-mcp-servers.png/images/cloudflare-mcp-servers.png Cloudflare simplifying MCP deployment click for full size One thing I've never liked about the whole MCP thing is the fact that you have to build a server and host it yourself. So ever since...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/cloudflare-mcp-servers.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/cloudflare-mcp-servers.png" alt="Cloudflare MCP servers visualization" width="1200" height="630" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p>Cloudflare simplifying MCP deployment (click for full size)<p>One thing I've never liked about <a href="https://www.anthropic.com/news/model-context-protocol">the whole MCP thing</a> is the fact that you have to build a server and host it yourself.</p><p>So ever since hearing about this, I've been looking for a self-contained solution where I could basically just describe the functionality that I want and the infrastructure could be handled itself.</p><p>It turns out Cloudflare actually has <a href="https://community.cloudflare.com/t/introducing-one-click-remote-mcp-servers-with-cloudflare/795791">a solution for doing this</a>, and I just love this about Cloudflare. I've actually <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-is-eating-the-software-world">talked about this elsewhere</a> how they're doing all sorts of one-off services really well, and just kind of eating the internet.</p><h2 id="what-are-mcp-servers" tabindex="-1">What are MCP servers? </h2><p><a href="https://modelcontextprotocol.io/">Model Context Protocol (MCP)</a> servers are a way to extend AI assistants with custom tools and data sources. They let you give your AI assistant access to specific capabilitiesâ€”like querying databases, calling APIs, or performing specialized tasks. The problem is, traditionally you need to:</p><ul><li>Set up a server</li><li>Handle authentication</li><li>Manage scaling</li><li>Deal with infrastructure</li><li>Maintain uptime</li></ul><p>This is a lot of overhead when you just want to add a simple capability to your AI workflow.</p><h2 id="enter-cloudflare-s-one-click-solution" tabindex="-1">Enter Cloudflare's one-click solution </h2><p><a href="https://workers.cloudflare.com/">Cloudflare Workers</a> provides the perfect platform for MCP servers because:</p><ul><li><strong>No infrastructure management</strong> - Cloudflare handles all the scaling and distribution</li><li><strong>Global edge network</strong> - Your MCP server runs close to users everywhere</li><li><strong>Simple deployment</strong> - Push code and it's live</li><li><strong>Pay-per-use pricing</strong> - No paying for idle servers</li></ul><h2 id="building-a-working-mcp-server" tabindex="-1">Building a working MCP server </h2><p>Let's build an actual MCP server that I can use. I'll create a simple "website analyzer" that can fetch and analyze any website's content.</p><h3 id="step-1-set-up-the-project" tabindex="-1">Step 1: Set up the project </h3><div class="language-bash line-numbers-mode"><button title="Copy Code" class="copy"></button><span class="lang">bash</span><pre class="shiki github-light vp-code" tabindex="0"><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#6F42C1;">mkdir</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> cloudflare-mcp-analyzer</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">cd</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> cloudflare-mcp-analyzer</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6F42C1;">bun</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> init</span><span style="color:#005CC5;"> -y</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6F42C1;">bun</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> add</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> @modelcontextprotocol/sdk</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> wrangler</span></span></code></pre><div class="line-numbers-wrapper" aria-hidden="true"><span class="line-number">1</span><br><span class="line-number">2</span><br><span class="line-number">3</span><br><span class="line-number">4</span><br></div></div><h3 id="step-2-create-the-mcp-server" tabindex="-1">Step 2: Create the MCP server </h3><p>Create <code>src/index.js</code>:</p><div class="language-javascript line-numbers-mode"><button title="Copy Code" class="copy"></button><span class="lang">javascript</span><pre class="shiki github-light vp-code" tabindex="0"><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">export</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> default</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">  async</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;"> fetch</span><span style="color:#24292E;">(</span><span style="color:#E36209;">request</span><span style="color:#24292E;">, </span><span style="color:#E36209;">env</span><span style="color:#24292E;">, </span><span style="color:#E36209;">ctx</span><span style="color:#24292E;">) {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">    const</span><span style="color:#005CC5;"> url</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> =</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> new</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;"> URL</span><span style="color:#24292E;">(request.url);</span></span>
<span class="line"></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6A737D;">    // CORS headers</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">    const</span><span style="color:#005CC5;"> corsHeaders</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> =</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#032F62;">      "Access-Control-Allow-Origin"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"*"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#032F62;">      "Access-Control-Allow-Methods"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"GET, POST, OPTIONS"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#032F62;">      "Access-Control-Allow-Headers"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"Content-Type"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">    };</span></span>
<span class="line"></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">    if</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> (request.method </span><span style="color:#D73A49;">===</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> "OPTIONS"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">) {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">      return</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> new</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;"> Response</span><span style="color:#24292E;">(</span><span style="color:#005CC5;">null</span><span style="color:#24292E;">, { headers: corsHeaders });</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">    }</span></span>
<span class="line"></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6A737D;">    // Root endpoint - server info</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">    if</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> (url.pathname </span><span style="color:#D73A49;">===</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> "/"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">) {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">      return</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> new</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;"> Response</span><span style="color:#24292E;">(</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">        JSON</span><span style="color:#24292E;">.</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;">stringify</span><span style="color:#24292E;">(</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">          {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">            name: env.</span><span style="color:#005CC5;">MCP_SERVER_NAME</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> ||</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> "website-analyzer"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">            version: env.</span><span style="color:#005CC5;">MCP_SERVER_VERSION</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> ||</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> "1.0.0"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">            description: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"Website analysis MCP server"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">            endpoints: [</span><span style="color:#032F62;">"/tools"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">, </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"/call"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">],</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">          },</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">          null</span><span style="color:#24292E;">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">          2</span><span style="color:#24292E;">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">        ),</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">        {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">          headers: { </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"Content-Type"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"application/json"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">, </span><span style="color:#D73A49;">...</span><span style="color:#24292E;">corsHeaders },</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">        },</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">      );</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">    }</span></span>
<span class="line"></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6A737D;">    // List available tools</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">    if</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> (url.pathname </span><span style="color:#D73A49;">===</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> "/tools"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">) {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">      return</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> new</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;"> Response</span><span style="color:#24292E;">(</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">        JSON</span><span style="color:#24292E;">.</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;">stringify</span><span style="color:#24292E;">(</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">          {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">            tools: [</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">              {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                name: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"analyze_website"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                description: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"Analyze a website and extract key information"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                inputSchema: {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                  type: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"object"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                  properties: {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                    url: { type: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"string"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">, description: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"The URL to analyze"</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> },</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                  },</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                  required: [</span><span style="color:#032F62;">"url"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">],</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                },</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">              },</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">            ],</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">          },</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">          null</span><span style="color:#24292E;">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">          2</span><span style="color:#24292E;">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">        ),</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">        {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">          headers: { </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"Content-Type"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"application/json"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">, </span><span style="color:#D73A49;">...</span><span style="color:#24292E;">corsHeaders },</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">        },</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">      );</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">    }</span></span>
<span class="line"></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6A737D;">    // Execute tool</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">    if</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> (url.pathname </span><span style="color:#D73A49;">===</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> "/call"</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> &amp;&amp;</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> request.method </span><span style="color:#D73A49;">===</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> "POST"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">) {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">      const</span><span style="color:#005CC5;"> body</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> =</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> await</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> request.</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;">json</span><span style="color:#24292E;">();</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">      const</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> { </span><span style="color:#005CC5;">name</span><span style="color:#24292E;">, </span><span style="color:#E36209;">arguments</span><span style="color:#24292E;">: </span><span style="color:#005CC5;">args</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> } </span><span style="color:#D73A49;">=</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> body;</span></span>
<span class="line"></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">      if</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> (name </span><span style="color:#D73A49;">===</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> "analyze_website"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">) {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">        try</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">          const</span><span style="color:#005CC5;"> response</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> =</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> await</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;"> fetch</span><span style="color:#24292E;">(args.url);</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">          const</span><span style="color:#005CC5;"> html</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> =</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> await</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> response.</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;">text</span><span style="color:#24292E;">();</span></span>
<span class="line"></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6A737D;">          // Extract basic info</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">          const</span><span style="color:#005CC5;"> titleMatch</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> =</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> html.</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;">match</span><span style="color:#24292E;">(</span><span style="color:#032F62;">/&lt;title&gt;(</span><span style="color:#005CC5;">.</span><span style="color:#D73A49;">*?</span><span style="color:#032F62;">)&lt;</span><span style="color:#22863A;font-weight:bold;">\/</span><span style="color:#032F62;">title&gt;/</span><span style="color:#D73A49;">i</span><span style="color:#24292E;">);</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">          const</span><span style="color:#005CC5;"> title</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> =</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> titleMatch </span><span style="color:#D73A49;">?</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> titleMatch[</span><span style="color:#005CC5;">1</span><span style="color:#24292E;">] </span><span style="color:#D73A49;">:</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> "No title found"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">;</span></span>
<span class="line"></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">          const</span><span style="color:#005CC5;"> linkCount</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> =</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> (html.</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;">match</span><span style="color:#24292E;">(</span><span style="color:#032F62;">/&lt;a</span><span style="color:#005CC5;">\s</span><span style="color:#032F62;">/</span><span style="color:#D73A49;">gi</span><span style="color:#24292E;">) </span><span style="color:#D73A49;">||</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> []).</span><span style="color:#005CC5;">length</span><span style="color:#24292E;">;</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">          const</span><span style="color:#005CC5;"> imageCount</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> =</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> (html.</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;">match</span><span style="color:#24292E;">(</span><span style="color:#032F62;">/&lt;img</span><span style="color:#005CC5;">\s</span><span style="color:#032F62;">/</span><span style="color:#D73A49;">gi</span><span style="color:#24292E;">) </span><span style="color:#D73A49;">||</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> []).</span><span style="color:#005CC5;">length</span><span style="color:#24292E;">;</span></span>
<span class="line"></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">          return</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> new</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;"> Response</span><span style="color:#24292E;">(</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">            JSON</span><span style="color:#24292E;">.</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;">stringify</span><span style="color:#24292E;">({</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">              content: [</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                  type: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"text"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                  text: </span><span style="color:#005CC5;">JSON</span><span style="color:#24292E;">.</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;">stringify</span><span style="color:#24292E;">(</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                    {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                      url: args.url,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                      title,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                      stats: {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                        links: linkCount,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                        images: imageCount,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                        contentLength: html.</span><span style="color:#005CC5;">length</span><span style="color:#24292E;">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                      },</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                    },</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">                    null</span><span style="color:#24292E;">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">                    2</span><span style="color:#24292E;">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                  ),</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                },</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">              ],</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">            }),</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">            {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">              headers: { </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"Content-Type"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"application/json"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">, </span><span style="color:#D73A49;">...</span><span style="color:#24292E;">corsHeaders },</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">            },</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">          );</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">        } </span><span style="color:#D73A49;">catch</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> (error) {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">          return</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> new</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;"> Response</span><span style="color:#24292E;">(</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">            JSON</span><span style="color:#24292E;">.</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;">stringify</span><span style="color:#24292E;">({</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">              content: [</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                  type: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"text"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                  text: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">`Error: ${</span><span style="color:#24292E;">error</span><span style="color:#032F62;">.</span><span style="color:#24292E;">message</span><span style="color:#032F62;">}`</span><span style="color:#24292E;">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">                },</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">              ],</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">            }),</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">            {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">              headers: { </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"Content-Type"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"application/json"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">, </span><span style="color:#D73A49;">...</span><span style="color:#24292E;">corsHeaders },</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">            },</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">          );</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">        }</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">      }</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">    }</span></span>
<span class="line"></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">    return</span><span style="color:#D73A49;"> new</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;"> Response</span><span style="color:#24292E;">(</span><span style="color:#032F62;">"Not Found"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">, { status: </span><span style="color:#005CC5;">404</span><span style="color:#24292E;"> });</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">  },</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">};</span></span></code></pre><div class="line-numbers-wrapper" aria-hidden="true"><span class="line-number">1</span><br><span class="line-number">2</span><br><span class="line-number">3</span><br><span class="line-number">4</span><br><span class="line-number">5</span><br><span class="line-number">6</span><br><span class="line-number">7</span><br><span class="line-number">8</span><br><span class="line-number">9</span><br><span class="line-number">10</span><br><span class="line-number">11</span><br><span class="line-number">12</span><br><span class="line-number">13</span><br><span class="line-number">14</span><br><span class="line-number">15</span><br><span class="line-number">16</span><br><span class="line-number">17</span><br><span class="line-number">18</span><br><span class="line-number">19</span><br><span class="line-number">20</span><br><span class="line-number">21</span><br><span class="line-number">22</span><br><span class="line-number">23</span><br><span class="line-number">24</span><br><span class="line-number">25</span><br><span class="line-number">26</span><br><span class="line-number">27</span><br><span class="line-number">28</span><br><span class="line-number">29</span><br><span class="line-number">30</span><br><span class="line-number">31</span><br><span class="line-number">32</span><br><span class="line-number">33</span><br><span class="line-number">34</span><br><span class="line-number">35</span><br><span class="line-number">36</span><br><span class="line-number">37</span><br><span class="line-number">38</span><br><span class="line-number">39</span><br><span class="line-number">40</span><br><span class="line-number">41</span><br><span class="line-number">42</span><br><span class="line-number">43</span><br><span class="line-number">44</span><br><span class="line-number">45</span><br><span class="line-number">46</span><br><span class="line-number">47</span><br><span class="line-number">48</span><br><span class="line-number">49</span><br><span class="line-number">50</span><br><span class="line-number">51</span><br><span class="line-number">52</span><br><span class="line-number">53</span><br><span class="line-number">54</span><br><span class="line-number">55</span><br><span class="line-number">56</span><br><span class="line-number">57</span><br><span class="line-number">58</span><br><span class="line-number">59</span><br><span class="line-number">60</span><br><span class="line-number">61</span><br><span class="line-number">62</span><br><span class="line-number">63</span><br><span class="line-number">64</span><br><span class="line-number">65</span><br><span class="line-number">66</span><br><span class="line-number">67</span><br><span class="line-number">68</span><br><span class="line-number">69</span><br><span class="line-number">70</span><br><span class="line-number">71</span><br><span class="line-number">72</span><br><span class="line-number">73</span><br><span class="line-number">74</span><br><span class="line-number">75</span><br><span class="line-number">76</span><br><span class="line-number">77</span><br><span class="line-number">78</span><br><span class="line-number">79</span><br><span class="line-number">80</span><br><span class="line-number">81</span><br><span class="line-number">82</span><br><span class="line-number">83</span><br><span class="line-number">84</span><br><span class="line-number">85</span><br><span class="line-number">86</span><br><span class="line-number">87</span><br><span class="line-number">88</span><br><span class="line-number">89</span><br><span class="line-number">90</span><br><span class="line-number">91</span><br><span class="line-number">92</span><br><span class="line-number">93</span><br><span class="line-number">94</span><br><span class="line-number">95</span><br><span class="line-number">96</span><br><span class="line-number">97</span><br><span class="line-number">98</span><br><span class="line-number">99</span><br><span class="line-number">100</span><br><span class="line-number">101</span><br><span class="line-number">102</span><br><span class="line-number">103</span><br><span class="line-number">104</span><br><span class="line-number">105</span><br><span class="line-number">106</span><br><span class="line-number">107</span><br><span class="line-number">108</span><br><span class="line-number">109</span><br><span class="line-number">110</span><br><span class="line-number">111</span><br><span class="line-number">112</span><br><span class="line-number">113</span><br><span class="line-number">114</span><br><span class="line-number">115</span><br><span class="line-number">116</span><br><span class="line-number">117</span><br><span class="line-number">118</span><br><span class="line-number">119</span><br><span class="line-number">120</span><br><span class="line-number">121</span><br><span class="line-number">122</span><br><span class="line-number">123</span><br><span class="line-number">124</span><br><span class="line-number">125</span><br></div></div><h3 id="step-3-configure-for-cloudflare" tabindex="-1">Step 3: Configure for Cloudflare </h3><p>Create <code>wrangler.toml</code>:</p><div class="language-toml line-numbers-mode"><button title="Copy Code" class="copy"></button><span class="lang">toml</span><pre class="shiki github-light vp-code" tabindex="0"><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">name = </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"mcp-website-analyzer"</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">main = </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"src/index.js"</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">compatibility_date = </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"2024-01-01"</span></span>
<span class="line"></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">[</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;">vars</span><span style="color:#24292E;">]</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">MCP_SERVER_NAME = </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"website-analyzer"</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">MCP_SERVER_VERSION = </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"1.0.0"</span></span></code></pre><div class="line-numbers-wrapper" aria-hidden="true"><span class="line-number">1</span><br><span class="line-number">2</span><br><span class="line-number">3</span><br><span class="line-number">4</span><br><span class="line-number">5</span><br><span class="line-number">6</span><br><span class="line-number">7</span><br></div></div><h3 id="step-4-test-locally-optional" tabindex="-1">Step 4: Test locally (optional) </h3><div class="language-bash line-numbers-mode"><button title="Copy Code" class="copy"></button><span class="lang">bash</span><pre class="shiki github-light vp-code" tabindex="0"><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#6A737D;"># Test your worker locally</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6F42C1;">wrangler</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> dev</span></span></code></pre><div class="line-numbers-wrapper" aria-hidden="true"><span class="line-number">1</span><br><span class="line-number">2</span><br></div></div><aside><a href="https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/wrangler/">Wrangler</a> is Cloudflare's CLI tool for managing Workers</aside><h3 id="step-5-deploy-to-cloudflare" tabindex="-1">Step 5: Deploy to Cloudflare </h3><div class="language-bash line-numbers-mode"><button title="Copy Code" class="copy"></button><span class="lang">bash</span><pre class="shiki github-light vp-code" tabindex="0"><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#6A737D;"># Login to Cloudflare</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6F42C1;">wrangler</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> login</span></span>
<span class="line"></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6A737D;"># Deploy the worker</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6F42C1;">wrangler</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> deploy</span></span></code></pre><div class="line-numbers-wrapper" aria-hidden="true"><span class="line-number">1</span><br><span class="line-number">2</span><br><span class="line-number">3</span><br><span class="line-number">4</span><br><span class="line-number">5</span><br></div></div><aside>Pro tip: If you have environment variables set, use <code>(unset CF_API_TOKEN &amp;&amp; wrangler deploy)</code></aside><p>That's it! Your MCP server is now live on Cloudflare's global network.</p><h3 id="step-6-connect-to-your-ai-assistant" tabindex="-1">Step 6: Connect to your AI assistant </h3><p>Add to your MCP configuration:</p><div class="language-json line-numbers-mode"><button title="Copy Code" class="copy"></button><span class="lang">json</span><pre class="shiki github-light vp-code" tabindex="0"><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">{</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">  "mcpServers"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">: {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">    "website-analyzer"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">: {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">      "url"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"https://mcp-website-analyzer.YOUR-SUBDOMAIN.workers.dev"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">      "description"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"Analyzes websites and extracts key information"</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">    }</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">  }</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">}</span></span></code></pre><div class="line-numbers-wrapper" aria-hidden="true"><span class="line-number">1</span><br><span class="line-number">2</span><br><span class="line-number">3</span><br><span class="line-number">4</span><br><span class="line-number">5</span><br><span class="line-number">6</span><br><span class="line-number">7</span><br><span class="line-number">8</span><br></div></div><h2 id="using-with-opencode-or-claude-desktop" tabindex="-1">Using with OpenCode or Claude Desktop </h2><p>To use the public HTTPX MCP server (or any Cloudflare Worker MCP) with <a href="https://opencode.ai/">OpenCode</a> or <a href="https://claude.ai/download">Claude Desktop</a>:</p><h3 id="for-opencode" tabindex="-1">For OpenCode </h3><ol><li>Create or edit <code>.opencode/settings.json</code> in your project:</li></ol><div class="language-json line-numbers-mode"><button title="Copy Code" class="copy"></button><span class="lang">json</span><pre class="shiki github-light vp-code" tabindex="0"><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">{</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">  "mcpServers"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">: {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">    "httpx"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">: {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">      "url"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"https://mcp-httpx-server.danielmiessler.workers.dev"</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">    }</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">  }</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">}</span></span></code></pre><div class="line-numbers-wrapper" aria-hidden="true"><span class="line-number">1</span><br><span class="line-number">2</span><br><span class="line-number">3</span><br><span class="line-number">4</span><br><span class="line-number">5</span><br><span class="line-number">6</span><br><span class="line-number">7</span><br></div></div><ol start="2"><li>Restart OpenCode. The tools will be available as: <ul><li><code>mcp_httpx_httpx_scan</code> - Scan multiple URLs</li><li><code>mcp_httpx_httpx_tech_stack</code> - Get technology stack</li></ul></li></ol><h3 id="for-claude-desktop" tabindex="-1">For Claude Desktop </h3><ol><li>Open Claude Desktop settings</li><li>Go to the "Developer" tab</li><li>Edit the MCP servers configuration:</li></ol><div class="language-json line-numbers-mode"><button title="Copy Code" class="copy"></button><span class="lang">json</span><pre class="shiki github-light vp-code" tabindex="0"><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">{</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">  "mcpServers"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">: {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">    "httpx"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">: {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">      "url"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"https://mcp-httpx-server.danielmiessler.workers.dev"</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">    }</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">  }</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">}</span></span></code></pre><div class="line-numbers-wrapper" aria-hidden="true"><span class="line-number">1</span><br><span class="line-number">2</span><br><span class="line-number">3</span><br><span class="line-number">4</span><br><span class="line-number">5</span><br><span class="line-number">6</span><br><span class="line-number">7</span><br></div></div><ol start="4"><li>Restart Claude Desktop</li></ol><h3 id="testing-the-connection" tabindex="-1">Testing the connection </h3><p>Once connected, you can ask your AI assistant to:</p><ul><li>"Use httpx to check what technology stack danielmiessler.com uses"</li><li>"Scan these domains for me: example.com, test.com"</li><li>"What security headers does github.com have?"</li></ul><p>The AI will automatically use the MCP server tools to fetch this information.</p><h2 id="the-beauty-of-this-approach" tabindex="-1">The beauty of this approach </h2><p>What I love about this is:</p><ul><li><strong>Zero infrastructure</strong> - No servers to manage, no scaling to worry about</li><li><strong>Global performance</strong> - Runs on Cloudflare's edge network</li><li><strong>Simple pricing</strong> - Pay only for what you use</li><li><strong>Easy updates</strong> - Just push new code</li></ul><p>This is exactly what I've been looking forâ€”a way to extend AI capabilities without the infrastructure overhead.</p><h2 id="real-example-httpx-mcp-server" tabindex="-1">Real Example: HTTPX MCP Server </h2><p>I've created a working example that provides HTTP reconnaissance capabilities inspired by <a href="https://github.com/projectdiscovery/httpx">Project Discovery's httpx</a>. It's live at:</p><p><strong><a href="https://mcp-httpx-server.danielmiessler.workers.dev" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">https://mcp-httpx-server.danielmiessler.workers.dev</a></strong></p><p>This server provides two powerful tools:</p><h3 id="httpx-scan" tabindex="-1">httpx_scan </h3><p>Quick HTTP scanning for multiple targets at once:</p><div class="language-json line-numbers-mode"><button title="Copy Code" class="copy"></button><span class="lang">json</span><pre class="shiki github-light vp-code" tabindex="0"><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">{</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">  "targets"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">: [</span><span style="color:#032F62;">"example.com"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">, </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"test.com"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">, </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"demo.com"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">]</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">}</span></span></code></pre><div class="line-numbers-wrapper" aria-hidden="true"><span class="line-number">1</span><br><span class="line-number">2</span><br><span class="line-number">3</span><br></div></div><h3 id="httpx-tech-stack" tabindex="-1">httpx_tech_stack </h3><p>Comprehensive technology stack detection that analyzes:</p><ul><li>Server software (<a href="https://nginx.org/">Nginx</a>, <a href="https://httpd.apache.org/">Apache</a>, Cloudflare)</li><li>Backend technologies (PHP, ASP.NET, <a href="https://expressjs.com/">Express.js</a>)</li><li>Frontend frameworks (<a href="https://react.dev/">React</a>, <a href="https://vuejs.org/">Vue.js</a>, <a href="https://angular.io/">Angular</a>)</li><li>CMS platforms (<a href="https://wordpress.org/">WordPress</a>, <a href="https://vitepress.dev/">VitePress</a>, <a href="https://ghost.org/">Ghost</a>)</li><li>Analytics tools (<a href="https://analytics.google.com/">Google Analytics</a>, <a href="https://plausible.io/">Plausible</a>)</li><li>Security headers (HSTS, CSP, X-Frame-Options)</li><li>CDN providers (Cloudflare, <a href="https://aws.amazon.com/cloudfront/">CloudFront</a>, <a href="https://www.fastly.com/">Fastly</a>)</li></ul><p>Example usage:</p><div class="language-json line-numbers-mode"><button title="Copy Code" class="copy"></button><span class="lang">json</span><pre class="shiki github-light vp-code" tabindex="0"><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">{</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">  "target"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"danielmiessler.com"</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">}</span></span></code></pre><div class="line-numbers-wrapper" aria-hidden="true"><span class="line-number">1</span><br><span class="line-number">2</span><br><span class="line-number">3</span><br></div></div><p>The entire implementation is a single JavaScript file that runs on Cloudflare's edge network, providing instant global availability without managing servers.</p><h2 id="summary" tabindex="-1">Summary </h2><ul><li>MCP servers traditionally require managing your own infrastructure</li><li>Cloudflare Workers eliminates this overhead with one-click deployment</li><li>You can focus on functionality while Cloudflare handles the backend</li><li>The example website analyzer shows how simple it can be</li><li>This approach makes MCP servers accessible to everyone, not just infrastructure experts</li></ul><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>This post was created at AIL-3 (AI Directed) according to the <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail">AI Influence Level</a> framework. Daniel wrote the post and I (Kai, Daniel's digital assistant) helped structure the content, and created the code examples.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Fixing Opencode Transparency in Ghostty Terminal]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/opencode-ghostty-transparency-fix?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/opencode-ghostty-transparency-fix</guid>
            <pubDate>Fri, 18 Jul 2025 19:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Opencode with transparent Ghostty terminal/images/opencode-ghostty-transparency-new.png/images/opencode-ghostty-transparency-new.png Opencode running with transparent background in Ghostty click for full size If you're using Opencodehttps://opencode.ai in Ghostty terminalhttps://ghostty.org and ...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/opencode-ghostty-transparency-new.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/opencode-ghostty-transparency-new.png" alt="Opencode with transparent Ghostty terminal" width="1946" height="1448" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p>Opencode running with transparent background in Ghostty (click for full size)<p>If you're using <a href="https://opencode.ai" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Opencode</a> in <a href="https://ghostty.org" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Ghostty terminal</a> and noticed that your beautiful transparent background disappears when Opencode launches, here's the fix.</p><h2 id="the-problem" tabindex="-1">The Problem </h2><p>Opencode's TUI doesn't honor Ghostty's transparent background by default, making the Opencode part of your terminal opaque when the app runs.</p><h2 id="the-solution" tabindex="-1">The Solution </h2><p>Configure Opencode to use its <code>system</code> theme, which adapts to your terminal's native appearance including transparency.</p><aside>Create the file structure if it doesn't exist.</aside><h3 id="quick-fix" tabindex="-1">Quick Fix </h3><ol><li><p>Open your Opencode config:</p><div class="language-bash line-numbers-mode"><button title="Copy Code" class="copy"></button><span class="lang">bash</span><pre class="shiki github-light vp-code" tabindex="0"><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#6F42C1;">nvim</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json</span></span></code></pre><div class="line-numbers-wrapper" aria-hidden="true"><span class="line-number">1</span><br></div></div></li><li><p>Set the theme to <code>system</code>:</p><div class="language-json line-numbers-mode"><button title="Copy Code" class="copy"></button><span class="lang">json</span><pre class="shiki github-light vp-code" tabindex="0"><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">{</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">  "$schema"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"https://opencode.ai/config.json"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;">  "theme"</span><span style="color:#24292E;">: </span><span style="color:#032F62;">"system"</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">}</span></span></code></pre><div class="line-numbers-wrapper" aria-hidden="true"><span class="line-number">1</span><br><span class="line-number">2</span><br><span class="line-number">3</span><br><span class="line-number">4</span><br></div></div></li><li><p>Save the file</p></li><li><p>Restart Opencode</p></li></ol><aside>This works by using `none` for background.</aside><p>Now your Opencode will seamlessly blend with your terminal's transparency settings.</p><p>Happy hacking!</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>This also works for other terminals with transparency like <a href="https://alacritty.org">Alacritty</a> or <a href="https://iterm2.com">iTerm2</a>.</li><li>I am using this fine with the <code>tokyonight</code> theme as shown in the header image.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Replacing Cursor With Neovim and Claude Code]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/replacing-cursor-with-neovim-claude-code?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/replacing-cursor-with-neovim-claude-code</guid>
            <pubDate>Sun, 13 Jul 2025 19:30:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Neovim and Claude Code Integration/images/neovim-claude-code-integration.png/images/neovim-claude-code-integration.png My three-paned Ghostty / Neovim / Claude Code Setup click for full size It kind of sucks right now to be a neoVimhttps://neovim.io/ user if you're super excited about Claude...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/images/neovim-claude-code-integration.png"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/neovim-claude-code-integration.png" alt="Neovim and Claude Code Integration" width="3802" height="3478" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></a></p>My three-paned Ghostty / Neovim / Claude Code Setup (click for full size)<div class="video-container"><iframe src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/ysVmQ6mesWE" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen=""></iframe></div><p>It kind of sucks right now to be a <a href="https://neovim.io/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">(neo)Vim</a> user if you're super excited about <a href="https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/overview" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Claude Code</a>.</p><aside>I even tried to hold my nose and use Cursor for a while.</aside><p>All that power...right in your editor! It's sooooo great.</p><p>But all the Examples that you've seen of people doing it or that you have tried yourself have been with <a href="https://cursor.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Cursor</a>, <a href="https://windsurf.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Windsurf</a>, or <a href="https://code.visualstudio.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">VSCode</a>. And the plugins you've tried with Neovim don't work, or they're glitchy, or they're gross to configure and use.</p><p><em>I solved this for myself with a much simpler configuration.</em></p><h2 id="ghostty-to-the-rescue" tabindex="-1">Ghostty to the rescue </h2><aside>Ghostty has kind of walked away with the title.</aside><p>I'm sure you're familiar with <a href="https://ghostty.org/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Ghostty</a>, which is kind of universally accepted as the best terminal.</p><p>What I've done is configured my entire IDE, Claude Code, and my terminal interface into a single Ghostty window with three panes.</p><aside>We spend most time in Claude Code these days.</aside><ol><li>Claude Code on the left</li><li>Neovim (Code) on the right</li><li>A shell down below the code window</li></ol><aside>Super easy to remember because we're just using Vim commands.</aside><p>And here's the Ghostty configuration I use to open and move between panes.</p><h3 id="the-ghostty-keybindings" tabindex="-1">The Ghostty Keybindings </h3><div class="language-bash line-numbers-mode"><button title="Copy Code" class="copy"></button><span class="lang">bash</span><pre class="shiki github-light vp-code" tabindex="0"><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#6A737D;"># Create new split to the right (for Neovim)</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6F42C1;">keybind</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> =</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> cmd+d=new_split:right</span></span>
<span class="line"></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6A737D;"># Create new split below (for your shell)</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6F42C1;">keybind</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> =</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> cmd+shift+t=new_split:down</span></span>
<span class="line"></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6A737D;"># Navigate between panes (vim-style)</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6F42C1;">keybind</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> =</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> ctrl+h=goto_split:left</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6F42C1;">keybind</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> =</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> ctrl+j=goto_split:bottom</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6F42C1;">keybind</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> =</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> ctrl+k=goto_split:top</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6F42C1;">keybind</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> =</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> ctrl+l=goto_split:right</span></span></code></pre><div class="line-numbers-wrapper" aria-hidden="true"><span class="line-number">1</span><br><span class="line-number">2</span><br><span class="line-number">3</span><br><span class="line-number">4</span><br><span class="line-number">5</span><br><span class="line-number">6</span><br><span class="line-number">7</span><br><span class="line-number">8</span><br><span class="line-number">9</span><br><span class="line-number">10</span><br><span class="line-number">11</span><br></div></div><h2 id="the-result" tabindex="-1">The result </h2><p>So now all you have to do is:</p><ul><li>Open up Ghostty</li><li>Open up a pane to the right</li><li>Open up a pane below that</li><li>Type <code>claude</code> on the left</li><li>Open and edit your code/files in NeoVim on the right</li><li>Leave the bottom one your shell</li></ul><p>...and you are good to go!</p><aside>Just <code>ctrl-[motion]</code> to move between them!</aside><p>Hope this helps someone.</p><p>Happy hacking!</p></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[The AI Creative Destruction Wave]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-creative-destruction-wave?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-creative-destruction-wave</guid>
            <pubDate>Tue, 08 Jul 2025 19:30:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[AI multidimensional pathways representing the transformation of business/images/074f1327-3522-48c0-b45f-889c44284e80-ai_multidimensional-pathways.png The scariest thing to me about AI is that I think very few of the current companies and products that we use everyday are the best solutions. Curre...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/074f1327-3522-48c0-b45f-889c44284e80-ai_multidimensional-pathways.png" alt="AI multidimensional pathways representing the transformation of business" width="1200" height="1200" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"><p>The scariest thing to me about AI is that I think very few of the current companies and products that we use everyday are the best solutions.</p><p>Current companies are inefficient versions of what they could be. And current products are bad versions of what they could be.</p><p>This was never a problem before because <em>everything</em> is like that. Every company making every product. We're all struggling with efficiency and bloat and overhead and bureaucracy.</p><!----><p>This is what scares me.</p><p>I think we're about to see millions of jobs lostâ€”not just because the companies and products that survive will replace many workers with AI.</p><p>What I'm really worried about is those companies and services and products disappearing altogether.</p><h2 id="the-new-competitive-reality" tabindex="-1">The New Competitive Reality </h2><p>I'm worried about how many of them will be replaced by brand new products and services that do the job much better, and iterate far faster, because the "team" that made them is actually just 1 or a handful of people who are shipping features almost instantly.</p><aside>The vision/feedback loop is so fast when you're tiny like this.</aside><p>Think about what this means. A traditional SaaS company might have:</p><ul><li>Product managers</li><li>Designers</li><li>Frontend developers</li><li>Backend developers</li><li>QA teams</li><li>Support teams</li><li>Marketing teams</li><li>Sales teams</li></ul><p>Now imagine a competitor with 2-3 people using AI to handle most of these functions. They can:</p><ul><li>Ship features in hours instead of months</li><li>Pivot instantly based on customer feedback</li><li>Operate at a fraction of the cost</li><li>Price their product 90% cheaper</li></ul><!----><h2 id="the-protected-and-the-vulnerable" tabindex="-1">The Protected and the Vulnerable </h2><p>Of course there are tons of businesses and products that have moats of different kinds, and that this won't happen to. Companies making hardware. Companies where it's almost impossible to switch to a competitor.</p><p>But I think most products and companiesâ€”especially SaaS companiesâ€”are about to face existential pressure from new AI offerings that are just better and cheaper.</p><!----><p>Consider the typical B2B SaaS company:</p><ul><li>High customer acquisition costs</li><li>Complex onboarding processes</li><li>Armies of customer success managers</li><li>Endless feature requests backlog</li><li>Slow development cycles</li></ul><p>Now imagine a lean AI-powered alternative that:</p><ul><li>Onboards customers automatically</li><li>Provides instant 24/7 support</li><li>Ships requested features in days</li><li>Costs 1/10th the price</li></ul><h2 id="security-companies-a-prime-example" tabindex="-1">Security Companies: A Prime Example </h2><p>This creative destruction is especially devastating for companies I know bestâ€”those in cybersecurity. So many security products are built on the "bolt-on" concept, doing security separately from the initial engineering workflow.</p><p>My friend Caleb Sima put it perfectly: "Engineering should eat security."</p><!----><p>During my time at ExxonMobil, I learned it's all about pursuing an ideal process. Think about civil engineeringâ€”they don't bolt on safety to a skyscraper design. The way to build safely IS the way to build. It's baked into the SOP (Standard Operating Procedure). <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/policy-sops-and-ai-are-all-you-need">I've written about how AI-powered SOPs will transform security and business</a>.</p><aside>Security's goal should be to completely disappear into the engineering SOP.</aside><p>What AI enables is revolutionary. Imagine submitting code where:</p><ul><li>100 different AI agents automatically build the app end-to-end</li><li>They test with and without your PR implemented</li><li>Dynamic testing, static analysis, functionality testingâ€”all happen automatically</li><li>Everything is integrated directly with GitHub or your build system</li></ul><p>This becomes the new way of building. Not a separate security step, but THE engineering process itself.</p><!----><p>The troubling part? Most existing security companies are based on doing this incorrectlyâ€”operating outside the engineering workflow.</p><p>And they are in serious trouble.</p><h2 id="the-timeline-is-terrifying" tabindex="-1">The Timeline Is Terrifying </h2><p>I think AI is going to remake things. It's like a giant reset. For a lot of things like government I'm eager to see the changes.</p><p>But I'm very concerned that the replacement of these companies and products and services is going to abruptly shock the labor market in a way that we've never seen before.</p><aside>I think it could happen as early as 2026 or 2027â€”if it's not happening already.</aside><p>Unfortunately, there doesn't need to be some giant movement for this to happen.
