It's not like a bunch of people are gonna coordinate and say hey, let's do this.</p><p>It's a whole bunch of tiny cuts happening independently.</p><h2 id="death-by-a-thousand-startups" tabindex="-1">Death by a Thousand Startups </h2><p>Every week, another tiny team launches something that makes an entire category of software obsolete.</p><ul><li>That project management tool your company pays $50/user/month for? Someone just built a better one for $5.</li><li>The customer support platform requiring a team of 10 to manage? Now it's an AI agent that needs zero management.</li><li>The data analytics dashboard that took 6 months to implement? Built in a weekend by one person.</li></ul><!----><p>Each one seems insignificant. But collectively, they're hollowing out entire industries.</p><h2 id="why-listen-to-me" tabindex="-1">Why Listen to Me? </h2><p>But wait, why do I have an opinion on this? And why should you consider anything I say?</p><p>First off, I could be wrong, and no amount of experience can negate that. But for those skeptical of my perspective and/or experience, I've consulted for hundreds of companies across multiple industries in the last 25 years. I've worked full-time at companies like Apple and HP, and have seen a lot under the covers of what makes companies move slowly. And that's why I'm worried.</p><!----><p>When one person can build what used to take a team of 50, and when AI can handle what used to require entire departments, the old way of doing business is going to face extraordinary pressure.</p><p>This isn't about whether AI will take your job. It's about whether your entire company, or product, has a reason to exist at all in a market full of options that move faster than you.</p><p>And this doesn't need to happen all at once. It's not like there's going to be some meeting where people decide to replace "old" products and services. And companies aren't going to decide to lay off workers en masse.</p><p>But all of them individually are now facing that pressure. And I think a large number of them will either go out of business or lay off many or most of their people.</p><p>I'm worried. Let's hope I'm wrong.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>AIL Level 3 (80% Human Written) - Security companies section added by AI based on voice transcription <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Learn more about AIL levels</a>.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[How to Integrate Claude Code with Neovim Using Ghostty Terminal Panes]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/claude-code-neovim-ghostty-integration?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/claude-code-neovim-ghostty-integration</guid>
            <pubDate>Tue, 08 Jul 2025 17:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Claude Code and Neovim integration through Ghostty terminal/images/claude-neovim-ghostty.png If you're a Neovim user feeling left out watching everyone use Claude Code with VS Code, here's my dead simple solution: three terminal panes. The Setup One Ghostty window, three panes: - Left: Cl...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/claude-neovim-ghostty.png" alt="Claude Code and Neovim integration through Ghostty terminal" width="1200" height="630" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"><div class="video-container"><iframe src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/ysVmQ6mesWE" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen=""></iframe></div><p>If you're a Neovim user feeling left out watching everyone use Claude Code with VS Code, here's my dead simple solution: three terminal panes.</p><h2 id="the-setup" tabindex="-1">The Setup </h2><p>One Ghostty window, three panes:</p><ul><li><strong>Left</strong>: Claude Code</li><li><strong>Right</strong>: Neovim</li><li><strong>Bottom</strong>: Terminal</li></ul><!----><h2 id="the-configuration" tabindex="-1">The Configuration </h2><p>Add these to your Ghostty config:</p><div class="language-toml line-numbers-mode"><button title="Copy Code" class="copy"></button><span class="lang">toml</span><pre class="shiki github-light vp-code" tabindex="0"><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">keybind = c</span><span style="color:#B31D28;font-style:italic;">trl+h=goto_split:left</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">keybind = c</span><span style="color:#B31D28;font-style:italic;">trl+j=goto_split:bottom</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">keybind = c</span><span style="color:#B31D28;font-style:italic;">trl+k=goto_split:top</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">keybind = c</span><span style="color:#B31D28;font-style:italic;">trl+l=goto_split:right</span></span></code></pre><div class="line-numbers-wrapper" aria-hidden="true"><span class="line-number">1</span><br><span class="line-number">2</span><br><span class="line-number">3</span><br><span class="line-number">4</span><br></div></div><p>Now use Control + vim keys to jump between panes instantly. No mouse, no window switching.</p><h2 id="why-it-works" tabindex="-1">Why It Works </h2><p>This setup eliminates the need for VS Code or Cursor. You keep your Neovim config, your muscle memory, and gain AI assistance exactly when you need it.</p><aside>The best integrations don't feel like integrations.</aside><h2 id="getting-started" tabindex="-1">Getting Started </h2><ol><li>Install Ghostty (or any terminal with panes)</li><li>Add the keybindings</li><li>Open three panes</li><li>Start Claude Code, Neovim, and a terminal</li></ol><p>That's it. We get all the CC goodness without losing vim!</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>AIL Level 4 (AI Created, Human Basic Idea) <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">AIL Levels</a></li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Claude Code is the Biggest AI Development Since ChatGPT]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/claude-code-proto-agi?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/claude-code-proto-agi</guid>
            <pubDate>Tue, 08 Jul 2025 06:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Claude Code interface showing AI-powered coding assistance/images/agi-asi-miessler-2025.png I've had a number of thoughts around Claude Code in the last couple of days. I've especially been trying to put into words a feeling of how big this is, and why I think so. Here's what I've come up with: ...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/agi-asi-miessler-2025.png" alt="Claude Code interface showing AI-powered coding assistance" width="1200" height="630" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"><p>I've had a number of thoughts around Claude Code in the last couple of days. I've especially been trying to put into words a feeling of how <em>big</em> this is, and why I think so.</p><p>Here's what I've come up with:</p><ol><li>Claude Code is as big a jump as ChatGPT was in 2022</li><li>ChatGPT was the ChatGPT for knowledge, and Claude Code is the ChatGPT for <em>action</em></li><li>Claude Code is as good as it is because of <em>scaffolding</em>, not model IQ</li><li>I think Claude Code is <em>proto-AGI</em>, meaning it just needs more scaffolding, tools, and long-term memory and it'll be able to replace an average knowledge worker</li></ol><p>I was having trouble putting this into words, and I think the above captures it.</p><p>Basically, it's as big as ChatGPTâ€”<em>but for action instead of knowledge</em>.</p><h2 id="agi-really" tabindex="-1">AGI? Really? </h2><!----><p>I use the "AGI" word for a very specific reason: being able to replace human workers.</p><p>CC is already doing the work of a knowledge worker. And in fact way more and way better. But:</p><ul><li>It can only do so many tasks</li><li>It only has so many tools</li><li>Its memory is only so large</li><li>It can't incorporate its entire career of knowledge into the next task</li><li>Etc.</li></ul><p>So it basically has tools and memory / knowledge management limitations. But, fundamentally, it's doing the work of someone in a corporate type job already.</p><p>This is huge to me, because to get to an actual AI product/service that can replace an employee I don't think requires some new, theoretical advance in AI.</p><p>What we have already just needs more wiring and plumbing. And I think Claude Code has made this very clear.</p></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[This is Intelligence]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/this-is-intelligence?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/this-is-intelligence</guid>
            <pubDate>Sun, 06 Jul 2025 10:30:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Claude Report/images/claude-report.png pnpm install -g ccusage I have a friend named Marcus Hutchinshttps://marcushutchins.com who doesn't believe modern AI is intelligent. He thinks it's basically autocomplete. We actually had a full debate about it herehttps://youtu.be/I9-iD_rLRjA that you...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/claude-report.png" alt="Claude Report" width="1200" height="630" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high">pnpm install -g ccusage<div class="video-container"><iframe src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/N4PI8VIFAw8" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen=""></iframe></div><p>I have a friend named <a href="https://marcushutchins.com" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Marcus Hutchins</a> who doesn't believe modern AI is intelligent. He thinks it's basically autocomplete. We actually had <a href="https://youtu.be/I9-iD_rLRjA" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">a full debate about it here</a> that you should check out. It was a very civil and brilliant conversation, and we both learned a lot about each other's positions.</p><p>But I wanted to post a follow-up to that debate because I have some evidenceâ€”and a challengeâ€”to share.</p><p>Over the last few days, I've been doing a ton of projects, and one of them has been cleaning up my site and adding some functionality.</p><h2 id="_1-tagging-my-entire-site" tabindex="-1">1. Tagging my entire site </h2><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/archives-display.png" alt="Archives Display" width="1200" height="630" loading="lazy"><aside>This required a "human" to actually read and understand all the content.</aside><ol><li>Go read all 3061 blog posts</li><li>Retag them with one or more of my 20 content tags using AI to understand the content apply them appropriately</li><li>Rewrite all the tags</li><li>Push the changes</li></ol><h2 id="_2-bring-my-images-home" tabindex="-1">2. Bring my images home </h2><p>Over the years I've had images hosted in many other CMSs, and most recently the whole site was on Beehiiv. I wanted to bring all the images home to my own local image store, so I had AI do the following:</p><ol><li>Go find all instances of images that pointed to subdomains and third party content</li><li>Download that image</li><li>Rename it</li><li>Put it in my new local image store</li><li>Change the links multiple places in the post to point to the new location</li><li>Push the changes yy And keep in mind that was just a simple command I gave it, and it figured that all out, built a plan, and carried it out.</li></ol><h2 id="_3-converting-super-nasty-html-bundles-to-markdown" tabindex="-1">3. Converting super nasty HTML bundles to Markdown </h2><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/nasty-html.png" alt="Nasty HTML" width="1200" height="630" loading="lazy"><p>When I moved my site from Beehiiv, I had a lot of content that was wrapped in super nasty HTML bundles. This was because Beehiiv is a newsletter platform, and it doesn't really care about Markdown or clean HTML. It just wants to send out emails.</p><!----><p>So it wasn't just images that were super broken, it was also the core content as well. When I brought my content back over from Beehive, it was all wrapped in this super nasty embedded HTML bundle.</p><aside>Getting someone to do this consistently across 3,000 posts would also have been impossible.</aside><p>And the whole point of going to my new static site was to have everything be pristine Markdown, with none of the content modified during the conversion.</p><p>So now I have an AI function where I basically just say, "Clean up this post", and I hand it any URL, and it goes and:</p><ol><li>Completely rewrites all the HTML to clean Markdown</li><li>Fixes all the images</li><li>Applies all my custom formatting, which is like 15 different unique theme things</li><li>Makes sure nothing is broken through testing</li><li>Pushes the changes</li></ol><p>It's completely insane.</p><h2 id="the-takeaway" tabindex="-1">The takeaway </h2><aside>Here's an example of the system working on one of the tasks.</aside><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/ai-work.png" alt="AI Or" width="1200" height="630" loading="lazy"><p>Anyone who has done any technical work around maintaining a website, or complex HTML, will instantly recognize how tedious this work is.</p><p>And most importantlyâ€”several things here <em>you can't just script</em>.</p><aside>This was mostly Claude Code, by the way. For anyone curious.</aside><ul><li>No technology prior to modern AI would read posts for you and tag them based on their meaning</li><li>Same with cleaning posts full of garbage HTML and rewriting it as clean Markdown</li></ul><aside>Think about all the work on Upwork and Fiverr.</aside><p>Even for the tasks that someone <em>could</em> code, it'd still be shit work to do, and it'd take forever to troubleshoot. Claude wrote dozens of these over the course of the work, with many being a couple hundred lines of code, and it did it all in a few seconds.</p><p>Including testing afterwards. And providing monitoring and status updates all along so I could watch its progress.</p><p>These all would have required a human to do the work, and it would have taken weeks or months to do it all manually. Or you pay someone to do itâ€”probably poorlyâ€”and it would cost thousands of dollars.</p><p><strong>TL;DR: This was hundreds of hours and thousands of dollars worth of work that I just had AI do in a few days.</strong></p><h2 id="summary-my-argument" tabindex="-1">Summary (my argument) </h2><p>I don't see how it's possible to argue this isn't intelligence.</p><p>Again, this is not possible to do without either manual <em>human</em> work, or AI. In fact, that's my actual definition of AI.</p><!----><aside>I do agree with him that this is a special and still-only-human type of intelligence.</aside><p>Marcus disagrees. As we cover in the debate, he defines it more as a <em>completely</em> new thingâ€”like Einstein's <code>e=mc^2</code>, or the invention of the wheel.</p><p>I have multiple problems with this:</p><ol><li>It devalues 99.99% of all cognitive work being done by people</li><li>It implies it's ok for anyone <em>not</em> doing that kind of work to get replaced</li></ol><p>Essentially, this definition is so restrictive as to be useless.</p><p>It doesn't apply to the difficult cognitive work that <em>hundreds of millions of people</em> are being paid lots of money to do. Work that they might no longer be paid for because of the technology shown above.</p><p>Whatever we want to call that.</p><p>Do we really need a new name?</p><p><em>Cognitive Work Replacement Technology</em>?</p><p>I don't see the need for it. The existing name already works.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>I'll do a separate post addressing Marcus' definition of intelligence itself, which I think is the heart of the disagreement. Basically he thinks it's the ability to fill in the gaps when there's information missing, and I think it's demonstrably clear that AI can already do that. And that it could in 2022.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[AI Excites and Depresses Me]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-morose-mania?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-morose-mania</guid>
            <pubDate>Fri, 04 Jul 2025 15:30:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Hide the Pain Harold - smiling through the existential dreadhttps://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/ai-morose-mania-harold-screenshot/public I think, looking back, we might say that the first week of July 2025 was the start of AGI. Like, proto-AGI. All because of Cl...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/ai-morose-mania-harold-screenshot/public" alt="Hide the Pain Harold - smiling through the existential dread" width="1200" height="630" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"><p>I think, looking back, we might say that the first week of July 2025 was the start of AGI. Like, <em>proto-AGI</em>. All because of Claude Code.</p><p>It feels like it hit critical mass in terms of people using it and people realizing how powerful it is.</p><!----><p>What struck me waking up this morning was how this excitement is like a blade for a handle.</p><p>Think about how excited I am right now as a builder/developer. Well, <em>that's how excited business owners are going to be about how many people they can replace.</em></p><p>In other words, this giant surge in power we just felt pulse through the world of AI development is going to mean far more jobs lost, and far faster.</p><aside>Our enthusiasm is directly proportional to most peoples' obsolescence.</aside><p>I'm still excited. But I'm increasingly feeling like Harold the Pain Guy, smiling through the existential dread.</p><p>I'm starting to think there's a very real chance of a recession starting in 2026 or 2027, and it will be because of AI.</p><p>Yes, AI will produce extraordinary improvements in productivity. We'll be able to make way more stuff. And faster. And that for sure will lead to lots of positive things.</p><p>But will it offset the potential loss of hundreds of thousandsâ€”or maybe even millionsâ€”of jobs?</p><p>I don't see how it can. As I keep saying, I don't see the point of all this new amazing stuff if people are losing their jobs and suffering. Which also happens to mean fewer people can afford to buy the stuff being made.</p><p>And again, I'm not saying this as some sort of statement that we should change course. We can't. This is happening. I just want to have my eyes open, and hopefully be able to help some people see and adjust along the way.</p></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[We're All in Fractal Microcults]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/were-all-in-microcults-now?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/were-all-in-microcults-now</guid>
            <pubDate>Thu, 03 Jul 2025 13:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[We now have an infinite number of ways to strongly disagree with someone]]></description>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[How to Talk to AI]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/how-to-talk-to-ai?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/how-to-talk-to-ai</guid>
            <pubDate>Mon, 30 Jun 2025 12:45:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Clear thinking in AI conversations/images/clear-thinking-ai.png There's a popular idea going around right now about renaming "prompt engineering" to "context engineering." The argument is that context engineering better captures the true nature of what we're trying to do with AI. I think that's w...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/clear-thinking-ai.png" alt="Clear thinking in AI conversations" width="1024" height="1024" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"><p>There's a popular idea going around right now about renaming "prompt engineering" to "context engineering." The argument is that context engineering better captures the true nature of what we're trying to do with AI.</p><p>I think that's wrong. Let me give you an analogy for why.</p><p>The pure way to think about interacting with AI is that it comes down to clear thinking. What's interesting about this is that it's the same for writing, coding, and giving presentations.</p><!----><p>You can't really be good at any of those things without being able to think clearly and articulate exactly what you're trying to accomplish.</p><h2 id="the-director-analogy" tabindex="-1">The Director Analogy </h2><p>Imagine you're a director for a key scene in a major movie. The staff comes up to you and asks, "OK, what's in this scene?"</p><p>This question isn't about process or tools. It's about idea, goal, and vision. The degree to which you have a clear answer is the degree to which you're good at writing, coding, or interacting with AI.</p><h3 id="the-prompting-answer" tabindex="-1">The Prompting Answer </h3><p>A "prompting" answer to the director question would be:</p><blockquote> When I tell people what to do on this set, I issue commands in the following way: I say 'action,' I say 'stop rolling,' and I say 'more energy!' That's how I'm a good director. <cite>The Prompt Engineer</cite></blockquote><p>But you haven't described the scene, the movie, the vision, or what we're all trying to accomplish. You've just given details about syntax.</p><h3 id="the-context-engineering-answer" tabindex="-1">The Context Engineering Answer </h3><p>The "context engineering" version would be:</p><blockquote> I'm an expert in making this set look exactly like it's supposed to, with all the different things that need to be on it. I can get old cars, vintage living room setups, any kind of costume, and any type of extras. I'm really good at doing this. <cite>The Context Engineer</cite></blockquote><p>And the whole staff is still looking at you saying, "OK cool, but what is the scene? What are we trying to do?"</p><div class="x-embed-container" data-v-eb8b3cb6=""><blockquote class="twitter-tweet" data-v-eb8b3cb6=""><a href="https://twitter.com/DanielMiessler/status/1940097678047944946" data-v-eb8b3cb6="">Loading tweet...</a></blockquote></div><h2 id="the-real-skill" tabindex="-1">The Real Skill </h2><p>Both prompt engineering and context engineering focus on the wrong thing. They're like arguing whether a director needs to be better at shouting "action!" or better at sourcing props.</p><aside>Prompting is likely becoming less important, and context engineering <strong>more</strong> important.</aside><p>Whether you're writing an essay, coding a feature, or interacting with AI, success comes from clarity of purpose and vision. The tools and techniques are secondary to understanding what you're trying to accomplish.</p><h2 id="summary" tabindex="-1">Summary </h2><ol><li>The Prompting vs. Context Engineering debate misses the point.</li><li>Both are critical, but they're secondary to the bigger problem of unclear thinking.</li><li>If you can't articulate what you want, prompting and context won't help you much.</li><li>Focus on the clarity of <em>what you want</em>, and then it'll be a lot clearer what contextâ€”and formattingâ€”you'll need to improve the outcome.</li></ol></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Dumping Cursor for VSCode + Claude Code]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/dumping-cursor-for-vscode-claude-code?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/dumping-cursor-for-vscode-claude-code</guid>
            <pubDate>Wed, 25 Jun 2025 20:30:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Claude Code for VSCode extension in the marketplacehttps://danielmiessler.com/images/claude-code-extension-marketplace.png Don't get me wrongâ€”Cursor is genuinely awesome. It's probably the best AI-native code editor ever built, with incredibly thoughtful integrations and a user experience that act...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/claude-code-extension-marketplace.png" alt="Claude Code for VSCode extension in the marketplace" width="1558" height="420" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"><p>Don't get me wrongâ€”Cursor is genuinely awesome. It's probably the best AI-native code editor ever built, with incredibly thoughtful integrations and a user experience that actually works. But now that Claude Code has launched with full VSCode integration, there's simply no reason to pay for another subscription or deal with a separate tool.</p><h2 id="cursor-actually-pretty-great" tabindex="-1">Cursor: Actually Pretty Great </h2><p>Let me be clearâ€”Cursor deserves massive credit for showing the industry what AI-assisted coding should look like. The team built something genuinely innovative:</p><aside> Cursor proved AI assistance could be seamless rather than disruptive in development workflows. </aside><h3 id="what-cursor-got-right" tabindex="-1">What Cursor Got Right </h3><ul><li><strong>Inline suggestions</strong> that actually understand context</li><li><strong>Smooth tab completion</strong> with AI awareness</li><li><strong>Built-in chat</strong> that feels natural and integrated</li><li><strong>Codebase understanding</strong> that goes beyond single files</li><li><strong>Smart refactoring</strong> suggestions that make sense</li></ul><p>The UX is polished, the AI integration feels thoughtful, and it genuinely makes coding faster and more enjoyable. For a purpose-built AI editor, they nailed the fundamentals.</p><h3 id="but-then-claude-code-happened" tabindex="-1">But Then Claude Code Happened </h3><p>Here's the thing: Claude Code just made Cursor unnecessary for my workflow. Not because Cursor is badâ€”it's notâ€”but because Claude Code delivers the same benefits while being:</p><ol><li><strong>Free</strong> (no monthly subscription)</li><li><strong>Inside VSCode</strong> (the editor I already know and love)</li><li><strong>Integrated with MCP</strong> (Model Context Protocol for tool access)</li><li><strong>More powerful</strong> (access to Claude Sonnet 4)</li><li><strong>More flexible</strong> (I control when and how to engage it)</li></ol><h2 id="why-vscode-claude-code-wins" tabindex="-1">Why VSCode + Claude Code Wins </h2><p>The combination isn't just competitive with Cursorâ€”it's actually superior in several key ways:</p><h3 id="no-subscription-required" tabindex="-1">No Subscription Required </h3><p>Cursor costs $20/month for Pro features. Claude Code is free with your existing Claude subscription (which I already had). The math is simple.</p><h3 id="vscode-s-mature-ecosystem" tabindex="-1">VSCode's Mature Ecosystem </h3><p>VSCode has thousands of extensions, years of refinement, and a workflow I've already optimized. Why learn a new editor when I can get better AI assistance in the tool I already love?</p><!----><h3 id="more-powerful-ai" tabindex="-1">More Powerful AI </h3><p>Claude Sonnet 4 through Claude Code is consistently more capable than Cursor's AI models. The responses are more nuanced, the code suggestions more sophisticated, and the problem-solving more thorough.</p><h3 id="better-context-management" tabindex="-1">Better Context Management </h3><p>Unlike Cursor's AI which feels somewhat contained, Claude Code can understand and work with my entire project context, maintain state across sessions, and even execute terminal commands when needed.</p><h2 id="the-economic-reality" tabindex="-1">The Economic Reality </h2><p>Here's what really sealed the decision: I was paying for both Cursor Pro ($20/month) and Claude Pro ($20/month). Now I just need Claude Pro and get better AI assistance directly in VSCode.</p><aside> Saving $240/year while getting better functionality isn't exactly a hard choice. </aside><h2 id="the-new-workflow" tabindex="-1">The New Workflow </h2><p>Here's how VSCode + Claude Code actually works in practice:</p><ol><li><strong>Regular coding</strong> happens in VSCode with all my existing extensions and shortcuts</li><li><strong>Complex problems</strong> get handed off to Claude Code for deep analysis and solutions</li><li><strong>File operations</strong> are seamlessâ€”Claude Code can read, write, and execute across my entire project</li><li><strong>Context persistence</strong> means Claude remembers our entire conversation and project state</li></ol><aside> I engage Claude Code intentionally rather than having AI baked into every keystroke like Cursor. </aside><h2 id="what-i-actually-miss-from-cursor" tabindex="-1">What I Actually Miss from Cursor </h2><p>Let me be honest about what Cursor does better:</p><ul><li><strong>Inline suggestions</strong> appear more smoothly</li><li><strong>Tab completion</strong> with AI context is more seamless</li><li><strong>Immediate AI feedback</strong> without needing to actively engage</li></ul><p>These are real conveniences, and Cursor's UX team deserves credit for making AI assistance feel effortless.</p><h2 id="but-the-math-is-simple" tabindex="-1">But The Math is Simple </h2><p>Here's the reality check:</p><ul><li><strong>Cursor Pro</strong>: $20/month for AI-assisted coding</li><li><strong>Claude Pro</strong>: $20/month for the best AI available</li><li><strong>VSCode</strong>: Free, with my entire workflow already optimized</li><li><strong>Claude Code</strong>: Free with Claude Pro subscription</li></ul><p>Why pay for two subscriptions when one gives me better AI in the editor I already prefer?</p><h2 id="for-current-cursor-users" tabindex="-1">For Current Cursor Users </h2><p>If you're happy with Cursor, stick with itâ€”it's genuinely excellent. But if you're already paying for Claude Pro, or if you're deeply invested in VSCode's ecosystem, Claude Code offers a compelling alternative that might save you money while delivering superior AI capabilities.</p><!----><h2 id="the-bottom-line" tabindex="-1">The Bottom Line </h2><p>Cursor proved that AI-native editors could work brilliantly. But now that the best AI assistant is available directly in VSCode, the extra tool and subscription feel unnecessary.</p><p>Claude Code isn't just competing with Cursorâ€”it's making it redundant.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>This post was AI-generated at Level 4 - "AI Created, Human Basic Idea" per my <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-influence-level-ail" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">AI Influence Level framework</a>. I provided the direction and feedback while Claude wrote the entire piece, including this note.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Why Google I/O Scared This 2007 Apple Fanboy for the First Time]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/apple-fanboy-worried-googleio?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/apple-fanboy-worried-googleio</guid>
            <pubDate>Mon, 09 Jun 2025 09:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Apple fanboy worried about Google's AI pivothttps://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/apple-fanboy-worried-googleio-apple-fanboy-worried-googleio/public As an Apple Fanboy going back to 2007https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-iphone-will-dominate-but-not-because-of-f...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/apple-fanboy-worried-googleio-apple-fanboy-worried-googleio/public" alt="Apple fanboy worried about Google's AI pivot" width="1200" height="630" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"><p>As an Apple Fanboy <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-iphone-will-dominate-but-not-because-of-features-functionality" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">going back to 2007</a>, this is the first year I've felt fear for Apple's future. And after just watching Apple's WWDC I am still scared for them.</p><p>I have not been super worried for the last several months about Siri not being fully AI-enabled yetâ€”because I understand the difficulty of protecting against prompt injection. Especially since Apple is the Privacy/Security company, and they have the most context on the most people anywhere. Security-wise, they have to get it perfectly right, not just a little bit.</p><!----><p>So the delays didn't freak me out. Especially since they're low-key implementing Apple Intelligence all over the place throughout the operating systems and apps. It's a typical Apple game of continuous subtle integrations of a new technology. They don't blow people over, but they add up over time.</p><aside>Apple worked at Apple in security</aside><p>What scared me for the first time was this year's Google I/O.</p><p>It wasn't that it was just better than Apple across everything, or that it did Apple better than Apple. It didn't.</p><p>What scared me is that they showed they're going in a completely new directionâ€”a direction that I wish Apple were going in.</p><p>Rather than being an ad companyâ€”which I've always hated about Googleâ€”I got the distinct sense that they just became an AI company.</p><p>Everything that they showed, and just like the whole feel of the presentation, felt really big. Really different. Like the vision and focus of the company had moved from being this nasty "Android is a mobile platform to help bolster search and ads" to something like "We're the best at data and AI, and now all our products are based around that."</p><p>I've not heard that from Apple, even after their 2025 WWDC.</p><aside>Apple Intelligence is everywhere</aside><p>I loved WWDC this year. I love the design update. I love all the new Apple Intelligence features and how they're integrated with everything. It's all great stuff. Especially how they're merging things more smoothly, like Phone on macOS. Google is still way behind on this stuffâ€”especially since they don't have a desktop/laptop OS.</p><p>But all this was still old Apple improving at old Apple things, which is the UI/UX of our digital lives. They're building LifeOS, basically, and it's gorgeous. But Google is coming at that from the Data / AI angle first, and thinking less about the UI/UX (which they've always sucked at).</p><p>My concern for Apple is that Google's vision here is so damn powerful. Gemini is in everything now. Deeper than Apple Intelligence. It's in most of their Workplace services, it's deeper in their Mobile OS.</p><p>It's kind of like Google's OS is now Gemini, and the question is just where and how Gemini runs.</p><h2 id="diverging-focus" tabindex="-1">Diverging focus </h2><p>My friend was watching WWDC with me just now and they were like, "I'm concerned for Apple because Google I/O was way better."</p><p>The way I explained my take was to say that I see them in completely different ways now.</p><ol><li>Apple is the UI/UX to the world</li><li>Google is the Data/AI layer</li></ol><p>So it's not that Google just beat Apple at being Apple. I don't think they did. Apple is still the way I want to interact with the world. I like the hardware better. I like the software better. And I like the unified experience better.</p><aside>Google isn't on the desktop</aside><p>But I wish Apple had Google's AI-first mentality to things.</p><p>So for me the real question is whether Apple can fix their AI before Google can fix their UI/UX.</p><p>My gut says Google is moving way faster on their path than Apple is on theirs. And this concerns me, because I feel like anyone who wins at AI has advantages everywhere else as well.</p><p>Tim basically said the big upgrade to Siri isn't coming until 2026. That's insane to me. It's 2025 and it works like 40% of the time for me, for doing basic things like turning on the lights.</p><p>I don't know if Apple has that long, honestly. It could be that by the time they get Siri figured out, Gemini is already miles ahead.</p><p>And in the meantime Meta and OpenAI will be trying to replace the iPhone as the primary interface to AI, and to LifeOS itself.</p><p>I want Apple to win. But they're running out of time.</p></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[The Chinese Room Problem With the 'LLMs only predict the next token' Argument]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/problem-chinese-room-tokens?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/problem-chinese-room-tokens</guid>
            <pubDate>Sun, 08 Jun 2025 09:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Opaque Thinking Machines/images/chinese-room-brain-architecture.png I'm sure you've heard the argument that LLMs aren't really thinking because, according to them, > LLMs are just predicting the next token... And that output is also non-deterministic. The problem with this argument is the same ...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/chinese-room-brain-architecture.png" alt="Opaque Thinking Machines"></p><p>I'm sure you've heard the argument that LLMs aren't really thinking because, according to them,</p><blockquote><p>LLMs are just predicting the next token...</p></blockquote><aside>And that output is also non-deterministic.</aside><p>The problem with this argument is the same exact thing applies to the human brain. You can just as easily say,</p><aside>And Shakespeare is just vibrations of vocal folds.</aside><blockquote><p>The human brain is nothing but a collection of neurons that finds patterns and generates output.</p></blockquote><h2 id="the-reason-output-argument" tabindex="-1">The reason output argument </h2><p>There's a related argument <em>for</em> something's ability to reason, that says something like,</p><blockquote><p>If you give a problem and the output could only have been the result of reason, then the thing you gave it to must be able to reason.</p></blockquote><aside>This was by John Searle in a 1980 paper called Minds, Brains, and Programs.</aside><p>And one counter to that argument is called the <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_room" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Chinese Room Argument</a>, which basically says that computers can't ever <em>really</em> be thinking because they don't truly understand what they're doing.</p><p>The paper's analogy is a person inside a room with a set of instructions on how to translate Chinese characters into English. The person inside the room doesn't actually understand Chinese; they just follow the instructions to produce the correct output.</p><p>The paper argues that this room itself is like a computerâ€”any and all computers actuallyâ€”where there is complex work coming out of it, but at no point is there actual <em>thinking</em> or <em>understanding</em> happening.</p><h2 id="the-problem-with-the-chinese-room-argument" tabindex="-1">The problem with the Chinese Room Argument </h2><aside>I'm not sure why this isn't brought up more.</aside><p>The extraordinary mistake in this argument is not realizing that the brain of a Chinese-speaking humanâ€”<em>is also a Chinese machine</em>.</p><p>When Chinese goes into the ears or eyes of a Chinese speaker, what comes out is understanding of what that Chinese meant. The speaker has no idea how that happened. They simply look at the characters on the page, or hear the words, and they somehow know what they mean.</p><p>This is exactly as if those Chinese characters are being passed into another room inside of the Chinese speakerâ€™s brain, and inside the brain there is someone in a room with actual Chinese understanding.</p><p>We know for certain that this is what's happening inside the brain of every human who understands a language. Or anything else.</p><aside>What if your black box got replaced with another? Would you even know?</aside><!----><p>Once we understand something, the ability to produce out output from that understanding turns into a Chinese room. We throw input, and our brain throws back output. From where? We have no earthly idea.</p><p>Our brains are Chinese rooms.</p><p>Somehow, we have forgotten this. We've completely overlooked the fact that we havenâ€™t a slightest idea where all this knowledge, and understanding, and creativity, is coming from when we pose problems to our own minds.</p><h2 id="confusing-substrate-and-output" tabindex="-1">Confusing substrate and output </h2><aside>Not strictly true, but you get the point.</aside><p>If you slice into a human brain and look at what it's made of, you will find nothing but neurons, axions, and synapses. Just as you will find nothing but layers of weights in the brain of a Transformer. Two different substrates, but ultimately an architecture of connections and activations.</p><aside>*Mostly* opaque, these days.</aside><p>In both cases, you have a completely opaque, non-deterministic, and <em>highly mysterious</em> process around how it generates output.</p><p>If you want to know if something can reason, I propose the only way to find out is to:</p><ol><li>Decide on a definition of reasoning</li><li>Give it problems that require reasoning to solve</li><li>See if it can solve them</li></ol><p>Anything less is dishonest at some fundamental level.</p><p>Yes, humans can reason. But we're really sloppy with how we think we know we can. And as a result, we're similarly sloppy with our definition of reasoning.</p><p>Most attempts end up being <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/No_true_Scotsman" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">No True Scotsman</a> appeals that basically say,</p><aside>And of course they can't actually explain how humans do.</aside><blockquote><p>It's truly reasoning if and only if it reasons like I think humans do.</p></blockquote><p>We have no idea how humans are able to reason or be creative.</p><p>Very much like Transformers, our brains are black boxes that produce wondrous output through a vast network of small nodes that light up together when given input.</p><p>Any materialist who claims that we are fundamentally different than Transformers has all their work in front of them, since they neither know how humans do it nor how transformers do it.</p></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[A Better Way to Think About AI Job Replacement]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/better-way-think-AI-replacement?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/better-way-think-AI-replacement</guid>
            <pubDate>Wed, 04 Jun 2025 09:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Founders Replacing Workers with Automationhttps://danielmiessler.com/images/founders-replacing-workers-automation.png You donâ€™t have to believe that companies want to fire all their employees to see AIâ€™s threat to jobs. Thatâ€™s negative framing. Most company leaders are regular, decent people wh...]]></description>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[How I See AI Affecting Education]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-effect-education?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-effect-education</guid>
            <pubDate>Sun, 01 Jun 2025 09:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[AI Effect on Education Gaphttps://danielmiessler.com/images/ai-education-split-screen.png I've got a one-liner for what AI will do to education. The simplest answer to how AI will affect education is that it'll dramatically widen existing gaps: it'll make great students extraordinary, and bad s...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/ai-education-split-screen.png" alt="AI Effect on Education Gap" width="1024" height="1024" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"><p>I've got a one-liner for what AI will do to education.</p><!----><p>And it'll be the same <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/great-bifurcation" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">with everything else</a>. But I want to be clear about something:</p><p><em>Iâ€™m not saying this is how it should be, or how it could be, or how I intend for it to be</em>.</p><p>Iâ€™m actually working on a system for how this can <em>not</em> be the caseâ€”where we can have AI close the gap rather than widen it.</p><p>What Iâ€™m saying is: this is whatâ€™s happening nowâ€”by default. In education, but also everywhere else AI touches.</p><p>AI, <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-becoming-reading" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">just like reading</a>, is a powerful tool for self-improvement. But like all such tools, theyâ€™re mostly used by people who are into self-improvement.</p><p>So the real game is getting more people into that self-improvement culture and mindset.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>My 2023 post on the Bifurcation of Society: <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/bifurcation-of-society/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">The Great Bifurcation</a></li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[I Have Two Groups of AI Friends]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-friend-groups?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-friend-groups</guid>
            <pubDate>Sat, 31 May 2025 09:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[AI Friend Groups in Cybersecurityhttps://danielmiessler.com/images/ai-two-groups-cybersecurity.png I have two groups of very smart cybersecurity friends, and they see AI completely differently. The radical skeptics The first group thinks AI is mostly a scam, like NFT / mint grifting in crypto,...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/ai-two-groups-cybersecurity.png" alt="AI Friend Groups in Cybersecurity" width="1024" height="1024" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"><p>I have two groups of very smart cybersecurity friends, and they see AI completely differently.</p><h1 id="the-radical-skeptics" tabindex="-1">The radical skeptics </h1><p>The first group thinks AI is mostly a scam, like NFT / mint grifting in crypto, and that we're wasting billions of dollars on a bunch of vaporware.</p><aside>Not all of them believe all of these, obviously.</aside><p>They do things like:</p><ul><li>Say AI can be useful for some useful stuff, but it's <em>way</em> overhyped</li><li>Claim modern AI like Gemini 2.5 or Sonnet 4 are <a href="https://marcushutchins.com/blog/tech/opinions/the-ai-intelligence-paradox.html" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">not even real intelligence</a> (my friend Marcus Hutchins).</li></ul><p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/linkedin-hoff-ai-post.png" alt="Making Fun of AI"></p><ul><li>Constantly make fun of silly mistakes that AI's make, like counting the number of r's in the word Strawberry (my friend Christofer Hoff)</li><li>Saying repeatedly, in various ways, that AI is "just next-token prediction"â€”meaning it's just a parlour trick, and it doesn't actually understand or reason. And that they've "figured this out" because they looked into it, and people like me are deceived ourselves and are in turn deceiving others.</li><li>Claim the entire thing is a hype train powered by billionaires and VCs trying to get rich. In other words, just another example of Capitalism ruining everything.</li><li>Etc.</li></ul><aside>Not speaking specifically about anyone mentioned thus far here, but to the naysayers more generally.</aside><h2 id="my-issue-with-their-overall-arguments" tabindex="-1">My issue with their overall arguments </h2><p>The thing that bothers me about their stance isâ€”well...a few things, actually...</p><ol><li>I think they're lashing out at the wrong thing, and often times many wrong things in their pushback.</li><li>They way they argue their points with someone like me is often quite squishy and ephemeralâ€”on one hand saying they're embracing AI themselves and use it every day, and then constantly making the points above that counter that stance to younger and more impressionable people.</li><li>And most importantlyâ€”I think they're significantly damaging the lives and careers of people they convince to think the same way about AI.</li></ol><p>On the first point, I think they're often pushing back on out-of-control marketing groups who are saying everything is AI, and people scamming in ways similar to cryptoâ€”of which there are definitely many.</p><p>Some are also objecting to the prospect of a human-less future, where AI does everything for us and saps the meaning and joy out of life.</p><p>Many are also against the massive amounts of AI-slop being pushed onto the world due to vibe coding and the like.</p><p>Great! Fair points! <em>I don't like those things either.</em></p><p>For the second item, they can't have both. On the one hand they claim to be saying AI is useful and powerful and that it will be used to do some good things, but with nearly every public comment they're bashing the entire industry and movement with obvious vitriol. It doesn't feel internally self-consistent.</p><p>And as for the third itemâ€”of hurting people's futuresâ€”that one really upsets me. Whenever they do the public bashing of AI and how stupid it is, or what it can't do, or mistakes it makes, or how it's "just next-token prediction", they get this massive hooray from so many people.</p><p>And I think those people are worse off for it. Far worse off.</p><p>Of course, it could be that <em>I'm</em> the one who's wrong, and I'm going to be the one eating blackbird in a couple of years. But I don't think so.</p><h1 id="the-radical-embracers" tabindex="-1">The radical embracers </h1><p>And then I have my <em>other</em> group of friendsâ€”the ones who are extremely bullish, and excited, and afraid, and invigorated by what's coming. In this category I want to call out Joseph Thacker specifically, as well as Jason Haddix.</p><p>In late 2022 and the first couple of months of 2023, I had very few people I could talk to about AI, and Joseph was one of those few. Just like me, he <em>instantly</em> not just got it, but saw a million different implications for technology and society.</p><p>And whenever we talked, it was like we were thinking the same thoughts at the same time. My best friend Jason was a bit more skeptical for a whileâ€”because he successfully avoided the crypto thing and justifiably saw a lot of parallelsâ€”but he quickly caught up and is now completely religious on it like me as well.</p><aside>I'll spare the arguments for another time.</aside><p>I now have several friends who see the AI world in a similar way, not to mention people like Dwarkesh Patel and other public people who are similarly predicting that AI will be extraordinarily powerful and transformative in the next few years.</p><aside>Yeah, I'm going to just call them haters, and I think their public record supports that.</aside><p>What trips me out is how similar the haters are with the embracers. We're all smart. We're all in cybersecurity. We're all successful in our field. And yet we see things <em>so</em> differently.</p><p>I think it's mostly a deeper thing with outlook on life. A default-positive view of:</p><aside>Within the pessimist group there is obvious great variation in worldviews as well, of course.</aside><ul><li>CHANGE</li><li>business</li><li>technology's role with humanity</li><li>their understanding/perception of history</li><li>whether they're generally positive or negative about the future</li><li>etc.</li></ul><p>It's hard to pin down, but when I think of the pro and con groups overall, I just see and hear more overall positivity and embrace of change from the pro group. And more negative interpretation of life in general from the con group.</p><p>When I talk to the pro group, they're smiling. They're excited. They're freaked out just like me and everyone else in the group, but they're turning that into excitement and energy instead of anxiety and anger.</p><p>And againâ€”I'm talking about people who are bashing AI publiclyâ€”as a general movementâ€”multiple times a week, with clear anger in their words. I'm not talking about people who are worried about AI vulnerabilities or whatever. We all share that concern.</p><h1 id="going-forward" tabindex="-1">Going forward </h1><p>What I recommend you do is think very carefully about your own opinions on AI, and the opinions that you're receiving from others.</p><p>Listen for the overall outlook on life that underpins the position. Listen for their (and your) world view and see if it's affecting your AI view as well. Is the outlook generally positive or negative? Do they generally embrace change, or do they resist it? Etc.</p><aside>One could accuse me here of begging the question.</aside><p>This isn't a perfect heuristic, but I'd say be cautious of people who resist changeâ€”especially when that change is as inevitable as the transition from punch cards to Github. Or from steam to electricity. Although I guess that is the very question that's up for debate.</p><p>I guess just think about one's perception of change, and whether it's a good thing. And try to use that as a lens for interpreting their reaction to AI.</p><p>And if you have any other ideas on what might distinguish the two groups, please let me know. I'm curious.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>I want to explicitly call out that everyone I know in the con group, or at least in my friend group anyway, are doing this because they think it's the right thing to do. We both think we're protecting the users here. So it's just a matter of how we see the world.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[AI Job Replacement: Slow Then Fast, May 2025 Edition]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/slug?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/slug</guid>
            <pubDate>Fri, 30 May 2025 09:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Some napkin comments on how job replacement might play out]]></description>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[We Can't Really Affect AI Security]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-security?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-security</guid>
            <pubDate>Thu, 29 May 2025 09:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[AI Security - Human Behavior and Cyber Market Dynamicshttps://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/ai-security-Human_Behavior_Cyber_Market_Dynamics/public We're about to get precisely the right amount of AI Security. No more and no less. I've talked before about what I...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/ai-security-Human_Behavior_Cyber_Market_Dynamics/public" alt="AI Security - Human Behavior and Cyber Market Dynamics" width="1024" height="1024" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"><p>We're about to get precisely the right amount of AI Security. No more and no less.</p><p>I've talked before about what I called the <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/efficient-security-principle" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">Efficient Security Principle</a>, which says the excitement for a technology lowers its required security bar.</p><p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/ai-security-ESP-efficient-security-principle/public" alt="ESP"></p><p>That's definitely happening with AI, but I'm interested in another aspect of the principle.</p><p><strong>It doesn't really matter what any individual or group does to increase or reduce AI Security, because that security baseline is determined by overall human interaction with the technology.</strong></p><aside>The security of MCP is likely to be a great example of punctuated equilibrium here.</aside><ol><li><p>If we don't have enough security, but not enough bad things happen, security won't increase despite anyone's Heroic efforts.</p></li><li><p>Conversely, we can release a torrent of AI slop with no security whatsoever, and if one or more breaches are so bad that they slap people awakeâ€¦increased security will happen almost overnight.</p></li></ol><p>In other words, the amount of security that we get and maintain will be precisely the "right" amount.</p><p>Not the amount that us security people wantâ€”or that really anyone sees as the perfect or adequate amountâ€”but the "right" amount in terms of a business/risk functional equilibrium.</p><!----><p>All this to sayâ€¦</p><p><em>Don't worry too much about AI Security.</em></p><aside>Government / regulation seems to be an exception, but it too is largely guided by public outcry.</aside><p>It's going to be a horror show, and it's already started. But there's nothing that any one person or group can do to actually move the baseline.</p><p>The baseline moves on its own, with the mass of a dozen suns, based on how well the tech is working for people as a whole compared to how afraid people are to use it.</p><!----><p>That equilibrium is not something we can control. It moves by itself based on what actually happens in the world.</p><ul><li>When massive, significant incidents happenâ€”security will increase</li><li>When things are mostly quiet (like the billions lost every year from online banking security issues), people will yawn and accept, and security improvements will stagnate.</li></ul><p>As security people, we need to acknowledge that no amount of rooftop screaming will affect this dynamic.</p><p>Let's relax a bit, put on our shepherd's gear, and try to guide people the best we can through this insane moment in history.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/efficient-security-principle" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">The Efficient Security Principle</a></li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[AI Solution Factories]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-solution-factories?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-solution-factories</guid>
            <pubDate>Fri, 02 May 2025 09:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[AI Solution Factories - Thousands of Companieshttps://danielmiessler.com/images/thousands-tiny-companies-profile.png Everyone is trying to figure out whether developers are screwed or not. Some say they are, and others say more AI just means more need for software, so we'll need more software than...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/thousands-tiny-companies-profile.png" alt="AI Solution Factories - Thousands of Companies" width="1024" height="1024" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"><p>Everyone is trying to figure out whether developers are screwed or not. Some say they are, and others say more AI just means more need for software, so we'll need more software than ever.</p><p>It'll be interesting how it plays out, but I have a couple of questions that might help illuminate things.</p><ol><li><p>Will those companies be needing current developers or some broader type of builder?</p></li><li><p>What will it mean to have tens of thousands of builders inside of a giant tech company if that company is building thousands of products?</p></li></ol><p>Taking the first one, I don't think it makes sense to say we'll still need all the developers we have. We won't. Because those developers were trained to execute the vision of someone else using a narrow set of tools.</p><p>Some subset of those developers are broader creators/builders/idea people, but I don't think it's a large percentage. And it's obviously a gradient, not some magic threshold.</p><p>But I'd say something like 1-10% are capable of coming up with incredible ideas and implementing them end-to-end and building the necessary stuff around them like websites and marketing and such. And I don't mean they can do it themselves, but that they have a feel for what's needed, and have good taste about it.</p><p>Those are the people these companies will need, not today's average programmer. Because they will be in charge of full-stack idea creation, deployment, and integration.</p><p>Second, what does it mean to have 10,000 employees at a company like Google in 2035?</p><p>Or, maybe a better question isâ€”what does Google become in 2035? Not a search company. Not an ads company.</p><p>I think when large companies today switch from being human-based to AI-based, it means the companies themselves change from being high-leverage implementations of their few products to being conglomerates of hundreds of thousands or millions of micro-companies.</p><p>Why wouldn't they?</p><p>Why wouldn't they switch the goal of the company to identifying problems in the world and solving them better than anyone else?</p><p>This is what current companies do already, but they are forced to focus on a specific problem, or subset of problems, and they're very careful not to branch out into areas they're unfamiliar with.</p><p>That changes when it's possible to quickly get very smart about the problem space and start building solutions for it.</p><p>In 2025 I already know several people who are thinking of building AI-powered product companies. What kind of product? Any kind. Any kind where there's an annoying problem that people would pay money to solve, and where the current solutions are bad.</p><p>I think large companies start to turn into large versions of those. And the question is whether anything will stop the big players there from crushing everyone else. Regulation? Seems like the only option.</p><p>Here's the model, which is kind of like a universal business engine.</p><ol><li>Identify problems that people and businesses have</li><li>Identify the existing solutions</li><li>Identify issues with the existing solutions</li><li>Identify what a superior solution would look like</li><li>Build that</li><li>Use your vast resources in distribution to get in front of the AIs that look for superior solutions to pitch to their principals</li><li>Profit as more people/companies switch to your solution</li></ol><p>So business becomes a directory of products and services, provided by some builder shop, and the question is just which shop that is.</p><p>Is it the 5-person team who has an inspired idea for how to solve that one particular problem? And build the whole thing with AI?</p><p>Or is it Google 2035 who found that problem, and found that solution, and spawned a micro-team to go and build a better version of that product and get it adopted by users?</p><p>If we think this might be where things are headed, which I do, now ask yourself how many of our current developers are going to have jobs?</p><p>As I said before, I think very few will translate into this new world of full-stack-builder that has to deeply understand problems, and sentiment, and engineering, and go-to-market.</p><p>And then you have to start asking how quickly that itself can be automated.</p><p>But even without it being automated, I don't think there are that many people in the current market who can do this job.</p><p>Maybe education improves and there will be millions of them. Or maybe we just work really hard to automate that role as well.</p><p>I have no doubt that there will be roles for humans as this whole thing scales up, assuming it goes in this direction.</p><p>What I doubt is that it will inevitably lead to more and more existing developer types being needed.</p><p>It's true that more creators will be needed, but I think they'll be skilled generalists as talked about above, and they'll be like independent leaders of entire conglomerates of AI-powered companies, not the developers we have now.</p><p>So will we need people going forward? Sure.</p><p>But not the people we currently have.</p><p>And it's not clear how much of the gap will be filled by these new renaissance builders vs. AI that can do 90% of that job.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/path-to-asi" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">A Possible Path to ASI</a></li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[A Possible Path to ASI]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/path-to-asi?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/path-to-asi</guid>
            <pubDate>Wed, 02 Apr 2025 09:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[ASI Workflowhttps://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/path-to-asi-grid-ideas/public toc I've had what I think is a good definition for AGIhttps://danielmiessler.com/blog/raid-ai-definitionsartificial-general-intelligence-agi for a while now, but ASI has been more e...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/path-to-asi-grid-ideas/public" alt="ASI Workflow" width="1292" height="1609" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"><nav class="table-of-contents"><ul><li><a href="#a-definition-of-asi">A Definition of ASI</a></li><li><a href="#extending-agi-to-get-to-asi">Extending AGI to Get to ASI</a></li><li><a href="#a-possible-path-to-both-agi-and-asi">A Possible Path to Both AGI and ASI</a></li><li><a href="#summary">Summary</a></li></ul></nav><p>I've had what I think is <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/raid-ai-definitions#artificial-general-intelligence-agi" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">a good definition for AGI</a> for a while now, but ASI has been more elusiveâ€”at least for me.</p><p>The problem is that AGI is trying to get to something we know, which is roughly <em>our</em> intelligence level. And it seems much harder to intuit what it means to be <em>above</em> human intelligence.</p><p>So what I'm going to do here is:</p><ol><li>Give a working definition of ASI</li><li>Show how it relates to, and extends, AGI</li><li>Describe a practical methodology for pursuing it.</li></ol><h2 id="a-definition-of-asi" tabindex="-1">A Definition of ASI </h2><p>Similar to my <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/raid-ai-definitions#artificial-general-intelligence-agi" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">definition of AGI</a>, I think a definition of ASI should be <em>human-centric</em>. In other words, the definition should start to answer the question of:</p><blockquote><p>Why do we care?</p></blockquote><p>Or, what theoretical ASI capabilities could an AI have that would have the most impact on humans? I think the answer comes down to two main components:</p><aside><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-state-management">State Transition</a> is one of the ultimate use cases of ASI.</aside><ol><li>Creating net-new things that help or harm usâ€”like medicines or weapons</li><li>Managing things we care aboutâ€”like our lives, our businesses, our countries, and our society</li></ol><aside>Really an AI <em>system</em>.</aside><p>If an AI can do those things better than any human, I'd say that's a good basis for a definition of ASI.</p><p>But just to tighten it up and generalize it, let's go with:</p><!----><h2 id="extending-agi-to-get-to-asi" tabindex="-1">Extending AGI to Get to ASI </h2><p><a href="https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/path-to-asi-agi-asi-spectrum/public"><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/path-to-asi-agi-asi-spectrum/public"></a></p>AGI -&gt; ASI (Click for full size image)<p>So we're really saying ASI <em>extends</em> AGI.</p><p>It's the same thingâ€”general cognitive abilityâ€”but to a superhuman level. So, in condensed form:</p><aside>This is just using knowledge work as an accepted metric of cognitive competency.</aside><ul><li>AGI is an AI that's able to do cognitive work as well as an average knowledge worker</li><li>ASI is an AI that's able to do cognitive work better than any human</li></ul><p>It's a spectrum, as we see in <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/path-to-asi-agi-asi-spectrum/public" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">the chart above</a>.</p><h2 id="a-possible-path-to-both-agi-and-asi" tabindex="-1">A Possible Path to Both AGI and ASI </h2><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/path-to-asi-grid-ideas/public" alt="The Cognitive Progress Workflow" width="1292" height="1609" loading="lazy">The Cognitive Progress Workflow (Click for Full Size)<p>And that brings us to the main idea here, which is the question of how to to move up in this chart. Procedurallyâ€”as a general approach.</p><p>I think the answer is to emulate what we know works in humansâ€”which I'll capture and simplify as the following:</p><aside>Sometimes the magic is in the imperfect copying.</aside><ol><li>Have decent hardware (human brain, human evolution, etc.)</li><li>Have lots of experiences, combined with training/education</li><li>Face challenges / problems</li><li>Use your hardware, training, and experience to try to solve those problems</li><li>Learn from the results</li><li>Talk with other people who are doing the same</li><li>Take some of their ideas and copy them, modify them, or combine them with your own</li><li>Sleep and/or take time away from the problem, and let your subconscious work on the problem without you</li><li>Continue bombarding yourself with new inputs, through reading, conversation with others, etc.</li><li>Suddenly get inspiration for a new way to solve the problem, which you then go and try</li></ol><p><em>Repeat</em>.</p><p>This is what I find so promising about this whole challenge of getting to AGI and ASI:</p><p><strong>It seems like the iterative process described above can be easily orchestrated and scaled using techâ€”including current AI.</strong></p><aside>Not only scale what happens naturally, but with added variation. Accelerated evolution, basically.</aside><p>We basically take the human process of learning, thinking, combining and copying ideas, sleeping on them, trying them out, etc.â€”like we saw in The Enlightenment and like we see with places like San Franciscoâ€”<em>and we automate the hell of it at scale</em>.</p><aside>This is a big part of what I'm doing with <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/introducing-substrate">Substrate</a>.</aside><ul><li>We can collect ideas at scale</li><li>We can collect problems at scale</li><li>We can build AI that combines them with all sorts of randomness and errors to produce creative variance</li><li>We can build a system for testing them against the problems</li><li>We can build a system that interprets results and turns that into new ideas for top of funnel</li><li>Etc.</li></ul><aside>There are already lots of startups working on fully-automated labs working on this type real-world testing.</aside><p>The testing part is the most difficult because it often can't be theoretical.</p><p>If we're talking about medicine, for example, you have to actually see if the molecule does what you think it'll do. That means making the actual molecule and exposing it to the pathogen, or whatever. And same with many other types of problems.</p><p>Even without that, though, many other solution types can be tested in a purely digital/modeled environmentâ€”more like A/B testingâ€”and that by itself could multiply the creative output of humanity many times over.</p><aside>RL is already being done on Chain of Thought (CoT) flows, so this would just be an extension to that.</aside><p>Finally, this same model for approaching problemsâ€”which is loosely based around the scientific methodâ€”could serve as content for Reinforcement Learning for future AI.</p><p>As we start to learn the types of approaches to problems that are most fruitful, AI may become <em>generally</em> smarter about proposing smarter initial solutions, as well as iterating faster as we are faced with new problems.</p><aside>And if it achieves ASI, how much further does it take us?</aside><p>Will that takes us all the way to AGI? ASI?</p><p>Impossible to say. But I think it's a promising path.</p><h2 id="summary" tabindex="-1">Summary </h2><ol><li>AGI and ASI only matter in the context of human needs and desires</li><li>One of our general and primary needs is to create new solutions to our problems</li><li>AGI and ASI are on a spectrum of general AI cognitive ability, with ASI being AGI at a superhuman level</li><li>It may be possible to speedrun AI's ability to generate, evolve, and test ideas at scale using some fairly basic automation and AI (not counting the real-world testing piece)</li><li>This could be a path to both AGI and ASI level invention and problem-solving, which could have a tremendous positive impact on society overall.</li></ol><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>ONE-SENTENCE SUMMARY: <em>ASI is just an extension of AGI, and we might be able to get there through scalable creation, mixing, and testing of ideas.</em></li><li>Thanks to Joel Parish and Joseph Thacker for talking through parts of this over the years.</li><li>Here are all my AI Definitions <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/raid-ai-definitions" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">RAID</a></li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Star Wars Fail]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/star-wars-fail?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/star-wars-fail</guid>
            <pubDate>Wed, 02 Apr 2025 09:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Black Jedihttps://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/star-wars-fail-star-wars-black-jedi-promotional/public I'm suddenly re-pissed off about John Boyega's character in Star Wars. But way worse than before because it's all much clearer looking backwards. We basically h...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/star-wars-fail-star-wars-black-jedi-promotional/public" alt="Black Jedi"></p><p>I'm suddenly re-pissed off about John Boyega's character in <em>Star Wars</em>. But way worse than before because it's all much clearer looking backwards.</p><p>We basically had the worst possible version of The Left and Woke with this movie and its casting. The epitome of the worst kind of hidden, modern racism.</p><p>Basically, the franchise got watered-down by the desire to signal wokeness, and they never actually delivered on it. Perhaps there was some good intention there, but if there was it got destroyed by the execution.</p><aside>And yes, I know Sam Jackson was already a Black Jedi, but that still doesn't fix this.</aside><p><em>We were promised a Black Jedi.</em></p><p>Everyone was. That's what the trailers sold us.</p><p>It was a moment of celebration for anyone who was both a <em>Star Wars</em> fan and socially moderate or progressive, and I was thrilled.</p><p>It also looked like he'd be the main character, and possibly the love interest of the female main. It was implied. Not necessary, but it was implied.</p><p>But they did that the whole time knowing that he'd end up being a small, secondary character, with no love interest really. And not a fucking Jedi at all.</p><p>Let me just say it.</p><p><strong>They gave a Black guy a lightsaber for a second to sell tickets during <em>Peak Woke</em>, and then as soon as they reintroduced the real leadâ€”the White leadâ€”the pushed him into the sidelines.</strong></p><p>That's modern elite liberals on race in a nutshell.</p><ul><li>Support BLM but somehow don't have any Black friends who grew up struggling</li><li>Support public housing in theory, but vote against new affordable apartments in your neighborhood</li><li>Support "equality" but are members of clubs that do their best to control the racial makeup of their members</li></ul><p>Why not make him the lead? The <em>actual</em> fucking lead. Why?</p><p>I suppose an answer would be that "they" wouldn't fund it, because "they" are worried about backlash from the masses.</p><blockquote><p>People don't want to see a Black Jedi lead with the White female lead.
