They will skip the movie in protest and accuse us of wokeness...</p></blockquote><p>...or whatever.</p><p>Sure, could be true, but that progress needs to be made <em>some</em> time, and you had the perfect opportunity right there!</p><p>But noâ€”instead of having actual moral courage, and actually moving racial harmony forward, and <em>Star Wars</em> forward, they caved. They pretended to be woke, which seems to be a common theme with the entire movement.</p><p>Nothing harms fighting racism more than <strong>pretending to fight it</strong> while actually promoting it behind closed doors.</p><p>You literally fucking added a Black guy as a member of your Oxford, White-only Gentleman's Club, and then come to find out he's an "Auxiliary" Member, meaning he can't go to meetings and actually works nights watching the parking lot.</p><p>This film is visual crime footage.</p><p>It's visual proof of the disconnect between hollow woke rhetoric and the reality of the world they maintain.</p><p>Signal loud that you're woke. So loud. Show it off. Talk about it at parties. Feel good about yourself. Let the Black guy hold the Lightsaber. And then when Bryce White shows up, scurry the Black guy off the stage and into his support role.</p><p>Fuck you and your fakeness. You're worse than a racist. You're a racist pretending to be an anti-racist for social status.</p><p>Shame.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>I don't have insights into all the ways that this could have gone wrong. Maybe there were/are real anti-racists involved, and they tried to do a good thing. All I'm commenting on is what actually ended up happening, and my comments are on the system that let it happen, not on any particular creatorâ€”because I don't know their hearts. Still, you put your fucking name on it.</li><li>I'm specifically attacking fake liberals here, not real liberals. I'm attacking wokeness as a performance art, not actual progressivism on race. Knowing the difference between these is sadly and ironically becoming a skill you can't find on the left much anymore.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
```
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[I Figured Out What Sam Altman is Doing]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/altmans-plans?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/altmans-plans</guid>
            <pubDate>Sun, 16 Mar 2025 09:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Sam Altman's Futurescape UBI Planhttps://danielmiessler.com/images/sam-altman-futurescape-ubi-plan.png]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/sam-altman-futurescape-ubi-plan.png" alt="Sam Altman's Futurescape UBI Plan" width="1292" height="861" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Coding is Thinking]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/thinking-coding?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/thinking-coding</guid>
            <pubDate>Sun, 16 Mar 2025 00:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[thinking-codinghttps://danielmiessler.com/images/thinking-writing-coding-abstract.png Not learning to code just because there are AI coding agents is like not learning how to think because there are talk shows. - Writing = thinking. - Creating = imagining. - Coding = building. If you're in tech...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/images/thinking-writing-coding-abstract.png" alt="thinking-coding" width="1024" height="1024" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"><p>Not learning to code just because there are AI coding agents is like not learning how to think because there are talk shows.</p><ul><li>Writing = thinking.</li><li>Creating = imagining.</li><li>Coding = building.</li></ul><p>If you're in tech in 2025 and you can't do these things, your career is at risk.</p><p>You have to adapt or you will be replaced.</p><p>Thinkers and creators will be able to build/execute faster than executors will be able to think and create.</p><p>Become a thinker and/or creator. And if you're not sure how to start, start by reading.</p><p>2-4 good reading per month.</p></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[AI''s Ultimate Use Case: Transition from Current to Desired State]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-state-management?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-state-management</guid>
            <pubDate>Fri, 28 Feb 2025 00:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[AISM Miessler February 2024https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/ai-state-management-ai-state-transition-miessler-2025/public When most people think of AI's potential to help humanity in 10, 100, or 1,000 years, they think much too small. They think about what i...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/ai-state-management-ai-state-transition-miessler-2025/public" alt="AISM Miessler February 2024" width="1292" height="1292" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"><p>When most people think of AI's potential to help humanity in 10, 100, or 1,000 years, they think much too small.</p><p>They think about what it does today, and then just imagine "more of that." Like AI agents providing more employees, or tutors, or companions.</p><p>All tremendously useful, but still tactical vs. strategic.</p><p>The best use case I've thought of so far is something far more general. It's <em>a pattern for solving problems generally</em>, as opposed to just a set of one-off solutions.</p><h2 id="current-to-desired-state-transition" tabindex="-1">Current to Desired State Transition </h2><aside>Applies to humans and anything else alive, it seems.</aside><p>One way to characterize the universal challenge for anything alive is <strong>how to go from its current situation to its desired situation</strong>.</p><!----><aside>I wonder if play and meditation are states that break this rule.</aside><p>This applies to finances, employment, starting a business, relationships, raising children, health, and so on. You can imagine this as the eternal problem for anything with goals.</p><p>Ultimately it's a <em>world-model</em> problem. Here are the pieces:</p><aside>Context size seems even more important than intelligence here.</aside><ol><li>Understand how the world works at a very deep level</li><li>Be able to hold the current state of X in its mind</li><li>Be able to hold the desired state of X in its mind</li><li>Use its understanding of the world to determine the required steps to go from the current state to the desired state</li><li>Recommend or take the steps to make it happen</li></ol><aside>Likely one of many end-tier capabilities, such as the invention of new types of science. Impossible to say what they'll be.</aside><p>In other words, one end-tier capability for any intelligenceâ€”human or otherwiseâ€”is the ability to turn any problem into a current-to-desired-state transition problemâ€”at any scale.</p><p>This requires extraordinary understanding of how things work and significant intelligence, but what it needs most is what scientists call <em>working memory</em>. This is like the size of the space that a given intelligence can operate on at any given time.</p><aside>These exercises will be useful at almost any level of compression, but will likely scale in usefulness as the detail increases.</aside><p>It needs to hold all these things in its mind at once and then apply its intelligence, creativity, etc. These current and desired states will be summarized, compressed, and otherwise efficiently minified, but they will still be <em>vast</em>, so working memory (context size) matters a lot.</p><h3 id="fully-contextual-decision-making" tabindex="-1">Fully Contextual Decision Making </h3><p>One of the primary use cases for this model of problem solving comes down to the most common questions ever.</p><ol><li>Should I do this?</li><li>Which of these should I do?</li><li>Is this the right time?</li></ol><p>You ask these questions when you're looking for a life partner, when you're trying to hire employees, trying to raise a child, and a thousand other things.</p><p>Being human means constantly making decisions like these.</p><p>If you squint your eyes a bit, you'll be able to see that even these types of questions are just another way to think about state transition.</p><aside>This is not predicting the future, which is impossible at any scale. It's modeling likely outcomes and how similar they are to your desired state.</aside><p>While we are asking technically different questions, about different situations, and at different times, they can all be reframed as:</p><blockquote><p>Which of these choices will get me closer to my desired state?</p></blockquote><p>A State Managing AI will then model a dozen or a billion futures where you did this vs. that, and then advise you which one to choose.</p><!----><aside>The AI's intelligence will probably be far beyond any human by this time, but it's not even necessary for this to work.</aside><p>It's like asking a human life coach the same thing, except the life coach can't hold petabytes of world knowledge in its mind at the same time and then apply beyond-human intelligence to the analysis.</p><h2 id="examples" tabindex="-1">Examples </h2><p>To illustrate the point further, let's look at some examples of how this model can be applied to common problems in personal life, business, and other everyday challenges.</p><h3 id="dating" tabindex="-1">Dating </h3><p>The eternal problem of finding the right person to build a life with.</p><table tabindex="0"><thead><tr><th style="text-align:left;">Problem</th><th style="text-align:left;">State-Transition Implementation</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td style="text-align:left;">Which of these guys should I choose?</td><td style="text-align:left;">Model out a million lives with him vs. the other guy, using 200 billion life parameters, compare those outcomes to ideal state, provide recommendations</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">Should I still be dating her?</td><td style="text-align:left;">Model out a million lives with her vs. someone more ideal, calculating for current level of bonding and shared experience, compare that to desired state, and advise if you should stay or go</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">Where Should I Go to Meet People?</td><td style="text-align:left;">Model the top meeting places, including online forums, dating apps, bars, churches, hobby spots, etc., and the people likely to hang out in those places. Take representative people and model out a life with them and compare to ideal situation</td></tr></tbody></table><h3 id="customer-churn-at-a-business" tabindex="-1">Customer Churn at a Business </h3><p>One of the most common problems in business: seeing why people are cancelling or not renewing.</p><aside>Note that this could be an interaction type, not a discount or offer.</aside><table tabindex="0"><thead><tr><th style="text-align:left;">Problem</th><th style="text-align:left;">State-Transition Implementation</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td style="text-align:left;">How do I keep this customer from cancelling?</td><td style="text-align:left;">Create a desired state for the customer and compare that to their current state with your company. Advise on what you can say, or change about their expeirence to make their situation best match their ideal state.</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">Which of my current customers are likely to cancel?</td><td style="text-align:left;">Create a current state model for all current customers, create an ideal state where they're perfectly happy with your product/service, analyze the difference between the two, recommend changes / take actions</td></tr></tbody></table><h3 id="perfect-song-company-application" tabindex="-1">Perfect Song Company/Application </h3><p>This is for a startup that takes the context from any situation, like two friends riding bikes together in nature, and plays the perfect song (or soundtrack) as background music</p><table tabindex="0"><thead><tr><th style="text-align:left;">Problem</th><th style="text-align:left;">State-Transition Implementation</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td style="text-align:left;">What's the perfect song to be playing in the background at this moment?</td><td style="text-align:left;">Build the current state maps for all human listeners, build their ideal state maps (e.g., feeling more connected to each other in this intimate moment), look at their individual and shared pasts, pick the perfect song to play to make that moment one they'll remember forever</td></tr></tbody></table><h3 id="other-example-problem-spaces" tabindex="-1">Other example problem spaces </h3><p>These were just a few random examples of the types of problems that can be addressed using this framework. Here is a short list of others that I'm sure you could add 20 more to without much effort.</p><table tabindex="0"><thead><tr><th style="text-align:left;">Problem Space</th><th style="text-align:left;">State-Transition Model</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td style="text-align:left;">Vulnerability Management</td><td style="text-align:left;">Current systems, current vulnerabilities, lack of the worst ones on the most important systems, transition plan, action</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">Tutoring</td><td style="text-align:left;">Current curiosity, self-confidence, knowledge, and curiosity level, ideal self-confidence and competence level, transition plan, incoming questions / challenges, response interactions</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">Starting and Managing a Family</td><td style="text-align:left;">Current state without a partner, determining ideal state with partner and future family, break the steps into pieces, finding a partner, finding a place to live, schools, saving money for college, etc.</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">Career Growth</td><td style="text-align:left;">Current career with title, salary, responsibilities, ideal career with title, salary, responsibilities, transition plan, action</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">Starting a Business</td><td style="text-align:left;">Current life/business state, business idea, ideal business state, ideal life state with that business, transition plan, recommended set of actions, taking action for them</td></tr></tbody></table><aside>I'm sure there are some classes of problem this doesn't apply to, but I can't think of any.</aside><p>Nearly all human challenges can be viewed within this lens of, "I am here, but I wish I were there.", and the more powerful AI becomes the more capable it will be at helping people perform those transitions.</p><h2 id="state-transition-replaces-software" tabindex="-1">State Transition Replaces Software </h2><p>One of the biggest technology implications of this model is that it's a replacement for software.</p><p>Everything we do, and everything we build, is designed to help us change the world to a more desired state. Plumbing is designed to get water from here to there. Cars take people from A to B. Businesses change customer state from unhappy to happy, and your financial state from struggling to comfortable.</p><p>Software is the same. It's technology that helps you understand and manage state changes.</p><ul><li><em>Spreadsheets</em> - help you manage the state of your business, finances, etc.</li><li><em>Customer Relationship Management</em> - helps you manage the state of your relationships with customers</li><li><em>SEO Tools</em> â€” help you manage the state of your website's visibility in search engines</li><li><em>Financial Technology</em> â€” helps you manage the state of your or your customers' wealth</li></ul><h3 id="software-verticals-become-state-transition-use-cases" tabindex="-1">Software verticals become state transition use cases </h3><p>When you have sufficient context about current and desired state for a given problem set, you don't actually need different software.</p><p>You may need different data, and perhaps different types of UI/UX, but ultimately you're dealing with questions and actionsâ€”either point in time or continuously runningâ€”that operate against those different states.</p><aside>Many of these functions are already consolidating into single company offerings.</aside><p>Think about hiring and managing employees inside of a company, and making sure they're working on the right things for the business.</p><p>How many software verticals is that?</p><ul><li>Hiring software</li><li>Employee review software</li><li>Survey software</li><li>Job posting software</li><li>Interview management software</li><li>Employee retention software</li><li>Budget management software</li><li>Project management software</li><li>Etc.</li></ul><aside>Again, these will start as smaller contexts that combine due to the limitations of AI, but they'll quickly merge.</aside><p>Why have all these different software packages when you could just have their source data as part of a unified context that AI can hold in its mind all at once?</p><p>The advantages of this model are many, but the most obvious one is the most powerful.</p><p><em>These pieces all know about each other.</em></p><p>Here's are some examples of common business questions that are excruciatingly manual and time/talent intensive:</p><ul><li>What is everyone at the company working on?</li><li>How good of a job are they doing?</li><li>What gaps do we have in our ability to execute?</li><li>What projects are currently ongoing?</li><li>How much money are we spending on those projects?</li><li>Is our spend aligned with our priorities?</li></ul><!----><ul><li>Who should we hire to help us?</li><li>What efforts should be shut down to reallocate resources?</li><li>What skills do they need?</li><li>What's a good job req for that position?</li><li>Who are our top performers?</li><li>How screwed would be be if they left?</li><li>Such a person just put in their notice; how do we adjust our workload and/or hiring to compensate?</li></ul><p>Answering these types of questions is a full-time job for dozens, hundreds, or thousands of people within a company. And it's highly manual, requiring people to organize meetings, query dozens of systems, collect and analyze data, come up with conclusions, and then do another set of meetings to communicate those conclusions.</p><aside>It's impossible to overstate how frustrating it is to have all this work become stale within days or weeks.</aside><p><em>And that's just one point in time!</em></p><p>In 15-60 days the company has changed so much it needs to be done again. These types of analysis are perpetually stale, and they require many of your best people to try to maintain them.</p><p>AI State Management turns this into questions you ask the system as often as you want, and the answers easily cross all these "software" domains and return better results than if you'd spent weeks doing it manually (which costed millions of dollars in human work).</p><h3 id="project-management-becomes-ai-state-management" tabindex="-1">Project Management Becomes AI State Management </h3><p>As you might have noticed, that work above is often handled by Project Managers. You need multiple, dedicated people to try to do what comes naturally to AIâ€”<em>looking at all the moving pieces at once so you can manage them.</em></p><p>In this model, Project Management becomes questions asked to the system, and answers are returned in seconds rather than weeks or months. In any format you want.</p><p>Oh you need charts for that? Tables organized this way or that way? Reports on current state of the various projects? For different audiences? Those all take seconds to generate.</p><h3 id="product-management-becomes-ai-state-management" tabindex="-1">Product Management Becomes AI State Management </h3><p>The same applies to figuring out what to build next, and how to manage the product development process.</p><ul><li>What's the product we want to build? And why?</li><li>What's the product roadmap?</li><li>What is the problem we're trying to solve?</li><li>How are current customers liking the product?</li><li>What feedback do they have?</li><li>How should we prioritize the backlog?</li></ul><p>These don't remove the need for a Product Manager, actually, which I'm quite happy about. Because certain workâ€”like Project Managementâ€”requires one to be <em>opinionated</em> about what to build and why.</p><p>You can't just listen to customers, and you can't just listen to the engineers. You have to listen to all of them, and executive leadership, and the market, and the competition, and the future, and the past, and the present.</p><p>What this model does, however, is turn the entire endeavor into a current state and desired state problem.</p><p>The vision that comes from leadership, and the product manager, all goes into the desired state. Sentiment and feedback from users goes in as well. The roadmap. The current state of development. The engineers we have working on the team. All the support issues. Etc. Everything is part of the product's context (which is also aware of company goals as well).</p><p>Now the Product Manager can manage the entire thing by interaction with the system.</p><ul><li><em>Give me a list of all features we've shipped in the last 6 months and tell me how much they've addressed the central vision of the product vs. one-off requests by users.</em></li><li><em>Which feature could we build next to best satiate our most vocal and influential detractors online, while at the same time making investors happy?</em></li></ul><p>These types of questions will evaluate years of code changes, support tickets, internal meeting notes, countless design documents, investor comments, social media posts, etc., and respond back in seconds.</p><p><em>Today that's a full-time job for a team of people, takes days or weeks of research across multiple software products, and it's instantly stale as soon as you finish.</em></p><h2 id="summary" tabindex="-1">Summary </h2><ol><li>Human challenges largely reduce to navigating the transition from current state to desired state</li><li>The technology we've built thus far all helps us do this, but it in a fragile and fragmented way</li><li>AI State Management is a model for solving problems that abstracts reality into current state and desired state, and then uses AI to manage the transition between the two</li><li>Software will be replaced by AI State Management because it allows an AI to see related contexts of current and desired state all at once, providing a far more efficient way to accomplish the same tasks</li><li>What are currently software verticals will be replaced by questions asked of, and actions performed by, AI State Management systems</li><li>Much of today's human work inside companies is the continuous and extraordinarily inefficient (and soul crushing) execution of repetitive management tasks that are stale the moment they're completed</li><li>What takes multiple pieces of software, multiple teams of people, and weeks or months of work, will be replaced by a single AI system that can answer questions and take actions across all those domains in seconds</li><li>Most importantly, this model scales across human problemsâ€”from replacing a legacy CRM system, to starting and running a small business, to finding love and starting a family, or pursuing literally any other human goal</li><li>This is already possible today, but the tech is limited by context sizeâ€”i.e., how much world abstraction such a system can hold in its mind at once. This will increase over time, and it's accelerating quickly</li></ol><h2 id="recommendations" tabindex="-1">Recommendations </h2><aside>It will take a year or two for this use case to become common and obvious.</aside><ol><li>If you're managing anythingâ€”whether that's a personal workout plan, a small business, or a giant corporationâ€”start thinking about things as a current and desired state transition problem</li><li>Stop thinking of AI as text generation, summarization, or even as agents that can magnify your workforce. Those are powerful, but they're tactical vs. strategic</li><li>Instead think of AI as a meta-solution to almost any type of problem, where you can ask it to help you get from where you are to where you want to be</li><li>Your job nowâ€”for all current and future effortsâ€”is getting exceptionally good at 1) gathering data sources to build an accurate model of current state, andâ€”most importantlyâ€”<em>deeply thinking about and clearly articulating your ideal state</em></li></ol><p>That's the best part. The human part.</p><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>If/when we achieve ASI, AI State Management will give us the potential to do extraordinary good or evil in the world. Exciting, but it makes it more important than ever to ensure the right people get ASI first.</li><li>Imagine one world government giving its ASI control and saying, "Our desired state is The Federation from Start Trek The Next Generation.", and another world government gives control to theirs and says, "Create the Ideal World for the CCP in China." Very different worlds.</li><li>I built versions of this in early 2023, and have been enhancing it and presenting about it since then. Let me know if you'd like to see it in action, in a cybersecurity context, at small scale.</li><li>An example of what I use in the demos is also available for free on GitHub. <a href="https://github.com/danielmiessler/telos" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">LINK</a> | <a href="https://github.com/danielmiessler/Telos/blob/main/corporate_telos.md" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">CORPORATE CONTEXT FILE</a></li><li>This is essentially a more detailed version of the AI as a Software Replacement System concept I've been talking about since early 2023, most notably with my <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/spqa-ai-architecture-replace-existing-software/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">SPQA</a> post. It's the same idea, but with the implications broadened to (most) all problems.</li><li>Art by Midjourney using the <code>create_art_prompt</code> Fabric prompt on the text of the article.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[How I Think About Politics]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/my-politics?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/my-politics</guid>
            <pubDate>Mon, 17 Feb 2025 00:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Political Spectrumhttps://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/my-politics-political-spectrum/public See full-size I want to say a few things about whatâ€™s happening right now in politics. I especially want to do so because Iâ€™ve supported Elon and Andreessen and similar â€œ...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/my-politics-political-spectrum/public" alt="Political Spectrum" width="1024" height="1024" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high">See full-size<p>I want to say a few things about whatâ€™s happening right now in politics. I especially want to do so because Iâ€™ve supported Elon and Andreessen and similar â€œcentristâ€ types in the pastâ€”and continue to do so on certain topics. I want to explain how and why that is, which turns out requires an overview of how I see things more broadly.</p><h2 id="core-beliefs" tabindex="-1">Core beliefs </h2><p>In a phrase, I am a Star Trek (Next Generation) Liberal, and I would say the following are the tenets of my political / life philosophy:</p><aside>1, 2, 3, and 4 are the center of the philosophy, and subsequent items fall out of their implications. (Credit: Harris).</aside><ol><li>The goal of society is to maximize flourishing and minimize suffering</li><li>Science, compassion, and curiosity are the best mechanisms for pursuing this goal</li><li>Freedom from oppressive authority is a central requirement</li><li>Relentless pursuit of truth is essential, even when difficult or uncomfortable</li><li>Humans technically lack free will, and we must keep #1 in mind when thinking about how to adjust our behavior given this knowledge</li><li>Successful people are lucky peopleâ€”whether due to genes, parenting, environment, or some combination thereofâ€”and should therefore spend significant resources on building a society that gives everyone those same starting parameters</li><li>A mindset of hard work, self-discipline, and ignoring obstacles outside your control is critical to success, and is one of the most important of these starting parameters</li></ol><p>As such, I believe that taxes and social programs are incredibly important to overcome whatever causes the lack of equal opportunity.</p><aside>This doesn't mean I think they should be taken away, or that they're undeserved. It just means I think it should encourage the desire to give back and raise others.</aside><!----><p>I believe the rich are lucky, and the super-rich are super-lucky. Do they usually have good work ethics and self-disciple? Lots of talent and grit? Sure. But those come from luck as well. Good genes, good parenting, good schools, good environment, and/or a mindset bestowed onto them that makes them strive. Those are <em>luxuries</em> granted them by chance, and they shouldn't be. Theyâ€™re luxuries <em>every</em> human should have.</p><aside>I have <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/the-difference-between-classical-liberalism-and-libertarianism">a piece here</a> that gets to the roots of Liberalism, which is significantly about freedom from oppression.</aside><p>Most importantly, you canâ€™t look at someone who doesn't have themâ€”due to who their parents were, a history of discrimination or oppressionâ€”and judge them the same. Itâ€™s societyâ€™s responsibilityâ€”that is, <em>our</em> responsibilityâ€”to give everyone the best possible starting parameters we can.</p><p>When we see places where that's not the case, we have to use science, curiosity, and compassion to make adjustments to society that fix those issues.</p><p>Believing this is the core of what I believe makes someone Liberal, and itâ€™s why I will never stop being one.</p><h2 id="the-ideal-world-heuristic" tabindex="-1">The "Ideal World" Heuristic </h2><p>Aside from core beliefs, I have another way of capturing one's politics without talking about politics at allâ€”and that is to <em>describe the world you want to live in</em>.</p><aside>Individualistic in terms of freedom and creativity, not selfishness.</aside><p><em>I want to live in a multi-racial, multi-ethnic, multi-cultural, and highly individualistic society that values and supports everyone becoming the best version of themselves.</em></p><p>Or, as a set of bullets:</p><aside>Again, the Federation in STTNG is a decent (but not perfect) visual here.</aside><ul><li>People live in harmony with each other, with concepts like race, gender, and religion being extremely secondary</li><li>People are not oppressed by a government or religion</li><li>Society provides extraordinary support to every new person, giving them the education and resources to help them become the absolute best version of themselves</li><li>People can believe many different things, and pursue flourishing in vastly different ways, but they don't allow those beliefs to override the central goal of maximizing flourishing for <em>everyone</em></li><li>Society is largely based around art, exploration, and sharing of positive experiencesâ€”not on maximizing wealth or power</li></ul><p><em>That</em> is my politics.</p><p>It's largely the same as what I put in my tenets above, but it's a nice shortcut to just describe, or refer to, the type of world you wish existed.</p><h2 id="the-questions-i-ask-when-evaluating-someone-s-politics" tabindex="-1">The questions I ask when evaluating someone's politics </h2><p>With that out of the way, it becomes a lot easier to talk about current issues. I keep a few primary questions in mind when I evaluate someone's politics:</p><aside>#2 and #3 are why a rigorous and continuous defense of science and truth are so essential to building a good society.</aside><ol><li>What does <em>their</em> Ideal World look like?</li><li>What policies are they arguing for?</li><li>Do those policies help or hurt their or my Ideal World?</li></ol><p>These questionsâ€”and their answersâ€”are extremely effective at cutting through noise.</p><p>First, if they're trying to build a crappy world I don't want to live inâ€”<em>we've found our problem</em>. Like White Christian Nationalism, or Islamic Theocracy, or Chinese Communism, as examples. No thanks. I'm happily exiting that discussion.</p><p>If their ideal world looks somewhat similar to mine, however, then I move onto the next step, which is talking about policies and their possible implications. This is where the tenets of using science, compassion, radical honesty, and curiosity come inâ€”combined with needing a pretty expansive model of the world. As any economist knows, it's non-trivial to predict the effects of policies.</p><p>So obviously this is more of a discussion sinceâ€”without the science being doneâ€”it's often impossible to know what policy would do what. But at least it's a starting point for a discussion, and it's based on a solid foundation of where we're trying to get to from our respective Ideal Worlds.</p><h2 id="looking-left-right-and-center" tabindex="-1">Looking Left, Right, and Center </h2><aside>I consider myself a pretty clear thinker, but I'm definitely not an expert on politics as a field.</aside><p>Now let's talk about recent politics, going back 20 years or so. Short versionâ€”I've always been a Democrat. I've also only voted Democrat (except for Ron Paul one time back in the day), up to and including Kamala in November of 2024.</p><p>I wasn't thinking as clearly as the above back then, so I couldn't really articulate any of this, but it basically came down to:</p><ol><li>Democrats care about people.</li><li>Republicans care about money.</li></ol><p>Simple as that, so I've always been a Liberal / Democrat for those "reasons".</p><p>But politics got wonky as a hell starting somewhere around 2015 or so. Who knows when exactly that was. Maybe that's just when I started noticing or paying attention. Or perhaps I only then had the mental tools to break down various arguments and see the implications of policies and such.</p><aside>Again, no idea about the nuances of these shifts.</aside><p>Doesn't matter. Basically I think the far left of the last decade or so, but arguably going back to the 70â€™s, has lost its way.</p><p>Theyâ€™ve taken to a primary narrative of telling certain people that theyâ€™re lesser than others. That theyâ€™re disabled. That itâ€™s someoneâ€™s fault. And that they should be angry.</p><p>And worst of allâ€”<em>that this should become their identity</em>.</p><p>Itâ€™s a horrifically bad way to help them advance. In fact itâ€™s a nearly guaranteed way to do the opposite. And it just happens to greatly benefit the largest, most powerful, and best-funded political party in the world.</p><h3 id="the-importance-of-mindset" tabindex="-1">The importance of mindset </h3><p>I think a big part of this wasâ€”and still isâ€”the abandoning of a Stoic, grinder's mindset, and adopting the opposite mindset of victimhood. Not just adopting, but <em>promoting</em> it.</p><aside>This is quite common in immigrant cultures, and especially in Asian immigrant cultures today in the US.</aside><p>To me the grinder mindset is an absolute superpower. It gives people the ability to ignore obstacles and thrive almost regardless of circumstances. It's the mindset that says,</p><blockquote>Sure, racism or sexism might still exist to some degree, but I won't notice because I'll be too busy working and winning.</blockquote><p>This doesn't mean there aren't still problems, or that we should pretend they don't exist. As per our stated goal of maximizing flourishing and minimizing suffering, we should absolutely keep fighting racism, sexism, and other types of discrimination. Obviously.</p><p>But it does mean acknowledging that focusing on negativesâ€”especially as a core aspect of your identityâ€”is absolute <em>poison</em>. The key is to be able to hold both things in your mind simultaneously.</p><h2 id="the-complexity-of-simultaneous-conflicting-truths" tabindex="-1">The complexity of simultaneous conflicting truths </h2><p>I think clear thinking on politics requires an extraordinary ability to deal with complexity, and specifically the ability hold multiple truths in oneâ€™s mind at the same time. Here's an example:</p><ol><li>A 14-year-old Black kid helps rob a liquor store and is in juvenile detention</li><li>A hard-right conservative says, You do the crime you do the time.", and "We can't have a society where people aren't held accountable."</li><li>A hard-left activist says this is all the result of a failed, racist system</li><li>The family's Pastor says Black kids have been robbed of fathers and morality in the home by far-left politics giving Black people a pass on immoral behavior</li><li>An ex-military teacher who cares about the kid tells him to take responsibility, and become a man, and change his ways, because everyone has the power to change their future</li><li>A philosopher reads about it in the news and knows the kid has no free will, so noâ€”technically he can't make choices and he might be screwed</li></ol><p>So which is correct?</p><p><em>All of them</em>.</p><p>They all have truth in them. Politics <em>should be</em> about figuring out what to do once you've realized that.</p><p>Until then, the one thing you <em>can't</em> do is pick one of these angles and argue it to the exclusion of all the others, or dismiss and demonize people who acknowledge the truth in that angle.</p><p>It requires a sophisticated understanding of the world and significant courage, curiosity, and humility to see difficult situations from many perspectives simultaneously. Especially when giving any space to those different viewpoints might suggest something ghastly to a listener who's not thinking in a similar, multi-layered way. Unfortunately, I think this type of nuanced, complexity-based thinking is critical to making progress on anything significant in society.</p><h2 id="narrative-analysis" tabindex="-1">Narrative analysis </h2><p>So, moving closer to the topic of the piece here, let's look at some basic narratives that have been pushed by various groups in recent years.</p><h3 id="far-left" tabindex="-1">Far Left </h3><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/my-politics-far-left/public" alt="Far Left" width="200" height="200" loading="lazy"><ul><li>America is evil</li><li>America is the most racist and sexist country in the world</li><li>If you get ahead, youâ€™re benefiting from oppression, and are therefor basically an oppressor yourself</li><li>We should not talk about individuals and a mindset of overcoming obstacles, and should instead focus exclusively on the past and group identity</li></ul><aside>It's fascinating that both the extreme left and right think there's nothing you can do to escape their primary narrative, which thrives off of hatred and anger.</aside><p>I think one of the things thatâ€™s happened in the last few years is that the spell of this mindset has broken. It broke somewhat because people were tired of being blamed for oppressing others by succeeding, but more so broke because those being told theyâ€™re disabled, and that they should be full of rage, started hearing a different message.</p><p>That message came from people they were supposed to hate, and who were supposed to hate them, and it said,</p><blockquote><p>Youâ€™ve been lied to. We donâ€™t hate you. We hate being told weâ€™re the problem is all. The truth is we just like people who work their asses off and who love this country because itâ€™s pretty cool.</p></blockquote><p>So millions of people who have been told their whole lives that the system, or the man, or whatever, has been holding them down, suddenly realized that the biggest thing holding them down was <em>being told they were being held down</em>. And then they realized that it benefits a single party greatly to keep them believing that. The party that's been telling the lies.</p><p>Well, people woke up. Millions of Latinos and Blacks and Asians realized the whole thing is poisonous, and they went for a narrative that sounded more positive. Something like:</p><blockquote><p>Hard work is the most important thing, and Americaâ€™s not so bad at all.</p></blockquote><p>I voted for Kamala, but I saw that massive rejection of the previous far-left narrative to be a great thing. And I still do.</p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/my-politics-political-spectrum/public" alt="Political Spectrum" width="1024" height="1024" loading="lazy">See full-size<p>That's why in <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/my-politics-political-spectrum-v1/public" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">this diagram</a>, I focus on outer two positions saying "Nothing you do matters", and the inner two positions say, "What you do matters greatly".</p><p>I think this distinction is <em>everything</em>, because in the center it's about how we as people behave, vs. if you're an extremist all that matters is what you were born as.</p><h3 id="far-right" tabindex="-1">Far Right </h3><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/my-politics-far-right/public" alt="Far Right" width="200" height="400" loading="lazy"><p>But it's not so simple as blame it on the left. There's a reason they exist, and those reasons are good. In fact everything they're talking about has a good version.</p><p>DEI, for example, started as a way to remove obstacles to opportunity, and to the extent that it does that I'm all-in. Always will be. Where I can't get behind it is when its modern-day advocates ignore any unpopular discussions around behavioral causes of inequality and focus exclusively on outcomes.</p><aside>I'm worry a lot about this narrative gaining strength.</aside><p>But the Far Right has its own problems. I would say the main counterpart to the Far Left narrative above is one that's less damaging because it's not as prolific, but <em>would actually be much worse</em> if it were widespread. It goes something like this:</p><ul><li>Success is actually just 100% hard work or superior genetics or talent</li><li>Anyone can get ahead, and they all have equal chances to do so</li><li>Racism and sexism are all in the past or are so minor that we should ignore them</li><li>If people fail it's because they weren't talented enough of didn't try hard enough</li><li>Government programs need to be shut down because they just take from the successful</li><li>Things would be perfect if we just went back to God in the schools, women being in the kitchen, there being two genders, and (optionally, depending on degree), white people running everything</li></ul><p>There's obviously a spectrum here, not everyone believes all of these, and the numbers of people supporting that last racial one seem to still be quite small.</p><p>But it's important to call out this narrative and how it can live towards the middle in a nearly-centrist area (for the top 4 items at least), or reach all the way to the Far Right.</p><p>It should now be obvious where I stand on both of these narratives, and more importantly, <em>why</em> I stand there.</p><ol><li>I don't support the Far Right narrative because it doesn't acknowledge the responsibility of people to help others get to an equal place of opportunity, it downplays the need to keep fighting racism and sexism, and in its extreme forms it's just outright racist and authoritarian.</li><li>I don't support the Far Left narrative because it teaches a way of thinking and living that removes agency and responsibility, it vilifies success and striving, and it focuses on the negatives of the past rather than the potential for a better future.</li></ol><h3 id="my-version-of-centrism" tabindex="-1">My version of Centrism </h3><p>I've been calling myself a Centrist for years, which seriously irks all my friends to the right and left of me. Hopefully now you see why.</p><p><em>I'm simply charting to concepts that are separate from left and right.</em></p><p>Left and Right are not truths. They're not absolutes. They're not North Stars. They're labels, descriptions, or tags. We've lost the plot by placing so much importance on them.</p><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/my-politics-centrist/public" alt="Centrist" width="200" height="400" loading="lazy"><p>Anyway, sticking within that Left/Right paradigm that's hard to get away from, what I like about <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/my-politics-political-spectrum-v1/public" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">this political spectrum diagram</a> I put together here is <em>how similar the left center looks to the right center</em>.</p><p>They're largely identical, which makes me happy. What it tells me is that most people are moderate, that we mostly want the same things, and that the differences are not all that significant. It's the edges that are causing the issues.</p><p>If I had to call out a single, centrist narrative it would be something like:</p><blockquote><p>Freedom from oppressive government and religion, the belief that hard work and self-discipline are the most important ingredient for success, and that successful people have a responsibility to give back to society in order to give everyone the opportunities they had.</p></blockquote><aside>The 50's were not an ideal, of course, because not everyone was participating.</aside><p>This also tracks with numerous reading about American society in the 50's, where the middle and upper classes largely lived in the same places, read the same reading and newspapers, drove similar cars, and believed much the same things about society.</p><h2 id="deciding-when-to-support-or-stop-supporting-people-like-musk-rogan-and-andreessen" tabindex="-1">Deciding when to support or stop supporting people like Musk, Rogan, and Andreessen </h2><aside>I'm glad to have had a reason to describe what's above, actually, and you should try it too.</aside><p>So now we arrive at the purpose of this entire exercise above, which is to describe how I can still supportâ€”to some limited or significant degreeâ€”people like Thiel, Rogan, Musk, Andreeseen, etc.</p><p>These are all people who have been vilified by the Far Left for yearsâ€”sometimes deservedly and often not, I would say. And I have disagreed with them vehemently on many, many topics on just as many occasions. But here's theâ€”perhaps not surprisingâ€”answer to the riddle:</p><p><em>The way I judge someone's politics is by trying to determine their version of an Ideal World.</em></p><p>It's that simple.</p><p>I see everyone as multiple people, and I see many people worth fighting for as deeply flawed. When I see kindness, and compassion, and talent, but then I also see damage and flaws, I don't break contact. I don't separate (at least not permanently). That's not what I do. What I do is find the positive core that made me like them in the first place, and I try to lock onto it and nourish it.</p><p>When my friendsâ€”or anyone I care aboutâ€”behave badly, this is how I approach it. Always have. I simply try to extend this to people I know from reading or media or wherever in the world as well.</p><p>Let's look at a couple of these people:</p><ul><li>Joe Rogan: I've been following Joe since like 1994, and what most liberals don't get about him is how openminded and kind he is. Everyone who knows him talks about his kindness, and they've been saying that for decades. He also supports gay people and trans people, and his libertarian on drug use, etc. He's a liberal. But then the Far Left happens and he starts pushing back against that movement, and now he's considered Right or Far Right. He's not.</li></ul><p>I personally think he's <em>very</em> wrong about certain vaccines, and I think he's prone to conspiracy theories at times, but it's hard to know which he actually believes and which he's just having fun with (like his episode with Kat Williams).</p><p>But once againâ€”that's all noise. What world would Joe like to live in? The Far Right one? No. The Far Left one? No.</p><p><em>He's squarely in the middle that I laid out above.</em></p><p>Leave people alone and let them love who they want to. And he constantly says this, over and over. Just recently he was on with someone also considered Right by liberals, and they were talking about how Joe and he support taxation, because programs are absolutely needed to help people in need.</p><p>So do I disagree with Joe sometimes? Does he make me scream in my car sometimes? Yes. But I agree with the version of the world he wants to live in, and I think he's a good person. So I support him.</p><ul><li>Elon Musk: Elon is a lot more complex for me because I am not sure I've properly locked onto him. But I am 75% sure his vision of the future is similar to mine above. I think he's trying to solve the energy crisis, I think he's trying to make AI that brings abundance to the world, etc. In other words I think he's genuinely trying to improve the world, for regular people. For billions.</li></ul><p>That being said, I can't stand him on X. He's mean. He's nasty. He's petty. And he makes me extraordinarily angry sometimes. Then you have this Nazi salute stuff, which has me shopping electric cars.</p><p>So here's where the real calculus comes in that cuts through the noise. This is literally what I ask myself whenever I consider whether to still support him or not.</p><p><em>Am I wrong about the world that he's trying to build?</em></p><p>If he's still trying to build a good, open, liberal world, and he's a traumatized narcissistic asshole sometimes, I think I can still (somewhat) support him. I read a lot of biographies and pretty much everyone you read about who does a lot of good in the world has warts. Often really bad ones.</p><p>But the moment I see, or realize, or get shown, overwhelming evidence that he's actually trying to build the Far Right version of the world, where white people or "special" people win everything and everyone else losesâ€”<em>I'm fucking out.</em>. Instantly.</p><aside>An advantage I have that most do not is that I've read all their reading and watched hundreds of hours of their content. I am not guessing about their opinions; I get them from the source.</aside><ul><li>Peter Theil, Marc Andreeseen, Ben Horowitz, and similar types: For people like this I generally suppport them because, if you read their reading, you can easily tell that they're looking to build a quite liberal society for everyone on earth. Freedom from oppressive government and religion, freedom to love who you want, freedom to work hard and succeed, etc. It's straight down the line of moderation and centrism.</li></ul><p>What I think people are missing about the people above is that they are attacking the left not because they want to bring about the Far Right version of an Ideal World, but because they believe that the Far Left version is the #1 obstacle to the liberal world we all want. Basically they're acting Right because it's the means to counter the Left and build the society that we in the liberal center all want. To me this explains all the Trump support and everything around it.</p><p>And just like with Elon or anyone else on this list, the moment I see otherwise, I can no longer support them.</p><h3 id="constant-re-evaluation-of-peoples-ideal-world-model" tabindex="-1">Constant re-evaluation of peoples' Ideal World model </h3><aside>I also force myself to go to the source since narratives have become so powerful that I can't trust myself to see through them anymore.</aside><p>When I see a new statement from someone, or I hear second or third-hand about a statement from them, I'm always matching that up with what I think their ideal world model is. Is this statement congruent? Does it match perfectly? Is my model wrong? Is their model changing?</p><aside>I support trans people.</aside><p>A good example of this is Trump lately, who I have been criticising aggressively and publicly since like 2015 or so. My Ideal World model is actually giving me a <em>more</em> favorable view of him latelyâ€”despite him implementing a number of policies which I 100% do <em>not</em> supportâ€”, which pushes hard against the extreme dislike I have had for him across all these years.</p><p>When updating my ideal world model on him it's strangely starting to look like:</p><ol><li>Mostly non-committal liberal his whole life</li><li>Probably still mostly liberal or libertarian</li><li>Narcissist, power-hungry</li><li>Disconnected from reality (see 2020 election)</li><li>Uninformed, stupid, or evil on many issues (e.g., banning trans people in various places)</li><li>Wants to be revered as a peacemaker</li><li>Wants to de-nuclearize</li><li>Wants to make friends with Russia and China so everyone can get rich (including him and all his friends)</li><li>Ultimate goal would be a capitalist 1980s world-at-peace where he is cheered everywhere he goes for bringing peace and prosperity to the planet</li><li>Statues, Mouth Rushmore, etc. But not as a warlord or oppressor. As a peacemaker and a builder</li></ol><p>Like, no joke. I seriously think this is what's in his mind, and that drastically changes how I view him.</p><p>Would I vote for him? <em>Hell No.</em> Which is why I didn't. He supports too many policies I can't get behind, and not accepting election results is a non-starter for me.</p><aside>There's an argument I have with my friend Joel Parish about `being vs. doing` evil that's worth having here, and this is an active area of introspection for me.</aside><p>But what I'm trying to convey here is that the differenceâ€”in my mindâ€”between Trump (if I'm right) and a White Nationalist, Neo-Nazi party member trying to come to power is <em>vast</em>. Night and day.</p><p>Honestly I'm still working out the implications of this, in terms of tactics on the ground, but I think it comes down to whether or not I can give credit to people for good goals or good workâ€”even if I don't like other things they've done. Pol Pot might have made a great grilled cheese sandwich, or helped some kids learn to read, but I'm not going to be praising that on social media because I know his past actions and his world model.</p><aside>See what I say about bias in the notes.</aside><p>How it plays out in the real world is when I hear something that makes me say, "What the f***, <code>$PERSON</code>", I do some quick algebra trying to explain it in my current model of them, whether it's a friend, colleague, or someone in the news. That's when I try to figure out if this is a flaw showing itself in a good person, or if it's a sign that my model of them is broken.</p><p>Basically everything hinges on my model of what they're trying to build, and making sure my model of that is accurate.</p><h2 id="the-difference-between-centrism-and-both-sidesing" tabindex="-1">The difference between Centrism and "Both-Sidesing" </h2><p>I viscerally dislike the term "Both-Sidesing" because people don't know how to use it properly. The truly bad version of the termâ€”which I agree is badâ€”is when someone fails to take a position on a critical moral matter, and just gives some shallow talking points that are obviously true on both sides of the debate. It's intellectual and/or moral cowardice.</p><p>The problem is unsophisticated people who can't tell the difference between that and the <em>Complexity</em> we have been talking about. As an example, if you take that same boy in the scenario above, and say,</p><blockquote><p>Well we can't just let people get away with crime, but we also need to realize there's history to take into account.</p></blockquote><p>...you haven't really said anything. You're trying to score points and remain neutral without actually taking a position. That's bad.</p><!----><p>To me the solution to that, which turns this from Both-Sidesing to Complexity is:</p><ol><li>Identify the goal, which is to reduce crime and increase the chances for young Black kids to thrive</li><li>Identify all the various perspectives and truths that exist in the situation, such as the list above</li><li>And then to deliberate deeply in good-faith, ideally assisted by data from a well-run study, to determine the best possible policy to put into place to achieve the goal</li></ol><p>That's not Both-Sidesing. That's taking a messed up, complex reality, and trying to navigate it with compassion, communication, and data.</p><h2 id="answering-the-initial-question" tabindex="-1">Answering the initial question </h2><p>So after all that, I think I can answer the question of why, how, and when I criticize vs. support people like Rogan, Musk, and Andreessen, Horowitz, etc. And really anyone.</p><p><em>I support them when I think they're doing things that are heading in the direction of my ideal world, and criticize them when I think they're doing things that take us away from it.</em></p><p>And I do so within the outer framework of what I think they're <em>ultimately</em> trying to build.</p><aside>It's easy to attack a system that's this simple, but I've not heard anyone even propose a better alternative.</aside><p>Here's how it looks in pseudocode:</p><div class="language-bash line-numbers-mode"><button title="Copy Code" class="copy"></button><span class="lang">bash</span><pre class="shiki github-light vp-code" tabindex="0"><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">if</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;"> assess_ideal_world_model(person</span><span style="color:#24292E;">) == positive:</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6F42C1;">    evaluate</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> policy_or_statement</span><span style="color:#24292E;">(</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;">person</span><span style="color:#24292E;">)</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">      if</span><span style="color:#6F42C1;"> i_support_policy</span><span style="color:#032F62;"> =</span><span style="color:#005CC5;"> true</span><span style="color:#032F62;">:</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6F42C1;">         praise(policy_or_person</span><span style="color:#24292E;">)</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49;">      else</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6F42C1;">         criticize(policy_or_person</span><span style="color:#24292E;">)</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6F42C1;">         re-assess_ideal_world_model(person</span><span style="color:#24292E;">)</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E;">else:</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#6F42C1;">    do_not_support(person</span><span style="color:#24292E;">)</span></span></code></pre><div class="line-numbers-wrapper" aria-hidden="true"><span class="line-number">1</span><br><span class="line-number">2</span><br><span class="line-number">3</span><br><span class="line-number">4</span><br><span class="line-number">5</span><br><span class="line-number">6</span><br><span class="line-number">7</span><br><span class="line-number">8</span><br><span class="line-number">9</span><br></div></div><p>They could do something I really dislike, and think detracts from our shared, positive goal. Such things disappoint me and/or make me angry. If it's bad enough, it forces me to re-evaluate my assessment of their ideal world model. And if <em>that</em> changes to become negative, my support ends there.</p><p>This is my simple system for navigating the complexities of who to support and who not to.</p><h2 id="guarding-against-bias" tabindex="-1">Guarding against bias </h2><p>One other thing to mention about my nuanced support of controversial people like thisâ€”who I often disagree withâ€”is that I have to constantly guard against bias. I'm a nerd. I'm a tech person. I'm an AI person. I have driven Teslas since 2019. Etc.</p><p><em>So maybe I'm just tricking myself.</em></p><p>Maybe all this "complexity" bullshit is justâ€”um, <em>bullshit</em>â€”and really it's just a way to make me feel better about having a self-driving car and benefiting from the elitist world they're actually building.</p><p>I don't believe that, but I believe it's quite possible to deceive oneself in such a way. And specifically, I believe it's possible for <em>me</em> to deceive myself in that way.</p><p>So I have to constantly re-evaluate that. I think the ideal world heuristic is once again helpful here because it takes a lot of my analysis of specific issues out of it. If I see evidence that they're trying to build the far right world, then I have to turn off my "give them more chances" part of the brainâ€”no matter what other positive they are doing or I might have convinced myself they are doingâ€”stop making excuses, and get to the business of opposing them.</p><p>I also rely on my close friends, and people in my community, to help me navigate this as well, and call me out if necessary.</p><h2 id="summary" tabindex="-1">Summary </h2><aside>I say "figure out", because people often can't articulate it.</aside><ol><li>The best way to learn someone's politics is to figure out the world they want to live in</li><li>Political discussion should be more centered around 1) what is your ideal society?, 2) what policies are you arguing for?, and 3) Will those policies help or hurt our chances of getting to our ideal societies?</li><li>I support people based on my interpretation and judgement of the world they'd like to live in, and I am quite forgiving of flaws for those I think are mostly good</li><li>Centrism isn't a weak version of Far Left or Far Right. It's actually a principled position focused around freedom from oppression, hard work, and lifting the less fortunate</li><li>Both the Far Right and Far Left have core narratives that remove agency, as they place your group identity as more important than your behavior as an individual</li><li>I recommend you describe your own Ideal World as a set of tenets like I've done here. It's an extremely enlightening exercise, and will help you cut through a lot of noise in future political discussions</li></ol><div class="mt-4"><h4>Notes</h4><div class="text-sm font-concourse-t3 font-extralight text-gray-500"><!--[--><ol><li>I credit Sam Harris for solidifying my baseline of morality as the minimization of suffering and maximization of flourishing (for conscious creatures), from his book <a href="https://www.amazon.com/Moral-Landscape-Science-Determine-Values/dp/143917122X" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">The Moral Landscape</a>.</li></ol><!--]--></div></div></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[We have Enough AI for AGI]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/we-have-enough-ai-for-agi?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/we-have-enough-ai-for-agi</guid>
            <pubDate>Sat, 15 Feb 2025 00:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[AI Orchestrationhttps://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/we-have-enough-ai-for-agi-orchestration-cogs/public I wrote recently about the difference between Functional and Technical AGI/blog/functional-technical-agi. I argued that Technical AGI is the real deal AI t...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/we-have-enough-ai-for-agi-orchestration-cogs/public" alt="AI Orchestration" width="1024" height="1024" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"><p>I wrote recently about <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/functional-technical-agi">the difference between Functional and Technical AGI</a>.</p><p>I argued that <em>Technical AGI</em> is the real deal (AI that can generalize it's knowledge and learning), and <em>Functional AGI</em> is an <em>emulation</em> that's good enough to replace a human knowledge worker.</p><p>But there's a follow-on point that I want to make here as well.</p><p><strong>I don't think we need any additional model advances to get to Functional AGI.</strong></p><p>I think our best AIs in early 2025 <em>are already smart enough</em> to do the job. They just haven't been properly orchestrated into unified systems that can emulate general intelligence. <em>Yet</em>.</p><!----><p>People keep waiting for <em>the big one</em>â€”some new AI model that will conclusively give us AGI, ASI, or whatever acronym we are currently chasing.</p><p>But I've always thought of AGI as a <em>system</em>, not a model. A set of capabilities, not a single piece of tech.</p><p>So rather than there being one race for AGI, I see it as two.</p><ol><li>The race to make the models/ecosystems smarter.</li><li>The race to build the orchestration that emulates human-like general intelligence.</li></ol><p>I'm simply arguing that #2 does not require #1 anymore. A combination of <code>o3</code>, <code>deepmind</code>, and hellâ€”even <code>gpt-4</code>â€”if built properly into a cohesive system of coordinated agentsâ€”will be able to replicate or exceed human performance on a wide range of knowledge work tasks.</p><p>Orchestrated agent systems, turned into products, and built on 2024-level AI, can get us to Functional AGI. And I'm predicting that happens by late 2025 or sometime in 2026 (and no later than 2028).</p><p>Improvements in the fluid intelligence of the models themselves will continue, of course, but who knows how quickly. And each time that happens, reliance on the orchestration will decrease.</p><h2 id="summary" tabindex="-1">Summary </h2><ol><li>We can get to Functional AGI by models themselves getting there.</li><li>We can also get there by 2024-level AI being properly orchestrated into complex products that emulate general intelligence.</li><li>These tracks of improvement will continue in parallel, but the orchestration track is enough to get us there even if model progress were suddenly frozen tomorrow (like due to a terrorist attack that stopped development through regulation).</li><li>Either way, I expect Functional AGI by 2028, and more likely in 2026 or 2027.</li></ol></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[Functional vs. Technical AGI]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/functional-technical-agi?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/functional-technical-agi</guid>
            <pubDate>Mon, 10 Feb 2025 00:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[Functional vs. Technical AGI Miesslerhttps://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/functional-technical-agi-functional-vs-true/public I think we should make a distinction between Functional AGI and Technical AGI. - Functional AGI is what I use in my definition of AGIhtt...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/functional-technical-agi-functional-vs-true/public" alt="Functional vs. Technical AGI Miessler" width="1024" height="1024" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"><p>I think we should make a distinction between Functional AGI and Technical AGI.</p><ul><li><strong>Functional AGI</strong> is what I use in <a href="https://danielmiessler.com/blog/raid-ai-definitions" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer">my definition of AGI</a>â€”basically an AI system that can replace a knowledge worker from 2022.</li><li><strong>Technical AGI</strong> is more academic and pure, and honestly I donâ€™t feel qualified to even nail it down. But itâ€™s essentially an AI system that can generalize its current knowledge and learning to challenges itâ€™s never seen before.</li></ul><p>I think Functional AGI matters most, and I think itâ€™s likely to come before Technical AGI. And of course, if Technical comes first then it will achieve Functional naturally as a bonus.</p><p>The reason I think Functional AGI matters most is because if an AI system can replace an average knowledge worker, weâ€™re talking about a BILLION knowledge workers being affected (Forbes). It doesnâ€™t matter if itâ€™s not a Pure, or Technical, AGI if it can do the general part of AGI.</p><p>So that means:</p><ul><li>Going to employee onboarding</li><li>Meeting coworkers</li><li>Joining Zoom calls</li><li>Taking direction from management</li><li>Working with peers</li><li>Writing code</li><li>Testing other programmersâ€™ code</li><li>Taking feedback</li><li>Changing direction based on an adjusted project scope</li><li>Sending updates to their management</li><li>Writing reports</li><li>Etc.</li></ul><p>Basically doing lots of different types of work tasks, and adjusting to a dynamic work environment where tomorrow could look different than yesterday.</p><p>My argument is thatâ€”if an AI system can do this to the level of a decent knowledge workerâ€”<em>it doesnâ€™t matter</em> if itâ€™s actually 319 different AI models behind the scenes running some insane orchestration that make us THINK itâ€™s generalized when itâ€™s not. Outwardly, itâ€™s doing enough generalization to replace a human, and that's the part that matters.</p><p>Why is that the key fact?</p><p>Because either way, <em>thatâ€™s a role that a human being isnâ€™t going to fill</em>. The reason could be alien intelligence, smoke, mirrors, or duct tape. Either way, someone either lostâ€”or failed to getâ€”a good-paying job.</p><p>All this to say that we donâ€™t actually need true, technical AGI to happen for AGI to have a monumental impact on society.</p><p>If we hit <em>Functional AGI</em>â€”through some ingenious orchestration of narrowly intelligent agentsâ€”the effect on humans will be the same.</p><p>And itâ€™s that effect on humans that actually matters.</p></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[UL Top 10 AI Risks (v1)]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-risks?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-risks</guid>
            <pubDate>Sat, 08 Feb 2025 00:00:00 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[UL AI Risks Miesslerhttps://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/ai-risks-infographic/public Given the speed of AI improvement and adoption, it's hard for most people to know what to be concerned about, and what to do about it. Sometimes the risk is doing too much too ...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/ai-risks-infographic/public" alt="UL AI Risks Miessler" width="1024" height="1024" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"><p>Given the speed of AI improvement and adoption, it's hard for most people to know what to be concerned about, and what to do about it.</p><!----><p>In this resource I will present what I believe to be the top risks to both businesses and individuals regarding AI. I will order them by severity, and provide guidance on how to avoid the risks.</p><h2 id="personal-ai-risks-pair" tabindex="-1">Personal AI Risks (PAIR) </h2><aside>The list will be updated approximately twice a year.</aside><table tabindex="0"><thead><tr><th style="text-align:left;">ID</th><th style="text-align:left;">Risk</th><th style="text-align:left;">Negative Outcome</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td style="text-align:left;">PAIR1</td><td style="text-align:left;">Failure to Use AI to Grow</td><td style="text-align:left;">You fail to adopt AI to help you learn and improve, and you fall behind in your career and skills and other areas as a result</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">PAIR2</td><td style="text-align:left;">Your Soul is Leaked Online</td><td style="text-align:left;">You trust your entire life, including your most private thoughts, journal, etc., to an AI service, and it gets leaked</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">PAIR3</td><td style="text-align:left;">Failure to Use AI In Your Work</td><td style="text-align:left;">You don't learn AI well enough to compete at a particular job, and you get replaced by people who did</td></tr></tbody></table><h2 id="business-ai-risks-bair" tabindex="-1">Business AI Risks (BAIR) </h2><aside>The list will be updated approximately twice a year.</aside><table tabindex="0"><thead><tr><th style="text-align:left;">ID</th><th style="text-align:left;">Risk</th><th style="text-align:left;">Negative Outcome</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td style="text-align:left;">BAIR1</td><td style="text-align:left;">Failure to Adopt AI</td><td style="text-align:left;">Your company fails to adopt AI fast enough and is passed by companies that do</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">BAIR2</td><td style="text-align:left;">ChatBot</td><td style="text-align:left;">Your company fails to adopt AI fast enough and is passed by companies that do</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">BAIR2</td><td style="text-align:left;">Loss of Trust Due to Hasty Use of AI</td><td style="text-align:left;">You rush to adopt AI and connect it insecurely, resulting in the loss of customer data and trust</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left;">BAIR3</td><td style="text-align:left;"></td><td style="text-align:left;">You rush to adopt AI and connect it insecurely, resulting in the loss of customer data and trust</td></tr></tbody></table></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[My Web Crawling and Scraping Infrastructure]]></title>

        <item>
            <title><![CDATA[AI Novels Are Coming]]></title>
            <link>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-novels?utm_source=rss&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=website</link>
            <guid>https://danielmiessler.com/blog/ai-novels</guid>
            <pubDate>Wed, 29 Jan 2025 05:59:20 GMT</pubDate>
            <description><![CDATA[AI Novelshttps://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/ai-novels-Surreal_Cinematic_Scene_Create/public It's about to get a lot easier to write a decent novel. I'd guess that within 1-3 years, being an "author" of a novel is going to become AI prompting combined with verb...]]></description>
            <content:encoded><![CDATA[<div><img src="https://danielmiessler.com/cdn-cgi/imagedelivery/EcOiF3GdYQuwXdQn9UJBuA/ai-novels-Surreal_Cinematic_Scene_Create/public" alt="AI Novels" width="1024" height="1024" loading="eager" fetchpriority="high"><p>It's about to get a lot easier to write a decent novel. I'd guess that within 1-3 years, being an "author" of a novel is going to become AI prompting combined with verbal narration.</p><p>You'll start with an outline and then have long sessions talking to an AI where you describe the details of what you want. Things like:</p><ul><li>Historical context</li><li>Character pasts</li><li>Character interactions</li><li>Setting specifics</li><li>Vibes you want to convey</li><li>Big ideas you want to explore</li><li>Etc.</li></ul><h2 id="a-new-way-to-write" tabindex="-1">A new way to write </h2><aside>The bar is lower than you might think.</aside><p>As an author, you'll set up the outline of the story in an AI prompt structure, and that structure alone will be good enough for the new AIs to fill in the blanks and ship a decent novel.</p><p>But the best authors will only start with that as scaffolding, and then they'll spend hours upon hours describing settings, environments, aesthetics, andâ€”most importantlyâ€”key character components and interactions between the characters.</p><p>The AI will then take all that custom narrative and description you've provided, which could be tens of thousands of words, and it'll weave that into the scaffolding you provided in the beginning.</p><p>People are going to be able to put out novels like <em>Game of Thrones</em> in a matter of hours or days instead of years or decades. Good authors will be able to put out high-quality, cohesive content around a set of loved characters in a couple of days by just narrating to the AI the different events and different interactions that they want to highlight.</p><h2 id="existing-obstacles" tabindex="-1">Existing obstacles </h2><p>We almost have the tech for this now, but the creativity isnâ€™t quite there yet. And most importantly the <em>context size</em> isn't there yet.</p><p>The key limitation that we have right now is the inability for an AI to hold an entire multi-book novel or series in its brain all at once, and keep all the character detail and plot lines in context as itâ€™s creating new scenes.</p><p>Once AI can do that and actually write in a halfway compelling way, we will see an absolute explosion of good novels. And of course this also applies to other types of creative work like screenplays, scripts, etc.</p><p>Itâ€™s about to be an extra extraordinary time to be an author, but also a reader.</p><p>AI Outlines + Verbal Narration -&gt; Complete Novels.</p></div>]]></content:encoded>
            <author>daniel@danielmiessler.com (Daniel Miessler)</author>
        </item>
    </channel>
</rss>
```
